Derivatives play a vital role in modern financial markets by enabling **risk transfer, price discovery, arbitrage, and speculation**. They allow hedgers to protect against adverse price movements while giving speculators leveraged opportunities.

**The four basic types are:**

1. **Forwards:** OTC contracts between two parties to buy/sell an asset at a future date at a pre-agreed price. *Example:* An Indian IT exporter books a 6-month USDINR forward at ₹83.50 to lock in dollar receivables.

2. **Futures:** Exchange-traded standardized version of forwards, with daily mark-to-market and clearing-house guarantee. *Example:* NIFTY 50 Futures contract on NSE, lot size 25, expiring last Thursday of the month.

3. **Options:** Contracts giving the buyer the *right but not the obligation* to trade. Two variants: **Call** (right to buy) and **Put** (right to sell). *Example:* A trader buys a RELIANCE 2800 CE for ₹45, betting the stock will rise above ₹2845 at expiry.

4. **Swaps:** OTC contracts to exchange cash flows. Common types are **Interest Rate Swaps** and **Currency Swaps**. *Example:* A company with floating-rate debt swaps to fixed rate to avoid interest-rate volatility.

Derivatives enhance market efficiency but also introduce systemic risk if used without adequate risk management. (Word count: ~270)

**(b)** *An investor is confused between investing in a Treasury Bill and a Commercial Paper. Compare the two on the basis of issuer, maturity, risk, and yield.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Treasury Bill (T-Bill) | Commercial Paper (CP) |
|---|---|---|
| Issuer | Government of India (via RBI) | Large corporates (rated A2 or above) |
| Maturity | 91, 182, 364 days | 15 days to 1 year |
| Risk Level | Sovereign / Risk-free | Credit risk of issuing company |
| Liquidity | Very high, RBI repo eligibility | Moderate, secondary market limited |
| Yield | Lower (e.g., 6.5%) | Higher (e.g., 7.5–8.5%) — risk premium |
| Collateral | Sovereign guarantee | Unsecured promissory note |
| Denomination | ₹25,000 and multiples | ₹5 lakh minimum |
| KTU Conclusion | Better for safety-first investors | Better for yield-seeking short-term investors |

The investor should choose **T-Bills** if safety is paramount, and **CP** if slightly higher return is desired and creditworthiness of the issuer is verified. (Word count: ~180)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bond and Debenture:** A *Bond* is a generic term (any debt security), while *Debenture* is the Indian/UK term for corporate debt. Don't claim they are identical in all contexts.
>
> 2. **Confusing Forward with Future:** Most students forget to mention **daily MTM settlement** and **clearing corporation** for futures. This is a 2-mark deduction in 14-mark questions.
>
> 3. **Wrong regulator name:** T-Bills, CP, CD are regulated by **RBI**, NOT SEBI. SEBI regulates securities markets; RBI regulates money markets and banking. This is a frequent 1-mark loss.
>
> 4. **Confusing Call vs Put breakeven:** Call BEP = Strike + Premium. Put BEP = Strike − Premium. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to draw payoff diagram:** In any 7-mark option question, examiners *expect* a payoff diagram. Skipping it = automatic 2-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Writing vague definitions:** Saying *"future contract is a contract for future"* gets zero marks. Use **exact KTU textbook phrases**: "an exchange-traded standardized derivative contract obligating the buyer/seller to transact an asset at a predetermined price on a future date."
>
> 7. **Mixing ETF and Mutual Fund:** An ETF trades on an exchange *intraday*; a Mutual Fund is redeemed at NAV at end-of-day. Confusing these loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- [x] Trading instruments are classified into **Capital Market, Money Market, Derivatives, Pooled Assets, Commodities, and Currency Pairs**.
- [x] **Equity = Ownership + Voting + Variable Return + Last claim in liquidation**.
- [x] **Debenture = Debt + Fixed Coupon + No Ownership + Priority over equity in liquidation**.
- [x] **T-Bill = Zero coupon, 91/182/364 days, RBI-issued, sovereign risk-free**.
- [x] **CP = Corporate, 15–365 days, unsecured, discounted, min ₹5 lakh**.
- [x] **CD = Bank-issued, negotiable, min ₹1 lakh, max 1 year maturity**.
- [x] **Forward = OTC, customized, high counterparty risk, single settlement**.
- [x] **Future = Exchange, standardized, daily MTM, clearing-corp guarantee**.
- [x] **Option = Right but not obligation; max loss = premium; max gain = unlimited (Call) or K − P (Put)**.
- [x] **Swap = OTC exchange of cash flows; IRS = fixed vs floating; Currency Swap = exchange of principals + interest in two currencies**.
- [x] **Mutual Fund = Pooled money, AMC-managed, NAV-based; Open-ended = perpetual; Close-ended = fixed maturity**.
- [x] **ETF = Trades like stock, tracks index, lower expense ratio**.
- [x] **REIT/InvIT = Pooled real-estate / infrastructure income, SEBI regulated, mandatory 90% income distribution**.
- [x] **Commodity trades on MCX (Bullion, Metals, Energy) and NCDEX (Agriculture)**.
- [x] **Currency derivatives on NSE: USDINR lot = 1000 USD; settlement in INR**.
- [x] **SEBI regulates securities; RBI regulates money markets, banking, and FOREX**.
- [x] **Payoff of Forward Long = S_T − K; Short = K − S_T**.
- [x] **Bond price inversely related to YTM** — as YTM ↑, Bond price ↓.
- [x] **Option breakeven (Call) = K + C; (Put) = K − P**.
- [x] **Cost-of-Carry Formula (Forward Price):** $K = S_0 \cdot e^{(r + u - y) \cdot T}$.
- [x] **Indian exchanges to remember: NSE, BSE (equity), MCX (commodity), NCDEX (agri), NSE-CDS (currency)**.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — Trading Instruments in the Market**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Stock Exchanges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** International Stock Exchanges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **International Stock Exchanges** are regulated, organized marketplaces located in different sovereign nations where equities (shares), bonds, derivatives, commodities, and other financial instruments issued by domestic and foreign entities are bought and sold under a standardized legal, operational, and technological framework governed by that country's securities regulator (e.g., SEC in the USA, SEBI in India, FSA in Japan, FCA in the UK).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PEIET745**, an international stock exchange is a **system of interconnected trading infrastructure** — comprising the trading platform (matching engine), clearing corporation, depository (settlement system), and listed issuers — that enables cross-border capital formation and price discovery for globally tradable securities.

Key constituent entities of any international stock exchange:

- **Regulator** — Government body enforcing securities law (e.g., **U.S. SEC**, **UK FCA**, **Japan FSA**).
- **Exchange Operator** — The corporate entity running the trading platform (e.g., **NYSE Group**, **Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing — HKEX**).
- **Clearing House** — Counterparty risk guarantor (e.g., **DTCC**, **LCH**, **JSCC**).
- **Depository** — Holds securities in dematerialized/electronic form (e.g., **Cede & Co.** for NYSE).
- **Members/Brokers** — Licensed intermediaries authorized to execute trades.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Global Farmers' Market"**
> Imagine each country has its own giant, well-organized supermarket where companies come to "rent a shelf" to display their goods (shares) and shoppers (investors) come to buy them.
> 
> - The **supermarket building** = the **stock exchange** (NYSE, LSE, TSE, etc.).
> - The **goods on the shelf** = **listed securities** (shares, bonds, ETFs).
> - The **shopkeeper issuing goods** = **listed companies** (Apple, Tata, Toyota).
> - The **buyers** = **institutional and retail investors**.
> - The **cash counter / receipt system** = **clearing and settlement infrastructure**.
> - The **government inspector** = **securities regulator**.
> 
> An **international** stock exchange is simply one of these "supermarkets" that has earned global recognition — its goods are recognised worldwide, and foreign buyers can walk in (often via ADRs/GDRs) to shop. Some supermarkets (like NYSE or HKEX) are so famous that shoppers fly in from across the world; others (like smaller regional exchanges) primarily serve local neighbourhoods.

## 1.3 Why International Stock Exchanges Matter

- They form the **backbone of global capital flows** — trillions of dollars move through them daily.
- They enable **price discovery** at a global level.
- They allow **diversification** for investors across geographies and currencies.
- They provide **liquidity** — the ability to convert securities into cash quickly without major price impact.
- They are **barometers of economic health** — indices like the **S&P 500**, **FTSE 100**, **Nikkei 225**, and **Hang Seng** are watched as proxies for national and global economic sentiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to identify the **major international exchanges**, explain their **structural differences**, list **representative indices**, and describe **how cross-border trading is enabled** (ADRs, GDRs, dual listings).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Classification of International Stock Exchanges

International exchanges can be classified along three axes:

### A. By Trading Mechanism
- **Open-Outcry / Floor-Based Exchanges** — Traders physically meet on a trading floor (historically NYSE; today, most floors are largely symbolic).
- **Order-Driven / Electronic Exchanges** — All buy/sell orders are matched electronically by a computerized matching engine (NASDAQ, LSE, TSE, HKEX, NSE).

### B. By Listing Profile
- **National/Regional Exchanges** — Predominantly list domestic companies (BSE, KSE).
- **Global International Exchanges** — List companies from many countries and attract massive foreign participation (NYSE, NASDAQ, LSE, HKEX).

### C. By Ownership Structure
- **Member-Owned Mutuals** (older model — historically NYSE).
- **Demutualized Public Companies** (modern model — NYSE listed on its own exchange after 2006 merger with Euronext to form ICE; HKEX is publicly listed; NASDAQ Inc. is publicly traded).

## 2.2 The Major International Stock Exchanges — Quick Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarises the world's leading international stock exchanges. **KTU examiners frequently test these exact facts** in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| Exchange | Country | Estd. | Parent Operator | Benchmark Index | Trading Hours (Local) | Settlement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NYSE** | USA | 1792 | Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) | **Dow Jones Industrial Average** | 09:30 – 16:00 ET | T+1 (post May 2024) |
| **NASDAQ** | USA | 1971 | Nasdaq Inc. | **NASDAQ-100**, **S&P 500** | 09:30 – 16:00 ET | T+1 |
| **LSE** | UK | 1801 | LSEG (London Stock Exchange Group) | **FTSE 100** | 08:00 – 16:30 GMT | T+2 |
| **Euronext** | Pan-Europe | 2000 | Euronext N.V. | **Euronext 100**, **CAC 40** | 09:00 – 17:30 CET | T+2 |
| **Deutsche Börse / Xetra** | Germany | 1585 | Deutsche Börse AG | **DAX 40** | 09:00 – 17:30 CET | T+2 |
| **TSE (Tokyo)** | Japan | 1878 | Japan Exchange Group (JPX) | **Nikkei 225**, **TOPIX** | 09:00 – 15:30 JST | T+2 |
| **HKEX** | Hong Kong | 1891 | Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing | **Hang Seng Index (HSI)** | 09:30 – 16:00 HKT | T+2 |
| **SGX** | Singapore | 1973 | Singapore Exchange Ltd. | **Straits Times Index (STI)** | 09:00 – 17:00 SGT | T+2 |
| **ASX** | Australia | 1987 | ASX Limited | **S&P/ASX 200** | 10:00 – 16:00 AET | T+2 |
| **BSE** | India | 1875 | BSE Ltd. | **BSE Sensex** | 09:15 – 15:30 IST | T+1 |
| **NSE** | India | 1992 | NSE of India Ltd. | **Nifty 50** | 09:15 – 15:30 IST | T+1 |
| **TSX** | Canada | 1861 | TMX Group | **S&P/TSX 60** | 09:30 – 16:00 ET | T+2 |
| **SSE** | China (Shanghai) | 1990 | Shanghai Stock Exchange | **SSE Composite** | 09:30 – 15:00 CST | T+1 |
| **SZSE** | China (Shenzhen) | 1990 | Shenzhen Stock Exchange | **SZSE Component** | 09:30 – 15:00 CST | T+1 |

## 2.3 The Key Indices — Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Market Capitalisation** is the foundational metric for index construction. The general formula applies to almost every international stock exchange.

$$
\text{Market Capitalisation (Market Cap)} = \text{Number of Outstanding Shares} \times \text{Current Market Price per Share}
$$

### Price-Weighted Index (Dow Jones model)

$$
\text{Index Value} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i}{d}
$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Price of stock $i$ in the index.
- $d$ = **Dow Divisor** (adjusted for stock splits, spinoffs, substitutions).

### Market-Cap Weighted Index (S&P 500, FTSE 100, Nikkei 225 TOPIX, Nifty 50 model)

$$
\text{Index Value} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times Q_i)}{C}
$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Price of stock $i$.
- $Q_i$ = Number of shares outstanding for stock $i$.
- $C$ = **Divisor / Adjustment Factor** (base-period market cap).
- $n$ = Number of constituent stocks.

### Free-Float Adjusted Market-Cap Weighted Index (FTSE, MSCI model)

$$
\text{Index Value} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times FF_i \times Q_i)}{C_{ff}}
$$

Where $FF_i$ = Free-float factor (proportion of shares available to public investors).

### GDP-to-Market-Cap Ratio (Buffett Indicator)

$$
\text{Buffett Indicator (\%)} = \frac{\text{Total Market Capitalisation of Exchange}}{\text{Nominal GDP of Country}} \times 100
$$

| Ratio Range | Market Valuation Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- |
| Below 75% | Undervalued |
| 75% – 90% | Fair-valued |
| 90% – 115% | Modestly overvalued |
| Above 115% | Significantly overvalued |

## 2.4 Cross-Border Trading Mechanisms — Real-World Utility

Three instruments allow investors to access international stock exchanges without directly trading in foreign markets:

1. **American Depositary Receipt (ADR)** — A U.S.-dollar denominated, U.S.-traded certificate representing shares of a foreign company. Issued by U.S. depositary banks (e.g., **JPMorgan**, **Citibank**, **BNY Mellon**).
   - **Level 1** — Traded OTC (over-the-counter), no full SEC compliance.
   - **Level 2/3** — Listed on NYSE/NASDAQ with full SEC reporting.

2. **Global Depositary Receipt (GDR)** — Similar to ADR but traded on **multiple international markets simultaneously**, typically in **euros or dollars**, usually in the unregulated or lightly-regulated segments (e.g., London SEAQ, Luxembourg).

3. **Dual Listing / Direct Foreign Listing** — A company lists its shares directly on multiple exchanges (e.g., **Tata Motors** on NYSE + BSE/NSE; **Alibaba** on NYSE + HKEX).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Connection / Real-World Utility:** Although this is a finance course, modern international exchanges rely heavily on **low-latency distributed computing systems**, **FIX protocol messaging**, and **high-frequency matching engines** — areas where KTU Computer Science and Electronics students often cross-skill. Understanding the trading architecture makes algorithmic trading, fintech, and blockchain settlement systems far more intuitive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing a Price-Weighted Index (Dow Jones Style)

**Problem:** A newly formed index has 4 stocks. Calculate the index value.

| Stock | Price ($) |
| :--- | :--- |
| A | 150 |
| B | 90 |
| C | 60 |
| D | 300 |

Initial Dow Divisor $d_0 = 4$ (since the index started as a simple average).

**Step 1 — Sum the prices:**

$$
\sum P_i = 150 + 90 + 60 + 300 = 600
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by the divisor:**

$$
\text{Index Value} = \frac{600}{4} = 150
$$

**Step 3 — Now suppose Stock D undergoes a 3-for-1 stock split.** Its price falls from \$300 to \$100. The divisor must be adjusted so the index value remains unchanged.

New sum of prices:

$$
\sum P_i^{new} = 150 + 90 + 60 + 100 = 400
$$

Set the new index equal to the old index (150) and solve for the new divisor $d_1$:

$$
150 = \frac{400}{d_1} \implies d_1 = \frac{400}{150} = 2.6667
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> The divisor drops from **4** to **2.667**, so future price changes will have a slightly larger relative effect on the index. Stock splits themselves cause no index movement — only subsequent price changes do.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Market-Cap Weighted Index Construction

**Problem:** A 3-stock index has the following data at the base period (Day 0):

| Stock | Shares Outstanding ($Q_i$) | Base Price ($P_i^0$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| X | 1,000 | 50 |
| Y | 500 | 200 |
| Z | 2,000 | 25 |

Compute the index value at Day 0, then re-compute it on Day 1 when prices change to $P_X = 55$, $P_Y = 210$, $P_Z = 23$. Use base value = 100.

**Step 1 — Base market capitalisation:**

$$
\text{Base Market Cap} = (1000 \times 50) + (500 \times 200) + (2000 \times 25)
$$

$$
= 50{,}000 + 100{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 200{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Set the divisor:**

Since the base index = 100, we want:

$$
100 = \frac{\text{Base Market Cap}}{C} \implies C = \frac{200{,}000}{100} = 2{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Day 1 market cap (using Day 1 prices but original share counts):**

$$
\text{Day 1 Market Cap} = (1000 \times 55) + (500 \times 210) + (2000 \times 23)
$$

$$
= 55{,}000 + 105{,}000 + 46{,}000 = 206{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Day 1 index value:**

$$
\text{Day 1 Index} = \frac{206{,}000}{2{,}000} = 103
$$

**Interpretation:** The index rose from **100 to 103**, a **+3%** gain. Note that Stock Z's price fell, but Stocks X and Y rose more in market-cap terms, lifting the index.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — ADR Conversion Ratio Problem

**Problem:** An Indian company **Reliance Industries** has 1 ADR = 4 ordinary shares. The ADR trades on NYSE at **\$60**. The ordinary share trades on NSE at **₹2,400**. The USD/INR spot rate is **₹83/\$**.

**Step 1 — Implied ADR price from Indian market:**

$$
\text{ADR Price}_{\text{implied}} = \frac{4 \times 2{,}400}{83} = \frac{9{,}600}{83} \approx \$115.66
$$

**Step 2 — Arbitrage spread per ADR:**

$$
\text{Spread} = \$60 - \$115.66 = -\$55.66
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** The NYSE ADR is **deeply underpriced** relative to the Indian ordinary share. A trader would **buy ADRs in New York** and (if allowed) **simultaneously sell ordinary shares in Mumbai**, locking in the difference once transaction costs and currency-hedging costs are netted out. In efficient markets, this arbitrage closes the gap quickly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering/Mathematical Note:** Such triangular arbitrage pricing problems are mathematically identical to **No-Arbitrage Pricing Theory** in financial engineering — a critical concept for fintech engineers who design cross-border trading platforms.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Profile of Major International Exchanges

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix consolidates the "must-know" comparative facts. KTU 14-mark questions on this topic often ask for a structured comparison of 3–4 exchanges. Use this table as the template.

| Parameter | NYSE | NASDAQ | LSE | HKEX | TSE (Tokyo) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trading Model | Hybrid (DMM + electronic) | Pure electronic | Electronic (SETS) | Electronic (AMS) | Electronic (arrowhead) |
| Listing Standard | Highest globally | Tech-heavy | High | High (gateway to China) | High |
| Benchmark Index | DJIA | NASDAQ-100 | FTSE 100 | HSI | Nikkei 225 |
| Index Weighting | Price-weighted | Market-cap weighted | Free-float cap-weighted | Free-float cap-weighted | Price-weighted |
| Currency | USD | USD | GBP | HKD | JPY |
| T+1 / T+2 | T+1 | T+1 | T+2 | T+2 | T+2 |
| Foreign Access | ADRs / Direct | ADRs / Direct | GDRs / Direct | Stock Connect | Direct + ADR |
| Regulator | SEC | SEC | FCA | SFC | FSA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of an International Stock Exchange — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIssuer["Listed Companies Issuers"] --> nodeListing["Listing Compliance Department"]
    nodeRegulator["Securities Regulator e.g. SEC FCA FSA"] --> nodeListing
    nodeListing --> nodeTrading["Trading Engine Matching Engine"]
    nodeBroker["Broker Dealer Firms"] --> nodeTrading
    nodeTrading --> nodeClearing["Central Counterparty Clearing House"]
    nodeClearing --> nodeSettle["Settlement Depository"]
    nodeSettle --> nodeInvestor["Institutional and Retail Investors"]
    nodeInvestor --> nodeBroker
    nodeRegulator --> nodeTrading
    nodeRegulator --> nodeClearing
    nodeData["Market Data Feeds Indices"] --> nodeInvestor
    nodeTrading --> nodeData
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The trading engine sits at the heart of every international exchange. Issuers list their securities; brokers route investor orders; the engine matches bids and offers; clearing guarantees the trade; the depository settles it; and market data feeds flow back to investors and regulators.

## 4.2 Cross-Border Trading Architecture (ADR/GDR Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeCo["Foreign Company e.g. Tata Motors"] --> nodeDepo["Depositary Bank e.g. JPMorgan"]
    nodeDepo --> nodeADR["ADR traded on NYSE"]
    nodeADR --> nodeUSInv["US Investors USD"]
    nodeDepo --> nodeUnderly["Underlying Shares held at Indian Depository"]
    nodeCo --> nodeUnderly
    nodeUnderly --> nodeINInv["Indian Investors INR"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A depositary bank holds the underlying foreign shares in custody and issues ADRs in the U.S. market. Each ADR represents a fixed number of underlying shares (the **ADR ratio**). Investors buying ADRs in dollars do not need a brokerage account in the home country, dramatically lowering cross-border friction.

## 4.3 Global Trading Time-Zone Map — Sequential Market Openings

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Sydney ASX 10 00 AEST"] --> nodeB["Tokyo TSE 09 00 JST"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Hong Kong HKEX 09 30 HKT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Mumbai BSE NSE 09 15 IST"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Frankfurt Xetra 09 00 CET"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["London LSE 08 00 GMT"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["New York NYSE NASDAQ 09 30 ET"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world implication:** Because of overlapping sessions (London and New York overlap for ~4.5 hours, when ~50% of global forex turnover occurs), international stock exchanges are **not isolated systems** — they form a **24-hour continuum**, with information flowing across time zones and triggering correlated price movements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an International Stock Exchange. List any four major international stock exchanges along with their benchmark indices.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An international stock exchange is a regulated, organized marketplace located in a specific country where securities issued by domestic and foreign companies are bought and sold under the supervision of that country's securities regulator. Examples of major exchanges and their benchmark indices:

| Exchange | Country | Benchmark Index |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NYSE | USA | Dow Jones Industrial Average |
| NASDAQ | USA | NASDAQ-100 |
| LSE | UK | FTSE 100 |
| Tokyo Stock Exchange | Japan | Nikkei 225 |

**(1.5 marks for listing any four correctly.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between an ADR and a GDR. State one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | ADR (American Depositary Receipt) | GDR (Global Depositary Receipt) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trading Market | Primarily U.S. exchanges (NYSE/NASDAQ) | Multiple international markets simultaneously |
| Currency | U.S. Dollar | Usually USD or Euro |
| Regulation | SEC-regulated (Level 1/2/3) | Often lighter regulation, off-exchange |
| Example | Infosys ADR on NYSE | Reliance Industries GDR on London SEAQ |

**(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for examples.)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the structural architecture of a modern international stock exchange. Describe the role of the **regulator**, **trading engine**, **clearing house**, and **depository** in the trade lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **NYSE**, **NASDAQ**, **LSE**, and **Tokyo Stock Exchange** with respect to their **trading mechanism**, **benchmark indices**, **index-weighting methodology**, and **settlement cycle**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

The structural architecture of a modern international stock exchange consists of **four interacting pillars**:

1. **Securities Regulator (1.5 marks):**
   The regulator (e.g., **SEC** in the U.S., **FCA** in the UK, **FSA** in Japan) is a statutory government body responsible for:
   - Approving listing applications and prospectuses.
   - Enforcing disclosure norms (e.g., quarterly results, material events).
   - Investigating insider trading and market manipulation.
   - Granting broker-dealer licences.

2. **Trading Engine / Matching Engine (1.5 marks):**
   This is the **electronic heart** of the exchange. It receives buy and sell orders from brokers, matches them using algorithms (price-time priority), and generates trade confirmations. Modern engines like NYSE's **Pillar** or NASDAQ's **INET** can handle **millions of messages per second** with sub-millisecond latency.

3. **Clearing House / Central Counterparty (CCP) (2 marks):**
   After a trade is matched, the **CCP (e.g., DTCC, LCH, JSCC)** interposes itself as the buyer to every seller and the seller to every buyer, **eliminating counterparty risk**. The CCP also nets multilateral obligations and calculates **margin requirements** to cover potential defaults. This is the cornerstone of post-trade safety.

4. **Depository / Settlement System (2 marks):**
   Securities are held in **dematerialized/electronic form** in the depository (e.g., **Cede & Co.** for NYSE, **NSDL/CDSL** for India). Settlement — the actual exchange of cash for securities — now happens on a **T+1 (US/India)** or **T+2 (UK/Europe/Japan)** basis, dramatically reducing settlement risk relative to the older T+3 system.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | NYSE | NASDAQ | LSE | TSE (Tokyo) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trading Mechanism** | Hybrid — DMM + electronic | Pure electronic | Electronic (SETS) | Electronic (arrowhead) |
| **Benchmark Index** | Dow Jones Industrial Avg. | NASDAQ-100 | FTSE 100 | Nikkei 225 |
| **Index Weighting** | Price-weighted | Market-cap weighted | Free-float cap-weighted | Price-weighted |
| **Settlement Cycle** | T+1 | T+1 | T+2 | T+2 |
| **Currency** | USD | USD | GBP | JPY |
| **Notable Feature** | Oldest, most IPOs historically | Tech-heavy listings | Gateway to Europe | Largest in Asia by historical volume |

**(Allocation: 1 mark for trading mechanism, 2 marks for indices + weighting, 1 mark for settlement, 3 marks for comparative commentary.)**

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** With the help of a suitable example, explain the mechanism of **American Depositary Receipts (ADRs)**. How do ADRs facilitate cross-border investment? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-stock price-weighted index has the following constituents: **Stock A = \$120**, **Stock B = \$80**, **Stock C = \$200**. The initial divisor is **3**.
  (i) Calculate the initial index value.
  (ii) If Stock B undergoes a 2-for-1 stock split, calculate the new divisor.
  (iii) After the split, if Stock A rises to \$130, Stock B stays at \$40, and Stock C falls to \$180, compute the new index value. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**ADR Mechanism (3.5 marks):**
An **American Depositary Receipt (ADR)** is a U.S.-dollar-denominated negotiable certificate issued by a **U.S. depositary bank** (e.g., JPMorgan, Citibank, BNY Mellon) that represents a specific number of underlying shares of a **foreign company**. The depositary bank purchases the foreign shares in the home market and holds them in custody, while issuing ADRs to U.S. investors who can then trade them on NYSE or NASDAQ just like domestic shares.

**Example — Infosys ADR:**
Infosys Ltd. (India) has ADRs trading on NYSE under ticker **INFY**. Each ADR represents **1 ordinary share** of Infosys. Indian investors trade Infosys in rupees on NSE; U.S. investors trade INFY in dollars on NYSE. The depositary bank handles all currency conversion, dividend repatriation, and corporate-action forwarding.

**Levels of ADRs (1.5 marks):**
- **Level 1** — Traded OTC, minimal SEC disclosure. Most common.
- **Level 2** — Listed on a U.S. exchange, full SEC reporting but no capital raise.
- **Level 3** — Listed on a U.S. exchange, full SEC reporting, allows the foreign company to raise capital in the U.S.

**How ADRs facilitate cross-border investment (2 marks):**
1. **Currency convenience** — U.S. investors transact in USD; no foreign exchange account needed.
2. **Regulatory simplification** — Single SEC compliance framework.
3. **Dividend repatriation** — Depositary bank converts and forwards dividends.
4. **Liquidity** — ADRs can be traded during U.S. market hours, providing real-time price discovery.
5. **Lower transaction costs** — No need for a foreign brokerage account.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Initial Index Value (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Index}_0 = \frac{P_A + P_B + P_C}{d_0} = \frac{120 + 80 + 200}{3} = \frac{400}{3} \approx 133.33
$$

**(ii) New Divisor after Stock B's 2-for-1 split (2.5 marks):**
After the split, Stock B's price halves: $80 \to 40$. The divisor must be adjusted so the index value remains unchanged at 133.33.

New sum of prices:

$$
\sum P_i^{new} = 120 + 40 + 200 = 360
$$

Solve for new divisor $d_1$:

$$
133.33 = \frac{360}{d_1} \implies d_1 = \frac{360}{133.33} = 2.70
$$

**(iii) New Index Value after subsequent price changes (3 marks):**

Updated prices: $P_A = 130$, $P_B = 40$, $P_C = 180$.

$$
\sum P_i = 130 + 40 + 180 = 350
$$

$$
\text{Index}_{new} = \frac{350}{2.70} \approx 129.63
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite Stock A rising from \$120 to \$130, the combined effect of Stock B's halved price and Stock C's fall from \$200 to \$180 pulled the index down from **133.33 to 129.63** — a **−2.78% decline**. This illustrates the well-known **drawback of price-weighted indices**: high-priced stocks (regardless of company size) dominate the index movement.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps on This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "NYSE" with "Dow Jones & Company"** — NYSE is the *exchange*; the DJIA is the *index* published by S&P Dow Jones Indices. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if these are mixed up.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up Price-Weighted vs. Market-Cap Weighted** — The Dow Jones is **price-weighted**, not market-cap weighted. The S&P 500, FTSE 100, and Nifty 50 are **market-cap (free-float) weighted**. Confusing these will cost you 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the divisor adjustment in stock splits** — In price-weighted index problems, students often forget to recalculate the divisor after a stock split, leading to a wrong index value. Always state: *"The divisor is adjusted to keep the index unchanged immediately after the split."*
> 
> 4. **ADR vs. GDR confusion** — ADRs are *American* (U.S. market, USD, SEC-regulated). GDRs are *Global* (multiple markets, often USD/EUR, lighter regulation). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 
> 5. **Currency abbreviations** — Always write **USD, GBP, JPY, HKD, INR, EUR** — never ambiguous shorthand. Examiners penalise informal notation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — International Stock Exchanges**

- An **international stock exchange** is a regulated marketplace for trading securities, governed by a national securities regulator.
- The **four pillars** of any modern exchange are: **Regulator → Trading Engine → Clearing House → Depository**.
- **Top 5 exchanges by market capitalisation** (current global ranking): **NYSE, NASDAQ, Shanghai Stock Exchange, Euronext, Japan Exchange Group**.
- **Price-weighted index** = $\sum P_i / d$ → Example: **Dow Jones Industrial Average**.
- **Market-cap weighted index** = $\sum (P_i \times Q_i) / C$ → Examples: **S&P 500, NASDAQ-100, FTSE 100, Nifty 50**.
- **Free-float adjusted** variants exclude promoter/stakeholder-locked shares — used by **FTSE, MSCI, Hang Seng**.
- **Settlement cycle** — Global standard is shifting to **T+1** (US since May 2024, India since Jan 2023); Europe/UK/Japan still on **T+2**.
- **ADR** = U.S.-traded, USD-denominated receipt for a foreign share (e.g., Infosys INFY, Tata Motors TTM).
- **GDR** = Traded on multiple international markets, often off-exchange, lighter regulation.
- **Time-zone continuum** — Sydney → Tokyo → Hong Kong → Mumbai → Frankfurt → London → New York enables a **24-hour global trading cycle**.
- **Buffett Indicator** = Total Market Cap / GDP — a broad valuation yardstick.
- The **divisor** in price-weighted indices is **not fixed**; it adjusts for stock splits, spinoffs, and substitutions to keep the index continuous.
- **Cross-border arbitrage** in ADRs vs. underlying shares is mathematically grounded in the **Law of One Price**, modulo transaction costs and currency hedging.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indian Stock Exchanges - NSE, BSE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indian Stock Exchanges - NSE, BSE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Stock Exchange? — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stock Exchange (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A stock exchange is a regulated, organized marketplace where securities such as equities (shares), bonds, derivatives, and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are bought and sold under standardized rules, transparent price-discovery mechanisms, and the supervision of a recognized regulatory authority.

In the Indian context, the apex regulator is the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)**, established under the SEBI Act, **1992**. Every stock exchange in India must obtain recognition from SEBI under Section 4 of the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, **1956**.

### The Two Pillars of Indian Stock Exchanges

India currently hosts several recognized stock exchanges, but **two exchanges dominate nearly all trading volume**:

| Exchange | Full Name | Established |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **BSE** | Bombay Stock Exchange | **1875** (Asia's oldest) |
| **NSE** | National Stock Exchange | **1992** (incorporated) / **1994** (commenced trading) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Supermarket for Shares"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a stock exchange as a **giant supermarket for company ownership certificates (shares)**.
>
> Just as a supermarket:
> - Brings together **buyers** (shoppers) and **sellers** (companies/owners) under one roof,
> - Has **fixed operating hours**,
> - Displays **standardized prices** for every item,
> - Maintains **rules and quality checks**,
> - Provides a **billing counter (clearing & settlement system)**
>
> A stock exchange does the same for shares and securities. Without it, shares would be sold like old cars — privately, with no transparency, no fair price, and high risk of fraud.

---

## 1.3 Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) — Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **BSE Limited** (formerly Bombay Stock Exchange) is the **oldest stock exchange in Asia** and one of the oldest in the world. It was established in **1875** as "The Native Share & Stock Brokers' Association" by **Premchand Roychand** and a group of brokers under a banyan tree on Dalal Street, Mumbai.

### Key Features of BSE
- **Location:** Dalal Street, Fort, Mumbai — often called the "Wall Street of India."
- **Trading Platform:** BSE migrated fully to the **BOLT (BSE On-Line Trading) Plus** system and currently uses the **BSE EEB (Enterprise E-Business) Platform**.
- **Listed Companies:** Over **5,000+ companies** (one of the largest exchanges globally by number of listed entities).
- **Index:** Benchmark index is the **S&P BSE Sensex (Sensitive Index)** — comprising **30 companies**.
- **Market Capitalization:** Over **₹300+ lakh crore** (as per recent SEBI data).
- **Demutualization:** Became a demutualized, corporate entity in **2005**.

---

## 1.4 National Stock Exchange (NSE) — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NSE** was incorporated in **1992** under the Companies Act, 1956, as a tax-paying company, *not* as a mutual body of brokers. It was set up at the recommendation of the **Pherwani Committee (1991)** to bring transparency, automation, and efficiency to Indian capital markets.

### Key Features of NSE
- **Location:** Plot No. C/1, "Exchange Plaza", Bandra-Kurla Complex (BKC), Mumbai.
- **Trading Platform:** **NEAT (National Exchange for Automated Trading) System** — one of the most advanced trading platforms globally.
- **Listed Companies:** Around **2,000+ companies** (selective listing criteria).
- **Index:** Benchmark index is the **NIFTY 50** — comprising **50 companies** (managed by NSE Indices Limited).
- **Market Capitalization:** Over **₹350+ lakh crore** (the highest in India by trading volume).
- **Firsts:** NSE was the **first exchange in India** to launch:
  - **Index futures (2000)**
  - **Stock options (2001)**
  - **Currency derivatives (2008)**
  - **Interest rate futures**

---

## 1.5 Regulatory & Operational Umbrella

Both NSE and BSE operate under a **three-tier regulatory framework**:

> **Tier 1:** Ministry of Finance (Government of India) — Policy-level oversight
> **Tier 2:** SEBI — Market regulator
> **Tier 3:** The Exchanges themselves — Self-regulatory organizations (SROs)

---

## 1.6 The Settlement System

> [!NOTE]
> Both exchanges use the **T+1 (Trade plus one day)** settlement cycle for equities, implemented fully from **January 2023** (post SEBI's directive). This replaced the older T+2 cycle.
>
> **Definition (T+1):** If a trade is executed on Day T, settlement (i.e., payment and share transfer) is completed on the next working day (T+1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural & Functional Architecture of an Exchange

A modern stock exchange is built on **five interlocking subsystems**:

1. **Trading Engine (Matching System):** Matches buy and sell orders using algorithms (price-time priority).
2. **Order Routing Network:** Connects brokers to the exchange.
3. **Clearing House:** Interposes itself between buyer and seller, becoming the **counterparty** to eliminate settlement risk.
   - NSE → Clearing done by **NSE Clearing Limited (NCL)**.
   - BSE → Clearing done by **Indian Clearing Corporation Limited (ICCL)**.
4. **Settlement System:** Handles actual delivery of shares and funds.
5. **Surveillance System:** Real-time monitoring for manipulation, insider trading, and anomalies.

---

## 2.2 Price Discovery & Order Matching — How a Trade Happens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Price-Time Priority Rule (KTU High-Yield Concept):** On both NSE and BSE, the matching engine follows a strict two-tier priority:
>
> 1. **Price Priority:** The highest bid (buy) and lowest ask (sell) are matched first.
> 2. **Time Priority:** If multiple orders are at the same price, the one entered earlier gets executed first.

### Order Types Every Student Must Know

| Order Type | Symbol/Tag | Behavior |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Market Order | MKT | Executes immediately at the best available price |
| Limit Order | LMT | Executes only at the specified price or better |
| Stop-Loss Order | SL | Becomes a market order once a trigger price is hit |
| Stop-Loss-Market | SL-M | Above variant — guaranteed execution, not price |
| Good-Till-Cancelled | GTC | Remains active until executed or cancelled |
| Immediate-or-Cancel | IOC | Executes immediately (full or partial), rest cancelled |

---

## 2.3 Indices — The Barometers

> [!NOTE]
> An **index** measures the price performance of a basket of stocks relative to a base period. It serves as a proxy for the overall market sentiment.

### Major Indian Indices

| Index | Exchange | Constituents | Base Year | Base Value | Calculation Method |
|-------|----------|--------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------|
| **S&P BSE Sensex** | BSE | **30** | 1978-79 | **100** | Free-float Market Cap Weighted |
| **NIFTY 50** | NSE | **50** | 1995 | **1000** | Free-float Market Cap Weighted |
| **BSE 500** | BSE | 500 | 1999-2000 | 1000 | Free-float Market Cap Weighted |
| **NIFTY Next 50** | NSE | 50 | 1996 | 1000 | Free-float Market Cap Weighted |
| **Bank NIFTY** | NSE | 12 PSU + Pvt banks | 2000 | 1000 | Free-float Market Cap Weighted |
| **BSE MidCap** | BSE | 150 (approx.) | 2001-02 | 1000 | Free-float Market Cap Weighted |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All KTU-expected numerical and conceptual formulas for this topic are consolidated below. Memorize these for the **Part B 14-mark questions**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Market Capitalization** | $MC = P \times N$ | $P$ = Current Market Price per share (₹), $N$ = Total Number of Outstanding Shares |
| 2 | **Free-Float Market Cap** | $FFMC = P \times FF$ | $FF$ = Free-float shares (excludes promoter holding) |
| 3 | **Index Value (Market-Cap Weighted)** | $I_t = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t}}{B} \times I_0$ | $I_t$ = Index at time $t$, $P_{i,t}$ = Price of stock $i$, $Q_{i,t}$ = Free-float quantity, $B$ = Base market cap, $I_0$ = Base index value |
| 4 | **Index Divisor (for adjustments)** | $D_{new} = D_{old} \times \dfrac{MC_{before}}{MC_{after}}$ | Used during corporate actions (splits, bonus, replacement) |
| 5 | **Index Return (% change)** | $R = \dfrac{I_t - I_{t-1}}{I_{t-1}} \times 100$ | Percentage change between two index levels |
| 6 | **Beta ($\beta$) of a Stock** | $\beta_i = \dfrac{Cov(R_i, R_m)}{Var(R_m)}$ | Sensitivity of stock $i$ to market $m$ |
| 7 | **Simple Return** | $R = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ | One-period price return (no dividends) |
| 8 | **Total Shareholder Return** | $TSR = \dfrac{(P_t - P_{t-1}) + D_t}{P_{t-1}} \times 100$ | $D_t$ = Dividend per share during the period |
| 9 | **P/E Ratio (Price-to-Earnings)** | $\dfrac{P}{E} = \dfrac{Market\ Price}{EPS}$ | $EPS$ = Earnings per share |
| 10 | **Turnover Ratio (Liquidity Metric)** | $Turnover = \dfrac{Value\ of\ Trades}{Average\ Market\ Cap}$ | Higher = more liquid market |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

- **Algorithmic & High-Frequency Trading (HFT):** Engineers design low-latency matching engines, co-located servers, and FPGA-based order routing.
- **Risk Management Systems:** VaR (Value-at-Risk) computation needs real-time index data — directly relevant to financial engineering.
- **Data Analytics:** Index values, beta, and free-float data power portfolio optimization models used by every mutual fund.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Index Value Using Market-Cap-Weighted Method

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **foundational derivation** for understanding how Sensex and NIFTY are calculated. KTU frequently tests this in the 14-mark section.

### Step 1 — Define the Base Period

Let:
- Base period (e.g., 1978-79 for Sensex, 1995 for NIFTY 50)
- Base year market capitalization of all index constituents $= MC_{base}$
- Base index value $= I_{base}$ (Sensex = 100; NIFTY 50 = 1000)

### Step 2 — Define the Index Formula

$$
I_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{i,t} \times FF_{i,t})}{MC_{base}} \times I_{base}
$$

**Where:**
- $I_t$ = Current index value at time $t$
- $P_{i,t}$ = Current market price of constituent stock $i$
- $FF_{i,t}$ = Free-float shares of stock $i$
- $n$ = Number of constituents (30 for Sensex, 50 for NIFTY)
- $MC_{base}$ = Total free-float market cap at the base period

### Step 3 — Introduce the Divisor

Since corporate actions (splits, bonuses, rights, stock replacements) alter the market cap without reflecting real market movement, an **index divisor** $D$ is used:

$$
I_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{i,t} \times FF_{i,t})}{D}
$$

**Where:**
- $D$ = Index Divisor (adjusted each time a corporate action occurs)
- Initially, $D = \dfrac{MC_{base}}{I_{base}}$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (Sensex-style)

Suppose we have a simplified **5-stock Sensex clone**:

| Stock | Price (₹) | Free-Float Shares (Cr) | Market Cap (₹ Cr) |
|-------|----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| A | 800 | 50 | 40,000 |
| B | 1,200 | 30 | 36,000 |
| C | 500 | 60 | 30,000 |
| D | 950 | 25 | 23,750 |
| E | 1,100 | 20 | 22,000 |
| **Total** | — | — | **1,51,750** |

**Step 4a:** Calculate total market cap (base period).

$$
MC_{base} = 40{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 23{,}750 + 22{,}000 = 1{,}51{,}750 \text{ ₹ Cr}
$$

**Step 4b:** Calculate initial divisor (taking $I_{base} = 100$).

$$
D = \frac{MC_{base}}{I_{base}} = \frac{1{,}51{,}750}{100} = 1{,}517.5
$$

**Step 4c:** Suppose next day prices change to: A = 820, B = 1,190, C = 510, D = 940, E = 1,120. Compute new market caps:

| Stock | New Price | FF Shares (Cr) | New MC (₹ Cr) |
|-------|----------|----------------|---------------|
| A | 820 | 50 | 41,000 |
| B | 1,190 | 30 | 35,700 |
| C | 510 | 60 | 30,600 |
| D | 940 | 25 | 23,500 |
| E | 1,120 | 20 | 22,400 |
| **Total** | — | — | **1,53,200** |

**Step 4d:** Compute new index value (assuming no corporate action, divisor unchanged).

$$
I_{new} = \frac{1{,}53{,}200}{1{,}517.5} = 100.956
$$

**Step 4e:** Compute percentage change.

$$
\Delta I \, (\%) = \left(\frac{100.956 - 100}{100}\right) \times 100 = 0.956\%
$$

### Step 5 — Handling Corporate Actions (Adjusting the Divisor)

Suppose Stock A undergoes a **1:1 bonus** (every shareholder gets one free share). Free-float shares of A double from 50 Cr to 100 Cr, but the price halves from ₹800 to ₹400 (theoretically). New MC of A = $400 \times 100 = 40{,}000$ — **unchanged**. The divisor remains the same.

But suppose a **new stock F** replaces E with a different market cap. We adjust the divisor:

$$
D_{new} = D_{old} \times \frac{MC_{old}}{MC_{after\ replacement}}
$$

This ensures the index remains continuous and comparable over time.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: NSE vs BSE — KTU Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most exam-relevant comparison** for KTU. Memorize every row.

| Parameter | **NSE** | **BSE** |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Full Name** | National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. | BSE Ltd. (formerly Bombay Stock Exchange) |
| **Year Established** | 1992 (incorp.) / 1994 (trading) | 1875 |
| **Age Ranking (Globally)** | Newest major Indian exchange | **Asia's oldest** |
| **Location** | Bandra-Kurla Complex, Mumbai | Dalal Street, Fort, Mumbai |
| **Form of Organization** | Tax-paying company (from inception) | Demutualized in 2005 |
| **Trading Platform** | NEAT (National Exchange for Automated Trading) | BOLT Plus / BSE EEB |
| **Benchmark Index** | NIFTY 50 | S&P BSE Sensex |
| **Number of Constituents** | 50 | 30 |
| **Base Year** | 1995 | 1978-79 |
| **Base Value** | 1,000 | 100 |
| **Listed Companies** | ~2,000+ | ~5,000+ (largest in world) |
| **Trading Volume** | **~95%+ of equity derivatives** volume in India | Higher in some cash segment stocks |
| **Clearing House** | NSE Clearing Ltd. (NCL) | Indian Clearing Corp. Ltd. (ICCL) |
| **Settlement Cycle** | T+1 | T+1 |
| **First to Launch** | Index futures (2000), stock options (2001), currency derivatives (2008) | First exchange in Asia (1875) |
| **Global Ranking (by Market Cap)** | Top 10 globally | Top 10 globally |
| **Key Innovation** | Fully electronic, screen-based from day 1 | Shifted from open-outcry to electronic |
| **Index Provider** | NSE Indices Ltd. | Asia Index Pvt. Ltd. (JV with S&P Dow Jones) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Market Capitalization

**Question:** A company has 10 crore outstanding shares. The current market price is ₹450. The promoter holds 3.5 crore shares. Calculate:
1. Total Market Capitalization
2. Free-Float Market Capitalization

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Total Market Cap

$$
MC_{total} = P \times N = 450 \times 10 = 4{,}500 \text{ ₹ Crore}
$$

**Part (b):** Free-Float Shares = Total − Promoter

$$
FF = 10 - 3.5 = 6.5 \text{ Crore shares}
$$

$$
FFMC = 450 \times 6.5 = 2{,}925 \text{ ₹ Crore}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Index providers like NSE and BSE use **FFMC**, not total market cap, because promoter holdings are not actively traded and would distort index representation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow — Indian Stock Exchange Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Investor[Retail / Institutional Investor]
    Broker[Stock Broker - Member of Exchange]
    TradingApp[Trading Terminal / Mobile App]
    NEAT[NEAT System - NSE]
    BOLT[BOLT Plus System - BSE]
    MatchingEngine[Order Matching Engine]
    NCL[NSE Clearing Ltd - NCL]
    ICCL[Indian Clearing Corp Ltd - ICCL]
    Depository[Depository - NSDL / CDSL]
    BankSettlement[Bank Settlement - Funds]
    Company[Listed Company - Issues Shares]
    SEBI[SEBI - Regulator]
    Govt[Ministry of Finance]

    Investor --> TradingApp
    TradingApp --> Broker
    Broker --> NEAT
    Broker --> BOLT
    NEAT --> MatchingEngine
    BOLT --> MatchingEngine
    MatchingEngine --> NCL
    MatchingEngine --> ICCL
    NCL --> Depository
    ICCL --> Depository
    NCL --> BankSettlement
    ICCL --> BankSettlement
    Company --> Broker
    SEBI -.Regulates.-> NEAT
    SEBI -.Regulates.-> BOLT
    SEBI -.Regulates.-> NCL
    SEBI -.Regulates.-> ICCL
    Govt --> SEBI
```

---

## 4.2 T+1 Settlement Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Day T - Trade Day: Order Matched on NEAT or BOLT]
    Confirm[Trade Confirmation - T+0 itself by EOD]
    T1[Day T+1 - Settlement Day: Shares Debited Seller / Credited Buyer]
    Funds[Funds Debited Buyer / Credited Seller via Bank]
    Close[Trade Closed]

    T0 --> Confirm
    Confirm --> T1
    T1 --> Funds
    Funds --> Close
```

---

## 4.3 Index Calculation Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Constituents[Index Constituents Stocks: 50 or 30]
    Prices[Real-time LTP - Last Traded Price]
    FFShares[Free-float Share Quantity]
    CorpAction[Corporate Action Module]
    Divisor[Index Divisor D]
    Formula[Index Formula: Sum of Pi x QFi / D]
    Output[Final Index Value]

    Constituents --> Prices
    Constituents --> FFShares
    Prices --> Formula
    FFShares --> Formula
    CorpAction --> Divisor
    Divisor --> Formula
    Formula --> Output
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — NSE vs BSE Block-Level

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NSE
        N1[Established: 1992]
        N2[Platform: NEAT]
        N3[Index: NIFTY 50]
        N4[Listed: 2000 Plus]
        N5[Clearing: NCL]
        N6[Volume: Dominant in Derivatives]
    end

    subgraph BSE
        B1[Established: 1875]
        B2[Platform: BOLT Plus]
        B3[Index: Sensex]
        B4[Listed: 5000 Plus]
        B5[Clearing: ICCL]
        B6[Volume: Strong in Cash Segment]
    end

    Common[Common: SEBI Regulation / T+1 Settlement / Mumbai HQ]
    NSE --> Common
    BSE --> Common
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a stock exchange. List any two functions of a stock exchange.

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

A stock exchange is an organized, regulated marketplace where securities such as shares, bonds, and derivatives are bought and sold under standardized rules and the supervision of a recognized regulatory authority (in India, SEBI).

**Two functions of a stock exchange:**

1. **Price Discovery:** It provides a transparent platform where the forces of demand and supply interact continuously, leading to the discovery of fair market prices for securities.

2. **Liquidity Provision:** By bringing together a large number of buyers and sellers, the exchange ensures that investors can convert their securities into cash quickly, providing liquidity to investments.

*(Other acceptable functions: Mobilization of savings, capital formation, fair dealing, benchmarking via indices, dissemination of information.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Differentiate between NSE and BSE based on any three parameters.

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

| Parameter | NSE | BSE |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Year Established | 1992 | 1875 (Asia's oldest) |
| Benchmark Index | NIFTY 50 (50 stocks) | S&P BSE Sensex (30 stocks) |
| Listed Companies | ~2,000+ | ~5,000+ (largest globally) |
| Trading Platform | NEAT system | BOLT Plus |
| Clearing Arm | NSE Clearing Ltd. | Indian Clearing Corp. Ltd. |

NSE dominates the derivatives segment (~95%+ volume) while BSE has wider listed company coverage.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Confusing NIFTY vs Sensex constituents:** Many students write "Sensex has 50 stocks" — this is **wrong**. Sensex = 30, NIFTY = 50.
> 2. **Using total market cap instead of free-float:** Index weights are based on **free-float** market cap, not total. Marks are deducted heavily if this distinction is missed.
> 3. **Forgetting SEBI's role:** Always mention SEBI as the regulator in any question about Indian exchanges.
> 4. **Wrong settlement cycle:** Always write **T+1** (not T+2) for current KTU examinations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the structure and functions of the National Stock Exchange (NSE) of India. Discuss its trading platform NEAT in detail.

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of NSE:**
- Incorporated in 1992 under the Companies Act, 1956 as a public limited company.
- Set up at the recommendation of the Pherwani Committee (1991) to bring automation and transparency to Indian capital markets.
- Headquarters: Bandra-Kurla Complex (BKC), Mumbai.
- Became operational for wholesale debt market in 1994 and equities in 1994 itself.
- Owned by a consortium of leading Indian financial institutions — no single broker member holds more than a small percentage.

**Functions of NSE:**
1. Provides a screen-based, anonymous, order-driven trading platform.
2. Ensures fair price discovery through real-time matching.
3. Offers trading across multiple asset classes — equities, derivatives (futures & options), currency derivatives, debt instruments.
4. Provides clearing & settlement services through its wholly-owned subsidiary, **NSE Clearing Ltd. (NCL)**.
5. Disseminates market data and maintains benchmark indices (NIFTY 50, NIFTY Bank, etc.) through NSE Indices Ltd.

**NEAT (National Exchange for Automated Trading) System:**
- Fully automated, screen-based trading system deployed by NSE.
- Supports **price-time priority** order matching.
- Provides direct market access to brokers via VSATs and leased lines with redundancy.
- Capable of handling **millions of orders per day** with sub-millisecond latency.
- Enables algorithmic trading, co-location services, and DMA (Direct Market Access).
- Was a pioneer — NSE was the **first exchange in India to offer fully electronic trading**.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a suitable example, explain how the NIFTY 50 index is calculated using the free-float market capitalization method.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** NIFTY 50 is a free-float market capitalization-weighted index comprising 50 large-cap, liquid stocks listed on NSE. The base period is the financial year 1995, with a base value of **1,000**.

**Formula:**

$$
I_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{50} (P_{i,t} \times FF_i)}{D}
$$

Where $P_{i,t}$ is the current price of stock $i$, $FF_i$ is the free-float shares, and $D$ is the index divisor.

**Worked Example:**

Suppose the total free-float market cap of all 50 NIFTY stocks today is ₹80,00,000 crore. The current divisor is ₹80,000 crore. Then:

$$
I_t = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = 10{,}000
$$

So the NIFTY would stand at 10,000.

**Adjustment for Corporate Actions:**
Whenever a corporate action (bonus, split, rights, stock replacement) occurs, the divisor $D$ is adjusted so that the index value remains continuous. The formula used is:

$$
D_{new} = D_{old} \times \frac{MC_{before\ action}}{MC_{after\ action}}
$$

This ensures that artificial changes (e.g., from bonus shares) do not affect the index level.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the history, structure, and unique features of the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE).

**Model Answer:**

**History of BSE:**
- Established in **1875** as "The Native Share & Stock Brokers' Association" by Premchand Roychand and a group of brokers.
- Became Asia's first stock exchange.
- Originally operated as an **open-outcry** system on Dalal Street, Mumbai.
- The infamous **1992 Harshad Mehta scam** exposed weaknesses in the trading mechanism, prompting reforms.
- Migrated to electronic trading via the **BOLT (BSE On-Line Trading)** system in 1995.
- Was demutualized (separated ownership, management, and trading rights) in **2005**.
- Renamed "BSE Ltd." in 2017.

**Structure:**
- Recognized stock exchange under the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956.
- Regulated by SEBI.
- Headquartered at Dalal Street, Fort, Mumbai — the iconic Phiroze Jeejeebhoy Towers (also called the BSE building).

**Unique Features:**
1. **Largest by listed companies** in India (~5,000+).
2. Oldest exchange in Asia and one of the oldest globally.
3. Benchmark index is **S&P BSE Sensex (30 stocks)**, computed jointly with S&P Dow Jones.
4. Trading hours: 9:15 AM to 3:30 PM (pre-open session from 9:00–9:15 AM).
5. Settlements handled by **Indian Clearing Corporation Ltd. (ICCL)**.
6. Pioneered the introduction of **SME (Small and Medium Enterprise) Platform** in India.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the trading and settlement systems of NSE and BSE. Explain the T+1 settlement cycle.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison of Trading & Settlement Systems:**

| Feature | NSE | BSE |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Trading Platform | NEAT (National Exchange for Automated Trading) | BOLT Plus / BSE EEB |
| Order Matching | Price-Time Priority | Price-Time Priority |
| Trading Hours | 9:15 AM – 3:30 PM | 9:15 AM – 3:30 PM |
| Pre-Open Session | 9:00 AM – 9:15 AM | 9:00 AM – 9:15 AM |
| Clearing Corporation | NSE Clearing Ltd. (NCL) | Indian Clearing Corp. Ltd. (ICCL) |
| Settlement Cycle (Equity) | T+1 (since Jan 2023) | T+1 (since Jan 2023) |
| Depository Link | NSDL & CDSL | NSDL & CDSL |

**T+1 Settlement Cycle Explained:**

Under the T+1 settlement cycle:

- **Day T (Trade Day):** Order is matched and trade is executed.
- **Day T+1 (Next Working Day):** 
  - Shares are debited from the seller's demat account and credited to the buyer's demat account.
  - Funds are debited from the buyer's bank account and credited to the seller's bank account.
- **Net Effect:** The buyer becomes a shareholder on T+1 itself, and the seller receives cash on T+1.

**Advantages of T+1:**
1. **Lower Counterparty Risk:** Faster settlement reduces the window for default.
2. **Better Liquidity:** Investors receive cash/shares sooner, enabling redeployment.
3. **Lower Margin Requirements:** Reduced risk means lower capital blocked.
4. **Global Competitiveness:** Aligns India with developed markets moving toward T+0.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Indian Stock Exchanges**

- **BSE** = Established **1875**, oldest in Asia, Sensex (30 stocks), base 100, ~5,000+ listed.
- **NSE** = Incorporated **1992**, NIFTY 50 (50 stocks), base 1,000, ~2,000+ listed.
- Both exchanges are headquartered in **Mumbai** (Dalal Street vs BKC).
- Both are regulated by **SEBI** (Securities and Exchange Board of India, est. 1992).
- **Trading hours:** 9:15 AM to 3:30 PM (Pre-open: 9:00–9:15 AM).
- **Settlement:** **T+1** cycle (effective from January 2023).
- **Order priority:** Price priority → Time priority.
- **NSE clearing arm:** NSE Clearing Ltd. (NCL). **BSE clearing arm:** ICCL.
- **Index methodology:** Both Sensex and NIFTY use **free-float market capitalization** weighting.
- **Index formula:** $I_t = \dfrac{\sum P_i \times FF_i}{D}$
- **NSE Firsts:** Index futures (2000), Stock options (2001), Currency derivatives (2008).
- **Demutualization:** BSE in 2005; NSE was a company from inception (1992).
- **Depositories:** NSDL and CDSL — both linked to NSE and BSE for share settlement.
- **Key committees to remember:**
  - Pherwani Committee (1991) — recommended NSE.
  - Bhave Committee & various SEBI committees shaped modern market structure.
- **Key government acts:**
  - Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, **1956**.
  - SEBI Act, **1992**.
- **Common indices to know:** Sensex, NIFTY 50, Bank NIFTY, NIFTY Next 50, BSE 500, BSE MidCap.
- **BSE has the most listed companies globally** — a unique trivia often asked in KTU viva.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — Indian Stock Exchanges (NSE, BSE).**
> All formulas, derivations, comparisons, and KTU-style questions above are aligned with the **PEIET745 — Introduction to Stock Markets and Trading** syllabus under the KTU 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stock Market Indices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stock Market Indices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stock Market Index (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A *stock market index* is a statistical measure that reflects the composite value of a basket of carefully selected representative stocks. It is constructed using a standardized mathematical methodology (weighted by price, market capitalization, or equal-weighting) and serves as a benchmark to gauge the overall health, direction, and performance of a specific market, sector, or economy.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEIET745**, an index is treated as a *barometer* of the equity market. Since directly measuring the value of thousands of listed companies is impractical, index providers (like **NSE**, **BSE**, **S&P Dow Jones**, or **FTSE Russell**) select a representative sample of stocks and compute a single numeric value that tracks their collective performance.

The two most critical Indian indices highlighted in the KTU syllabus are:

- **BSE Sensex (Sensitive Index)** — comprises **30** large-cap, financially sound companies listed on the Bombay Stock Exchange.
- **NSE Nifty 50** — comprises **50** large-cap stocks listed on the National Stock Exchange, covering **13 sectors** of the Indian economy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Class Average

Imagine a classroom of **60 students** with varying heights. It would be cumbersome to report every student's height daily. Instead, the class teacher calculates a **Class Average Height** using a predefined formula. If the average goes up, the class (collectively) has grown taller. If it falls, the class has shrunk.

A *stock market index works exactly like this average*. The "students" are the constituent companies, the "average" is the index value, and any rise or fall in the index tells us whether the broader market (the "class") has gone up or down.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** An index does not represent the literal price of any stock. It is an *abstract, normalized indicator*. Two investors looking at the Nifty 50 at **22,147.50** understand the market sentiment instantly — no need to analyze 50 stocks individually.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Index Construction

When studying this topic for KTU exams, students must memorize these foundational constants:

- **Base Year** for BSE Sensex: **1978–79** (Base Value = **100**)
- **Base Year** for NSE Nifty 50: **3 November 1995** (Base Value = **1000**)
- **Free-float Market Capitalization** is the standard weighting methodology used by **Nifty 50** and **S&P 500**.
- **Price Weighting** is used by the **Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA)**.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often ask the *base year* and *base value* of major indices. Always remember **Sensex = 100** and **Nifty = 1000**. This distinction is a guaranteed **2-mark** short answer in KTU Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Stock Market Indices?

Indices serve **four primary economic functions** in financial markets:

1. **Benchmark for Performance Evaluation:** Portfolio managers compare their fund's returns against an index (e.g., "Our fund beat the Nifty 50 by 4%").
2. **Indicator of Market Sentiment:** Rising index = bullish (optimistic) market; Falling index = bearish (pessimistic) market.
3. **Basis for Passive Investment Products:** **Index Funds** and **Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs)** replicate an index's composition.
4. **Macroeconomic Barometer:** Economists and policymakers track indices to assess the financial health of a nation.

## 2.2 Classification of Indices by Weighting Methodology

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires understanding the **three principal methods** of index construction:

### A. Price-Weighted Index
The weight of each constituent stock is proportional to its **share price**.

$$PWI_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i,t}}{D_t}$$

Where:
- $P_{i,t}$ = Price of stock *i* at time *t*
- $D_t$ = *Divisor* (adjusted for stock splits, bonus issues, etc.)
- $n$ = Number of constituent stocks

> **Example:** The **Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA)** is the world's most famous price-weighted index.

### B. Market Capitalization (Market-Cap) Weighted Index
The weight of each stock is proportional to its **total market capitalization** (price × shares outstanding).

$$MCWI_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t})}{D_t}$$

Where:
- $P_{i,t}$ = Price of stock *i*
- $Q_{i,t}$ = Total shares outstanding of stock *i*
- $D_t$ = Base market cap divided by base index value (the divisor)

### C. Free-Float Market Capitalization Weighted Index
A refinement of (B) where only the **shares available for public trading** (excluding promoter holdings, government stakes, etc.) are counted.

$$FFMCWI_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{i,t} \times FF_{i,t})}{Base\_FFMC} \times Base\_Value$$

> **Example:** Both the **NSE Nifty 50** and **S&P 500** use this method.

### D. Equal-Weighted Index
Each constituent has the **same weight**, regardless of size or price.

$$EWI_t = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{i,t}$$

Where $R_{i,t}$ is the simple return of stock *i*.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Index Type** | **Weight Basis** | **Key Formula** | **Real-World Example** | **Base Value** |
|----------------|------------------|-----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Price-Weighted | Share Price | $\sum P_i \div \text{Divisor}$ | DJIA (30 stocks) | 100 |
| Market-Cap Weighted | Price × Total Shares | $\sum (P_i \times Q_i) \div \text{Divisor}$ | Nikkei 225 (modified) | Variable |
| Free-Float Cap Weighted | Price × Free-Float Shares | $\sum (P_i \times FF_i) \div \text{Base FFMC}$ | Nifty 50, S&P 500 | 1000 / 100 |
| Equal-Weighted | Same Weight per Stock | Arithmetic mean of returns | Some thematic ETFs | 1000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **divisor** $D_t$ is a *scaling factor* that prevents stock splits, bonuses, and inclusions/exclusions from distorting the index's continuity. KTU examiners frequently test the *role of the divisor* in price-weighted indices.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Financial Engineering

In modern quantitative finance, indices underpin:

- **Algorithmic Trading:** Passive funds must replicate index movements in real-time.
- **Derivatives Pricing:** **Index futures and options** (e.g., Nifty futures) are settled based on the index value.
- **Risk Management:** The **Beta** of a stock is calculated against a benchmark index: $\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$.
- **Wealth Creation Products:** **SIPs (Systematic Investment Plans)** and **index funds** democratize equity investing for retail KTU graduates entering the workforce.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Price-Weighted Index Calculation

We shall now derive the value of a hypothetical 3-stock price-weighted index step by step. This mirrors the construction methodology of the **Dow Jones Industrial Average**.

### Given Data:

| **Stock** | **Initial Price ($)** | **Shares Outstanding (Million)** |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| A Ltd.    | 250                  | 40                               |
| B Ltd.    | 400                  | 25                               |
| C Ltd.    | 150                  | 60                               |

### Step 1 — Compute Initial Sum of Prices

$$
S_{\text{initial}} = P_A + P_B + P_C
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
S_{\text{initial}} = 250 + 400 + 150 = 800
$$

### Step 2 — Set the Initial Divisor

For a price-weighted index, the initial divisor equals the **number of constituent stocks** when no adjustments are pending.

$$
D_{\text{initial}} = n = 3
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Initial Index Value

$$
\text{Index}_{\text{initial}} = \frac{S_{\text{initial}}}{D_{\text{initial}}} = \frac{800}{3} \approx 266.67
$$

### Step 4 — Adjustment After a Stock Split

Suppose **B Ltd.** undergoes a **2-for-1 stock split** (each share becomes 2). The new price of B becomes **$200**.

The sum of prices now becomes:

$$
S_{\text{new}} = 250 + 200 + 150 = 600
$$

If we naïvely used the divisor as **3**, the index would artificially drop to **200**, falsely indicating a market crash. To maintain **index continuity**, we recalculate the divisor:

$$
D_{\text{new}} = \frac{S_{\text{new}}}{\text{Index}_{\text{pre-split}}} = \frac{600}{266.67} \approx 2.25
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Post-Split Index Value

$$
\text{Index}_{\text{post-split}} = \frac{600}{2.25} \approx 266.67
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The index remains **unchanged at 266.67** despite the stock split. This is the *core purpose of the divisor* — to absorb corporate actions without distorting the index level. This exact concept is a guaranteed question in **KTU Part B**.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Free-Float Market-Cap Weighted Index (Nifty 50 Style)

### Given Data:

| **Stock** | **Price ($)** | **Total Shares (Crore)** | **Promoter Holding (%)** | **Free-Float Shares (Crore)** |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| X Ltd.    | 500           | 10                       | 40%                      | 6                             |
| Y Ltd.    | 800           | 5                        | 20%                      | 4                             |
| Z Ltd.    | 300           | 20                       | 50%                      | 10                            |

### Step 1 — Compute Free-Float Market Capitalization

For **X Ltd.**:

$$
FFMC_X = 500 \times 6 = 3000 \text{ Crore}
$$

For **Y Ltd.**:

$$
FFMC_Y = 800 \times 4 = 3200 \text{ Crore}
$$

For **Z Ltd.**:

$$
FFMC_Z = 300 \times 10 = 3000 \text{ Crore}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum Total Free-Float Market Cap

$$
\text{Total FFMC} = 3000 + 3200 + 3000 = 9200 \text{ Crore}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Index Value

Assuming the **Base FFMC** is **4600 Crore** and **Base Value** is **1000**:

$$
\text{Index}_t = \frac{\text{Current FFMC}}{\text{Base FFMC}} \times \text{Base Value} = \frac{9200}{4600} \times 1000 = 2000
$$

> [!TIP]
> The **weight** of each stock in the index is therefore:
> - **X Ltd.:** $3000 \div 9200 = 32.61\%$
> - **Y Ltd.:** $3200 \div 9200 = 34.78\%$
> - **Z Ltd.:** $3000 \div 9200 = 32.61\%$
>
> Notice **Y Ltd.** has the highest weight despite a smaller share count, because it has the highest *price × free-float* product.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Price-Weighted (DJIA)** | **Market-Cap Weighted** | **Free-Float Cap Weighted** | **Equal-Weighted** |
|---------------|---------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------------------|--------------------|
| Weighting Variable | Stock Price | Total Shares × Price | Free-Float Shares × Price | Uniform |
| Sensitivity to High-Priced Stocks | Very High | Moderate | Moderate | None |
| Reflects True Market Value | Poorly | Well | Very Well | Poorly |
| Adjustment for Splits | Divisor | No divisor change | No divisor change | Rebalanced periodically |
| Popularity | Declining | High | Highest (Global Standard) | Niche |
| Used in Indian Markets | No | No | Yes (Nifty, Sensex) | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Stock Market Index Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Stock Market Universe] --> node2[Selection Criteria Definition]
    node2 --> node3[Universe Filtering: Liquidity, Market Cap, Listing History]
    node3 --> node4[Constituent Stock Selection Buffer]
    node4 --> node5{Weighting Methodology}
    node5 --> node6[Price Weighted Module]
    node5 --> node7[Market Cap Weighted Module]
    node5 --> node8[Free Float Cap Weighted Module]
    node5 --> node9[Equal Weighted Module]
    node6 --> node10[Apply Divisor Adjustment]
    node7 --> node11[Compute Total Cap]
    node8 --> node12[Compute Free Float Cap]
    node9 --> node13[Arithmetic Mean Return]
    node10 --> node14[Final Index Value Output]
    node11 --> node14
    node12 --> node14
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node15[Benchmark for Funds ETFs Derivatives]
    node14 --> node16[Macroeconomic Barometer]
    node14 --> node17[Investor Sentiment Indicator]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — How an Index Is Rebalanced

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Periodic Review Cycle 6 months] --> nodeB[Data Snapshot Free Float Market Cap]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Apply Eligibility Filters Liquidity Market Cap]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Stock Meets Criteria}
    nodeD --> nodeYes[Include in Index] --> nodeE[Update Constituent List]
    nodeD --> nodeNo[Exclude from Index] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Calculate New Divisor if required]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Publish New Index Composition]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Index Funds ETFs Replicate Changes]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Global Indices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[Global Stock Market Indices] --> nodeUS[United States Indices]
    nodeRoot --> nodeIN[Indian Indices]
    nodeRoot --> nodeEU[European Indices]
    nodeRoot --> nodeAS[Asian Indices]
    nodeUS --> nodeDJIA[Dow Jones Industrial Average 30 stocks Price Weighted]
    nodeUS --> nodeSP500[S and P 500 500 stocks Free Float Cap Weighted]
    nodeUS --> nodeNASDAQ[NASDAQ Composite 3000 plus stocks]
    nodeIN --> nodeSensex[BSE Sensex 30 stocks Free Float Cap Weighted Base 100]
    nodeIN --> nodeNifty[NSE Nifty 50 50 stocks Free Float Cap Weighted Base 1000]
    nodeIN --> nodeBankNifty[Bank Nifty 12 banking stocks]
    nodeIN --> nodeMidCap[Nifty Midcap 100]
    nodeEU --> nodeFTSE[FTSE 100 London]
    nodeEU --> nodeDAX[DAX 40 Frankfurt]
    nodeEU --> nodeCAC[CAC 40 Paris]
    nodeAS --> nodeNikkei[Nikkei 225 Tokyo]
    nodeAS --> nodeHangSeng[Hang Seng Hong Kong]
    nodeAS --> nodeShanghai[Shanghai Composite China]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Flowchart — Role of the Divisor in Price-Weighted Indices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Corporate Action Detected] --> nodeType{Type of Action}
    nodeType --> nodeSplit[Stock Split]
    nodeType --> nodeBonus[Bonus Issue]
    nodeType --> nodeInclusion[New Stock Added]
    nodeSplit --> nodeComputeNew[Compute New Sum of Prices]
    nodeBonus --> nodeComputeNew
    nodeInclusion --> nodeComputeNew
    nodeComputeNew --> nodeDivisor[Recalculate Divisor D New equals S New divided by Old Index Value]
    nodeDivisor --> nodeContinuity[Index Value Remains Unchanged Continuity Preserved]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a stock market index. List any two major Indian indices with their base year and base value.**

**Model Answer:**

A stock market index is a statistical measure that tracks the performance of a predefined basket of stocks, providing a snapshot of market behavior. It serves as a benchmark for investors and an indicator of economic health.

> The two major Indian indices are:
> 1. **BSE Sensex** — Base Year: **1978–79**, Base Value: **100**
> 2. **NSE Nifty 50** — Base Year: **3 November 1995**, Base Value: **1000**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a price-weighted index and a market-capitalization weighted index with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Price-Weighted Index** | **Market-Cap Weighted Index** |
|---------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Basis of Weight | Share Price | Market Capitalization |
| Sensitivity | High-priced stocks dominate | Larger companies dominate |
| Adjustment Method | Uses a Divisor | No divisor needed |
| Example | DJIA | S&P 500 (in modified form) |

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks from any module, with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the construction methodology of the **NSE Nifty 50** index. Why is the *free-float market capitalization* method preferred over the simple market-cap method?

**Model Solution:**

The Nifty 50 index is constructed and maintained by **NSE Indices Limited (formerly India Index Services & Products Ltd. — IISL)**. The construction involves **four stages**:

1. **Universe Definition:** All equity stocks listed on the NSE form the parent universe.
