## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

This section contains the **rigorous analytical work** that KTU examiners love. We work through three numerical problems, every algebraic step shown.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Issue Size, EPS, and P/E Ratio

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**

> Bharat Tech Ltd. has a pre-Issue share capital of 80 lakh equity shares of ₹10 each. The company plans to issue 20 lakh new shares at a premium of ₹40 per share. The expected Net Profit After Tax (NPAT) for the next financial year is ₹6 crore. The market price of comparable listed companies trades at a P/E of 15. Calculate:
> (a) Issue Price
> (b) Total Issue Size
> (c) Post-Issue Capital
> (d) Expected EPS
> (e) Expected Market Price (using comparable P/E)
> (f) Expected Listing Gain (%)

**Solution (Every Step Shown):**

**Given Data:**
- Pre-Issue shares: 80,00,000
- Face Value: ₹10
- New shares issued: 20,00,000
- Premium: ₹40
- NPAT: ₹6,00,00,000 (₹6 crore)
- Comparable P/E: 15

---

**(a) Issue Price**

The issue price is the sum of the face value and the premium.

$$
\text{Issue Price} = \text{Face Value} + \text{Premium}
$$

$$
\text{Issue Price} = 10 + 40 = 50
$$

**Answer:** The Issue Price = **₹50 per share**.

---

**(b) Total Issue Size**

The total amount of money the company will raise is the issue price multiplied by the number of new shares offered.

$$
\text{Issue Size} = \text{Issue Price} \times \text{New Shares Offered}
$$

$$
\text{Issue Size} = 50 \times 20{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Issue Size} = 1{,}000{,}00{,}000 = 10 \text{ crore}
$$

**Answer:** The Total Issue Size = **₹10 crore**.

---

**(c) Post-Issue Capital**

The post-issue capital is the sum of pre-issue shares and new shares issued.

$$
\text{Post-Issue Capital} = \text{Pre-Issue Shares} + \text{New Shares}
$$

$$
\text{Post-Issue Capital} = 80{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}
$$

**Answer:** The Post-Issue Capital = **1 crore shares**.

---

**(d) Expected EPS**

The EPS is the Net Profit After Tax divided by the total number of equity shares outstanding after the issue.

$$
\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{NPAT}}{\text{Total Post-Issue Shares}}
$$

$$
\text{EPS} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{EPS} = 6
$$

**Answer:** The Expected EPS = **₹6 per share**.

---

**(e) Expected Market Price (using comparable P/E)**

The market price is the EPS multiplied by the P/E ratio.

$$
\text{Market Price} = \text{EPS} \times \text{P/E}
$$

$$
\text{Market Price} = 6 \times 15 = 90
$$

**Answer:** The Expected Market Price = **₹90 per share**.

---

**(f) Expected Listing Gain (%)**

Listing gain is the percentage difference between the expected market price and the issue price.

$$
\text{Listing Gain \%} = \dfrac{\text{Market Price} - \text{Issue Price}}{\text{Issue Price}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Listing Gain \%} = \dfrac{90 - 50}{50} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Listing Gain \%} = \dfrac{40}{50} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Listing Gain \%} = 80\%
$$

**Answer:** The Expected Listing Gain = **80%**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Underpricing and Dilution Analysis

**Problem Statement:**

> A company's IPO is priced at ₹200 per share. On the listing day, the share closes at ₹260. The company had 50 lakh shares pre-issue, and issued 10 lakh new shares. Calculate:
> (a) Underpricing Percentage
> (b) Promoter's dilution (Assume promoter held 100% pre-issue)
> (c) Post-Issue Promoter Holding %

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Issue Price: ₹200
- Listing Price: ₹260
- Pre-Issue Shares: 50,00,000 (all held by promoter)
- New Shares: 10,00,000

---

**(a) Underpricing Percentage**

Underpricing captures the discount at which the IPO was priced relative to the first-day market price.

$$
\text{Underpricing \%} = \dfrac{\text{Listing Price} - \text{Issue Price}}{\text{Issue Price}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Underpricing \%} = \dfrac{260 - 200}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Underpricing \%} = \dfrac{60}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Underpricing \%} = 30\%
$$

**Answer:** Underpricing = **30%**.

---

**(b) Promoter's Dilution (in shares)**

Dilution is the number of new shares issued that reduces the promoter's relative ownership.

$$
\text{Promoter Shares (Post)} = \text{Pre-Issue Shares} = 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{New Shares to Public} = 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Dilution Factor} = \dfrac{\text{New Shares}}{\text{Total Post-Issue Shares}}
$$

$$
\text{Dilution Factor} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Dilution Factor} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Dilution Factor} = \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667
$$

**Answer:** The Promoter's stake was diluted by approximately **16.67%** (or 1/6th of the post-issue company).

---

**(c) Post-Issue Promoter Holding %**

$$
\text{Promoter Holding \%} = \dfrac{\text{Promoter Shares}}{\text{Total Post-Issue Shares}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Promoter Holding \%} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Promoter Holding \%} = 83.33\%
$$

**Answer:** Post-Issue Promoter Holding = **83.33%**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Oversubscription and Pro-Rata Allotment

**Problem Statement:**

> An IPO offered 10 lakh shares. The issue was oversubscribed 5 times in the Retail Individual Investor (RII) category, where 6 lakh shares were reserved. 30 lakh shares were bid for. Calculate:
> (a) Total bids in RII category vs. allotted shares
> (b) Pro-rata allotment ratio
> (c) If you had bid for 100 shares, how many would you actually get?
> (d) Listing gain (if listing price is ₹120 and issue price is ₹100)

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- RII Quota: 6,00,000 shares
- Bids in RII: 30,00,000 shares
- Issue Price: ₹100
- Listing Price: ₹120

---

**(a) Oversubscription Multiple**

$$
\text{Oversubscription} = \dfrac{\text{Bids Received}}{\text{Shares Offered}}
$$

$$
\text{Oversubscription} = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 5 \text{ times}
$$

**Answer:** Oversubscribed **5 times**.

---

**(b) Pro-Rata Allotment Ratio**

The allotment ratio is the inverse of the oversubscription multiple.

$$
\text{Allotment Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Shares Offered}}{\text{Bids Received}}
$$

$$
\text{Allotment Ratio} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{1}{5}
$$

**Answer:** **1 share for every 5 shares bid**.

---

**(c) Allotment for 100 Shares Bid**

$$
\text{Shares Allotted} = \text{Shares Bid} \times \text{Allotment Ratio}
$$

$$
\text{Shares Allotted} = 100 \times \dfrac{1}{5} = 20 \text{ shares}
$$

**Answer:** You would receive **20 shares**.

---

**(d) Listing Gain**

$$
\text{Listing Gain per Share} = 120 - 100 = 20
$$

$$
\text{Total Listing Gain (on 20 shares)} = 20 \times 20 = 400
$$

**Answer:** Listing gain = **₹20 per share, or ₹400 total on 20 shares** (i.e., 20% return).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice the elegant connection — the oversubscription multiple (5x) directly tells the allotment ratio (1/5). The "magic number" of bid quantity is **5 (or any multiple of 5) — always bid 5, 10, 50, 100, 150, 200 shares** in IPOs that are likely to be oversubscribed to maximise full allotment.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Framework — When to Apply for an IPO

| IPO Characteristic | Favourable Sign (Apply) | Cautionary Sign (Skip) |
|---|---|---|
| **Promoter Holding Post-IPO** | Above 50% (skin in the game) | Below 20% (exit risk) |
| **P/E Ratio vs. Peers** | Lower than industry average | Higher than peers (overpriced) |
| **Issue Size** | ₹500–₹2,000 crore (institutional interest) | Below ₹10 crore (low liquidity) |
| **Subscription Status on Day 1** | Oversubscribed >5x | Under-subscribed |
| **Issue Price Discount to Peers** | 20%+ discount | At par or premium |
| **Anchor Investors' Profile** | Reputed mutual funds / FIIs | Unknown / weak names |
| **Track Record of Promoter** | Past listed companies' performance good | First-time or controversial |
| **Purpose of Issue** | Expansion / Debt repayment | Pure promoter exit |
| **Lock-in Period** | Standard (typically 90 days for non-promoters) | Long lock-ins for promoters |
| **Grey Market Premium (GMP)** | Positive GMP (unofficial premium) | Negative GMP or zero GMP |


> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each box represents a discrete stage in the IPO lifecycle. The arrows depict the linear sequence of events. The typical timeline for a Book-Built IPO in India is **3 to 6 months** from A1 to A15.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Key IPO Participants and Their Interactions

This block diagram visualises the **interconnected ecosystem** of intermediaries that collaborate (or are legally mandated) to execute an IPO.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["Issuer Company"]
    B2["Merchant Banker BRLM"]
    B3["SEBI Regulator"]
    B4["Stock Exchange NSE or BSE"]
    B5["Registrar and Transfer Agent RTA"]
    B6["QIB Institutional Investors"]
    B7["NII High Net Worth Investors"]
    B8["RII Retail Individual Investors"]
    B9["Anchor Investors"]
    B10["Depositories NSDL and CDSL"]
    B11["Bankers to the Issue"]
    B12["Auditors and Legal Counsel"]

    B1 --- B2
    B2 --- B3
    B2 --- B4
    B2 --- B5
    B5 --- B6
    B5 --- B7
    B5 --- B8
    B5 --- B9
    B5 --- B10
    B5 --- B11
    B1 --- B12
    B3 --- B4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The Issuer Company is at the centre. The Merchant Banker coordinates with the Regulator (SEBI), Stock Exchange, and Registrar. The Registrar is the operational backbone that processes bids from all three investor categories (QIBs, NIIs, RIIs) and works with Depositories for demat credit and Bankers for payment.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Tree: Fixed Price vs. Book Building Issue

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1["IPO Issue Types"]
    C2["Fixed Price Issue"]
    C3["Book Building Issue"]
    C4["Auction Method"]

    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4

    C2 --> C2A["Price known in advance"]
    C2 --> C2B["Demand known only after issue closes"]
    C2 --> C2C["Lottery or pro rata if oversubscribed"]
    C2 --> C2D["Investors pay full price at application"]

    C3 --> C3A["Price band announced floor to cap"]
    C3 --> C3B["Demand known during bidding window"]
    C3 --> C3C["Cut off price discovered after closure"]
    C3 --> C3D["Investors pay only the cut off price"]

    C4 --> C4A["Dutch auction style"]
    C4 --> C4B["Price determined at highest bid that fills the issue"]
    C4 --> C4C["Rarely used in India"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Tip:** If the exam asks *"Compare Fixed Price and Book Building"*, draw this comparison tree and write 4–5 lines under each branch. The examiner awards 3–4 marks just for a clean visual.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section mirrors **actual KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) patterns**. Questions are tagged with simulated KTU Past Year Question identifiers. Each question is solved with a complete model answer.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

> **Define Initial Public Offering (IPO). List any two advantages of an IPO from the perspective of the issuing company.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Initial Public Offering (IPO)** is the process by which a privately held company offers its equity shares (or other securities) to the public for the first time, in compliance with the **SEBI (Issue of Capital and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2018**, and gets its shares listed on a recognised stock exchange like **NSE or BSE**.

**Two Advantages of an IPO (from the issuer's perspective):**

1. **Raising of Fresh Capital:** The company can raise large amounts of money from the public for expansion, debt repayment, working capital, or strategic acquisitions without taking on additional debt.

2. **Liquidity and Exit Route for Existing Shareholders:** Promoters, venture capitalists, and private equity investors get a structured exit to monetise their holdings, providing partial liquidity on listing day.

*(3 marks awarded: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two advantages)*

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

> **What is meant by "Book Building" in the context of an IPO? Mention the SEBI-prescribed minimum subscription quota for QIBs, NIIs, and RIIs in a book-built issue.**

**Model Answer:**

**Book Building** is a mechanism used in IPOs to discover the issue price through a process of bidding within a specified **price band** announced by the issuer. Investors place bids indicating the price and quantity they are willing to buy. After the bidding window closes, the final price (known as the **cut-off price**) is determined based on the demand.

**SEBI-prescribed minimum subscription quota in a Book-Built IPO:**

$$
\text{QIB: 50\%} \quad \vert \quad \text{NII: 15\%} \quad \vert \quad \text{RII: 35\%}
$$

*(3 marks awarded: 1.5 marks for the definition + 1.5 marks for the quota breakdown)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

The KTU ESE allows **Internal Choice** in Part B. Two fully-solved alternative questions are provided below. Each sub-part is worth **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **process of an Initial Public Offering (IPO)** in India, listing the major steps and the role of the key intermediaries involved. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company has a pre-issue share capital of **60 lakh equity shares of ₹10 each**. It proposes to issue **15 lakh new shares** at a premium of **₹50 per share**. Expected NPAT for the next year is **₹9 crore**. Comparable listed companies in the same industry trade at a **P/E of 20**. Compute:
> 1. Issue Price
> 2. Total Issue Size
> 3. Expected EPS
> 4. Expected Market Price
> 5. Expected Listing Gain %
> **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a) — IPO Process (7 marks):**

The IPO process in India is a structured, multi-stage process that culminates in the listing of the company's shares on a stock exchange. The key steps are:

1. **Board Resolution & Appointment of Merchant Bankers:** The Issuer's Board passes a resolution authorising the IPO. The company then appoints one or more **Book Running Lead Managers (BRLMs)**, also called Merchant Bankers, who act as the primary intermediary coordinating the entire issue. Other intermediaries — Registrar to the Issue, Legal Counsel, Auditors, and Bankers to the Issue — are also appointed.

2. **Due Diligence & Documentation:** The BRLM conducts a thorough due diligence exercise, examining the company's financials, contracts, legal standing, and corporate governance. The **Draft Red Herring Prospectus (DRHP)** is prepared, containing all material information about the company, the issue, and the risks involved.

3. **Filing of DRHP with SEBI:** The DRHP is filed with the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)** for review. SEBI may issue observations or seek clarifications, which must be addressed by the company.

4. **In-Principle Approval from Stock Exchanges:** The company files applications with stock exchanges (NSE, BSE) for **in-principle approval** to list its shares.

5. **Pricing the Issue & Road Shows:** The issue price is decided based on the chosen method (Fixed Price or Book Building). Management, along with the BRLM, conducts **road shows** to market the issue to potential institutional investors.

6. **Subscription Window:** The issue is opened for subscription for a period of **3 to 5 working days**. In a book-built issue, the price band is announced. The subscription is divided into:
   - QIB (50% minimum)
   - NII (15% minimum)
   - RII (35% minimum)

7. **Allotment & Listing:** The Registrar processes the applications and prepares the **basis of allotment**. Shares are credited to the **demat accounts** of successful allottees, refunds are processed, and the shares are **listed** on the stock exchange, where trading begins the next day.

**Role of Key Intermediaries:**

| Intermediary | Role |
|---|---|
| **Merchant Banker (BRLM)** | Coordinates the issue, advises on pricing, structures the offer, and liaises with SEBI. |
| **Registrar to the Issue** | Processes applications, allots shares, handles refunds, and resolves investor grievances. |
| **Lead Bankers (Bankers to the Issue)** | Collect application money and manage escrow accounts. |
| **Stock Exchange (NSE/BSE)** | Provides the trading platform; grants in-principle and final listing approval. |
| **SEBI** | Regulates the issue, protects investor interest, and ensures disclosure compliance. |
| **Depositories (NSDL/CDSL)** | Hold the shares in dematerialised form and credit them to allottees. |
| **Auditors & Legal Counsel** | Certify financials and ensure legal compliance. |

---

**Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Pre-Issue Shares: 60,00,000
- Face Value: ₹10
- New Shares: 15,00,000
- Premium: ₹50
- NPAT: ₹9,00,00,000
- Comparable P/E: 20

**Step 1: Issue Price**

$$
\text{Issue Price} = \text{Face Value} + \text{Premium} = 10 + 50 = 60
$$

**Step 2: Total Issue Size**

$$
\text{Issue Size} = 60 \times 15{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 9 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 3: Post-Issue Total Shares**

$$
\text{Post-Issue Shares} = 60{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = 75{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Expected EPS**

$$
\text{EPS} = \dfrac{9{,}00{,}00{,}000}{75{,}00{,}000} = 12
$$

**Step 5: Expected Market Price**

$$
\text{Market Price} = \text{EPS} \times \text{P/E} = 12 \times 20 = 240
$$

**Step 6: Expected Listing Gain %**

$$
\text{Listing Gain \%} = \dfrac{240 - 60}{60} \times 100 = \dfrac{180}{60} \times 100 = 300\%
$$

**Final Answer Tabulation:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Issue Price | ₹60 |
| Total Issue Size | ₹9 crore |
| Expected EPS | ₹12 |
| Expected Market Price | ₹240 |
| Expected Listing Gain | 300% |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2022 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of an IPO** from the perspective of a company. Differentiate between **Fixed Price Issue and Book Building Issue** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company's IPO was subscribed **12 times** the offered quantity in the Retail category. The RII quota had **8 lakh shares**. The issue price was **₹150** and the listing price was **₹210**. An investor had applied for **300 shares**.
> 1. How many shares were bid in total in the RII category?
> 2. How many shares would the investor actually receive (pro-rata)?
> 3. Calculate the total listing gain in ₹ terms for this investor.
> 4. If the merchant banker charges **2.5% of the issue size** and the issue size is **₹1,200 crore**, calculate the merchant banker's fee. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a) — Advantages, Disadvantages & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Advantages of an IPO (from the company's perspective):**

1. **Large Pool of Capital:** An IPO enables the company to raise significant capital from a wide base of retail and institutional investors, typically far exceeding what private placements can offer.

2. **Liquidity for Existing Shareholders:** Promoters and early investors gain a defined exit mechanism, increasing the attractiveness of the company's pre-IPO shares.

3. **Enhanced Brand Equity & Visibility:** A public listing boosts the company's profile, attracting customers, suppliers, and skilled employees.

4. **Easier Future Fundraising:** Once listed, the company can access additional capital through follow-on offers, rights issues, or ADR/GDR issues at favourable valuations.

5. **Employee Incentives:** A listed stock serves as a liquid currency for ESOPs, aiding talent retention.

**Disadvantages of an IPO:**

1. **High Cost:** Underwriting commissions, legal fees, listing fees, and marketing costs can range from 3% to 7% of the issue size.

2. **Regulatory & Compliance Burden:** Quarterly results, board composition norms, audit requirements, and SEBI regulations impose ongoing costs and time commitments.

3. **Loss of Privacy & Strategic Disclosure:** Listed companies must disclose operational, financial, and strategic information that competitors can scrutinise.

4. **Dilution of Control:** Promoters lose a portion of their decision-making authority as new shareholders enter the cap table.

5. **Market Volatility Risk:** Share price can fall due to macroeconomic or sectoral factors, affecting the company's market capitalisation and reputation.

**Difference Between Fixed Price Issue and Book Building Issue:**

| Basis | Fixed Price Issue | Book Building Issue |
|---|---|---|
| **Price Determination** | Price is fixed in advance and disclosed in the RHP. | Price is discovered through bidding within a price band. |
| **Demand Visibility** | Demand is known only after the issue closes. | Demand is monitored continuously during the bidding window. |
| **Payment** | Investors pay the full issue price at the time of application. | Investors pay only the cut-off price after the issue closes. |
| **Allotment** | Lottery or pro-rata if oversubscribed. | Pro-rata based on bids at or above the cut-off price. |
| **Risk** | Higher risk of under-subscription; issuer bears price risk. | Price is market-discovered; reduces pricing risk. |
| **Example** | Earlier issues like **Sahara India IPO (2010)**. | Most modern Indian IPOs — **Zomato (2021)**, **Paytm (2021)**, **LIC (2022)**. |

---

**Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Subscription Multiple: 12 times
- RII Quota: 8,00,000 shares
- Issue Price: ₹150
- Listing Price: ₹210
- Investor's Bid: 300 shares
- Merchant Banker Fee: 2.5%
- Issue Size: ₹1,200 crore

**Step 1: Total Shares Bid in RII**

$$
\text{Total Bids} = \text{Quota} \times \text{Subscription Multiple}
$$

$$
\text{Total Bids} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 12 = 96{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}
$$

**Step 2: Pro-Rata Allotment Ratio**

$$
\text{Allotment Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Quota}}{\text{Total Bids}} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{96{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{1}{12}
$$

**Step 3: Shares Actually Received**

$$
\text{Allotted Shares} = 300 \times \dfrac{1}{12} = 25 \text{ shares}
$$

**Step 4: Listing Gain per Share**

$$
\text{Gain per Share} = 210 - 150 = 60
$$

**Step 5: Total Listing Gain**

$$
\text{Total Gain} = 25 \times 60 = 1500
$$

**Step 6: Merchant Banker's Fee**

$$
\text{Fee} = 2.5\% \times 1200 = 30 \text{ crore}
$$

**Final Answer Tabulation:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Total Bids in RII | 96,00,000 shares |
| Allotment Ratio | 1:12 |
| Shares Allotted | 25 |
| Listing Gain per Share | ₹60 |
| Total Listing Gain | ₹1,500 |
| Merchant Banker's Fee | ₹30 crore |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Face Value and Issue Price:** A common error is using the Face Value (e.g., ₹10) instead of the Issue Price (e.g., ₹60) while calculating the issue size. This causes a 6× calculation error.
> 2. **Forgetting to Add New Shares to Pre-Issue Shares when computing EPS:** The post-issue share count = pre-issue + new shares. Skipping this gives a wrong EPS and cascades into wrong P/E and listing gain calculations.
> 3. **Mixing up Oversubscription and Allotment Ratio:** Oversubscription of "5 times" means the allotment ratio is **1/5**, not 1/5th of the bid quantity in shares but the reciprocal. Many students invert this.
> 4. **Quoting the wrong QIB/NII/RII Quota:** The current SEBI rule is **50 / 15 / 35**. Older revisions had different splits; do not write outdated numbers.
> 5. **Skipping the "Anchor Investor" or "Grey Market Premium" terminology:** KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises these recent concepts; omitting them loses marks.
> 6. **No Diagram in the Answer:** Even a simple flowchart of the IPO process earns 1–2 extra marks in valuation.
> 7. **Spelling Mistakes:** "Prosepctus" instead of "Prospectus" looks unprofessional; examiners do deduct marks for clarity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** IPO is the first sale of shares by a private company to the public, regulated by **SEBI ICDR Regulations, 2018**.
- **Primary Market vs. Secondary Market:** The IPO is a primary market transaction. Once listed, trading on NSE/BSE is secondary.
- **Three Issue Types:** Fixed Price, Book Building (most common), Auction.
- **Quota in Book-Built Issues:** QIB 50% $\vert$ NII 15% $\vert$ RII 35%.
- **Key Documents:** DRHP → RHP → Final Prospectus.
- **Promoter Contribution:** Minimum 20% of post-issue capital.
- **Public Offer Minimum:** ≥ 25% of post-issue capital (or as per SEBI relaxations).
- **Anchor Investors:** Bid one day before the issue opens; can be up to 60% of the QIB portion; up to 25% discount allowed.
- **Top 5 Formulas:** Issue Price = Face Value + Premium; Issue Size = Issue Price × New Shares; EPS = NPAT / Total Shares; P/E = Market Price / EPS; Underpricing % = (Listing − Issue) / Issue × 100.
- **Pro-Rata Allotment:** Allotment Ratio = 1 / Oversubscription Multiple.
- **Eligibility (SEBI):** Net Tangible Assets ≥ ₹3 crore **OR** Avg. Operating Profit ≥ ₹15 crore **OR** Net Worth ≥ ₹1 crore, with a 3-year track record.
- **Big-Name Examples to Quote in Exam:** **Zomato (2021)**, **Paytm (2021)**, **LIC (2022)**, **Tata Technologies (2023)**, **Ireda (2023)** — these impress examiners.
- **Advantages:** Capital, Liquidity, Brand, Compliance → investor trust, easier future fundraising.
- **Disadvantages:** Cost, Compliance, Dilution, Loss of Privacy, Market Volatility.
- **Bidding Trick for Retail:** Always bid in multiples of the lot size, and in quantities like 150 (which is divisible by 1/12) to maximise chance of full allotment.
- **Lock-in Period:** Promoters' minimum contribution is locked in for 3 years (or 1 year for 25% of the post-issue capital under certain conditions).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investment Methods in Stock Markets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Investment Methods in Stock Markets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Investment Methods in Stock Markets** refer to the systematic, analytical frameworks and strategic approaches employed by investors and traders to evaluate, select, and manage equity securities with the objective of generating optimal risk-adjusted returns over a defined investment horizon. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, these methods are broadly classified into **Fundamental Analysis**, **Technical Analysis**, **Quantitative Analysis**, and **Hybrid/Qualitative Approaches**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):** Investment methods are decision-making frameworks that combine financial data, market behavior, and macroeconomic indicators to determine the intrinsic value, future price trajectory, or relative attractiveness of a stock.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **used car**. You have two broad ways to decide:

- **The Mechanic's Way (Fundamental Analysis):** Open the hood, check the engine, mileage, service history, and decide if the car is *actually worth* the asking price.
- **The Trend Watcher's Way (Technical Analysis):** Look at price charts of similar cars, see if prices are going up or down, and decide based on *market momentum* whether now is a good time to buy.

Stock market investors use exactly these two schools of thought — sometimes one, sometimes both — to make decisions.

### 1.3 Major Investment Methods — Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Equity Investment Decision-Making (KTU 2024):**

| **S.No** | **Investment Method** | **Core Question Answered** | **Time Horizon** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Fundamental Analysis | What is the stock *really worth*? | Long-term |
| 2 | Technical Analysis | *When* should I buy or sell? | Short to Medium |
| 3 | Quantitative Analysis | What does the *statistical model* suggest? | All horizons |
| 4 | Efficient Market Hypothesis | Can I consistently *beat* the market? | Theoretical |
| 5 | Value Investing (Buffett Style) | Is this a *high-quality bargain*? | Long-term |
| 6 | Growth Investing | Will *future earnings* explode? | Long-term |
| 7 | Income Investing | Does it pay me *steady dividends*? | Long-term |
| 8 | Passive Index Investing | Can I match the *market return* cheaply? | Long-term |

### 1.4 Key Metrics Used in Investment Decisions

- **Intrinsic Value (V):** The "true" worth of a stock based on fundamentals.
- **Market Price (P):** The current trading price in the exchange.
- **Risk-Free Rate (Rf):** Typically the **10-year Government Treasury yield** in India (~7%).
- **Beta (β):** Measures a stock's volatility relative to the market index (NIFTY 50 / SENSEX).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Analysis — The "Business Owner" Approach

Fundamental analysis assumes that a stock has an *intrinsic value* determined by the underlying company's financial health, and that the market price will eventually converge to this value.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Analyze Macroeconomic Factors:** GDP growth, inflation (CPI), repo rate, fiscal policy.
2. **Analyze Industry Conditions:** Porter's Five Forces, sector growth, regulatory environment.
3. **Analyze Company Financials:** Revenue, EBITDA, Net Profit, Debt-to-Equity, Free Cash Flow.
4. **Compute Valuation Ratios:** Compare with industry peers and historical averages.
5. **Decide:** If Intrinsic Value > Market Price → **BUY**; If < Market Price → **SELL/HOLD**.

### 2.2 Technical Analysis — The "Chart Pattern" Approach

Technical analysis assumes that *all known information* is already reflected in the price, and that price movements follow identifiable trends and patterns.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Plot price-volume charts** (candlestick, line, bar).
2. **Identify Trends:** Uptrend, Downtrend, Sideways.
3. **Apply Indicators:** Moving Averages, RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands.
4. **Spot Patterns:** Head & Shoulders, Double Top, Cup & Handle.
5. **Generate Signals:** Buy/Sell/Hold based on indicator crossovers and breakouts.

### 2.3 Quantitative Analysis — The "Algorithmic" Approach

Uses mathematical and statistical models to identify opportunities. Includes factor models (Fama-French), mean reversion, momentum, and machine learning-based signals.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU ESE Numerical Questions (Module 1).**

| **#** | **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables / Units** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Price-to-Earnings Ratio | $P/E = \dfrac{P_0}{EPS}$ | $P_0$ = Market Price (₹), $EPS$ = Earnings per Share (₹) |
| 2 | Earnings Per Share | $EPS = \dfrac{NI - D_p}{N_s}$ | $NI$ = Net Income, $D_p$ = Preferred Dividends, $N_s$ = Shares Outstanding |
| 3 | Price-to-Book Ratio | $P/B = \dfrac{P_0}{BVPS}$ | $BVPS$ = Book Value per Share (₹) |
| 4 | Book Value per Share | $BVPS = \dfrac{Total\ Equity}{N_s}$ | Total Equity in ₹, $N_s$ = Shares Outstanding |
| 5 | Dividend Yield | $DY = \dfrac{D_0}{P_0} \times 100$ | $D_0$ = Annual Dividend (₹), $P_0$ = Price (₹), Result in **%** |
| 6 | Return on Equity | $ROE = \dfrac{NI}{Shareholders'\ Equity} \times 100$ | Result in **%** |
| 7 | CAPM Expected Return | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot \left[E(R_m) - R_f\right]$ | $R_f$ = Risk-free rate, $\beta_i$ = Stock Beta, $E(R_m)$ = Market Return |
| 8 | Gordon Growth (DDM) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r - g}$ | $D_1$ = Next Year Dividend, $r$ = Required Return, $g$ = Growth Rate |
| 9 | Holding Period Return | $HPR = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}$ | $P_1$ = Sale Price, $P_0$ = Buy Price, $D$ = Dividend |
| 10 | Simple Moving Average | $SMA_n = \dfrac{P_1 + P_2 + \dots + P_n}{n}$ | $n$ = Number of periods, $P_i$ = Closing price |
| 11 | Intrinsic Value (Zero Growth) | $V_0 = \dfrac{D}{r}$ | $D$ = Constant Dividend, $r$ = Discount Rate |
| 12 | Current Yield (Bond Comp.) | $CY = \dfrac{Annual\ Coupon}{Current\ Price}$ | Used for comparison with equity dividend yield |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

These formulas are not just academic — they power:
- **SEBI-registered investment advisors** while recommending stocks.
- **Mutual fund portfolio managers** (e.g., HDFC Flexi Cap Fund).
- **Algorithmic trading desks** in firms like Zerodha, Groww, and global hedge funds.
- **Corporate valuation** during IPO pricing, M&A, and fairness opinions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Gordon Growth Model (DDM) Derivation & Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A company pays a current dividend of ₹10 per share. Dividends are expected to grow at a constant rate of **6% per year**. The required rate of return is **12%**. Calculate the intrinsic value of the stock.

**Step 1: State the assumptions of the Gordon Growth Model.**

$$
\text{Assumptions: } g < r, \text{ dividends grow at constant rate } g \text{ forever.}
$$

**Step 2: Write the dividend stream.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0 &= ₹10 \\
D_1 &= D_0 \cdot (1 + g) \\
D_2 &= D_0 \cdot (1 + g)^2 \\
D_t &= D_0 \cdot (1 + g)^t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Express present value of each dividend.**

$$
P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{(1+r)^1} + \dfrac{D_2}{(1+r)^2} + \dots + \dfrac{D_{\infty}}{(1+r)^{\infty}}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the geometric series sum formula for $r > g$.**

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{D_0 (1+g)^t}{(1+r)^t} = D_0 \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^t
$$

**Step 5: Use the infinite geometric series result $S = \dfrac{a}{1-k}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= D_0 \cdot \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+r}\right)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+r}\right)} \\
&= D_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+g)}{(1+r) - (1+g)} \\
&= D_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+g)}{r - g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0 &= ₹10,\quad g = 0.06,\quad r = 0.12 \\
P_0 &= \dfrac{10 \times (1 + 0.06)}{0.12 - 0.06} \\
&= \dfrac{10 \times 1.06}{0.06} \\
&= \dfrac{10.6}{0.06} \\
&= ₹176.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Intrinsic Value $P_0 = ₹176.67$. If market price < ₹176.67, the stock is **undervalued** → BUY.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: CAPM — Computing Expected Return

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A stock has a beta of **1.4**. The risk-free rate is **7%**. The expected market return is **14%**. Calculate the required rate of return using CAPM.

**Step 1: Recall CAPM equation.**

$$
E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot \left[E(R_m) - R_f\right]
$$

**Step 2: Identify the market risk premium.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Market\ Risk\ Premium &= E(R_m) - R_f \\
&= 0.14 - 0.07 \\
&= 0.07\ (or\ 7\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply beta by market risk premium.**

$$
\beta_i \cdot (E(R_m) - R_f) = 1.4 \times 0.07 = 0.098
$$

**Step 4: Add the risk-free rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_i) &= 0.07 + 0.098 \\
&= 0.168 \\
&= 16.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The investor should demand a **16.8%** return for holding this stock.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Investment Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to compare these methods in 7-mark questions. Use this matrix as a study template.**

| **Criterion** | **Fundamental Analysis** | **Technical Analysis** | **Quantitative Analysis** | **Passive Indexing** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | Intrinsic value | Price patterns | Statistical signals | Market returns |
| **Time Horizon** | Long-term (1–10 yrs) | Short-term (mins–weeks) | Medium to long | Very long (10+ yrs) |
| **Data Used** | Financial statements, ratios | Price, volume charts | Numerical datasets | Index constituents |
| **Tools** | P/E, P/B, ROE, DDM | RSI, MACD, MA, patterns | Factor models, ML | Index funds (NIFTYBEES) |
| **Investor Type** | Warren Buffett | Day traders | Hedge funds | Jack Bogle followers |
| **Strength** | Logic-based, durable | Quick signals, tradable | Data-driven, scalable | Low cost, low effort |
| **Weakness** | Slow, ignores sentiment | Subjective patterns | Overfitting risk | Cannot beat market |
| **KTU Cognitive Level** | L3 (Apply) | L3 (Apply) | L4 (Analyze) | L2 (Understand) |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Holding Period Return (HPR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** You bought a share at ₹500, received ₹20 as dividend, and sold it at ₹580 after 1 year. Calculate HPR.

**Step 1: Apply the HPR formula.**

$$
HPR = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HPR &= \dfrac{(580 - 500) + 20}{500} \\
&= \dfrac{80 + 20}{500} \\
&= \dfrac{100}{500} \\
&= 0.20 \\
&= 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The investor earned a **20% return** over 1 year.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Investment Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Starts with Capital] --> B{Choose Investment Method}
    B --> C1[Fundamental Analysis]
    B --> C2[Technical Analysis]
    B --> C3[Quantitative Analysis]
    B --> C4[Passive Indexing]
    C1 --> D1[Study Financial Statements and Ratios]
    C2 --> D2[Study Price Charts and Indicators]
    C3 --> D3[Run Statistical and ML Models]
    C4 --> D4[Buy Index Fund NIFTYBEES]
    D1 --> E[Compute Intrinsic Value V0]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> F[Track Index Returns]
    E --> G{Compare V0 with Market Price P0}
    G -->|V0 greater than P0| H1[BUY Signal]
    G -->|V0 less than P0| H2[SELL or AVOID]
    H1 --> I[Execute Trade via Broker]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Monitor and Rebalance Portfolio]
    F --> J
    J --> K[End of Investment Cycle]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stock Valuation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data Layer]
        I1[Company Financial Statements]
        I2[Market Price and Volume Data]
        I3[Macroeconomic Indicators]
        I4[News and Sentiment Data]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer]
        P1[Fundamental Module: P/E, P/B, ROE, DDM]
        P2[Technical Module: RSI, MACD, SMA]
        P3[Quantitative Module: Factor Models]
        P4[Risk Module: Beta, Standard Deviation, VaR]
    end
    subgraph DECISION[Decision Layer]
        DEC1[Compare Intrinsic Value vs Market Price]
        DEC2[Generate Buy Sell Hold Signal]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Trade Execution Order]
        O2[Portfolio Report]
        O3[Risk Dashboard]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I2 --> P3
    I3 --> P4
    I4 --> P3
    P1 --> DEC1
    P2 --> DEC1
    P3 --> DEC1
    P4 --> DEC2
    DEC1 --> DEC2
    DEC2 --> O1
    P1 --> O2
    P2 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Comparison of Investor Profiles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Investor Profile] --> Q1{What is your time horizon}
    Q1 -->|Short term minutes to weeks| T1[Technical Trader: Uses charts and indicators]
    Q1 -->|Long term 1 year and above| Q2{Do you want to beat the market}
    Q2 -->|Yes study stocks deeply| F1[Fundamental Investor: Uses DDM, CAPM, ratios]
    Q2 -->|No match the market easily| F2[Passive Indexer: Buys NIFTY 50 ETF]
    F1 --> ST1[Style: Value or Growth or Income]
    ST1 --> V1[Value: Buy undervalued stocks]
    ST1 --> G1[Growth: Buy high growth firms]
    ST1 --> IN1[Income: Buy high dividend stocks]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio. Why is it considered a key valuation metric in fundamental analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Price-to-Earnings ratio is the ratio of the current market price of a share to its earnings per share: $P/E = P_0 / EPS$.
- **Significance (2 marks):**
  1. It indicates how much investors are willing to pay for ₹1 of company earnings.
  2. It allows comparison of companies across the same industry to identify overvalued or undervalued stocks.
  3. A high P/E suggests growth expectations; a low P/E suggests value or distress.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between fundamental analysis and technical analysis in stock markets.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Fundamental Analysis** | **Technical Analysis** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Basis | Financial statements and intrinsic value | Historical price and volume patterns |
| Time Horizon | Long-term | Short-term |
| Key Output | Buy/Sell based on fair value | Buy/Sell based on trends |

*(1 mark for each correct contrasting point × 3 points = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two alternative choices are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** in detail. Derive the expected return of a stock with beta **1.25**, risk-free rate **6%**, and expected market return **13%**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
The CAPM, developed by **William Sharpe (1964)**, describes the relationship between systematic risk and expected return for an asset. It states that the expected return of a stock equals the risk-free rate plus a risk premium proportional to its beta.

**Step 2: CAPM Formula (1 mark):**

$$
E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]
$$

**Step 3: Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_i) &= 0.06 + 1.25 \times (0.13 - 0.06) \\
&= 0.06 + 1.25 \times 0.07 \\
&= 0.06 + 0.0875 \\
&= 0.1475 \\
&= 14.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The required return is **14.75%**.

---

**(b)** A company pays a current annual dividend of **₹8**. Dividends are expected to grow at **5% per year** perpetually. If your required rate of return is **12%**, calculate the intrinsic value using the **Gordon Growth Model**. Should you buy the stock if the market price is **₹100**? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall Gordon Growth Formula (1 mark):**

$$
P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r - g} = \dfrac{D_0(1+g)}{r - g}
$$

**Step 2: Compute D1 (1 mark):**

$$
D_1 = 8 \times (1 + 0.05) = 8.40
$$

**Step 3: Apply the formula (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \dfrac{8.40}{0.12 - 0.05} \\
&= \dfrac{8.40}{0.07} \\
&= ₹120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Investment Decision (2 marks):**
- Intrinsic Value = ₹120
- Market Price = ₹100
- Since $V_0 > P_0$, the stock is **undervalued** by ₹20.
- **Decision: BUY the stock.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of Holding Period Return (HPR)**. An investor buys a stock at ₹450, receives a dividend of ₹15, and sells it at ₹520 after 9 months. Calculate the HPR (annualized). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Concept (2 marks):**
Holding Period Return measures the total return earned from an investment over the period it was held, including both capital gains and dividend income.

**Step 2: HPR Formula (1 mark):**

$$
HPR = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}
$$

**Step 3: Compute 9-month HPR (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HPR_{9m} &= \dfrac{(520 - 450) + 15}{450} \\
&= \dfrac{70 + 15}{450} \\
&= \dfrac{85}{450} \\
&= 0.1889 = 18.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Annualize the return (2 marks):**

$$
HPR_{annual} = HPR_{9m} \times \dfrac{12}{9} = 0.1889 \times 1.333 = 0.2518 = 25.18\%
$$

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Value Investing, Growth Investing, and Income Investing**. Give one example strategy for each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| **Strategy** | **Philosophy** | **Stock Characteristics** | **Example Approach** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Value Investing** (Benjamin Graham, Warren Buffett) | Buy stocks trading below intrinsic value | Low P/E, Low P/B, high dividend yield | Buy NIFTY stocks with P/E < industry average |
| **Growth Investing** (Peter Lynch, T. Rowe Price) | Buy firms with above-average future earnings growth | High P/E, high revenue growth, low dividends | Invest in tech firms like Infosys in 1990s |
| **Income Investing** (Retiree focus) | Buy stocks paying consistent, high dividends | Mature firms, stable cash flows, high dividend yield | Buy ITC, Coal India, PSU bank stocks |

*(2 marks for correct philosophy per strategy × 3 strategies = 6 marks; 1 mark for valid example = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DDM variables:** Students often use $D_0$ instead of $D_1$ in the Gordon Growth Model. Always compute $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$ first.
> 2. **CAPM Sign Errors:** Writing $E(R_m) - R_f$ as negative — remember the market return is always *higher* than the risk-free rate in practice.
> 3. **Missing Units in P/E:** P/E is a *dimensionless ratio*. Do not write "₹" with it.
> 4. **HPR vs Annualized HPR:** The simple HPR is for the holding period. Annualization requires multiplying by 12/n.
> 5. **Beta interpretation:** A beta > 1 means the stock is *more volatile* than the market; β < 1 means less volatile. β = 1 means it moves with the market.
> 6. **Gordon Growth precondition:** Always state that $g < r$, otherwise the formula breaks down.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1, Investment Methods**

- **Investment methods** = frameworks to pick stocks: Fundamental, Technical, Quantitative, and Passive.
- **Fundamental Analysis** = "Business value" approach; uses ratios like **P/E, P/B, ROE**.
- **Technical Analysis** = "Price pattern" approach; uses indicators like **RSI, MACD, SMA**.
- **Passive Indexing** = Buy a market index (NIFTY 50, SENSEX) and hold — beats most active investors over time.
- **P/E Ratio** = $P_0 \div EPS$. High P/E = growth stock; Low P/E = value stock.
- **EPS** = $(Net\ Income - Preferred\ Dividends) \div Shares\ Outstanding$.
- **Dividend Yield** = $(D_0 \div P_0) \times 100\%$. Critical for income investors.
- **CAPM** = $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - R_f)$. Beta is the sensitivity of stock returns to market returns.
- **Gordon Growth Model** = $P_0 = D_1 \div (r - g)$. Valid only when $r > g$.
- **HPR** = $\dfrac{(P_1 - P_0) + D}{P_0}$. Used to measure actual realized return.
- **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)** = markets reflect all information; beating the market consistently is impossible in the strong form.
- **Value vs Growth vs Income** = Value buys cheap, Growth buys future, Income buys dividends.
- **Always check** if the question asks for *intrinsic value* (theoretical) or *market price* (actual).
- **Use ₹ for Indian currency units** in numerical answers; show all steps in CAPM, DDM, and HPR problems.
- **KTU 2024 cognitive level**: Be ready for **L3 (Apply)** numericals and **L4 (Analyze)** comparison questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trading Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trading Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Trading Process** refers to the structured, end-to-end sequence of activities through which a buy or sell instruction in a financial security (equity, derivative, commodity, currency, or bond) is initiated by an investor, transmitted through intermediaries, matched on an exchange's order-matching engine, executed at a validated price, and finally concluded through clearing and settlement in a depository account. In the Indian context under the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)** and **National Stock Exchange (NSE)** / **Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)** regulations, the trading process is a tightly regulated workflow governed by the *Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (SCRA)*, SEBI's *Stock Brokers and Sub-Brokers Regulations, 1992*, and the *Depositories Act, 1996*.

Mathematically, the trading process can be abstracted as a **state-transition function**:

$$
T = f(O, M, E, C, S)
$$

where:
- $O$ = Order origination
- $M$ = Order matching mechanism
- $E$ = Trade execution
- $C$ = Clearing
- $S$ = Settlement

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Trading Process):**
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to understand the *mechanics* of trading, not just the *theory*. You must be able to trace a single order from the moment the investor clicks "BUY" on a trading terminal to the moment the shares land in the Demat account.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Shop Walkthrough"

Imagine you walk into a busy **Café** to order a Cappuccino:

| Café Step | Stock Market Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You tell the cashier your order ("one cappuccino, less sugar") | **Order Placement** — you specify buy/sell, quantity, price type |
| The cashier hands a token to the barista | **Order Routing** — terminal → broker's server → exchange |
| The barista checks the queue and prepares matching orders first | **Order Matching Engine** — matches buy & sell orders by price-time priority |
| Coffee is handed to you with a bill | **Trade Execution & Contract Note** — trade is done; broker sends confirmation |
| The cashier clears the bill at the end of the day | **Clearing** — NSCCL/ICCL guarantees the trade |
| Money is deducted from your wallet, coffee is yours | **Settlement (T+1)** — cash and shares move between accounts |

This analogy makes it clear that **trading is a pipeline**, not a single click. Every step has a regulator, a time limit, and a paper trail.

### 1.3 Key Participants in the Trading Process

- **Investor / Trader** — Originates the buy/sell decision.
- **Stock Broker (Trading Member)** — Registered intermediary who routes orders (e.g., Zerodha, Upstox, ICICI Direct).
- **Sub-Broker / Authorised Person** — Sub-intermediary.
- **Stock Exchange (NSE/BSE)** — Provides the trading platform and matching engine.
- **Clearing Corporation (NSCCL/ICCL)** — Acts as the **Central Counterparty (CCP)** to guarantee settlement.
- **Depositories (NSDL/CDSL)** — Hold securities in electronic (Demat) form.
- **Depository Participants (DPs)** — Bank-like agents connecting investors to depositories.
- **Banks** — Handle cash leg of settlement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Term to Memorise — "T+1 Rolling Settlement":**
> Since **January 27, 2023**, SEBI has implemented **T+1 settlement** for all securities. This means a trade executed on Day 'T' must be settled (both money and shares delivered) by the **end of the next working day (T+1)**. Earlier it was T+2, and before 2002, it was even T+3/T+5. Always write the **current** cycle in exams unless asked historically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven-Stage Trading Process (Operational Decomposition)

The end-to-end trading workflow can be broken down into **seven logical stages**. Each stage is critical and traceable for audit and regulatory purposes.

#### **Stage 1 — Account Opening (Pre-Trading)**
Before any order can be placed, the investor must open:
1. A **Trading Account** (with a SEBI-registered broker) — to place buy/sell orders.
2. A **Demat Account** (with a Depository Participant linked to NSDL or CDSL) — to hold securities electronically.
3. A **Bank Account** (linked via CBS/IMPS/UPI) — to move cash.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the difference between a *Demat* and a *Trading* account. Memorise: **Trading = place orders, Demat = store shares**.

#### **Stage 2 — Order Origination**
The investor decides:
- **What** to trade (security — e.g., RELIANCE).
- **Which direction** — Buy (long) or Sell (short).
- **Quantity** — number of shares/lots.
- **Price type** — Market, Limit, Stop-Loss, etc.
- **Time validity** — Day, Immediate-or-Cancel (IOC), Good-Till-Triggered (GTT).

The order is entered via a **trading terminal**, **web platform**, or **mobile app** (broker-provided front-end).

#### **Stage 3 — Order Routing**
The order travels: **Investor's terminal → Broker's server → Exchange's Trading Engine**.
Routed via **CTCL (Computer-to-Computer Link)** terminals. The broker must maintain **risk checks** before forwarding.

#### **Stage 4 — Order Matching**
The exchange's **Order Matching Engine (OME)** uses a strict algorithm:

$$
\text{Match Priority} = \max(\text{Price}) \text{ for Buy, } \min(\text{Price}) \text{ for Sell, then earliest timestamp}
$$

This is called the **Price-Time Priority (PTP)** algorithm. Passive orders sit in the **Order Book** until a contra-order arrives.

#### **Stage 5 — Trade Execution**
A trade is "born" the moment a buy and sell order match. Trade attributes:
- Trade ID
- Price
- Quantity
- Time stamp (to the millisecond)
- Counter-party (CCP — Clearing Corp)

#### **Stage 6 — Clearing**
The **Clearing Corporation** (e.g., NSE Clearing Ltd — **NSCCL** for NSE; **ICCL** for BSE) becomes the **guarantor**. It:
- **Novates** the trade (steps between buyer & seller).
- Calculates **net obligations** (multilateral netting).
- Demands **margin** from members.

#### **Stage 7 — Settlement**
On **T+1 day**, before the **settlement deadline (currently 3:30 PM for T+1)**:
- **Securities leg**: Shares move from seller's Demat to buyer's Demat.
- **Funds leg**: Cash moves from buyer's bank to seller's bank.
- Depositories (NSDL/CDSL) execute the securities leg; Banks handle funds via **RTGS/NEFT/IMPS**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cost-Sheet Table

The following formulas are **high-frequency** in KTU ESE numericals.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Brokerage** | $B = P \times Q \times r_b$ | $P$ = price, $Q$ = qty, $r_b$ = brokerage rate (max **0.10%** of turnover per side for delivery, **0.03%** per side for intraday futures) |
| 2 | **Securities Transaction Tax (STT)** | $\text{STT}_{\text{Delivery Buy}} = 0.1\% \times P \times Q$ | Charged only on the **buy side** for delivery |
| 3 | **STT (Intraday)** | $\text{STT}_{\text{Intraday Sell}} = 0.025\% \times P \times Q$ | Charged only on the **sell side** for intraday equity |
| 4 | **Exchange Transaction Charge** | $\text{ETC} = P \times Q \times r_e$ | $r_e$ varies (e.g., NSE Eq: ~0.00297%) |
| 5 | **SEBI Charges** | $\text{SEBI} = P \times Q \times 0.0001\%$ | Flat across exchanges |
| 6 | **GST** | $\text{GST} = 18\% \times (B + \text{ETC} + \text{SEBI})$ | Applied on the sum of brokerage + transaction charges |
| 7 | **Stamp Duty** | $\text{SD} = P \times Q \times r_s$ | $r_s$ varies by state (Kerala ≈ 0.015% for delivery buy) |
| 8 | **DP Charges** | $\text{DP} = \text{Rs. }13 + \text{GST per ISIN per day}$ (CDSL) | Flat per scrip sold, debited from Demat on sell delivery |
| 9 | **Total Cost of Buying** | $C_{\text{buy}} = P \cdot Q + B + \text{STT} + \text{ETC} + \text{SEBI} + \text{GST} + \text{SD}$ | Investor outflow on a delivery buy |
| 10 | **Net Realisation on Selling** | $R_{\text{sell}} = P \cdot Q - B - \text{STT} - \text{ETC} - \text{SEBI} - \text{GST} - \text{DP}$ | Investor inflow on a delivery sell |
| 11 | **Net Profit** | $\Pi = R_{\text{sell}} - C_{\text{buy}}$ | Profit (after all charges) |
| 12 | **Settlement Cycle** | $\text{Settle Date} = T + 1$ | T+1 rolling for all segments since 27-Jan-2023 |
| 13 | **Capital Gains** — STCG (equity, <12 mo) | $\text{STCG}_{\text{rate}} = 15\%$ | Section 111A |
| 14 | **Capital Gains** — LTCG (equity, ≥12 mo) | $\text{LTCG}_{\text{rate}} = 10\%$ (above ₹1,00,000 exempt) | Section 112A, no indexation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Trap:** Many students **add STT on both buy and sell** for delivery trades. This is wrong. STT is **only on the buy side** for delivery. For intraday, **only on the sell side**. The KTU examiner deducts full marks if this distinction is missed.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

The trading process isn't just a market theory — it is the **operational backbone of every transaction** in:
- **Algorithmic trading desks** (HFT firms) where microseconds matter.
- **Mutual fund NAV calculation** (closing price = last traded price on exchange).
- **Corporate actions** (splits, dividends require correct settlement).
- **Forensic finance / SEBI surveillance** (anomaly detection in matching).

> [!NOTE]
> The order book itself is a **Level-3 financial dataset**. KTU students in data-mining electives often use it to study market microstructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical — Full Cost Calculation (KTU Pattern)

> **Question Pattern:** Mr. Arun buys 1,000 shares of XYZ Ltd. at ₹250 each on the NSE through a discount broker. He sells them one year later at ₹325 each. Compute the **net profit** if STT, brokerage, exchange, SEBI, GST, stamp duty, and DP charges apply as per current norms.

Assume the following industry-standard rates for the derivation:
- Brokerage rate, $r_b = 0.10\%$ per side (delivery)
- Exchange Transaction Charge, $r_e = 0.00297\%$
- SEBI Charge = $0.0001\%$
- Stamp Duty (Kerala), $r_s = 0.015\%$
- DP charge = ₹13 + 18% GST = ₹15.34 (rounded ₹15) per scrip per day

**Given Data:**
- Buy price $P_b = 250$, Sell price $P_s = 325$
- Quantity $Q = 1{,}000$
- Holding period = 1 year → **LTCG applicable**

#### **Step A — Compute Buy-side Turnover**

$$
\text{Turnover}_{\text{buy}} = P_b \times Q = 250 \times 1000 = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

#### **Step B — Compute Sell-side Turnover**

$$
\text{Turnover}_{\text{sell}} = P_s \times Q = 325 \times 1000 = 3{,}25{,}000
$$

#### **Step C — Brokerage on Both Legs**

$$
B_{\text{buy}} = 250000 \times 0.001 = 250.00
$$

$$
B_{\text{sell}} = 325000 \times 0.001 = 325.00
$$

Brokerage is **symmetric** in this model (some brokers charge zero on one leg — but we use the conservative textbook rate).

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 250 + 325 = 575.00
$$

#### **Step D — STT (Buy-side only, delivery)**

$$
\text{STT} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.001 = 250.00
$$

#### **Step E — Exchange Transaction Charges (Both Legs)**

$$
\text{ETC}_{\text{buy}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.0000297 = 7.425
$$

$$
\text{ETC}_{\text{sell}} = 3{,}25{,}000 \times 0.0000297 = 9.6525
$$

$$
\text{ETC}_{\text{total}} = 7.425 + 9.6525 = 17.0775 \approx 17.08
$$

#### **Step F — SEBI Charges (Both Legs)**

$$
\text{SEBI}_{\text{buy}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.000001 = 0.25
$$

$$
\text{SEBI}_{\text{sell}} = 3{,}25{,}000 \times 0.000001 = 0.325
$$

$$
\text{SEBI}_{\text{total}} = 0.25 + 0.325 = 0.575
$$

#### **Step G — GST @ 18% on (Brokerage + ETC + SEBI)**

$$
\text{GST} = 0.18 \times (575 + 17.08 + 0.575)
$$

$$
\text{GST} = 0.18 \times 592.655 = 106.6779 \approx 106.68
$$

#### **Step H — Stamp Duty (Kerala, Buy-side only)**

$$
\text{SD} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.00015 = 37.50
$$

#### **Step I — DP Charges (Sell-side only, on delivery)**

$$
\text{DP} = 15.00
$$

*(Charged as ₹13 + 18% GST = ₹15.34, but broker typically debits ₹15–₹25 flat.)*

#### **Step J — Total Buy Cost**

$$
C_{\text{buy}} = 2{,}50{,}000 + 250 + 250 + 7.425 + 0.25 + 106.68/2 + 37.50
$$

The GST is on **both legs combined**, so half (~₹53.34) is logically attributed to the buy side, half (~₹53.34) to the sell side. For simplicity in the KTU exam:

$$
C_{\text{buy}} \approx 2{,}50{,}591.00
$$

#### **Step K — Total Sell Realisation**

$$
R_{\text{sell}} = 3{,}25{,}000 - 325 - 9.6525 - 0.325 - 53.34 - 15
$$

$$
R_{\text{sell}} \approx 3{,}24{,}596.68
$$

#### **Step L — Pre-Tax Profit**

$$
\Pi_{\text{pre-tax}} = R_{\text{sell}} - C_{\text{buy}} = 3{,}24{,}596.68 - 2{,}50{,}591.00 = 74{,}005.68
$$

#### **Step M — Tax on LTCG**

Since holding period = 1 year and sell > buy, the gain is **LTCG**.

$$
\text{Gain} = (325 - 250) \times 1000 = 75{,}000
$$

Exempt amount under Section 112A = ₹1,00,000 → **fully exempt** in this case.

$$
\text{Tax} = 0
$$

#### **Step N — Net Profit**

$$
\Pi_{\text{net}} = 74{,}005.68 - 0 = \mathbf{Rs.\ 74{,}005.68}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalised Net-Profit Formula** (used by all KTU textbook numericals):

$$
\Pi_{\text{net}} = (P_s - P_b) \cdot Q - B_{\text{total}} - \text{STT} - \text{ETC}_{\text{total}} - \text{SEBI}_{\text{total}} - \text{GST} - \text{SD} - \text{DP} - \text{Tax}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Order Types vs. Use Case

| Order Type | Syntax Example | When to Use | Risk Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Order** | `Buy 100 RELIANCE @ MKT` | Need immediate execution | High slippage risk in volatile markets |
| **Limit Order** | `Buy 100 RELIANCE @ 2450` | Want a specific price | May not execute |
| **Stop-Loss (SL)** | `Sell 100 RELIANCE @ 2400 SL` | Limit losses on existing position | Triggers as market order |
| **Stop-Loss Market (SL-M)** | `Sell 100 RELIANCE @ 2400 SL-M` | Guaranteed exit at trigger | Higher slippage |
| **Bracket Order (BO)** | Buy + Target + Stop-Loss | Auto-managed risk-reward | Intraday only |
| **Cover Order (CO)** | Order + compulsory Stop-Loss | Margin-efficient intraday | Intraday only |
| **GTT (Good Till Triggered)** | Limit order valid up to 1 year | Long-term investors | No margin benefit |
| **AMO (After Market Order)** | Order for next session | Busy professionals | Liquidity risk at open |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Trading Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Decision: Buy or Sell] --> B[Step 1: Account Check - Trading Demat Bank]
    B --> C[Step 2: Order Origination - Price Type Quantity Validity]
    C --> D[Step 3: Order Routing via CTCL Terminal]
    D --> E[Step 4: Order Matching Engine Price-Time Priority]
    E --> F{Trade Matched?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 5: Trade Execution - Trade ID Generated]
    F -- No --> H[Order sits in Order Book]
    H --> E
    G --> I[Step 6: Clearing via NSCCL or ICCL CCP Novation]
    I --> J[Step 7: Settlement on T plus 1 Day]
    J --> K[Securities Leg - NSDL or CDSL Demat Transfer]
    J --> L[Funds Leg - Bank to Bank RTGS IMPS]
    K --> M[Final: Shares in Buyer Demat Cash in Seller Bank]
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Order Life Cycle (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Placed: Investor enters order
    Placed --> Validated: Broker risk check passed
    Placed --> Rejected: Insufficient margin or invalid scrip
    Validated --> Forwarded: Sent to exchange via CTCL
    Forwarded --> InOrderBook: Waiting in queue
    InOrderBook --> PartialTrade: Partial match occurs
    InOrderBook --> FullyExecuted: Full match occurs
    PartialTrade --> InOrderBook: Remaining qty waits
    PartialTrade --> FullyExecuted: Final match
    FullyExecuted --> Confirmed: Trade confirmation to broker
    Confirmed --> Clearing: NSCCL or ICCL takes over
    Clearing --> Settled: T plus 1 funds and shares moved
    Rejected --> [*]
    Settled --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Regulatory & Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG[Regulatory Layer]
        SEBI[SEBI - Market Regulator]
        RBI[RBI - Banking Settlements]
    end

    subgraph EXC[Exchange Layer]
        NSE[NSE Trading Engine]
        BSE[BSE Trading Engine]
    end

    subgraph CLR[Clearing Layer]
        NSCCL[NSCCL - NSE Clearing]
        ICCL[ICCL - BSE Clearing]
    end

    subgraph DEP[Depository Layer]
        NSDL[NSDL]
        CDSL[CDSL]
    end

    subgraph INT[Intermediary Layer]
        BR[Stock Broker]
        DP[Depository Participant]
        BANK[Commercial Bank]
    end

    INV[Investor / Trader] --> BR
    BR --> NSE
    BR --> BSE
    NSE --> NSCCL
    BSE --> ICCL
    NSCCL --> NSDL
    NSCCL --> CDSL
    ICCL --> NSDL
    ICCL --> CDSL
    INV --> DP
    DP --> NSDL
    DP --> CDSL
    BR --> BANK
    SEBI -. regulates .-> BR
    SEBI -. regulates .-> NSCCL
    SEBI -. regulates .-> NSE
    RBI -. regulates .-> BANK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** Differentiate between a **Trading Account** and a **Demat Account**. Why is both required for executing a trade?

> **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
>
> - **Trading Account** (1 mark): A trading account is opened with a SEBI-registered stock broker. Its sole purpose is to **place buy and sell orders** on the exchange. The broker provides a unique **UCC (Unique Client Code)** and a trading terminal/web access.
> - **Demat Account** (1 mark): A Demat (Dematerialised) account is opened with a **Depository Participant (DP)** linked to NSDL or CDSL. It electronically **holds securities** (shares, bonds, MF units) in the investor's name.
> - **Why both?** (1 mark): Trading account **transmits the instruction** (e.g., "Buy 100 RELIANCE"), but it cannot store the shares. Once the trade is executed, the shares must land in a Demat account. Hence, both are mandatory and typically linked — buy orders need the Demat to receive shares; sell orders need both to deliver shares from the Demat and credit cash to the bank.

---

**Q2.** What is **T+1 Rolling Settlement**? Mention the current cycle and the regulator who introduced it.

> **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
>
> T+1 Rolling Settlement is the mechanism by which a trade executed on a particular trading day (Day 'T') is settled — both **securities and funds transferred** — by the end of the **next working day (T+1)** (1.5 marks).
> - **Current cycle:** T+1, applicable to all segments of the Indian securities market (1 mark).
> - **Implemented by:** SEBI, with the rollout completed on **27 January 2023** (0.5 mark). Earlier cycles were T+2 (effective 2003) and T+3/T+5 (pre-2003).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail the seven stages of the trading process in the Indian securities market. Draw a neat flow diagram. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> 1. **Account Opening (1 mark):** Investor opens (i) Trading Account with broker (UCC issued), (ii) Demat Account with DP of NSDL/CDSL, (iii) Bank Account for funds flow. KYC documents (PAN, Aadhaar, bank proof) submitted.
>
> 2. **Order Origination (1 mark):** Investor decides security (e.g., TCS), direction (buy/sell), quantity, price type (MKT/LMT/SL), and validity. Order entered via broker's terminal or app.
>
> 3. **Order Routing (1 mark):** Order travels from investor → broker's server → exchange's matching engine via **CTCL (Computer-to-Computer Link)**. Broker applies risk checks (margin, position limits).
>
> 4. **Order Matching (1 mark):** Exchange's OME uses **Price-Time Priority** algorithm. Best buy price ↔ best sell price matched. Unmatched orders sit in the **Order Book**.
>
> 5. **Trade Execution (1 mark):** A unique **Trade ID** is generated. Trade contract note is sent to the broker. The clearing corporation is notified.
>
> 6. **Clearing (1 mark):** **NSCCL (for NSE)** or **ICCL (for BSE)** acts as **Central Counterparty (CCP)**, novating the trade. Net obligations are calculated via multilateral netting. Margins are collected.
>
> 7. **Settlement (1 mark):** On **T+1 day** (before 3:30 PM), the **securities leg** (NSDL/CDSL transfers shares) and **funds leg** (bank-to-bank transfer) are completed simultaneously.
>
> *Flow diagram:* Refer to SECTION 4.1 (1 mark for neat labelled diagram).

---

**(b) Mr. Karthik buys 500 shares of ABC Ltd. at ₹420 per share through an online broker. He sells them 14 months later at ₹510 per share. Compute the net profit considering brokerage 0.1% per side, STT 0.1% on delivery buy, ETC 0.00297% on both legs, SEBI 0.0001% on both legs, GST 18%, stamp duty 0.015% (Kerala), and DP charges ₹15. Tax: LTCG 10% above ₹1,00,000. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Turnover values**

$$
\text{Turnover}_{\text{buy}} = 420 \times 500 = 2{,}10{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Turnover}_{\text{sell}} = 510 \times 500 = 2{,}55{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Brokerage (both sides, 0.1% each)**

$$
B_{\text{buy}} = 2{,}10{,}000 \times 0.001 = 210
$$

$$
B_{\text{sell}} = 2{,}55{,}000 \times 0.001 = 255
$$

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 465
$$

**Step 3 — STT (delivery, buy side only)**

$$
\text{STT} = 2{,}10{,}000 \times 0.001 = 210
$$

**Step 4 — Exchange Transaction Charge (both sides)**

$$
\text{ETC}_{\text{buy}} = 2{,}10{,}000 \times 0.0000297 = 6.237
$$

$$
\text{ETC}_{\text{sell}} = 2{,}55{,}000 \times 0.0000297 = 7.5735
$$

$$
\text{ETC}_{\text{total}} = 13.81
$$

**Step 5 — SEBI Charges (both sides)**

$$
\text{SEBI}_{\text{buy}} = 2{,}10{,}000 \times 0.000001 = 0.21
$$

$$
\text{SEBI}_{\text{sell}} = 2{,}55{,}000 \times 0.000001 = 0.255
$$

$$
\text{SEBI}_{\text{total}} = 0.465
$$

**Step 6 — GST @ 18% on (Brokerage + ETC + SEBI)**

$$
\text{GST} = 0.18 \times (465 + 13.81 + 0.465) = 0.18 \times 479.275 = 86.27
$$

**Step 7 — Stamp Duty (Kerala, buy side)**

$$
\text{SD} = 2{,}10{,}000 \times 0.00015 = 31.50
$$

**Step 8 — DP Charges (sell side)**

$$
\text{DP} = 15
$$

**Step 9 — Pre-tax Charges Total**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 465 + 210 + 13.81 + 0.465 + 86.27 + 31.50 + 15 = 822.05
$$

**Step 10 — Pre-tax Profit**

$$
\Pi_{\text{pre-tax}} = (510 - 420) \times 500 - 822.05 = 45{,}000 - 822.05 = 44{,}177.95
$$

**Step 11 — Tax (LTCG)**

Since holding = 14 months (>12) and total gain = ₹45,000 < ₹1,00,000:

$$
\text{LTCG exempt} = \mathbf{Rs.\ 0 \ Tax}
$$

**Step 12 — Net Profit**

$$
\Pi_{\text{net}} = \mathbf{Rs.\ 44{,}177.95}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Discuss the role of the Clearing Corporation in the Indian trading process. Explain the concepts of **novation**, **multilateral netting**, and **margin collection**. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> 1. **Role of Clearing Corporation (2 marks):** The Clearing Corporation (NSCCL for NSE, ICCL for BSE) is the **Central Counterparty (CCP)** between every buyer and seller. It guarantees the settlement of every trade, irrespective of whether the original counterparty defaults. It is the **backbone of trade settlement safety** in India, regulated by SEBI's *Clearing Corporations Regulations, 2018*.
>
> 2. **Novation (2 marks):** Once a trade is executed, the clearing corporation **steps in** and **replaces itself as the buyer to the seller and as the seller to the buyer**. The original counterparty risk is eliminated. Buyer now deals with NSCCL; Seller now deals with NSCCL.
>
> 3. **Multilateral Netting (1.5 marks):** If an investor has multiple buy and sell trades in different scrips, the clearing corporation **nets** all obligations so the investor pays/receives a **single net amount** instead of scrip-wise separate amounts. This drastically reduces fund and share movement.
>
> 4. **Margin Collection (1.5 marks):** The CCP collects **margins** from members to cover potential losses. Common types:
>    - **Initial Margin (IM):** Upfront, based on VaR.
>    - **Extreme Loss Margin (ELM):** 1% additional cushion.
>    - **Mark-to-Market (MTM):** Daily settlement of profits/losses.
>    - **Concentration Margin:** For oversized positions.
>    - **Special Margin:** Risk-based, imposed by clearing corp.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast **Market Order**, **Limit Order**, and **Stop-Loss Order** with one real-world example for each. State their advantages and disadvantages. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution (Tabular Format for 7 Marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Market Order (1.5 marks) | Limit Order (1.5 marks) | Stop-Loss Order (1.5 marks) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Buy/sell at the best available current price | Buy/sell at a specified price or better | Triggers a market/limit order when price hits a set level |
> | **Execution Guarantee** | Almost certain | Not guaranteed | Triggers then may face slippage |
> | **Price Control** | None | Full control | Trigger price control only |
> | **Use Case** | Liquidity is priority; index tracking | Disciplined entries; target buying | Risk management; trailing stop |
> | **Example** | Buying INFY at 9:15 AM to capture gap-up opening (1) | Buying RELIANCE at ₹2400 even if it opens at ₹2450 (1) | Holding HDFCBANK long from ₹1600; set SL at ₹1550 to limit loss (1) |
>
> *KTU marking scheme awards 0.5 mark each for any of: definition, execution, price-control, advantage, disadvantage, example.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read carefully):**
> 1. **STT misapplication (1–2 marks lost):** Students often charge STT on both buy & sell for delivery. It is **only on the buy side** for delivery equity. Marks deducted without excuse.
> 2. **Confusing DP charges with DP fee on buys (1 mark lost):** DP charge is only on **sell delivery**, not on buy.
> 3. **Settlement cycle outdated (1 mark lost):** Writing "T+2" or "T+3" in 2024 scheme is **factually wrong**. The examiner may give 0 if asked specifically.
> 4. **Confusing Trader and Investor**: There is a tax difference — speculative vs. non-speculative. Frequent intraday trades attract **speculative business income**, not STCG.
> 5. **GST base mistake**: GST is calculated on **Brokerage + ETC + SEBI**, NOT on turnover. Marks lost if student applies it on turnover.
> 6. **In Q&A, do not write "exchange charges" without numbers**: The examiner expects the specific ETC rate (0.00297%) for NSE. Vague answers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **Skip of "Contract Note"**: Most students forget the **broker-issued contract note** is the legal evidence of the trade, sent within 24 hours (SEBI rule). Mention it for full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Trading process** = end-to-end pipeline from order to settlement (7 stages).
- ✅ **Trading Account** places orders; **Demat Account** stores shares — both mandatory.
- ✅ **Settlement cycle** is now **T+1** (since 27-Jan-2023) for all equity segments in India.
- ✅ **Order matching** uses **Price-Time Priority** algorithm on NSE/BSE.
- ✅ **NSCCL** is the clearing corp for NSE; **ICCL** for BSE — both are **Central Counterparties (CCP)**.
- ✅ **Novation** = CCP replaces itself between buyer & seller, eliminating counterparty risk.
- ✅ **Multilateral netting** reduces the number and value of settlements.
- ✅ **STT (delivery buy)** = 0.1%; **STT (intraday sell)** = 0.025%; never both sides for delivery.
- ✅ **Brokerage cap** = 0.10% per side for delivery; 0.03% per side for intraday futures.
- ✅ **GST** = 18% applied on (Brokerage + ETC + SEBI), **never on turnover**.
- ✅ **Stamp duty** = state-specific; Kerala ≈ 0.015% on delivery buy.
- ✅ **DP charges** = flat (~₹13 + GST) per scrip **only on sell delivery**.
- ✅ **LTCG** (equity, >12 months) = 10% above ₹1,00,000 exempt; **STCG** (<12 months) = 15%.
- ✅ **Order types to remember**: Market, Limit, Stop-Loss, SL-M, GTT, Bracket, Cover, AMO.
- ✅ **CTCL** = Computer-to-Computer Link — the protocol connecting broker to exchange.
- ✅ **Contract Note** = legal trade confirmation sent by broker within 24 hours (SEBI mandate).
- ✅ **SEBI** is the apex regulator; **RBI** regulates banking leg of settlement.
- ✅ **NSDL & CDSL** are the two depositories in India; investor can choose either.
- ✅ **UCC (Unique Client Code)** identifies the investor across all exchanges.
- ✅ **PAN** is mandatory for every trade in India (Section 139A of Income Tax Act).
- ✅ A **Trade ID** uniquely identifies each executed trade; needed for grievance redressal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Trading Strategies - Scalping, Intraday Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Trading Strategies - Scalping, Intraday Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scalping** is an ultra-short-duration trading strategy where traders attempt to capture **micro price movements** by entering and exiting positions within **seconds to a few minutes**, accumulating a large number of small profits that collectively generate significant daily returns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intraday Trading (Day Trading)** is a strategy wherein a trader opens and closes all positions within the **same trading session** (typically 9:15 AM to 3:30 PM IST on NSE/BSE), ensuring **no overnight exposure** and thereby avoiding overnight risk.

| Parameter | Scalping | Intraday Trading |
|---|---|---|
| Holding Period | **1 second to 5 minutes** | **5 minutes to entire session** |
| Number of Trades/Day | **20 – 200+** | **1 – 10** |
| Target Profit/Trade | **0.05% – 0.3%** | **0.5% – 3%** |
| Risk Per Trade | **0.1% – 0.2%** | **0.5% – 1%** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Cricket Stadium Analogy** 🏏

Imagine you are at a cricket stadium where the price of a cold drink fluctuates:
- A **Scalper** is like a **street-side vendor** who buys 100 bottles when the price dips by ₹1 and immediately sells them when it rises by ₹1, repeating this 50 times during the match. He makes small profits per transaction but compounds them into a meaningful income.
- An **Intraday Trader** is like a **café owner inside the stadium** who buys a bulk stock of cold drinks at the opening (when prices are lowest) and sells them during the peak heat hours (afternoon peak) when demand and prices are highest, closing his entire inventory before the match ends.

Both strategies require the trader to **exit before the session closes**, but they differ in **frequency**, **speed**, and **profit-per-trade expectations**.

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> The following **bold metrics** are standard evaluation parameters for trading strategies in KTU assessments:
> - **Tick Size** = Smallest permissible price movement (e.g., ₹0.05 on NSE equities)
> - **Slippage** = Difference between expected trade price and actual execution price
> - **Bid-Ask Spread** = Gap between the highest bid and lowest ask price
> - **Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP)** = Benchmark for intraday execution quality

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Scalping

Scalping follows a strict **5-step decision loop**:

1. **Identify Liquidity Pools** — The trader scans for stocks/contracts with high volume and tight spreads (typically large-cap Nifty 50 constituents or index futures).
2. **Set Trigger Levels** — Micro support/resistance levels are plotted on a **1-minute or tick chart**.
3. **Rapid Order Entry** — Using **bracket orders** (entry + stop-loss + target placed simultaneously) to eliminate manual latency.
4. **Capture Spread/Swing** — Profit target hit before the opposite trigger is touched.
5. **Repeat at Scale** — Compounding small wins through **high trade frequency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Scalping?** The mathematical edge comes from the **Law of Large Numbers**: even a small 60% win-rate, applied across 100 trades, statistically yields strong returns when wins exceed losses by even a small margin.
