## 2.2 Operational Logic of Intraday Trading

Intraday trading follows a structured **4-phase cycle**:

- **Phase 1 — Pre-Market Analysis (8:30 AM – 9:15 AM):** Study global cues (SGX Nifty, Dow futures), economic calendar, and gap-up/gap-down candidates.
- **Phase 2 — Opening Range Breakout (9:15 AM – 9:30 AM):** Mark the high and low of the first 15 minutes; trade breakouts from this range.
- **Phase 3 — Momentum Continuation (9:30 AM – 2:30 PM):** Ride the trend using **moving averages (9 EMA, 21 EMA)** and **RSI (14-period)**.
- **Phase 4 — Forced Square-off (3:00 PM – 3:20 PM):** All open positions must be closed to avoid broker-imposed **auction penalties**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Notation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Gross P\&L} = (P_{\text{exit}} - P_{\text{entry}}) \times Q$ | $P$ = Price, $Q$ = Quantity | Raw profit/loss before costs |
| 2 | $\text{Net P\&L} = \text{Gross P\&L} - (\text{Brokerage} + \text{STT} + \text{Exchange Fees})$ | All in ₹ | True take-home profit |
| 3 | $\text{Return \%} = \dfrac{\text{Net P\&L}}{\text{Capital Deployed}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Strategy performance |
| 4 | $\text{Risk:Reward} = \dfrac{P_{\text{entry}} - P_{\text{stop-loss}}}{P_{\text{target}} - P_{\text{entry}}}$ | Ratio | Trade selection filter |
| 5 | $\text{Win Rate \%} = \dfrac{\text{Winning Trades}}{\text{Total Trades}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Strategy edge |
| 6 | $\text{Expectancy} = (W \times \text{Avg Win}) - (L \times \text{Avg Loss})$ | ₹/trade | Expected value per trade |
| 7 | $\text{Position Size} = \dfrac{\text{Risk Capital}}{\vert P_{\text{entry}} - P_{\text{stop-loss}} \vert}$ | Number of shares | Risk management |
| 8 | $\text{VWAP} = \dfrac{\sum(P_i \times V_i)}{\sum V_i}$ | Cumulative | Intraday benchmark |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Finance

> [!NOTE]
> These strategies are used by **proprietary trading firms (prop desks)** at investment banks like Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and by **quantitative hedge funds**. Algorithmic versions dominate **70% of NSE equity volumes** as of recent SEBI disclosures. Even retail traders employ these methods via platforms like Zerodha Kite and Upstox.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Scalping P&L Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A scalper places 50 trades during the day. Each trade uses a quantity $Q = 500$ shares. Entry price $P_{\text{entry}} = ₹100.50$, Exit price $P_{\text{exit}} = ₹100.65$. Transaction cost per trade = ₹15 (combined brokerage + taxes).

**Step 1: Calculate Gross Profit Per Trade**

$$\text{Gross P\&L}_{\text{trade}} = (P_{\text{exit}} - P_{\text{entry}}) \times Q$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Gross P\&L}_{\text{trade}} = (₹100.65 - ₹100.50) \times 500$$

$$\text{Gross P\&L}_{\text{trade}} = ₹0.15 \times 500$$

$$\text{Gross P\&L}_{\text{trade}} = ₹75.00 \text{ per trade}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Net Profit Per Trade (after costs)**

$$\text{Net P\&L}_{\text{trade}} = \text{Gross P\&L}_{\text{trade}} - \text{Transaction Cost}$$

$$\text{Net P\&L}_{\text{trade}} = ₹75.00 - ₹15.00$$

$$\text{Net P\&L}_{\text{trade}} = ₹60.00 \text{ per trade}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Daily Profit**

$$\text{Total Net P\&L} = \text{Net P\&L}_{\text{trade}} \times \text{Number of Trades}$$

$$\text{Total Net P\&L} = ₹60.00 \times 50$$

$$\text{Total Net P\&L} = ₹3{,}000.00$$

**Step 4: Calculate Return on Capital Deployed**

$$\text{Capital Deployed} = Q \times P_{\text{entry}} = 500 \times ₹100.50 = ₹50{,}250$$

$$\text{Return \%} = \dfrac{₹3{,}000}{₹50{,}250} \times 100$$

$$\text{Return \%} \approx 5.97\% \text{ per day}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Intraday Position Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A trader has risk capital $R = ₹10{,}000$. Stock entry price $P_{\text{entry}} = ₹450$, stop-loss $P_{\text{SL}} = ₹440$. Calculate maximum quantity to buy.

**Step 1: Compute Risk Per Share**

$$\text{Risk per Share} = \vert P_{\text{entry}} - P_{\text{SL}} \vert = \vert ₹450 - ₹440 \vert = ₹10$$

**Step 2: Apply Position Sizing Formula**

$$Q = \dfrac{\text{Risk Capital}}{\text{Risk per Share}} = \dfrac{₹10{,}000}{₹10} = 1{,}000 \text{ shares}$$

**Step 3: Verify Capital Required**

$$\text{Capital Required} = Q \times P_{\text{entry}} = 1{,}000 \times ₹450 = ₹4{,}50{,}000$$

This is the **margin blocked**, but only ₹10 per share (₹10,000 total) is **at risk** if stop-loss is hit.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Scalping vs Intraday

| Dimension | Scalping | Intraday |
|---|---|---|
| Time Horizon | Seconds – Minutes | Minutes – Hours |
| Capital Efficiency | **Very High** (rapid recycling) | Moderate |
| Emotional Load | **Extreme** (rapid decisions) | High |
| Technology Need | **Co-located servers, low-latency feeds** | Standard charting software |
| Regulatory Risk | SEBI algo-trading norms apply | Standard brokerage account sufficient |
| Slippage Sensitivity | **Very High** | Moderate |
| Scalability | Easily automated via algorithms | Semi-manual preferred |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trading Strategy Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Trader Enters Market]) --> nodeA{Available Time per Day?}
    nodeA -- Less than 2 hours --> nodeB{Hold Period Preference?}
    nodeA -- More than 4 hours --> nodeE[Intraday Method]
    nodeB -- Seconds to Minutes --> nodeC{Latency & Tech Available?}
    nodeB -- 15 min to 1 hour --> nodeD[Swing Intraday]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeF[Scalping Strategy]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD
    nodeF --> nodeG[Bracket Orders on Liquid Stocks]
    nodeE --> nodeH[VWAP and ORB Setup]
    nodeG --> nodeI([Daily Target Achieved: Stop Trading])
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeI
```

## 4.2 Intraday Session Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph phase1["Phase 1: Pre-Market 0830 to 0915"]
        nA1[Global Cues Check] --> nA2[Gap Up Gap Down Scan]
        nA2 --> nA3[Define Watchlist]
    end
    subgraph phase2["Phase 2: Opening Range 0915 to 0930"]
        nB1[Mark 15 min High Low] --> nB2[Set Entry Triggers]
    end
    subgraph phase3["Phase 3: Active Trading 0930 to 1430"]
        nC1[Apply 9 EMA and 21 EMA Crossover] --> nC2[RSI Filter 14]
        nC2 --> nC3[Bracket Order Execution]
    end
    subgraph phase4["Phase 4: Square Off 1500 to 1520"]
        nD1[Close All Open Positions] --> nD2[Calculate Daily P and L]
        nD2 --> nD3[Journal Trade Notes]
    end
    phase1 --> phase2 --> phase3 --> phase4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define scalping and state any three characteristics that distinguish it from intraday trading.**

*Model Answer:*
Scalping is an ultra-short-duration trading strategy where traders hold positions for a few seconds to minutes, capturing micro price movements through high trade frequency. It differs from intraday trading in:
1. **Holding Period** — Scalping: seconds to 5 minutes; Intraday: minutes to entire session.
2. **Trade Frequency** — Scalping executes 20–200 trades daily; Intraday executes 1–10 trades.
3. **Profit Target** — Scalping aims for 0.05%–0.3% per trade; Intraday targets 0.5%–3% per trade.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of "square-off" in intraday trading. Why is it mandatory?**

*Model Answer:*
Square-off refers to the mandatory closure of all open trading positions before market close (typically 3:20 PM on NSE) on the same day they were opened. It is mandatory because:
1. SEBI regulations prohibit carrying forward **unsettled intraday positions** without adequate margin.
2. Brokers impose an **auction penalty** (typically 1%–2%) for forced square-off by the broker.
3. It eliminates **overnight risk** arising from global news events, earnings surprises, and gap openings.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.A.** **(a)** Explain the operational steps involved in executing a scalping strategy. Discuss the role of liquidity, spread, and latency in scalping success. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A scalper executes 80 trades in a day. Each trade buys 1,000 shares at ₹250.20 and sells at ₹250.40. Transaction cost per round-trip trade is ₹25. Calculate the **total gross P&L, total net P&L, and percentage return** if the total capital deployed per trade is ₹2,50,200. **(7 Marks)**

*Model Solution for (b):*

**Step 1 — Gross P&L per trade:**

$$\text{Gross P\&L} = (₹250.40 - ₹250.20) \times 1{,}000 = ₹0.20 \times 1{,}000 = ₹200$$

**Step 2 — Net P&L per trade:**

$$\text{Net P\&L} = ₹200 - ₹25 = ₹175$$

**Step 3 — Total Net P&L for 80 trades:**

$$\text{Total Net P\&L} = ₹175 \times 80 = ₹14{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Return:**

$$\text{Return \%} = \dfrac{₹14{,}000}{₹2{,}50{,}200} \times 100 \approx 5.60\%$$

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.B.** **(a)** Compare intraday trading with scalping across **five key parameters**. Justify which strategy is more suitable for a retail trader with limited capital and time. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A day trader has risk capital of ₹15,000. He wants to buy a stock at ₹600 with a stop-loss at ₹588 and a target at ₹624. Calculate **(i)** the Risk-Reward Ratio, **(ii)** the position size in shares, and **(iii)** the expected profit if the target is hit. **(7 Marks)**

*Model Solution for (b):*

**Step 1 — Risk per share:**

$$\text{Risk} = ₹600 - ₹588 = ₹12$$

**Step 2 — Reward per share:**

$$\text{Reward} = ₹624 - ₹600 = ₹24$$

**Step 3 — Risk-Reward Ratio:**

$$\text{R:R} = \dfrac{₹12}{₹24} = 1:2$$

**Step 4 — Position Size:**

$$Q = \dfrac{₹15{,}000}{₹12} = 1{,}250 \text{ shares}$$

**Step 5 — Expected Profit at Target:**

$$\text{Profit} = (₹624 - ₹600) \times 1{,}250 = ₹24 \times 1{,}250 = ₹30{,}000$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often forget to multiply price difference by quantity, yielding a tiny ₹24 answer instead of ₹30,000. Always check that your answer is dimensionally consistent.
> 2. **Direction Confusion:** Long positions use (Exit − Entry); short positions use (Entry − Exit). Wrong direction yields incorrect signs.
> 3. **Ignoring Transaction Costs:** In scalping problems, omitting brokerage/STT leads to **overstated net P&L** and loss of 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Position Sizing Mistake:** Dividing capital by *entry price* instead of *risk per share* is a common error — always divide by risk magnitude.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scalping & Intraday Trading**
> - **Scalping** = Ultra-fast, high-frequency, low-margin trades (seconds to 5 min).
> - **Intraday** = Same-day buy-and-sell with no overnight holding.
> - Key metric: **Net P&L = Gross P&L − Transaction Costs**.
> - Position sizing rule: $Q = \dfrac{\text{Risk Capital}}{\vert P_{\text{entry}} - P_{\text{stop-loss}} \vert}$.
> - **Risk-Reward Ratio ≥ 1:2** is the professional standard for KTU answers.
> - **VWAP** and **Opening Range Breakout (ORB)** are core intraday tools.
> - Always **square off by 3:20 PM** to avoid broker auction penalty.
> - **Slippage** and **spread** are the silent killers of scalping profits.
> - For KTU numerical problems: show every step; never skip units (₹, shares, %).
> - SEBI requires **algorithmic trading registration** for fully automated scalping systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Momentum Trading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Momentum Trading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Momentum Trading** is a quantitative trading strategy that seeks to profit from the continuance of an existing market trend. The strategy is founded on the empirical observation that securities which have recently exhibited strong upward (or downward) price movement will, with a statistically significant probability, continue to move in the same direction over the short-to-medium term horizon (typically **3 to 12 months**).

In the precise terminology of the **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)** debate, momentum trading is a deliberate, systematic violation of the weak-form efficiency assumption, exploiting the well-documented **"Momentum Effect"** first formally identified by **Jegadeesh and Titman (1993)**. The strategy operates on the foundational principle that **price trends, once established, exhibit persistence** due to behavioral biases (herding, anchoring, disposition effect) and structural market frictions (information diffusion lag, under-reaction to news).

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Surfing Wave Principle**
> Imagine you are standing on a beach and a large ocean wave begins to form and roll toward the shore. The wave has *momentum* — it is already moving forward with significant energy. A skilled surfer does not paddle *against* the wave trying to stop it; instead, the surfer **paddles in the same direction** as the wave, catches it at the right moment, and rides its existing energy forward to the shore.

In financial markets, "waves" are **price trends** that develop over time. A momentum trader is essentially the surfer of the financial world:
- They **identify** a strong, established wave (an existing uptrend or downtrend).
- They **enter** the market in the *same direction* as the wave.
- They **ride** the wave until its energy begins to fade (signs of trend exhaustion).
- They **exit** before the wave breaks (trend reversal).

The trader does not try to predict the *start* of the wave (that would be "bottom-fishing" or "top-picking") and does not fight the wave. They patiently wait for confirmed directional movement, then align their capital with it.

## Key Standard Metrics in Momentum Trading

The following standardized metrics are universally used to quantify and identify momentum in financial securities:

- **RSI (Relative Strength Index)**: A bounded oscillator ranging from **0 to 100**, with readings above **70** indicating **overbought** conditions and readings below **30** indicating **oversold** conditions.
- **ROC (Rate of Change)**: A pure momentum oscillator expressed as a percentage, measuring the percentage change in price between the current period and a defined look-back period.
- **MACD (Moving Average Convergence Divergence)**: A trend-following momentum indicator that shows the relationship between two exponential moving averages of a security's price.
- **Standard Look-back Period**: **12 months** (or 252 trading days) — the most academically validated momentum measurement window.
- **Holding Period**: **3 to 12 months** — the documented window during which momentum effects persist strongest before mean-reversion sets in.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to understand that momentum trading is a **trend-following** strategy (as opposed to mean-reversion or contrarian strategies) and that its profitability is directly tied to the **time horizon** chosen for both entry signal generation and position holding.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic of Momentum Trading — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The momentum trading process can be decomposed into a rigorous, repeatable, five-stage operational framework:

### Step 1: Universe Definition & Asset Screening
- The trader first defines a **tradable universe** of securities (e.g., Nifty 50 constituents, all stocks above ₹500 in market cap).
- Liquidity filters are applied: minimum average daily volume, minimum market capitalization, and exclusion of penny stocks.
- **Why this step matters**: Momentum strategies require *liquid* markets to allow rapid entry and exit without excessive slippage.

### Step 2: Momentum Scoring & Ranking
- Each asset in the screened universe is assigned a **momentum score** based on its past price performance.
- Common scoring methods include: rate of change, 12-month return, 6-month return (excluding the most recent month to avoid short-term reversal effects), and risk-adjusted momentum (Sharpe ratio of returns).
- Assets are then **ranked in descending order** of their momentum scores.
- **Why this step matters**: Quantification removes subjective bias and allows systematic, rule-based decision-making.

### Step 3: Long/Short Portfolio Construction
- **Top-decile assets** (highest momentum) are purchased **long**.
- **Bottom-decile assets** (lowest/most negative momentum) are either sold short or excluded (in long-only strategies).
- The "self-financing" long-short portfolio isolates *pure momentum premium* by neutralizing market beta exposure.
- **Why this step matters**: This construction eliminates market-direction risk and isolates the momentum factor as the sole source of return.

### Step 4: Position Sizing & Risk Allocation
- Capital is allocated proportionally to momentum strength (stronger momentum = larger position) or equally weighted across the top decile.
- **Volatility targeting** is often applied: position size is inversely proportional to recent realized volatility.
- **Why this step matters**: Equal exposure to a high-volatility and a low-volatility asset would create unbalanced risk.

### Step 5: Rebalancing & Exit
- The portfolio is rebalanced at fixed intervals (**monthly** is the academic standard; **weekly** is common for retail traders).
- Positions are exited when:
  - The rebalancing date arrives (time-based exit).
  - The momentum score drops below the long threshold (signal-based exit).
  - A stop-loss is triggered (risk-based exit, typically at **-7% to -10%**).
- **Why this step matters**: The momentum premium is **time-decaying**; delayed rebalancing erodes returns and increases crash risk.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Momentum Indicators

| Indicator | Mathematical Formula | Interpretation Rule | Typical Bounds |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rate of Change (ROC)** | $ROC_n = \frac{P_t - P_{t-n}}{P_{t-n}} \times 100$ | Positive value = upward momentum | Unbounded (% scale) |
| **Relative Strength Index (RSI)** | $RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{1 + RS}$ where $RS = \frac{\text{Avg Gain}}{\text{Avg Loss}}$ | RSI > 70 = overbought, RSI < 30 = oversold | **0 to 100** |
| **MACD Line** | $MACD = EMA_{12}(P) - EMA_{26}(P)$ | Positive = bullish momentum | Unbounded |
| **Signal Line** | $\text{Signal} = EMA_{9}(MACD)$ | MACD crosses above signal = buy trigger | Unbounded |
| **Momentum (raw)** | $Mom_n = P_t - P_{t-n}$ | Positive = uptrend, negative = downtrend | Unbounded (price units) |
| **12-1 Month Momentum** | $Mom_{12,1} = \frac{P_{t-1}}{P_{t-12}} - 1$ | Skips most recent month to avoid reversal | Unbounded (decimal) |
| **Sharpe Ratio of Momentum** | $SR = \frac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Higher = better risk-adjusted momentum | Unbounded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Note**: The notation $P_t$ refers to the price at the current time $t$, while $P_{t-n}$ refers to the price $n$ periods prior. The value $n$ is the *look-back window* and is a **user-defined parameter** that fundamentally changes the strategy's behavior.

## Real-World Utility

Momentum trading is not merely an academic curiosity; it is one of the **three pillars of factor investing** alongside Value and Quality. It is employed by:
- **Hedge Funds**: Most systematic hedge funds (e.g., AQR Capital, Renaissance Technologies) run momentum as a core strategy.
- **Proprietary Trading Desks**: Banks use momentum signals for short-term directional trading.
- **Retail Algorithmic Traders**: Python-based retail systems frequently use simple moving average crossovers (a momentum proxy).
- **Portfolio Construction**: Even long-term institutional investors use momentum *tilt* strategies to overweight recent winners within a diversified portfolio.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## Worked Example 1: Rate of Change (ROC) Calculation

### Problem Statement
A trader is evaluating two stocks for a momentum strategy. The current prices and the prices 10 trading days ago are:
- **Stock A**: $P_{t} = ₹550$, $P_{t-10} = ₹500$
- **Stock B**: $P_{t} = ₹1{,}200$, $P_{t-10} = ₹1{,}150$

Calculate the 10-day Rate of Change for each stock and determine which exhibits stronger momentum.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the ROC formula.**

$$ROC_n = \frac{P_t - P_{t-n}}{P_{t-n}} \times 100$$

This formula expresses the percentage change between the price $n$ periods ago and the current price. Multiplying by 100 converts the decimal result into a percentage for easier interpretation.

**Step 2: Substitute the values for Stock A.**

$$ROC_A = \frac{550 - 500}{500} \times 100$$

We perform the subtraction in the numerator first.

$$ROC_A = \frac{50}{500} \times 100$$

Then we compute the division: $50 \div 500 = 0.10$.

$$ROC_A = 0.10 \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values for Stock B.**

$$ROC_B = \frac{1200 - 1150}{1150} \times 100$$

We perform the subtraction in the numerator first.

$$ROC_B = \frac{50}{1150} \times 100$$

Then we compute the division: $50 \div 1150 \approx 0.04348$.

$$ROC_B = 0.04348 \times 100 \approx 4.35\%$$

**Step 4: Compare the two results and interpret.**

$$ROC_A = 10\% \quad \text{vs.} \quad ROC_B = 4.35\%$$

Since $10\% > 4.35\%$, **Stock A exhibits stronger momentum** despite both stocks appreciating by the same absolute amount of ₹50. This is a crucial insight: **momentum measures relative, not absolute, price change.**

---

## Worked Example 2: Relative Strength Index (RSI) Calculation

### Problem Statement
Using the standard 14-period RSI calculation, suppose over the last 14 trading days, a stock has had:
- **Total Gains**: Sum of all up-day price changes = ₹70
- **Total Losses**: Sum of all down-day price changes (taken as positive values) = ₹30

Calculate the RSI value and interpret the signal.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Average Gain and Average Loss.**

$$\text{Average Gain} = \frac{\text{Total Gains}}{n} = \frac{70}{14} = 5$$

$$\text{Average Loss} = \frac{\text{Total Losses}}{n} = \frac{30}{14} \approx 2.143$$

Here, $n = 14$ is the standard Wilder look-back period for RSI.

**Step 2: Compute the Relative Strength (RS).**

$$RS = \frac{\text{Average Gain}}{\text{Average Loss}} = \frac{5}{2.143} \approx 2.333$$

The RS value represents the ratio of upward price movement to downward price movement over the look-back window.

**Step 3: Substitute RS into the RSI formula.**

$$RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{1 + RS}$$

$$RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{1 + 2.333}$$

$$RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{3.333}$$

$$RSI = 100 - 30.0 = 70$$

**Step 4: Interpret the signal.**

Since $RSI = 70$, the stock is at the **boundary of overbought territory**. In standard interpretation, the trader should be cautious about initiating *new* long positions, as the trend may be overextended and vulnerable to short-term pullback. This is a **momentum exhaustion signal**, not a momentum confirmation signal.

---

## Worked Example 3: Momentum Portfolio Construction

### Problem Statement
A trader has ₹10,00,000 to deploy using a momentum strategy across 5 stocks. The 12-month returns of the candidate universe are:
- **Stock P**: +45%
- **Stock Q**: +30%
- **Stock R**: +25%
- **Stock S**: -15%
- **Stock T**: -40%
- **Stock U**: +5%

If the strategy dictates going **long the top 3** and **short the bottom 2** with **equal-weight** allocation, calculate the capital deployed long and short, and the dollar profit/loss if the next month yields -5% on longs and +3% on shorts.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Rank the stocks in descending order of momentum.**

The ranking from highest to lowest 12-month return is: **P (+45%), Q (+30%), R (+25%), U (+5%), S (-15%), T (-40%)**.

**Step 2: Identify long and short baskets.**

- **Long basket (Top 3)**: P, Q, R
- **Short basket (Bottom 2)**: S, T

**Step 3: Compute equal-weight allocation.**

Total capital = ₹10,00,000. The strategy allocates capital *long* and receives an equivalent amount from *short sales* (in a classic long-short setup, the short proceeds are typically reinvested in the long side, but for simplicity here we use half-half).

$$\text{Long Capital} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{2} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Short Capital} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{2} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

Per-stock allocation (long): $5{,}00{,}000 \div 3 \approx ₹1{,}66{,}667$ per stock.

Per-stock allocation (short): $5{,}00{,}000 \div 2 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$ per stock.

**Step 4: Compute P&L on the long side (next month return = -5%).**

$$\text{Long P\&L} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (-0.05) = -₹25{,}000$$

**Step 5: Compute P&L on the short side (next month return = +3% on shorts, meaning the shorted stocks rose, hurting the short seller).**

$$\text{Short P\&L} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (-0.03) = -₹15{,}000$$

(Short P&L is the negative of the stock's return.)

**Step 6: Compute total portfolio P&L.**

$$\text{Total P\&L} = -25{,}000 + (-15{,}000) = -₹40{,}000$$

$$\text{Total Return} = \frac{-40{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = -4\%$$

**Interpretation**: The momentum strategy lost **4%** in this month. The loss on the long side is consistent with the well-documented **momentum crash** phenomenon, where the long-leg of the momentum portfolio undergoes sharp drawdowns during market reversals while the short leg rallies. This is a critical risk dimension of momentum strategies that students must understand.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Figure 1: Momentum Trading Decision Workflow

The following block diagram illustrates the complete operational architecture of a momentum-based trading system, from signal generation to exit logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Market Open] --> B[Define Tradable Universe]
    B --> C[Apply Liquidity Filters]
    C --> D[Compute Momentum Score for Each Asset]
    D --> E[Rank Assets by Momentum Score]
    E --> F{Score Above Threshold?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Add to Long Basket]
    F -->|No| H{Score Below Negative Threshold?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Add to Short Basket]
    H -->|No| J[Exclude Asset from Trade]
    G --> K[Compute Position Size via Volatility Targeting]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Execute Trades at Market]
    L --> M[Monitor Position Continuously]
    M --> N{Rebalance Date Reached?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Recompute Momentum and Re-rank]
    N -->|No| P{Stop Loss Triggered?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Exit Position Immediately]
    P -->|No| M
    O --> F
    Q --> R[End: Position Closed]
```

## Figure 2: Momentum Strategy Architecture and Feedback Loops

This diagram depicts the high-level functional architecture of a momentum trading system, showing the interaction between signal generation, execution, and risk management modules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIG[Signal Generation Module]
        SIG1[Price Data Input] --> SIG2[Indicator Calculator: RSI, ROC, MACD]
        SIG2 --> SIG3[Momentum Scorer]
        SIG3 --> SIG4[Rank Generator]
    end
    subgraph EXE[Execution Module]
        EXE1[Order Management System] --> EXE2[Position Sizer]
        EXE2 --> EXE3[Broker API: Order Placement]
    end
    subgraph RISK[Risk Management Module]
        RISK1[Stop Loss Monitor] --> RISK2[Volatility Tracker]
        RISK2 --> RISK3[Drawdown Limit Checker]
    end
    SIG4 --> EXE1
    EXE3 --> RISK1
    RISK1 -->|Trigger Exit| EXE1
    RISK1 -->|Reduce Size| EXE2
```

## Figure 3: Momentum vs. Mean-Reversion Decision Tree

This figure clarifies the strategic distinction between momentum trading and its opposite, mean-reversion trading, helping students situate momentum trading within the broader universe of quantitative strategies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Market Observation] --> Q1{Is the Recent Trend Strong and Persistent?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the Trend Confirmed by Multiple Indicators?}
    Q1 -->|No| MR1[Consider Mean-Reversion Strategy]
    Q2 -->|Yes| M1[Enter Momentum Trade: Ride the Trend]
    Q2 -->|No| M2[Wait for Confirmation: Avoid False Signal]
    M1 --> M3{Is Stop Loss Defined?}
    M3 -->|Yes| M4[Execute Trade with Risk Controls]
    M3 -->|No| M5[Define Stop Loss First]
    M5 --> M4
    M4 --> M6[Hold Position for 3 to 12 Months]
    M6 --> M7{Trend Intact?}
    M7 -->|Yes| M6
    M7 -->|No| EXIT[Exit Position and Reassess]
    MR1 --> EXIT
```

## Figure 4: Lifecycle of a Momentum Trade

The following timeline diagram traces the typical lifecycle of a single momentum trade from identification to exit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Screening] --> P2[Phase 2: Confirmation]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Entry]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Management]
    P4 --> P5{Exit Signal}
    P5 -->|Stop Loss Hit| P6[Phase 5a: Forced Exit]
    P5 -->|Trend Exhausted| P7[Phase 5b: Signal Exit]
    P5 -->|Rebalance Date| P8[Phase 5c: Time Exit]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define momentum trading. What is the underlying market anomaly that this strategy seeks to exploit?

**Model Answer**:
Momentum trading is a trend-following investment strategy that involves buying securities that have shown an upward price trend over a recent look-back period and selling (or short-selling) securities that have shown a downward price trend. The strategy is based on the principle that price trends tend to persist over the short-to-medium term. The underlying market anomaly is the **"Momentum Effect"**, which contradicts the weak form of the Efficient Market Hypothesis. The effect is driven by behavioral biases such as investor herding, anchoring on recent prices, and the disposition effect, as well as by slow information diffusion across market participants. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** List any three commonly used technical indicators for measuring momentum in a security. State the standard interpretation threshold for each.

**Model Answer**:
- **Relative Strength Index (RSI)**: A bounded oscillator from 0 to 100. Reading above **70** indicates overbought (momentum exhaustion on the upside), while reading below **30** indicates oversold (momentum exhaustion on the downside).
- **Rate of Change (ROC)**: An unbounded percentage indicator. A positive ROC value indicates positive momentum; a negative value indicates negative momentum. There is no universal threshold; it is interpreted relative to the asset's historical range.
- **MACD (Moving Average Convergence Divergence)**: The MACD line crossing above the signal line generates a **bullish momentum** buy signal; crossing below generates a **bearish momentum** sell signal. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | PEIET745]**

**(a)** Explain the operational framework of a momentum trading strategy in detail. Include in your answer the five key stages from universe definition to exit logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A trader evaluates four stocks with the following 10-day Rate of Change values: Stock X = +8%, Stock Y = -3%, Stock Z = +15%, Stock W = -12%. If the strategy is to long stocks with ROC > +5% and short stocks with ROC < -5%, determine the long and short baskets, the percentage allocation per stock (assume ₹6,00,000 total capital, split equally between long and short sides), and compute the next-day P&L if all long stocks gain 2% and all short stocks lose 1%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The operational framework of a momentum trading strategy consists of five sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Universe Definition and Screening** (1 mark): The trader defines a tradable universe of securities, typically drawn from a stock index or a market-cap filtered pool. Liquidity filters are applied: minimum average daily trading volume (e.g., ₹5 crore), minimum market capitalization (e.g., ₹500 crore), and exclusion of illiquid penny stocks. This step ensures the universe is tradable in scale.

**Stage 2 — Momentum Scoring** (2 marks): Each asset is assigned a numerical momentum score using indicators like 12-month return, 6-month return, Rate of Change, or Relative Strength Index. The 12-1 month momentum (12-month return skipping the most recent month) is the academically preferred metric, as it avoids the well-documented short-term reversal effect. Assets are then ranked from highest to lowest momentum.

**Stage 3 — Portfolio Construction** (1.5 marks): The top-decile ranked assets form the **long basket**; the bottom-decile assets form the **short basket**. In a long-only retail variation, only the long basket is implemented. In an institutional long-short setup, equal dollar amounts are allocated to each side, creating a market-neutral portfolio that isolates the momentum premium.

**Stage 4 — Position Sizing and Risk Allocation** (1.5 marks): Capital is allocated using either equal weighting or volatility targeting. Volatility targeting sets each position's size inversely proportional to the asset's recent realized volatility, ensuring that high-volatility assets receive smaller positions. A maximum position cap (e.g., 5% of capital) and a stop-loss level (e.g., -8%) are defined.

**Stage 5 — Rebalancing and Exit** (1 mark): The portfolio is rebalanced at fixed intervals (monthly in academic literature, weekly or daily in high-frequency variants). Exits occur on three triggers: the rebalance date (time-based), momentum score falling below threshold (signal-based), or stop-loss breach (risk-based). Failure to rebalance promptly leads to **momentum crashes** during trend reversals.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Apply the strategy's entry rules to the four stocks.** (1 mark)

The rule is: long if ROC > +5%, short if ROC < -5%.

- Stock X: ROC = +8% > +5% → **LONG**
- Stock Y: ROC = -3%, which is *not* less than -5% → **EXCLUDE**
- Stock Z: ROC = +15% > +5% → **LONG**
- Stock W: ROC = -12% < -5% → **SHORT**

**Long basket**: {X, Z}. **Short basket**: {W}.

**Step 2: Compute capital allocation.** (2 marks)

Total capital = ₹6,00,000. Split equally:

$$\text{Long Capital} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{2} = ₹3{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Short Capital} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{2} = ₹3{,}00{,}000$$

Equal weight per long stock: $3{,}00{,}000 \div 2 = ₹1{,}50{,}000$ per stock.

Equal weight per short stock: $3{,}00{,}000 \div 1 = ₹3{,}00{,}000$ (only one short).

**Step 3: Compute P&L on the long side.** (2 marks)

Long stocks gained 2%:

$$\text{Long P\&L} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 = +₹6{,}000$$

**Step 4: Compute P&L on the short side.** (1.5 marks)

Short stocks lost 1% (i.e., the shorted stock's price fell, which benefits the short seller):

$$\text{Short P\&L} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 = +₹3{,}000$$

**Step 5: Total P&L.** (0.5 marks)

$$\text{Total P\&L} = +6{,}000 + 3{,}000 = +₹9{,}000$$

$$\text{Total Return} = \frac{9{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 1.5\%$$

The strategy generated a **+1.5% return** on the deployed capital in one day.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | PEIET745]**

**(a)** Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of momentum trading as a quantitative strategy. What is a "momentum crash" and why does it occur? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 14-period RSI calculation for a stock yields an Average Gain of ₹4 and an Average Loss of ₹6. Calculate the RSI value, interpret the signal, and explain what a momentum trader should do in response. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Strengths of Momentum Trading** (3 marks):
1. **Empirically robust across markets and time periods**: The momentum effect has been documented in equity, bond, commodity, and currency markets across more than 200 years of data, making it one of the most replicated anomalies in financial economics.
2. **Intuitive and rule-based**: Unlike discretionary strategies, momentum trading follows clearly defined entry and exit rules, eliminating emotional decision-making.
3. **Performs well in trending markets**: Momentum strategies generate superior returns during sustained bull or bear runs, which constitute a significant fraction of historical market regimes.
4. **Diversification benefit**: Momentum returns have low correlation with traditional market-cap weighted returns, making momentum a valuable portfolio diversifier.

**Weaknesses of Momentum Trading** (2 marks):
1. **High transaction costs**: Frequent rebalancing and turnover erode gross returns; momentum is one of the most cost-sensitive strategies.
2. **Whipsaw losses in range-bound markets**: When markets oscillate without a clear trend, momentum signals generate repeated false breakouts and stop-loss triggers.
3. **Tail risk and momentum crashes**: The strategy is vulnerable to sharp, sudden reversals.

**Momentum Crash** (2 marks): A **momentum crash** is a sudden, severe, and persistent drawdown in the returns of a momentum portfolio, typically occurring during abrupt trend reversals. The classic example is **March 2009**, when the long-leg of the global momentum portfolio lost approximately 50% in two months while the short-leg rallied sharply. The crash occurs because momentum positions are implicitly **leveraged bets on continued volatility**; when volatility spikes and trends reverse, the long positions are forced to liquidate at the worst possible prices, amplifying the reversal through a feedback loop.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Recall the RSI formula.** (1 mark)

$$RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{1 + RS}, \quad \text{where } RS = \frac{\text{Average Gain}}{\text{Average Loss}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Relative Strength (RS).** (1.5 marks)

$$RS = \frac{4}{6} = 0.6667$$

**Step 3: Substitute RS into the RSI formula.** (2 marks)

$$RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{1 + 0.6667}$$

$$RSI = 100 - \frac{100}{1.6667}$$

$$RSI = 100 - 60.0 = 40$$

**Step 4: Interpret the RSI value.** (1.5 marks)

Since $RSI = 40$, the stock is in **neutral-to-slightly-bearish territory**. It is above the **30 oversold threshold**, so it is not yet at an extreme low, but it is well below the midline of 50, indicating that **downward momentum has been dominant** over the past 14 periods. The Average Loss (₹6) exceeds the Average Gain (₹4), confirming the bearish bias.

**Step 5: Recommended action for a momentum trader.** (1 mark)

A momentum trader should **not initiate a new long position** at this stage, as there is no confirmed positive momentum. Two valid approaches are:
- **Wait for confirmation**: A long entry is warranted only when RSI crosses back above 50 (momentum reversal confirmation) *and* the price breaks above a key moving average.
- **Short-side opportunity**: A contrarian or short-bias momentum trader may consider initiating a short position, given the bearish momentum, with a tight stop-loss above the recent swing high.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: A common error students make is interpreting any RSI value below 70 as a "buy signal" or any value above 30 as a "sell signal." This is **incorrect**. RSI is a *momentum* indicator, not a mean-reversion indicator. The thresholds of 70 and 30 are *overbought/oversold* warnings, not direct trade triggers. Many KTU answers lose 1–2 marks by confusing the two interpretations. Always state the **direction of momentum** (up or down) before stating the **trade action** (long, short, or wait).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following high-density checklist summarizes the most critical, exam-ready concepts from this topic:

- **Momentum trading** is a **trend-following** strategy that profits from the persistence of existing price trends over a **3-to-12 month** holding period.
- The strategy is based on the **Momentum Effect** (Jegadeesh & Titman, 1993), which contradicts the weak-form **Efficient Market Hypothesis**.
- The **12-1 month momentum** (12-month return excluding the most recent month) is the academic gold standard for momentum measurement.
- **Rate of Change (ROC)** measures the percentage price change over a look-back window; it is unbounded and must be interpreted relative to historical norms.
- **RSI** is bounded between **0 and 100**; readings above **70** are overbought, below **30** are oversold. RSI is bounded; ROC is not.
- **MACD** is the difference between the **12-period** and **26-period** exponential moving averages, with a **9-period signal line**.
- The five operational stages are: **Universe Definition → Momentum Scoring → Portfolio Construction → Position Sizing → Rebalancing/Exit**.
- **Momentum crashes** are sudden, severe drawdowns that occur during sharp trend reversals, most notably in March 2009.
- **Transaction costs** are the single largest detractor from gross momentum returns; the strategy requires low-cost execution.
- **Rebalancing frequency** is critical: monthly rebalancing is the academic standard; weekly or daily is used in high-frequency variants.
- The momentum premium is **time-decaying**; delayed rebalancing erodes the alpha and increases drawdown risk.
- Always remember the **surfing wave analogy**: a momentum trader does not start the wave, does not stop the wave — they *ride* the wave.
- For KTU exams, always state the **time horizon** and the **indicator formula explicitly** when explaining a momentum strategy; vague answers lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Positional Trading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Swing Positional Trading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Swing Trading** is a speculative trading methodology in financial markets wherein a trader holds a position in an equity, derivative, or currency instrument for a period ranging from **a few days to several weeks**, attempting to capture short-to-medium-term price movements ("swings") within a broader trend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Positional Trading**, in contrast, is a longer-duration trading style wherein the trader holds positions for **weeks to months**, often riding a primary trend while ignoring minor fluctuations. The combined discipline of holding trades across these horizons — analyzing both swing and positional setups — is broadly termed **Swing Positional Trading** in the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus.

The KTU 2024 syllabus defines the trading horizon reference as follows:

| Trading Style | Typical Holding Period | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| Intraday | Minutes to 1 day | Capture daily volatility |
| **Swing** | **2 days – 3 weeks** | **Capture medium-term swings** |
| **Positional** | **3 weeks – 6 months** | **Ride major trends** |
| Long-Term Investing | 6 months – Years | Wealth compounding |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Surfer and the Wave:**
> Imagine you are at the beach watching ocean waves.
> - **Intraday traders** are kids splashing in the shallow foam at the shoreline.
> - **Swing traders** are surfers who paddle out, catch a medium-sized wave, ride it for 30–60 seconds, then exit before the next crash.
> - **Positional traders** are large ships that sail along the entire ocean current, riding the tide for hours.
> - **Long-term investors** are people who bought coastal land decades ago — they don't care about today's wave.

In Swing Positional Trading, you are the **surfer with a sailboat hybrid**: you catch the medium waves (swings) for quick profits, but if a particularly large ocean current (positional trend) appears, you extend your ride for weeks or months.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Defining Features of Swing Positional Trading:**
> 1. **Time-flexible holding** — Position is held across days/weeks, never closed at day's end.
> 2. **Trend-following with mean-reversion hybrid** — Uses both momentum indicators and pullback entries.
> 3. **Fundamental + Technical blend** — Swing trades lean on charts; positional trades layer in earnings, sector rotation, and macro news.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Swing Positional Trade

The lifecycle of a swing-positional trade follows a strict five-stage pipeline:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Market Scanning (Universe Selection)**
> Identify liquid instruments: NIFTY 50 stocks, Bank NIFTY futures, USD/INR, top-100 liquid equities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 — Setup Recognition**
> Look for one of the following classical setups:
> - Breakout from consolidation
> - Pullback to a moving average
> - Cup-and-handle formation
> - Fibonacci retracement zone (38.2% / 50% / 61.8%)

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 — Entry Trigger**
> Use a candlestick confirmation (e.g., Bullish Engulfing, Hammer, Inside Bar breakout).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 4 — Position Sizing & Risk Allocation**
> Risk per trade is generally capped at **1–2% of total trading capital**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 5 — Exit Management**
> Two exits must be pre-defined:
> - **Stop Loss (SL)** — Maximum acceptable loss.
> - **Target (T)** — Pre-calculated profit objective (often based on Risk:Reward ≥ 1:2).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position Size | $\text{Qty} = \dfrac{\text{Capital at Risk}}{\vert \text{Entry} - \text{Stop Loss} \vert}$ | Shares / Lots |
| 2 | Risk per Trade (%) | $R\% = \dfrac{\vert E - SL \vert}{E} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 3 | Reward-to-Risk Ratio | $RRR = \dfrac{\vert T - E \vert}{\vert E - SL \vert}$ | Dimensionless (must be ≥ 2) |
| 4 | Expected Value per Trade | $EV = (P_w \times \text{Avg Win}) - (P_l \times \text{Avg Loss})$ | Currency (₹) |
| 5 | Win Rate (Probability) | $P_w = \dfrac{\text{Winning Trades}}{\text{Total Trades}}$ | Decimal (0 to 1) |
| 6 | Simple Moving Average (SMA) | $SMA_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Price unit |
| 7 | Exponential Moving Average (EMA) | $EMA_t = \alpha P_t + (1-\alpha) EMA_{t-1}$, where $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{n+1}$ | Price unit |
| 8 | Relative Strength Index (RSI) | $RSI = 100 - \dfrac{100}{1 + RS}$, where $RS = \dfrac{\text{Avg Gain}}{\text{Avg Loss}}$ | Index 0–100 |
| 9 | Fibonacci Retracement Level | $L_k = H - k \times (H - L)$, where $k \in \{0.236, 0.382, 0.5, 0.618, 0.786\}$ | Price unit |
| 10 | Breakout Volume Confirmation | $V_{conf} \geq 1.5 \times \bar{V}_{20}$ | Multiple (×) |
| 11 | Capital at Risk | $C_{risk} = \text{Total Capital} \times R\%_{max}$ | Currency (₹) |
| 12 | Profit Factor | $PF = \dfrac{\sum \text{Gross Profits}}{\sum \text{Gross Losses}}$ | Ratio (must be > 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** The Course Outcome (CO) for this module emphasizes the ability to "compute position size, risk-reward, and expected value for given trade setups." Memorize formulas 1, 3, 4, and 7 — these appear most frequently in KTU ESE papers.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Quantitative Fields

Swing Positional Trading is now deeply integrated into **Algorithmic & Quantitative Trading** — a field heavily populated by B.Tech engineers. Key applications include:

- **Signal Processing Analogy:** Moving averages are mathematically identical to **low-pass filters** in DSP. RSI resembles an **autocorrelation indicator**.
- **Stochastic Calculus:** Positional trend modeling uses **Geometric Brownian Motion**: $dS_t = \mu S_t dt + \sigma S_t dW_t$.
- **Risk Engineering:** Value-at-Risk (VaR) for positional books uses the same statistical machinery as **reliability engineering**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Position Sizing Formula

> **Problem Setup:** A trader has a trading capital of **₹5,00,000**. Risk per trade is capped at **1.5%**. Entry price = **₹1,250**, Stop Loss = **₹1,180**. Find the position size.

**Step 1:** Compute the **Capital at Risk**.

$$
C_{risk} = \text{Total Capital} \times R\%_{max}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{risk} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.015
$$

$$
C_{risk} = 7{,}500 \; \text{₹}
$$

*Explanation:* We are willing to lose at most ₹7,500 if the stop loss gets hit.

**Step 2:** Compute the **Per-Share Risk** (the absolute price difference between entry and stop loss).

$$
\Delta P = \vert E - SL \vert
$$

$$
\Delta P = \vert 1{,}250 - 1{,}180 \vert
$$

$$
\Delta P = 70 \; \text{₹ per share}
$$

*Explanation:* Each share we buy will cost us ₹70 if the stop loss triggers.

**Step 3:** Compute the **Quantity to Buy**.

$$
\text{Qty} = \dfrac{C_{risk}}{\Delta P}
$$

$$
\text{Qty} = \dfrac{7{,}500}{70}
$$

$$
\text{Qty} \approx 107.14
$$

*Explanation:* Since fractional shares are generally not allowed in Indian cash markets, we **round down to 107 shares**.

**Step 4:** Verify actual risk with rounded quantity.

$$
\text{Actual Risk} = 107 \times 70 = 7{,}490 \; \text{₹}
$$

*Explanation:* The actual loss if SL hits is ₹7,490, which is within the ₹7,500 cap. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Value (EV) of a Trading Strategy

> **Problem Setup:** A swing trader analyzes 200 past trades. Wins = 120, Losses = 80. Average winning trade = **₹4,500**. Average losing trade = **₹2,200**. Compute Expected Value per trade and interpret.

**Step 1:** Compute the **Win Probability** $P_w$.

$$
P_w = \dfrac{120}{200} = 0.60
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **Loss Probability** $P_l$.

$$
P_l = 1 - P_w = 0.40
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Expected Value formula**.

$$
EV = (P_w \times \text{Avg Win}) - (P_l \times \text{Avg Loss})
$$

$$
EV = (0.60 \times 4{,}500) - (0.40 \times 2{,}200)
$$

$$
EV = 2{,}700 - 880
$$

$$
EV = 1{,}820 \; \text{₹ per trade}
$$

*Explanation:* On average, every trade the trader takes is expected to yield **₹1,820 profit**. With 200 trades, expected total profit ≈ ₹3,64,000.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule:** A positive EV confirms the strategy is statistically profitable. KTU questions often ask "Is this system viable?" — answer with: *Yes, if EV > 0 over a statistically significant sample (n ≥ 30).*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reward-to-Risk Ratio (RRR) Verification

> **Problem Setup:** Entry = ₹1,250, Stop Loss = ₹1,180, Target = ₹1,420. Compute RRR and decide whether to take the trade.

**Step 1:** Compute the **Risk component**.

$$
\text{Risk} = \vert E - SL \vert = \vert 1{,}250 - 1{,}180 \vert = 70
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **Reward component**.

$$
\text{Reward} = \vert T - E \vert = \vert 1{,}420 - 1{,}250 \vert = 170
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **RRR**.

$$
RRR = \dfrac{170}{70}
$$

$$
RRR \approx 2.43
$$

*Explanation:* For every ₹1 risked, the trader stands to make ₹2.43. Since **RRR ≥ 2**, the trade qualifies under standard KTU 2024 trading criteria.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — EMA Calculation (Worked Numerical Example)

> **Problem Setup:** Compute a 5-period EMA for the closing prices: 100, 102, 104, 103, 105. Use SMA seed for the first EMA.

**Step 1:** Compute **SMA of first 5 periods** (this acts as the seed EMA).

$$
SMA_5 = \dfrac{100 + 102 + 104 + 103 + 105}{5}
$$

$$
SMA_5 = \dfrac{514}{5} = 102.8
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **smoothing constant** $\alpha$.

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{2}{n + 1} = \dfrac{2}{5 + 1} = \dfrac{2}{6} \approx 0.3333
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **next EMA** (assuming a 6th price of 107).

$$
EMA_6 = \alpha \times P_6 + (1 - \alpha) \times EMA_5
$$

$$
EMA_6 = 0.3333 \times 107 + 0.6667 \times 102.8
$$

$$
EMA_6 = 35.66 + 68.53
$$

$$
EMA_6 \approx 104.19
$$

*Explanation:* The EMA reacts faster than SMA to the price jump from 105 → 107, which is the principal advantage of EMA in swing entries.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Swing vs. Positional

| Market Condition | Recommended Style | Indicator Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| Strong Trending (ADX > 25) | **Positional** | 50/200 EMA crossover |
| Range-Bound / Mild Trend | **Swing** | RSI(14), Bollinger Bands |
| High Volatility (VIX > 20) | Avoid both, or **Swing** with tight SL | ATR-based stops |
| Earnings Season | **Positional pre-event**, Swing post-event | Options IV |
| Macro Event Window (RBI, Budget) | **Stay out or hedge** | Event calendar |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Positional Trade Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Market Scan] --> B[Stage 2: Identify Setup]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Entry Trigger]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Position Sizing and Risk Allocation]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Active Trade Management]
    E --> F{Exit Condition Met}
    F -->|Stop Loss Hit| G[Exit with Loss]
    F -->|Target Hit| H[Exit with Profit]
    F -->|Time Exit| I[Exit at Max Holding Period]
    G --> J[Log Trade in Journal]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Review and Refine Strategy]
    K --> A
```

*Caption:* The cyclical nature of swing positional trading — every closed trade informs the next setup.

## 4.2 Risk Management Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Total Capital]
        A2[Risk Per Trade Percent]
        A3[Entry Price]
        A4[Stop Loss Price]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[Calculation Engine]
        B1[Compute Capital at Risk]
        B2[Compute Per Share Risk]
        B3[Compute Quantity]
        B4[Verify Reward to Risk Ratio]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        C1[Position Size in Shares]
        C2[Capital Deployed]
        C3[Maximum Loss in Rupees]
        C4[Trade Decision Approve or Reject]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C4
```

*Caption:* Block-level functional architecture showing how raw trade inputs flow through the calculation engine to produce validated trade decisions.

## 4.3 Swing Positional Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[New Trade Candidate] --> S1{Trend Direction}
    S1 -->|Uptrend| S2[Look for Pullback Entry]
    S1 -->|Downtrend| S3[Look for Rally Short Entry]
    S1 -->|Sideways| S4[Avoid or Range Trade]
    S2 --> S5{Pullback to MA or Fib Level}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Candlestick Confirmation]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Wait]
    S6 --> S8{RRR at least 1 to 2}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Execute Trade]
    S8 -->|No| S10[Skip Trade]
    S9 --> S11[Set Stop Loss and Target]
    S11 --> S12[Monitor and Manage]
```

*Caption:* Decision tree used by swing-positional traders before committing capital to a setup.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define Swing Trading and Positional Trading. List two differences between them.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Swing Trading** is a trading style where positions are held for a few days to several weeks to capture short-to-medium-term price swings within a larger trend.
- **Positional Trading** is a style where positions are held for several weeks to months, with the objective of profiting from the primary trend while ignoring short-term noise.

**Two Differences:**

| Parameter | Swing Trading | Positional Trading |
|---|---|---|
| Holding Period | 2 days to 3 weeks | 3 weeks to 6 months |
| Trade Frequency | Higher (multiple per month) | Lower (1–3 per quarter) |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: What is the Reward-to-Risk Ratio (RRR)? Why is an RRR ≥ 2 considered the minimum threshold in swing trading?**

**Model Answer:**
RRR is the ratio of potential profit to potential loss on a trade:

$$
RRR = \dfrac{\vert T - E \vert}{\vert E - SL \vert}
$$

An RRR ≥ 2 ensures that even if the trader's win rate is only **40–50%**, the strategy remains profitable due to asymmetric payoffs. Combined with position sizing, this forms the core of professional risk management.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students often write the position size formula without stating units (shares, lots) — leading to a **1-mark deduction**.
> **Pitfall #2:** In EV problems, forgetting to convert percentages to decimals (e.g., using 60 instead of 0.60) is a **recurring 2-mark error**.
> **Pitfall #3:** RRR problems require **explicit mention of the threshold (≥ 2)** with justification, not just the numerical answer.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** A swing trader has a trading capital of **₹8,00,000**. She caps risk per trade at **2%** of capital. She identifies a long setup on Stock XYZ: Entry = **₹640**, Stop Loss = **₹600**, Target = **₹760**.
> - (i) Compute the **position size** (number of shares).
> - (ii) Compute the **Reward-to-Risk Ratio** and comment on trade viability.
> - (iii) If the trader historically has a **55% win rate** with average win = ₹6,000 and average loss = ₹3,500, compute the **Expected Value per trade**. **[7 Marks]**

**(i) Position Size:**

$$
C_{risk} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 = 16{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \vert 640 - 600 \vert = 40 \; \text{₹}
$$

$$
\text{Qty} = \dfrac{16{,}000}{40} = 400 \; \text{shares}
$$

> **Answer: 400 shares**

**(ii) RRR:**

$$
\text{Reward} = 760 - 640 = 120
$$

$$
\text{Risk} = 40
$$

$$
RRR = \dfrac{120}{40} = 3.0
$$

> **Answer: RRR = 3.0** — Trade is highly viable since RRR ≥ 2.

**(iii) Expected Value:**

$$
EV = (0.55 \times 6{,}000) - (0.45 \times 3{,}500)
$$

$$
EV = 3{,}300 - 1{,}575 = 1{,}725 \; \text{₹}
$$

> **Answer: EV = ₹1,725 per trade** — Positive, so the strategy is statistically profitable.

---

> **(b)** Explain the **five-stage lifecycle of a swing positional trade** with a labelled flowchart. Discuss the role of **stop loss** and **target** in capital preservation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer Structure:**

1. **Stage 1 — Market Scanning** (1 mark): Identify liquid instruments using volume filters and watchlists (e.g., NIFTY 50, Bank NIFTY futures).
2. **Stage 2 — Setup Recognition** (1 mark): Identify patterns like breakouts, pullbacks, cup-and-handle, Fibonacci retracements.
3. **Stage 3 — Entry Trigger** (1 mark): Confirm entry with candlestick patterns (Hammer, Bullish Engulfing).
4. **Stage 4 — Position Sizing** (2 marks): Risk 1–2% per trade; use formula:
   $$\text{Qty} = \dfrac{C_{risk}}{\vert E - SL \vert}$$
5. **Stage 5 — Exit Management** (2 marks): Two pre-defined exits — Stop Loss caps downside; Target locks in profit at predefined RRR.

> **Role of Stop Loss:** Acts as a **circuit breaker** that limits loss to a pre-decided amount, preserving capital for future opportunities. Without SL, a single bad trade can wipe out months of gains.

> **Role of Target:** Locks in profits before market reversals occur. A target based on RRR ensures asymmetric payoff, which is the mathematical foundation of long-term profitability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Define **Risk per Trade** and **Expected Value**. A positional trader's last 50 trades show: 28 winners with average profit ₹5,200, and 22 losers with average loss ₹3,800.
> - (i) Compute the win rate and loss rate.
> - (ii) Compute the Expected Value per trade.
> - (iii) Comment on whether the strategy is viable. **[7 Marks]**

**(i) Win Rate and Loss Rate:**

$$
P_w = \dfrac{28}{50} = 0.56
$$

$$
P_l = \dfrac{22}{50} = 0.44
$$

> **Answer: Win Rate = 56%, Loss Rate = 44%**

**(ii) Expected Value:**

$$
EV = (0.56 \times 5{,}200) - (0.44 \times 3{,}800)
$$

$$
EV = 2{,}912 - 1{,}672
$$

$$
EV = 1{,}240 \; \text{₹}
$$

> **Answer: EV = ₹1,240 per trade**

**(iii) Comment:**

Since **EV > 0** and the sample size (n = 50) is statistically meaningful, the strategy is **viable**. Over 50 trades, expected total profit ≈ ₹62,000. However, the trader should still apply position sizing and stop losses to manage intra-trade variance.

---

> **(b)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **decision tree for entering a swing trade**. Mention how **RSI(14)** and **EMA(20/50) crossovers** are used as filters. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer Structure:**

1. **Decision Flow** (3 marks):
   - Identify trend using EMA(20) and EMA(50).
   - If EMA(20) > EMA(50): Uptrend → look for long setups on pullbacks.
   - If EMA(20) < EMA(50): Downtrend → look for short setups on rallies.
   - If RSI(14) > 70: Overbought → avoid fresh longs.
   - If RSI(14) < 30: Oversold → look for reversal long entries.

2. **Entry Filters** (2 marks):
   - **EMA(20)/EMA(50) crossover** confirms trend direction.
   - **RSI(14)** confirms momentum and warns of overbought/oversold conditions.

3. **Risk Filters** (2 marks):
   - Volume confirmation: $V \geq 1.5 \times \bar{V}_{20}$.
   - RRR ≥ 2 mandatory.

> **Conclusion:** Swing entries that combine EMA trend + RSI filter + Volume confirmation yield a higher probability of success than unfiltered entries.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Swing Positional Trading:**

- **Swing Trading horizon:** 2 days to 3 weeks; **Positional Trading horizon:** 3 weeks to 6 months.
- **Core rule:** Always pre-define Stop Loss **before** entering any trade.
- **Risk per trade:** Standard institutional cap is **1–2% of total capital**.
- **RRR threshold:** Minimum **1:2** for swing; ideally **1:3** for positional trades.
- **Position Size formula:** $\text{Qty} = \dfrac{\text{Capital at Risk}}{\vert E - SL \vert}$ — memorize verbatim.
- **EV > 0** is a necessary (but not sufficient) condition for strategy viability.
- **EMA vs. SMA:** EMA reacts faster to recent prices; use for entry timing, SMA for trend identification.
- **RSI(14)** boundaries: 30 = oversold (buy signal), 70 = overbought (sell signal).
- **Fibonacci key levels:** 38.2%, 50%, 61.8% — these are the **golden retracement zones**.
- **Volume confirmation:** Breakouts are only valid if volume ≥ 1.5× the 20-day average volume.
- **Trade journal:** Every swing trade — win or loss — must be logged for post-trade analysis.
- **Psychology rule:** Never average down on a losing swing trade; reassess the setup instead.

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Closing Note:** This module directly contributes to Course Outcome **CO2** of PEIET745 — *"Apply quantitative techniques to evaluate trading strategies in equity and derivatives markets."* Master the formulas and the decision logic, and you will secure full marks in this section.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk in Trading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Risk in Trading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Risk in Trading

In the context of financial markets and the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Introduction to Stock Markets and Trading*, **Risk in Trading** is formally defined as the quantifiable probability that the actual return on a financial asset, portfolio, or trading position will deviate from its expected return, with the potential consequence of financial loss. The deviation is typically measured in terms of **volatility (σ)**, and the risk is mathematically captured by the **standard deviation** of returns over a defined holding period.

The universally accepted KTU Board definition is:

> **Risk (R) = The standard deviation (σ) of the probability distribution of possible future returns on a given asset, position, or portfolio.**

A formal financial risk statement therefore takes the form:

$$R = \sigma(R_t) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(R_t)}$$

where $R_t$ represents the return realized at time $t$, and $\text{Var}(R_t)$ is the variance of those returns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly distinguishes between **Systematic Risk** (non-diversifiable, market-wide) and **Unsystematic Risk** (diversifiable, asset-specific). Every KTU question on this topic will test your ability to classify, quantify, and mitigate both forms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Mountain Climber"

Imagine you are a **mountain climber** preparing for two possible expeditions:

- **Expedition A** — A short, well-paved hill trail. You are almost certain to reach the summit, but the view is limited. The path is predictable, narrow, and the variation in your outcome is **low**. This represents a **low-risk, low-volatility asset**, like a Government of India bond or a blue-chip utility stock.

- **Expedition B** — A high-altitude Himalayan peak with unpredictable weather, sudden storms, and crevasses. The summit offers a breathtaking view (high reward), but there is a real chance you may not return, or may suffer significant injury. The path is wide in outcome, unpredictable, and the variation in your result is **high**. This represents a **high-risk, high-volatility asset**, like a small-cap tech IPO or a cryptocurrency position.

The "risk" in trading is essentially the **width of the cone of possible outcomes** around your expected return. The wider the cone, the greater the risk. The **Standard Deviation (σ)** is simply a mathematical ruler that measures the width of that cone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — The Risk-Return Tradeoff:** Higher risk is the price a trader pays for the *possibility* of higher returns. Risk itself is not inherently bad — it is only undesirable when it is **uncompensated** or **unmanaged**. The discipline of trading is therefore not the elimination of risk, but its intelligent allocation.

## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Categories of Trading Risk

| Dimension | Systematic Risk (Market Risk) | Unsystematic Risk (Idiosyncratic Risk) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Affects the entire market | Affects a single company, sector, or asset |
| **Diversifiable** | **No** — cannot be eliminated through diversification | **Yes** — can be eliminated through diversification |
| **Source** | Macroeconomic factors (interest rates, inflation, GDP, war, pandemics) | Company-specific events (CEO resignation, product recall, fraud) |
| **KTU Marker Term** | **Non-diversifiable / Aggregate Risk** | **Diversifiable / Residual Risk** |
| **Measurement Tool** | **Beta (β)** | **Residual Standard Deviation (σ_e)** |
| **Example** | 2008 Global Financial Crisis affecting NIFTY 50 | Satyam Computer Services accounting fraud (2009) |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Framework

The KTU 2024 Scheme, aligned with **NEP 2020** and **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**, requires students to demonstrate:

- **CO1 (Remember):** Recall the definitions of the six major trading risk categories.
- **CO2 (Understand):** Distinguish systematic from unsystematic risk with real examples.
- **CO3 (Apply):** Compute Beta, Sharpe Ratio, and Value at Risk (VaR) on a sample portfolio.
- **CO4 (Analyze):** Evaluate a hedging strategy for a given trading position.
- **CO5 (Evaluate):** Justify the choice of risk metric for a given trader profile (retail vs. institutional).

Every concept in this study note is mapped against these five Course Outcomes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Trading Risk (KTU Board Classification)

The KTU 2024 syllabus enumerates **six principal categories of risk** that a trader or investor must understand. Each is presented below with its operational logic and real-world trigger event.

### 2.1.1 Market Risk (Systematic Risk)
Market risk is the risk that an investor will experience losses due to factors that affect the overall performance of the financial markets. It is **non-diversifiable**. A trader holding a portfolio of 50 NIFTY 50 stocks still cannot escape a market-wide crash.

- **Drivers:** Interest rate changes (RBI repo rate), inflation, GDP contraction, geopolitical war, currency depreciation, pandemics.
- **Sub-types:** **Equity Risk, Interest Rate Risk, Currency Risk, Commodity Risk**.
- **Primary Metric:** **Beta (β)**, which measures sensitivity to a benchmark index.

### 2.1.2 Credit Risk (Default Risk)
Credit risk is the risk that a borrower or counterparty **fails to meet its obligations** in accordance with agreed-upon terms. In trading, it is the risk that the other side of your trade (your broker, counterparty bank, or bond issuer) defaults.

- **In Bonds:** The risk that the issuer (e.g., a corporate like DHFL historically) cannot pay coupon or principal.
- **In Derivatives:** The risk that the counterparty in a swap or forward contract defaults before settlement.
- **Metric:** **Credit Spread, Probability of Default (PD), Loss Given Default (LGD)**.

### 2.1.3 Liquidity Risk
Liquidity risk is the risk that a trader **cannot buy or sell an asset quickly enough** at a stable price to prevent a loss. It has two sub-forms:

- **Market Liquidity Risk:** The asset is in a market where there are not enough buyers/sellers (e.g., penny stocks, thinly traded small-caps).
- **Funding Liquidity Risk:** The trader does not have enough cash to meet margin calls or to exit a position.

- **Primary Metric:** **Bid-Ask Spread**, **Average Daily Volume (ADV)**, **Days to Liquidate**.

### 2.1.4 Operational Risk
Operational risk arises from **failed internal processes, people, systems, or external events**. It is the human and technological error component.

- **Examples:** Server downtime at a broker, fat-finger trade errors, rogue trader scandals (e.g., Barings Bank, 1995 — Nick Leeson), accounting fraud, cybersecurity breach.
- **Metric:** Loss Event Frequency × Loss Severity. Basel III classifies it under Pillar 1 capital requirements.

### 2.1.5 Systemic Risk
Systemic risk is the risk of **collapse of an entire financial system or market**, as opposed to risk of collapse associated with any single entity. It triggers a **contagion effect**.

- **Examples:** 2008 Lehman Brothers collapse, 1997 Asian Financial Crisis, 2020 COVID-19 market crash.
- **Metric:** **CoVaR (Conditional Value at Risk)**, **SRISK (Systemic Risk Index)**.

### 2.1.6 Inflation Risk (Purchasing Power Risk)
Inflation risk is the risk that the **real return** earned on an investment is eroded by rising consumer prices.

- **Example:** A Government bond yielding 6% nominal return with 7% inflation delivers a -1% real return.
- **Metric:** **Fisher Equation** linking nominal and real rates.

## 2.2 Risk Quantification — The KTU Formula Sheet

The KTU 2024 Board Exam requires mastery of the following formulae. They are consolidated in a single cheat-sheet for last-minute revision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Latex Pipe Rule:** Throughout this table, the vertical bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables Explained | Typical Use in KTU Board |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Return** | $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1} + D_t}{P_{t-1}}$ | $P_t$ = Price at time $t$, $D_t$ = Dividend received | Base for all risk calculations |
| 2 | **Variance of Returns** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (R_i - \bar{R})^2$ | $R_i$ = Individual return, $\bar{R}$ = Mean return, $N$ = Number of observations | Risk of single asset |
| 3 | **Standard Deviation (Total Risk)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Square root of variance | Volatility measure |
| 4 | **Portfolio Variance (2 assets)** | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | $w$ = weight, $\rho$ = correlation coefficient | Diversification math |
| 5 | **Beta (β)** | $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ | $R_m$ = Market return, $\text{Cov}$ = Covariance, $\text{Var}$ = Variance | Systematic risk measure |
| 6 | **CAPM Expected Return** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = Risk-free rate | Pricing of risk |
| 7 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $S = \dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | $\bar{R}_p$ = Portfolio return | Risk-adjusted return |
| 8 | **Treynor Ratio** | $T = \dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | $\beta_p$ = Portfolio Beta | Performance per unit systematic risk |
| 9 | **Value at Risk (Parametric)** | $\text{VaR} = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma \cdot V - \mu \cdot V$ | $Z_{\alpha}$ = Z-score, $V$ = Portfolio value, $\mu$ = Expected return | Maximum loss at confidence level |
| 10 | **Risk-Reward Ratio** | $\text{RRR} = \dfrac{\text{Potential Loss}}{\text{Potential Gain}}$ | Distance to stop-loss vs. target | Trade quality check |
| 11 | **Fisher Equation** | $(1 + r) = \dfrac{(1 + R)}{1 + \pi}$ | $r$ = real rate, $R$ = nominal rate, $\pi$ = inflation | Inflation-adjusted return |
| 12 | **Maximum Drawdown** | $\text{MDD} = \dfrac{\text{Peak} - \text{Trough}}{\text{Peak}}$ | Highest equity peak, lowest trough after | Worst peak-to-trough loss |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Risk Quantification System

These metrics are the **lingua franca of the global financial industry**. Every institutional trading desk — from the proprietary desk of a Mumbai-based broker to the algorithmic trading system of a global investment bank in New York — quantifies risk using these formulae.

- **A Portfolio Manager** uses Beta and the CAPM to determine whether a stock is fairly priced relative to its risk.
- **A Risk Manager** uses Value at Risk (VaR) to report daily potential loss to the SEBI and to the board.
- **A Retail Trader** uses the Risk-Reward Ratio to filter out low-probability trades.
- **A Bank Treasury** uses the Fisher Equation to price inflation-linked bonds.
- **A Hedge Fund Manager** uses Maximum Drawdown to evaluate the worst-case psychological capital erosion of an investor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Parallel for KTU Context:** Think of these risk metrics exactly like the **Factor of Safety (FoS)** calculations you study in Strength of Materials (ME204). In mechanics, FoS tells you how much load a beam can bear before failure. In trading, σ and VaR tell you how much market movement your portfolio can absorb before catastrophic loss. The mathematics — variance, sensitivity, threshold — is structurally identical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **complete, exhaustive derivation** of the most heavily weighted KTU Board risk formulae. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: Beta (β) from Covariance

**Starting Premise:** Beta is defined as the slope of the regression line of asset returns on market returns. By the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** regression of $R_i$ on $R_m$:

$$R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \epsilon_i$$

where $\alpha_i$ is the intercept (Jensen's alpha), $\beta_i$ is the slope, and $\epsilon_i$ is the residual (the unsystematic component).

**Step 1: The OLS slope estimator.** The slope of an OLS regression line is given by:

$$\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$$

**Step 2: Expand the covariance numerator.** By definition of covariance:

$$\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{i,t} - \bar{R}_i)(R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m)$$

**Step 3: Expand the variance denominator.**

$$\text{Var}(R_m) = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m)^2$$

**Step 4: Substitute back.** The $1/(N-1)$ cancels, giving:

$$\beta_i = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{i,t} - \bar{R}_i)(R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m)}{\sum_{t=1}^{N} (R_{m,t} - \bar{R}_m)^2}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation in words.** Beta is the ratio of *how much the asset's return co-moves with the market* (covariance) to *how much the market itself moves* (variance).

- If $\beta_i = 1$: The asset moves exactly with the market.
- If $\beta_i > 1$ (e.g., 1.5): The asset is **15% more volatile** than the market (aggressive).
- If $\beta_i < 1$ (e.g., 0.6): The asset is **40% less volatile** than the market (defensive).
- If $\beta_i < 0$: The asset **moves opposite** to the market (e.g., gold hedge).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Portfolio Variance of Two Assets (The Diversification Engine)

**Starting Premise:** Given two assets $A$ and $B$ with weights $w_A$ and $w_B = 1 - w_A$, the portfolio return at time $t$ is:

$$R_{p,t} = w_A R_{A,t} + w_B R_{B,t}$$

**Step 1: Variance of a linear combination.** For any two random variables $X$ and $Y$ with weights $a$ and $b$:

$$\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2 \text{Var}(X) + b^2 \text{Var}(Y) + 2ab \cdot \text{Cov}(X, Y)$$

**Step 2: Apply to portfolio.** Substitute $a = w_A$, $X = R_A$, $b = w_B$, $Y = R_B$:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \text{Cov}(R_A, R_B)$$

**Step 3: Substitute the correlation identity.** Since $\text{Cov}(R_A, R_B) = \rho_{AB} \cdot \sigma_A \cdot \sigma_B$:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

**Step 4: Special case — perfect negative correlation ($\rho_{AB} = -1$).** The third term becomes $-2 w_A w_B \sigma_A \sigma_B$. By choosing $w_A = \sigma_B / (\sigma_A + \sigma_B)$, the portfolio variance becomes **exactly zero** — a perfectly hedged riskless portfolio.

**Step 5: The Diversification Insight.** The portfolio variance is always less than the weighted average of individual variances whenever $\rho_{AB} < 1$. This is the mathematical proof that **diversification reduces risk**, and is the cornerstone of Modern Portfolio Theory (Markowitz, 1952).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem Statement (Typical 7-Mark Sub-Question):**
> A trader's portfolio is valued at ₹10,00,000. The portfolio's daily expected return is $\mu = 0.08\%$, and the daily standard deviation is $\sigma = 1.5\%$. The trader uses a 99% confidence level. Assuming returns are normally distributed, calculate the **1-day 99% Value at Risk (VaR)**.

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters.**
- Portfolio value: $V = 10{,}00{,}000$ rupees
- Expected daily return: $\mu = 0.08\% = 0.0008$
- Daily standard deviation: $\sigma = 1.5\% = 0.015$
- Confidence level: $99\% \Rightarrow Z_{0.99} = 2.33$ (from the standard normal table, KTU-approved value: $Z_{0.99} = 2.326$)

**Step 2: State the parametric VaR formula.**

$$\text{VaR} = \left( Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma - \mu \right) \cdot V$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values into the formula.**

$$\text{VaR} = \left( 2.326 \times 0.015 - 0.0008 \right) \times 10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: Compute the bracketed term.**

$$2.326 \times 0.015 = 0.03489$$

$$0.03489 - 0.0008 = 0.03409$$

**Step 5: Multiply by portfolio value.**

$$\text{VaR} = 0.03409 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 34{,}090 \text{ rupees}$$

**Step 6: Verbal interpretation for full marks.**
