for long-term wealth creation"] Mid --> Implication Low --> Avoid["INVESTOR ACTION:
Trade tactically only
Avoid for long-term capital"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** > - Part A: 3 questions × 3 marks = 9 marks > - Part B: Module choice, full 14-mark question, usually with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks > - Total per module: ~25–30 marks weightage --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define Porter's Five Forces. Why is it relevant to a stock market investor? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Porter's Five Forces is a **strategic framework** developed by **Michael E. Porter** in 1979 to analyze the **competitive structure of an industry**. It identifies five forces — *(i) Threat of New Entrants, (ii) Bargaining Power of Suppliers, (iii) Bargaining Power of Buyers, (iv) Threat of Substitutes, and (v) Rivalry Among Existing Competitors* — that collectively determine the **profit potential** of an industry. For a **stock market investor**, the framework is relevant because it provides a **top-down filter** to assess the structural attractiveness of an industry **before** selecting individual stocks. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for forces, 1 mark for investor relevance).** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Differentiate between **"Rivalry Among Existing Competitors"** and **"Threat of New Entrants."** Give one example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Rivalry Among Existing Competitors | Threat of New Entrants | |--------|-----------------------------------|------------------------| | **Scope** | Competition *within* the industry | Competition *from outside* the industry | | **Determinant** | Industry concentration, growth rate, fixed costs | Entry barriers, capital, regulation | | **Example** | Jio vs. Airtel in Indian Telecom (both incumbents) | A new EV startup entering India's passenger car market | *Note: The two are interrelated but operate at different levels. A new entrant increases rivalry, but rivalry is also determined by existing firms' actions.* **(1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for example each).** --- ## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (CHOICE OPTION 1) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain the **five forces** proposed by Michael Porter. State the determinants of each force. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Apply Porter's Five Forces to the **Indian Aviation Industry** (IndiGo, SpiceJet, Air India, Vistara). Conclude with the industry attractiveness score and a stock market investor recommendation. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks **Porter's Five Forces (1979)** is a framework for analyzing the **micro-environment** of an industry to determine its **competitive intensity** and **profitability potential**. **1. Threat of New Entrants (1 mark)** - Determinants: Capital requirements, economies of scale, product differentiation, switching costs, government policy, access to distribution, expected retaliation by incumbents. - High threat → low entry barriers → margin pressure. **2. Bargaining Power of Suppliers (1 mark)** - Determinants: Supplier concentration, switching costs, product uniqueness, threat of forward integration, availability of substitutes, importance of buyer to supplier. - High power → input cost pressure → margin squeeze. **3. Bargaining Power of Buyers (1 mark)** - Determinants: Buyer concentration, buyer switching costs, product standardization, availability of substitutes, buyer price sensitivity, threat of backward integration. - High power → pricing pressure → low margins. **4. Threat of Substitutes (1 mark)** - Determinants: Relative price-performance of substitutes, buyer propensity to switch, perceived level of product differentiation, technology evolution. - High threat → cap on industry prices and growth. **5. Rivalry Among Existing Competitors (1 mark)** - Determinants: Industry concentration, growth rate, fixed costs, exit barriers, product differentiation. - High rivalry → price wars, advertising wars, innovation races. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The collective strength of these five forces determines the long-run profit potential. A strategically positioned firm defends against these forces or influences them in its favour. **Stock Market Implication (1 mark):** High collective force intensity → low long-run ROE → compressed equity multiples. Low collective force intensity → durable rents → premium valuations (e.g., HUL, Nestle). --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks **Indian Aviation Industry Analysis (IndiGo, SpiceJet, Air India, Vistara):** | Force | Score $F_i$ | Detailed Justification | |-------|-------------|-------------------------| | 1. Threat of New Entrants | 2 | Aircraft leasing market exists, but huge capital (₹50,000+ crore), tight slot availability at metros, pilot scarcity — barriers are moderate-high. Akasa Air entered recently showing some entry is possible. | | 2. Supplier Power (Aircraft OEMs: Airbus, Boeing; Fuel: PSU OMCs; Pilots) | 5 | **Aircraft duopoly** (Airbus/Boeing), **PSU fuel monopoly** (price volatility hits margins directly), **severe pilot shortage** post-COVID. This is a textbook *high-supplier-power* industry. | | 3. Buyer Power (Passengers) | 5 | Highly price-sensitive customers, low switching cost (one click on MakeMyTrip), standardized product, multiple alternatives — buyer power is very high. | | 4. Threat of Substitutes | 4 | **Indian Railways** (Rajdhani, Shatabdi, Vande Bharat), **road transport**, **video conferencing for business travel**. Strong substitutes in price-conscious India. | | 5. Rivalry | 5 | Multiple carriers, slow industry growth, high fixed cost (aircraft lease, parking, salaries), no differentiation (commodity product), exit barriers (cannot redeploy aircraft easily) — **fierce rivalry**. | **Compute Industry Attractiveness Score:** $$ A_{aviation} = \frac{1}{5} \big[ (5-2) + (5-5) + (5-5) + (5-4) + (5-5) \big] $$ $$ A_{aviation} = \frac{1}{5} \big[ 3 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 \big] = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 $$ **Interpretation (1 mark):** With $A_{aviation} = 0.8$, the industry is **deeply structurally unattractive**. This is empirically validated by: - Kingfisher, Jet Airways — bankrupt - SpiceJet — chronic financial distress - Air India — sold back to Tata after government bailout **Stock Market Recommendation (1 mark):** **Avoid Indian aviation equities for long-term capital.** Tactical trades may work (e.g., post-COVID boom), but long-run ROE is structurally negative. Invest only via **defensive plays** like engine MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Overhaul) suppliers. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Students often write "Five Forces" only by name and lose **3 marks**. You MUST explain **determinants**, not just list the force. > - For numerical scoring, students forget the formula $A = \frac{1}{5}\sum(5-F_i)$ and just write adjectives. Always **show the arithmetic**. > - Students forget to **conclude with an investment action**. An analysis without an action is incomplete — that costs **1 mark**. --- ### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (CHOICE OPTION 2 — ALTERNATIVE) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain **Porter's Three Generic Strategies**. How are they connected to the Five Forces framework? *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** "A firm can be in a structurally attractive industry and still fail if it picks the wrong generic strategy." Discuss this statement with an **Indian example** for each strategy. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks **Porter's Three Generic Strategies (1985)** describe how a firm can **position itself** to defend against the Five Forces and earn **above-average returns** within its industry. **1. Cost Leadership (2 marks)** - Aim: Become the **lowest-cost producer** in the industry. - Tactics: Scale operations, efficient supply chain, tight cost control. - Indian Example: **D-Mart (Avenue Supermarts)**, **IndiGo Airlines**. **2. Differentiation (2 marks)** - Aim: Offer a **uniquely valued product** that customers will pay a premium for. - Tactics: Brand building, R&D, design, customer service. - Indian Example: **Apple India**, **Titan watches**, **Asian Paints**. **3. Focus (Niche) (2 marks)** - Aim: Serve a **narrow segment** excellently — either via cost focus or differentiation focus. - Indian Example: **Sabyasachi** (luxury bridal couture focus), **Rolls-Royce** (global ultra-luxury auto focus). **Connection to Five Forces (1 mark):** The Three Generic Strategies are the *firm-level response* to the *industry-level* Five Forces. A firm picks a generic strategy to **defend against the dominant force** in its industry. For example: - A commodity industry (high rivalry) → Cost Leadership is the only defense. - An industry with high buyer power (low switching cost) → Differentiation builds brand moat. - An industry with high supplier power → Scale (Cost Leadership) gives bargaining power. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks The statement is **correct**. Industry attractiveness is **necessary but not sufficient** — firm-level strategy determines actual returns. | Industry | Five Forces Score | Firm | Generic Strategy | Outcome | |----------|-------------------|------|------------------|---------| | **Aviation** | Unattractive (A = 0.8) | **IndiGo** | Cost Leadership | Survived & dominated; SpiceJet, Jet, Kingfisher failed despite being in the same industry. | | **Mobile Handsets** | Moderate rivalry | **Apple** | Differentiation | Sustained 30%+ gross margins; Xiaomi in cost-leadership, Micromax stuck-in-the-middle. | | **Luxury Bridal** | Narrow niche, low competition | **Sabyasachi** | Focus (Differentiation) | Premium pricing power, brand monopoly in segment. | | **Telecom (Pre-Jio)** | Attractive but mis-positioned | **Vodafone Idea** | Stuck in the Middle | Eroded by Jio's cost-leadership disruption. | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Industry structure sets the **opportunity set**, but only the right generic strategy converts opportunity into **sustainable economic profit**. **Stuck-in-the-Middle** firms are squeezed by all five forces. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Students confuse *Cost Leadership* with *Low Price*. Cost leadership enables low price **only if the firm chooses** — but Apple also has high costs and high prices. Differentiated firms can have **high cost** but charge premium. > - Do NOT write "Porter's strategies are about marketing." They are about **strategic positioning** at the firm level. > - For "Indian example," examiners expect named companies. Writing vague "Indian FMCG company" loses 1 mark. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam):** - ✅ **Porter** is a **Harvard** professor; framework is from **HBR 1979** article *"How Competitive Forces Shape Strategy."* - ✅ Five forces = **New Entrants, Suppliers, Buyers, Substitutes, Rivalry** — recite in order. - ✅ **Substitutes ≠ Competitors** — substitutes are from a *different* industry solving the *same need*. - ✅ Strong forces = **unattractive industry** = **low long-run ROE** = **compressed P/E** in stock market. - ✅ Weak forces = **attractive industry** = **durable moat** = **premium valuations**. - ✅ Three Generic Strategies = **Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus** — choose one; **avoid Stuck-in-the-Middle**. - ✅ Scoring formula: $A_{industry} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(5 - F_i)$; threshold for attractiveness = **2.5**. - ✅ **Indian Examples** to memorize: - *Attractive industry:* Pharmaceuticals (Sun, Dr. Reddy's), FMCG (HUL, Nestle) - *Unattractive industry:* Airlines (IndiGo, SpiceJet), Telecom (Jio, Airtel, Vi), Steel (Tata Steel, JSW) - ✅ **High supplier power** = commodity input + concentrated suppliers (e.g., aviation fuel PSU OMCs). - ✅ **High buyer power** = concentrated buyers + standardized product (e.g., Walmart with FMCG suppliers). - ✅ **Low entry threat** = heavy capital + regulation (e.g., Indian Banks, Defence, Telecom spectrum). - ✅ Limitations to know: Framework is **static**, ignores **disruption**, weak on **government policy** shifts, doesn't account for **complementary products** (e.g., apps for phones). - ✅ The Five Forces framework is used as the **first step of fundamental analysis** in stock selection — before you look at **financial statements, ratios, or valuations**.

topics.title: Financial Statements - Balance Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Financial Statements - Balance Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Balance Sheet** (also known as the **Statement of Financial Position**) is a formal, point-in-time financial statement that reports a company's assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity at a specific date. It is one of the three core financial statements mandated under the **Companies Act 2013** (India), the **SEBI (LODR) Regulations 2015**, and **Ind AS / IFRS** frameworks.

The fundamental identity that governs the balance sheet is the **Accounting Equation**, also known as the **Balance Sheet Equation**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Accounting Equation**
>
> $$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Shareholders' Equity}$$
>
> This equation **MUST** always balance — hence the name "Balance Sheet."

Where:
- **Assets** = Economic resources controlled by the entity as a result of past events, from which future economic benefits are expected to flow.
- **Liabilities** = Present obligations of the entity arising from past events, the settlement of which is expected to result in an outflow of resources.
- **Shareholders' Equity (Capital/Net Worth)** = The residual interest in the assets of the entity after deducting all liabilities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Family Photo" Analogy**
>
> Think of a balance sheet as a **financial photograph of a person** taken on a specific date (e.g., 31st March 2025).
>
> - **Assets** = Everything the person **OWNS** (house, car, savings, jewellery, phone, investments).
> - **Liabilities** = Everything the person **OWES** to others (home loan, car loan, credit card bills, money borrowed from friends).
> - **Net Worth / Equity** = What would be **LEFT OVER** if the person sold everything they own and paid off every single debt. It's the person's true wealth.
>
> **Assets − Liabilities = Net Worth**
>
> If your friend has a ₹50 lakh flat (asset) but owes ₹40 lakh on a home loan (liability), their actual wealth from this property is only **₹10 lakhs** (equity). The balance sheet captures exactly this snapshot.

## 1.3 Why the Balance Sheet Matters in Stock Markets

For a stock market investor, the balance sheet is the **single most important document** for evaluating a company's **financial health, solvency, and intrinsic value**. Warren Buffett famously said:

> *"In the business world, the rearview mirror is always clearer than the windshield."* — The balance sheet is that rearview mirror.

| Stock Market Use | What it tells the Investor |
|---|---|
| Solvency Analysis | Can the company survive long-term? |
| Liquidity Analysis | Can the company pay short-term dues? |
| Capital Structure | Debt vs. Equity mix — risk assessment |
| Asset Quality | Are the assets productive or "dead"? |
| Valuation | Book Value per Share = Equity ÷ No. of Shares |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structure of the Balance Sheet

A balance sheet is conventionally presented in a **vertical (T-account) format** as per **Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013**. It is divided into **two sides that MUST balance**.

### 2.1.1 Assets Side (Debit Side)

Assets are listed in **descending order of liquidity** (most liquid first):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Assets** — Those expected to be converted to cash, sold, or consumed **within 12 months** (operating cycle).
>
> - Cash and Cash Equivalents
> - Bank Balances
> - Short-term Investments / Marketable Securities
> - Trade Receivables (Sundry Debtors)
> - Inventory (Raw Material, WIP, Finished Goods)
> - Prepaid Expenses
> - Short-term Loans and Advances

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Current Assets** — Those held for **more than 12 months** for long-term use.
>
> - Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE)
> - Capital Work-in-Progress
> - Investment Property
> - Goodwill
> - Intangible Assets (Patents, Trademarks, Software)
> - Long-term Loans and Advances
> - Deferred Tax Assets (DTA)

### 2.1.2 Equity and Liabilities Side (Credit Side)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shareholders' Equity**
>
> - **Share Capital**: Equity Share Capital + Preference Share Capital
> - **Reserves & Surplus**: General Reserve, Capital Reserve, Securities Premium, Retained Earnings (P&L Balance), Revaluation Reserve
> - **Other Comprehensive Income (OCI)** items
> - **Money Received against Share Warrants**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Current Liabilities**
>
> - Long-term Borrowings (Debentures, Term Loans)
> - Deferred Tax Liabilities (DTL)
> - Long-term Provisions (Gratuity, Pension)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Liabilities**
>
> - Short-term Borrowings (Working Capital Loans, Cash Credit)
> - Trade Payables (Sundry Creditors)
> - Other Current Liabilities
> - Short-term Provisions
> - Current Maturities of Long-term Debt

## 2.2 Core Analytical Concepts

### 2.2.1 Working Capital

The **lifeblood of operations** — short-term funds available for daily business.

$$WC = \text{Current Assets (CA)} - \text{Current Liabilities (CL)}$$

A **positive** working capital = good short-term health. A **negative** working capital = potential liquidity crisis.

### 2.2.2 Net Working Capital vs Gross Working Capital

| Concept | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Gross Working Capital** | Total Current Assets of the firm |
| **Net Working Capital** | Current Assets − Current Liabilities |

### 2.2.3 Book Value vs Market Value

- **Book Value** = Total Assets − Total Liabilities (from balance sheet)
- **Book Value Per Share (BVPS)** = $\dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}{\text{Number of Outstanding Equity Shares}}$

> [!NOTE]
> BVPS is a **floor valuation** indicator. If the market price falls below BVPS for a sustained period, the market is essentially saying the company is worth more dead than alive.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = L + E$ | Fundamental Accounting Equation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $E = A - L$ | Shareholders' Equity (Net Worth) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $NWC = CA - CL$ | Net Working Capital | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $BVPS = \dfrac{E}{N}$ | Book Value Per Share | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $CR = \dfrac{CA}{CL}$ | Current Ratio (ideal ≈ 2:1) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $QR = \dfrac{CA - \text{Inventory}}{CL}$ | Quick / Acid Test Ratio (ideal ≈ 1:1) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $DR = \dfrac{TD}{TE}$ | Debt-Equity Ratio | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $DAR = \dfrac{TD}{TA}$ | Debt-Asset Ratio | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $WC \text{ Ratio} = \dfrac{CA}{CL}$ | Working Capital Ratio | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $PPE_{net} = PPE_{gross} - \text{Accumulated Depreciation}$ | Net Property, Plant & Equipment | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> **Legend:** A = Assets, L = Liabilities, E = Equity, N = Number of shares, CA = Current Assets, CL = Current Liabilities, TD = Total Debt, TE = Total Equity, TA = Total Assets.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Stock Markets

The balance sheet is used for:
1. **Fundamental Analysis** — Calculating ratios (P/B = Market Price ÷ BVPS).
2. **Credit Rating** — Agencies like CRISIL, ICRA, Moody's use balance sheet metrics to assign ratings.
3. **M&A Decisions** — Acquirers study the target's balance sheet for goodwill calculation.
4. **Loan Sanction** — Banks (RBI guidelines) insist on audited balance sheets for corporate lending.
5. **Insolvency Resolution** — Under **IBC 2016**, the balance sheet determines resolution plan viability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Accounting Equation

Let every transaction in business be analyzed using the **dual-aspect concept** of accounting (every debit has an equal and opposite credit).

**Step 1: Owner starts business with capital of ₹10,00,000.**

Effect: Cash ↑ by ₹10,00,000 (Asset) and Capital ↑ by ₹10,00,000 (Equity).

$$\text{Assets (Cash)} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}$$
$$10{,}00{,}000 = 0 + 10{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Buys machinery for ₹3,00,000 in cash.**

Effect: Machinery ↑ ₹3,00,000; Cash ↓ ₹3,00,000. (Asset composition changes; total unchanged.)

$$\text{Cash } (7,00,000) + \text{Machinery } (3,00,000) = 0 + 10{,}00{,}000$$
$$10{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Borrows ₹5,00,000 from bank (loan = liability).**

Effect: Cash ↑ ₹5,00,000; Loan (Liability) ↑ ₹5,00,000.

$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}$$
$$(7{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000) = 5{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000$$
$$15{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Buys inventory worth ₹2,00,000 on credit.**

Effect: Inventory ↑ ₹2,00,000; Creditors ↑ ₹2,00,000.

$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Total Equity}$$
$$(7{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000) = (5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000) + 10{,}00{,}000$$
$$17{,}00{,}000 = 17{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

**The equation ALWAYS balances. This is the beauty of double-entry bookkeeping.**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Constructing a Balance Sheet

> **Question:** From the following information, prepare the Balance Sheet of ABC Ltd. as on 31-03-2025:
>
> - Equity Share Capital: ₹50,00,000
> - Preference Share Capital: ₹10,00,000
> - General Reserve: ₹8,00,000
> - 9% Debentures: ₹20,00,000
> - Bank Loan (Long-term): ₹15,00,000
> - Trade Payables: ₹12,00,000
> - Land & Building: ₹40,00,000
> - Plant & Machinery: ₹35,00,000
> - Inventory: ₹18,00,000
> - Trade Receivables: ₹14,00,000
> - Cash in hand: ₹2,00,000
> - Cash at bank: ₹6,00,000

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Classify items into Assets, Equity, and Liabilities.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Shareholders' Funds} &= \text{Equity Capital} + \text{Preference Capital} + \text{Reserves} \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 68{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Classify Liabilities (Non-Current vs Current).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Non-Current Liabilities} &= 9\% \text{ Debentures} + \text{Bank Loan} \\
&= 20{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 35{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Current Liabilities} = \text{Trade Payables} = 12{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Classify Assets (Non-Current vs Current).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Non-Current Assets} &= \text{Land \& Building} + \text{Plant \& Machinery} \\
&= 40{,}00{,}000 + 35{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 75{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Assets} &= \text{Inventory} + \text{Receivables} + \text{Cash} + \text{Bank} \\
&= 18{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 40{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Verify the Equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Assets} &= \text{Non-Current} + \text{Current Assets} \\
&= 75{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}15{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Equity \& Liabilities} &= \text{Equity} + \text{Non-Curr. Liab.} + \text{Curr. Liab.} \\
&= 68{,}00{,}000 + 35{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}15{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Equity \& Liabilities} = \text{₹ } 1{,}15{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Present the Final Balance Sheet (Vertical Form).**

| **ABC LIMITED — BALANCE SHEET AS ON 31.03.2025** | | |
|---|---:|---:|
| **I. EQUITY AND LIABILITIES** | | |
| **Shareholders' Funds** | | |
| Equity Share Capital | 50,00,000 | |
| Preference Share Capital | 10,00,000 | |
| General Reserve | 8,00,000 | 68,00,000 |
| **Non-Current Liabilities** | | |
| 9% Debentures | 20,00,000 | |
| Long-term Bank Loan | 15,00,000 | 35,00,000 |
| **Current Liabilities** | | |
| Trade Payables | | 12,00,000 |
| **TOTAL** | | **1,15,00,000** |
| **II. ASSETS** | | |
| **Non-Current Assets** | | |
| Land & Building | 40,00,000 | |
| Plant & Machinery | 35,00,000 | 75,00,000 |
| **Current Assets** | | |
| Inventory | 18,00,000 | |
| Trade Receivables | 14,00,000 | |
| Cash at Bank | 6,00,000 | |
| Cash in Hand | 2,00,000 | 40,00,000 |
| **TOTAL** | | **1,15,00,000** |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Ratios from the Above Balance Sheet

If ABC Ltd. has **5,00,000 equity shares outstanding** and the **market price is ₹120**, compute the analytical metrics:

$$\begin{aligned}
BVPS &= \frac{\text{Total Equity}}{\text{No. of Shares}} = \frac{68{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = \text{₹ } 13.60
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Price-to-Book (P/B)} &= \frac{\text{Market Price}}{BVPS} = \frac{120}{13.60} \approx 8.82
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{CA}{CL} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 3.33
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{TD}{TE} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000}{68{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.51
\end{aligned}$$

| Ratio | Value | Inference |
|---|---|---|
| BVPS | ₹13.60 | Book value per share |
| P/B Ratio | 8.82× | Premium valuation (market is optimistic) |
| Current Ratio | 3.33 | Excellent short-term liquidity (>2 ideal) |
| D/E Ratio | 0.51 | Moderate leverage; financially stable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Balance Sheet Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BAL["BALANCE SHEET (Statement of Financial Position)"]
        subgraph EQ["EQUITY AND LIABILITIES SIDE (CREDIT)"]
            SE["SHAREHOLDERS EQUITY"]
            SE --> ESC["Equity Share Capital"]
            SE --> PSC["Preference Share Capital"]
            SE --> RES["Reserves and Surplus"]
            SE --> RE["Retained Earnings"]
            NCL["NON-CURRENT LIABILITIES"]
            NCL --> LTB["Long-term Borrowings (Debentures, Term Loans)"]
            NCL --> DTL["Deferred Tax Liabilities"]
            NCL --> LTP["Long-term Provisions"]
            CL["CURRENT LIABILITIES"]
            CL --> STB["Short-term Borrowings (Cash Credit)"]
            CL --> TP["Trade Payables (Creditors)"]
            CL --> OCL["Other Current Liabilities"]
        end
        subgraph AS["ASSETS SIDE (DEBIT)"]
            NCA["NON-CURRENT ASSETS"]
            NCA --> PPE["Property Plant and Equipment"]
            NCA --> INT["Intangible Assets (Goodwill, Patents)"]
            NCA --> LTI["Long-term Investments"]
            NCA --> DTA["Deferred Tax Assets"]
            CA["CURRENT ASSETS"]
            CA --> INV["Inventory (Raw Material, WIP, FG)"]
            CA --> TR["Trade Receivables (Debtors)"]
            CA --> CE["Cash and Cash Equivalents"]
            CA --> STI["Short-term Investments"]
        end
    end
    EQ -.BALANCE CHECK.-> AS
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — How the Balance Sheet is Prepared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Collect Trial Balance"] --> B["Step 2: Identify Real and Personal Accounts"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Classify into Assets and Liabilities"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Sub-classify into Current and Non-Current"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Arrange in Vertical Format (Schedule III)"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Verify Accounting Equation A = L + E"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Apply Auditor Sign-off and Disclose Notes"]
    G --> H["Final Balance Sheet Published"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — How Balance Sheet Links to Other Statements

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FS["Three Core Financial Statements"]
        BS["BALANCE SHEET\n(Point-in-time)"]
        PL["PROFIT AND LOSS STATEMENT\n(Period-based)"]
        CF["CASH FLOW STATEMENT\n(Period-based)"]
    end
    PL -- "Net Profit flows to Retained Earnings" --> BS
    BS -- "Opening Cash = Closing Cash of previous year" --> CF
    CF -- "Closing Cash reconciles with Cash in BS" --> BS
    PL -- "Net Profit + Non-cash items" --> CF
    BS -- "Depreciation on PPE" --> PL
```

## 4.4 Liquidity Hierarchy of Assets (Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["Most Liquid"] --> A1["Cash in Hand"]
    A1 --> A2["Cash at Bank"]
    A2 --> A3["Marketable Securities / Short-term Investments"]
    A3 --> A4["Trade Receivables (Debtors)"]
    A4 --> A5["Inventory (Finished Goods)"]
    A5 --> A6["Inventory (Work-in-Progress)"]
    A6 --> A7["Inventory (Raw Material)"]
    A7 --> A8["Prepaid Expenses"]
    A8 --> BTM["Least Liquid: PPE, Goodwill, Intangibles"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the Balance Sheet. State the fundamental accounting equation on which it is based.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** A Balance Sheet is a statement of financial position of a company as on a specific date. It shows the assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity of the entity in accordance with the **Companies Act 2013 (Schedule III)** and applicable accounting standards (Ind AS / IFRS).
- **Equation (1 Mark):**
$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Shareholders' Equity}$$

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between Current Assets and Non-Current Assets with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Basis | Current Assets (1½ Marks) | Non-Current Assets (1½ Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Period** | Realisable within 12 months | Held for more than 12 months |
| **Purpose** | Operating cycle use | Long-term operational use |
| **Examples** | Inventory, Trade Receivables, Cash | Land & Building, Plant & Machinery, Goodwill |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) Explain in detail the major components of a Balance Sheet as per Schedule III of the Companies Act 2013. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Balance Sheet as per Schedule III is presented in **vertical format** with two main heads:

**1. Equity and Liabilities (7 Marks breakdown given as sub-bullets):**

- **(i) Shareholders' Funds (2 Marks):** This includes Equity Share Capital, Preference Share Capital, and Reserves & Surplus. Share Capital represents the amount raised by issuing shares; Reserves include General Reserve, Capital Reserve, Securities Premium, and the undistributed Profit & Loss (Retained Earnings).

- **(ii) Non-Current Liabilities (2 Marks):** Long-term borrowings (debentures, term loans from banks/financial institutions), Deferred Tax Liabilities (DTL), and Long-term provisions (employee benefits like gratuity that mature beyond 12 months).

- **(iii) Current Liabilities (3 Marks):** Short-term borrowings (working capital, cash credit, overdraft), Trade Payables (amounts owed to suppliers), other current liabilities (statutory dues, unclaimed dividends), short-term provisions, and the current portion of long-term debt maturing within 12 months.

**(b) From the following balances of XYZ Ltd. as on 31.03.2025, prepare the Balance Sheet: (7 Marks)**

| Particulars | ₹ |
|---|---:|
| Equity Share Capital | 30,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 15,00,000 |
| Land & Building | 25,00,000 |
| Plant & Machinery | 20,00,000 |
| Inventory | 10,00,000 |
| Trade Receivables | 8,00,000 |
| Cash at Bank | 4,00,000 |
| Trade Payables | 6,00,000 |
| General Reserve | 5,00,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 1,00,000 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Classify Items.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Shareholders' Funds} &= 30{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 35{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Non-Current Liabilities} &= 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ (Debentures)} \\
\text{Current Liabilities} &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Non-Current Assets} &= 25{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Current Assets} &= 10{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 22{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Verify Balance Equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Assets} &= 45{,}00{,}000 + 22{,}00{,}000 = 67{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Total Equity \& Liab.} &= 35{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000 = 57{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Note: There is a mismatch of ₹10,00,000.** This is **Common Practical Adjustment**: A typical KTU exam question will provide "additional information" like a "Bank Loan of ₹10,00,000" omitted. If we add it to non-current liabilities, total becomes ₹67,00,000 and the sheet balances. Students must check for omissions — this is a classic examiner trap.

**Step 3: Final Balance Sheet (assuming Bank Loan ₹10,00,000 included in Non-Current Liabilities).**

| XYZ LIMITED — BALANCE SHEET AS ON 31.03.2025 | ₹ | ₹ |
|---|---:|---:|
| **I. Equity and Liabilities** | | |
| Share Capital (Equity) | 30,00,000 | |
| General Reserve | 5,00,000 | 35,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 15,00,000 | |
| Bank Loan | 10,00,000 | 25,00,000 |
| Trade Payables | 6,00,000 | |
| Outstanding Expenses | 1,00,000 | 7,00,000 |
| **Total** | | **67,00,000** |
| **II. Assets** | | |
| Land & Building | 25,00,000 | |
| Plant & Machinery | 20,00,000 | 45,00,000 |
| Inventory | 10,00,000 | |
| Trade Receivables | 8,00,000 | |
| Cash at Bank | 4,00,000 | 22,00,000 |
| **Total** | | **67,00,000** |

✅ **A = L + E → 67,00,000 = 67,00,000 (Balanced)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the significance and limitations of the Balance Sheet in stock market analysis. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance (4 Marks):**
- **Solvency Assessment:** Investors use the Debt-Equity ratio derived from the balance sheet to judge a company's long-term solvency. A company with high debt is risky in a recession.
- **Liquidity Check:** Current and Quick ratios help assess if the firm can meet short-term obligations — vital for survival.
- **Valuation:** Book Value Per Share (BVPS) and Price-to-Book (P/B) ratio are direct stock-picking tools used by **value investors** like Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett.
- **Capital Structure Insights:** Shows debt vs equity mix, helping in assessing **financial leverage** and **WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)**.
- **Trend Analysis:** Comparing balance sheets over multiple years reveals growth in assets, retained earnings, and capital infusion.

**Limitations (3 Marks):**
- **Historical Cost Bias:** Assets are recorded at original purchase price, NOT current market value. Land bought 30 years ago for ₹1 lakh may be worth ₹50 crores now.
- **Static Snapshot:** It is a single date picture — does not capture performance or efficiency (P&L is needed).
- **Window Dressing:** Companies can manipulate balance sheet figures at year-end (e.g., paying off loans just before 31st March).
- **Ignores Intangibles:** Brand value, human capital, customer loyalty are not captured.
- **Ignores Price-Level Changes:** No inflation adjustment under historical cost accounting (Ind AS allows revaluation, but not always adopted).

**(b) Calculate Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, and Debt-Equity Ratio from the following: (7 Marks)**

| Particulars | ₹ |
|---|---:|
| Current Assets | 6,00,000 |
| Inventory | 2,00,000 |
| Current Liabilities | 3,00,000 |
| Total Debt | 8,00,000 |
| Total Equity | 10,00,000 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Current Ratio**

$$CR = \frac{CA}{CL} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 2.0$$

Interpretation: 2:1 — **Ideal** short-term liquidity. ✅

**Step 2: Quick Ratio (Acid Test)**

$$QR = \frac{CA - \text{Inventory}}{CL} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \approx 1.33$$

Interpretation: Above 1:1 — Strong immediate liquidity. ✅

**Step 3: Debt-Equity Ratio**

$$D/E = \frac{TD}{TE} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 0.8$$

Interpretation: For every ₹1 of equity, there is ₹0.80 of debt — **Moderate leverage**; acceptable for a manufacturing firm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to balance the equation** at the end of balance sheet preparation → 1 to 2 marks lost. Always verify $A = L + E$ explicitly.
> 2. **Misclassifying "Debentures"** — 5-year debentures are NON-CURRENT, but the portion maturing within the next 12 months must be shown under CURRENT liabilities. Most students miss this.
> 3. **Confusing Reserves with Cash** — Reserves are NOT cash. They are accounting provisions. Reserves and Surplus includes "Securities Premium" which cannot be used for dividend distribution (Companies Act restriction).
> 4. **BVPS Calculation Mistake** — Only **Equity Share Capital** is in the denominator logic, NOT Preference Capital. Use only equity portion of shareholders' funds.
> 5. **No mention of "as on date"** — Balance sheet is point-in-time, not for a period. The header MUST read "as on 31.03.2025," not "for the year ended."
> 6. **Missing Note to Accounts** — Under Ind AS / Schedule III, every balance sheet must accompany Notes 1–30. Skipping them in long answers loses presentation marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Balance Sheet**
>
> ✅ Balance Sheet is a **point-in-time** statement (as on date), not a flow statement.
>
> ✅ It follows the **Accounting Equation: A = L + E** — ALWAYS, without exception.
>
> ✅ It has **two sides**: Equity & Liabilities (credit) and Assets (debit), presented in **vertical format** as per Schedule III.
>
> ✅ Assets are listed in **descending order of liquidity** — Cash first, PPE last.
>
> ✅ **Current** items mature within 12 months; **Non-current** mature beyond 12 months.
>
> ✅ **Reserves & Surplus** includes Securities Premium, General Reserve, Capital Reserve, Retained Earnings, Revaluation Reserve.
>
> ✅ **Goodwill** appears only on acquisition (under Ind AS — internally generated goodwill is not recognised).
>
> ✅ **BVPS** = Shareholders' Equity (excluding preference) ÷ Number of Equity Shares.
>
> ✅ **Current Ratio** ideal = **2:1**; **Quick Ratio** ideal = **1:1**; **D/E** varies by industry.
>
> ✅ **Limitations**: Historical cost, ignores inflation, static, manipulable, ignores intangibles.
>
> ✅ **Stock market uses**: Solvency, liquidity, valuation (P/B), capital structure, M&A.
>
> ✅ Balance Sheet is one of **3 financial statements** — links to **P&L** (via Net Profit → Retained Earnings) and **Cash Flow** (via Closing Cash).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Profit & Loss Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Profit & Loss Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Profit and Loss (P&L) Statement**, also formally called the **Income Statement** or **Statement of Profit and Loss**, is one of the three core financial statements mandated under the **Companies Act, 2013 (Schedule III)** and the **Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS)** framework applicable in India. It is a flow statement that reports a company's **financial performance** over a specific accounting period (quarterly or annually), summarizing **revenues, expenses, gains, and losses** to arrive at the **net profit or net loss** for that period.

The P&L statement is governed in India by:
- **Ind AS 1** – Presentation of Financial Statements
- **Ind AS 18 / Ind AS 115** – Revenue Recognition
- **Ind AS 19** – Employee Benefits
- **Ind AS 23** – Borrowing Costs
- **Ind AS 38** – Intangible Assets (Amortization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** In the context of stock markets and trading, the P&L Statement is the **single most important document** used by fundamental analysts, traders, and investors to evaluate a company's earning power, operational efficiency, and future growth potential. The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) mandates listed companies to publish quarterly and annual P&L statements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Profit & Loss" Water Tank Analogy** 💧
>
> Imagine your business is a **large water tank** with water flowing in from the top and draining out from various holes:
> - **Water flowing IN** (Credits): Sales revenue, interest income, other income → These are your **"Top Line" inflows**.
> - **First hole (highest)**: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) → What drains out is the **Gross Profit** (water remaining at the first checkpoint).
> - **Second hole (middle)**: Operating Expenses (salaries, rent, marketing) → What remains is the **Operating Profit (EBIT)**.
> - **Third hole (lower)**: Interest, Depreciation, and Tax → The final water left at the bottom is the **Net Profit** (your "Bottom Line").
>
> The **P&L Statement is simply a vertical, organized report of all these inflows and outflows** showing exactly how much "water" (profit) finally reaches the bottom of the tank. If the tank is empty at the bottom, you have a **loss**; if it's full, you have **profit**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Standard Metrics Highlighted)

| Term | Meaning | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Top Line** | Gross Revenue / Total Sales | Starting point – the largest inflow |
| **Bottom Line** | Net Profit After Tax (PAT) | Final residual figure – the most-watched number |
| **EBIT** | Earnings Before Interest and Tax | Operating performance measure |
| **EBITDA** | Earnings Before Interest, Tax, Depreciation & Amortization | Cash-operating performance |
| **PAT** | Profit After Tax | Synonym of Net Profit |
| **EPS** | Earnings Per Share | Profit attributable to one share |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU examinations, examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between **EBIT, EBITDA, EBT, and PAT** — these four acronyms are the most frequently tested terms from this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of P&L Construction

The P&L Statement is built in a **cascading, deductive format** — every line is derived by subtracting the previous level of expenses from the line above it. This creates a logical "ladder" of profit metrics.

1. **Step 1 – Revenue Recognition**: Report **Net Sales** (Gross Sales minus Sales Returns, Allowances, and Trade Discounts).
2. **Step 2 – Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)**: Deduct direct production costs: raw materials, direct labor, direct manufacturing overhead, freight inward.
3. **Step 3 – Gross Profit**: Net Sales − COGS = **Gross Profit**.
4. **Step 4 – Operating Expenses**: Subtract Selling, General & Administrative Expenses (SG&A), R&D, Marketing, Depreciation (sometimes), Salaries.
5. **Step 5 – Operating Profit (EBIT)**: Gross Profit − Operating Expenses = **Operating Profit**.
6. **Step 6 – Non-Operating Items**: Add Non-Operating Income (interest received, dividend received); subtract Non-Operating Expenses (interest paid, losses on asset sale).
7. **Step 7 – EBT (PBT)**: Operating Profit ± Non-Operating Items = **Earnings Before Tax (PBT)**.
8. **Step 8 – Tax Expense**: Deduct current tax and deferred tax as per **Ind AS 12**.
9. **Step 9 – Net Profit (PAT)**: PBT − Tax = **Profit After Tax (Net Profit)**.
10. **Step 10 – EPS**: Net Profit attributable to equity shareholders ÷ Weighted Average Number of Equity Shares Outstanding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" is this cascading structure useful?**
> It allows analysts to identify **where** a company is making or losing money. A company with strong gross profit but weak net profit may have high debt (high interest) or poor tax planning. This is the basis of **Vertical Analysis (Common-Size Statement)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Purpose in Trading/Investing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Sales** | Gross Sales − Sales Returns − Discounts | True revenue earned |
| 2 | **Gross Profit (GP)** | Net Sales − COGS | Manufacturing/service efficiency |
| 3 | **Gross Profit Margin (GPM)** | $(\text{GP} \div \text{Net Sales}) \times 100$ | Pricing power & cost control |
| 4 | **Operating Profit (EBIT)** | GP − Operating Expenses | Core business profitability |
| 5 | **Operating Margin (OM)** | $(\text{EBIT} \div \text{Net Sales}) \times 100$ | Operational efficiency |
| 6 | **EBITDA** | EBIT + Depreciation + Amortization | Cash operating performance |
| 7 | **EBITDA Margin** | $(\text{EBITDA} \div \text{Net Sales}) \times 100$ | Cash flow proxy |
| 8 | **PBT / EBT** | EBIT + Non-Op Income − Non-Op Expenses | Profit before government takes its share |
| 9 | **Net Profit (PAT)** | PBT − Tax Expense | Final distributable profit |
| 10 | **Net Profit Margin (NPM)** | $(\text{PAT} \div \text{Net Sales}) \times 100$ | Overall profitability |
| 11 | **EPS (Basic)** | $(\text{Net Profit} - \text{Preferred Dividends}) \div \text{Weighted Avg. Equity Shares}$ | Per-share profit |
| 12 | **P/E Ratio** | $\text{Market Price per Share} \div \text{EPS}$ | Valuation multiple |
| 13 | **Tax Rate (Effective)** | $(\text{Tax Expense} \div \text{PBT}) \times 100$ | Real tax burden |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Note:** Although this is a finance course, the analytical rigor required (cascading logical deductions, percentage analysis, and ratio computation) directly mirrors the engineering problem-solving methodology taught across all KTU streams — making this topic an important skill even for non-commerce students.

### 2.3 The Two Methods of P&L Presentation

- **Nature of Expense Method (Ind AS Compliant)**: Expenses are grouped by **nature** (e.g., Raw Materials, Employee Costs, Depreciation separately).
- **Function of Expense Method / Cost of Sales Method**: Expenses are grouped by **function** (e.g., COGS, Selling Expenses, Administrative Expenses). This is the **traditional Indian format** taught in most commerce courses and KTU papers.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, the **Function of Expense Method** is the most commonly tested format.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Solved Example — P&L Preparation

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Board Exam Style):**

> From the following particulars of **M/s Karunya Tech Solutions Pvt. Ltd.** for the year ended 31st March 2024, **prepare the Statement of Profit and Loss** as per Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013, and compute key profitability ratios.

**Given Data (₹ in Lakhs):**

| Particulars | Amount (₹ in Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Revenue from Operations (Net Sales) | 5,000 |
| Other Income (Interest on Investments) | 200 |
| Cost of Materials Consumed | 1,200 |
| Purchases of Stock-in-Trade | 800 |
| Changes in Inventories (Opening − Closing) | 300 |
| Employee Benefits Expense | 700 |
| Finance Costs (Interest on Loan) | 250 |
| Depreciation and Amortization Expense | 350 |
| Other Expenses (Admin + Selling) | 600 |
| Tax Rate (Effective) | 25% |
| Outstanding Equity Shares (Weighted Avg.) | 1,00,00,000 shares |
| Market Price per Share (Closing) | ₹48 |

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction of P&L Statement

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Identify Net Revenue from Operations} \\
\text{Net Revenue from Operations} &= \text{Revenue from Operations (given)} \\
&= \mathbf{5{,}000 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 2: Compute Total Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)} \\
\text{COGS} &= \text{Materials Consumed} + \text{Purchases} + \text{Change in Inventory} \\
&= 1{,}200 + 800 + 300 \\
&= \mathbf{2{,}300 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 3: Calculate Gross Profit} \\
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Net Sales} - \text{COGS} \\
&= 5{,}000 - 2{,}300 \\
&= \mathbf{2{,}700 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 4: Compute Total Operating Expenses} \\
\text{Employee Benefits} &= 700 \\
\text{Depreciation \& Amortization} &= 350 \\
\text{Other Expenses (Admin + Selling)} &= 600 \\
\text{Total Operating Expenses} &= 700 + 350 + 600 \\
&= \mathbf{1{,}650 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 5: Calculate Operating Profit (EBIT)} \\
\text{EBIT} &= \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Total Operating Expenses} \\
&= 2{,}700 - 1{,}650 \\
&= \mathbf{1{,}050 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 6: Adjust for Non-Operating Items} \\
\text{Add: Other Income} &= +200 \\
\text{Less: Finance Costs (Interest)} &= -250 \\
\text{Earnings Before Tax (PBT)} &= 1{,}050 + 200 - 250 \\
&= \mathbf{1{,}000 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 7: Compute Tax Expense} \\
\text{Tax Expense} &= 25\% \text{ of PBT} \\
&= 0.25 \times 1{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{250 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 8: Compute Net Profit After Tax (PAT)} \\
\text{PAT} &= \text{PBT} - \text{Tax} \\
&= 1{,}000 - 250 \\
&= \mathbf{750 \text{ Lakhs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Final P&L Statement (Format Expected in KTU Answer Sheet)

| Particulars | Note No. | Amount (₹ in Lakhs) | Amount (₹ in Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Revenue from Operations** | | | **5,000** |
| **II. Other Income** | | | **200** |
| **III. Total Income (I + II)** | | | **5,200** |
| **IV. Expenses:** | | | |
|    (a) Cost of Materials Consumed | | 1,200 | |
|    (b) Purchases of Stock-in-Trade | | 800 | |
|    (c) Changes in Inventories | | 300 | |
|    (d) Employee Benefits Expense | | 700 | |
|    (e) Finance Costs | | 250 | |
|    (f) Depreciation & Amortization | | 350 | |
|    (g) Other Expenses | | 600 | |
|    **Total Expenses** | | | **4,200** |
| **V. Profit Before Tax (III − IV)** | | | **1,000** |
| **VI. Tax Expense (25%)** | | | **250** |
| **VII. Profit After Tax / Net Profit (V − VI)** | | | **750** |

---

### 3.4 Key Ratios Computed from the P&L

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit Margin} &= \frac{2{,}700}{5{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{54\%} \\
\text{Operating Margin (EBIT Margin)} &= \frac{1{,}050}{5{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{21\%} \\
\text{Net Profit Margin} &= \frac{750}{5{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{15\%} \\
\text{EBITDA} &= 1{,}050 + 350 = \mathbf{1{,}400 \text{ Lakhs}} \\
\text{EBITDA Margin} &= \frac{1{,}400}{5{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{28\%} \\
\text{EPS} &= \frac{750 \times 1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{₹7.50 \text{ per share}} \\
\text{P/E Ratio} &= \frac{48}{7.50} = \mathbf{6.4 \text{ times}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Analytical Insight:** A P/E of 6.4 is significantly below the Indian market average (~22–25 for Nifty 50). This signals the company is **undervalued or facing challenges** — a fundamental analyst's first clue to dig deeper.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Year-on-Year Comparison (Vertical Analysis)

| Particulars | Current Year (₹ Lakhs) | % of Net Sales | Previous Year (₹ Lakhs) | % of Net Sales | Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net Sales | 5,000 | 100% | 4,500 | 100% | ↑ |
| COGS | 2,300 | 46% | 2,400 | 53.3% | Improving |
| Gross Profit | 2,700 | 54% | 2,100 | 46.7% | Improving |
| Operating Expenses | 1,650 | 33% | 1,400 | 31.1% | Slight rise |
| EBIT | 1,050 | 21% | 700 | 15.6% | Strong ↑ |
| PBT | 1,000 | 20% | 650 | 14.4% | ↑ |
| PAT | 750 | 15% | 487.5 | 10.8% | ↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this analytical matrix crucial?** It allows the trader/investor to instantly see **margin expansion or contraction**, which is more important than absolute profit growth. A company growing sales at 5% but margins expanding by 3 percentage points is a **far better investment** than one growing at 20% with shrinking margins.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cascading P&L Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This block diagram shows the **logical flow of fund deduction** from top-line revenue to bottom-line net profit, and how each profitability metric is derived.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["REVENUE FROM OPERATIONS (Net Sales)"]:::top
    B["OTHER INCOME (Interest, Dividends)"]:::income
    C["TOTAL REVENUE A + B"]:::total
    D["COST OF GOODS SOLD (COGS)"]:::cost
    E["GROSS PROFIT C minus D"]:::profit1
    F["EMPLOYEE BENEFITS EXPENSE"]:::opex
    G["DEPRECIATION AND AMORTIZATION"]:::opex
    H["SELLING AND ADMIN EXPENSES"]:::opex
    I["OPERATING PROFIT EBIT E minus F plus G plus H"]:::profit2
    J["FINANCE COSTS Interest on Loan"]:::nonop
    K["PROFIT BEFORE TAX PBT I plus B minus J"]:::profit3
    L["TAX EXPENSE As per Ind AS 12"]:::tax
    M["NET PROFIT AFTER TAX PAT K minus L"]:::profit4
    N["EPS M divided by Weighted Avg Shares"]:::eps

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G
    E --> H
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    B --> K
    I --> K
    K --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L --> M
    M --> N

    classDef top fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3b66,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef income fill:#17becf,stroke:#0d6b6b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef total fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#1a4d1a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cost fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a1212,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef profit1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#5a3a85,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef opex fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#a8520a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef profit2 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#5a3a85,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nonop fill:#8c564b,stroke:#5a322a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef profit3 fill:#e377c2,stroke:#8a4a7a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tax fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#4a4a4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef profit4 fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#6b6d12,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef eps fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.2 P&L Statement — Hierarchical Vertical Format (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["I. REVENUE FROM OPERATIONS"]:::layer1
    L2["II. OTHER INCOME"]:::layer1
    L3["III. TOTAL INCOME L1 plus L2"]:::layer2
    L4["IV. TOTAL EXPENSES a through g"]:::layer3
    L5["V. PROFIT BEFORE TAX L3 minus L4"]:::layer4
    L6["VI. TAX EXPENSE"]:::layer3
    L7["VII. PROFIT AFTER TAX NET PROFIT L5 minus L6"]:::layer5
    L8["KEY OUTPUTS EPS, P/E, Margins"]:::output

    L1 --> L3
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> L8

    classDef layer1 fill:#4a90e2,stroke:#1c3d6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef layer2 fill:#5cb85c,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#ffffff
    classDef layer3 fill:#d9534f,stroke:#6b1e1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef layer4 fill:#f0ad4e,stroke:#8a5a1a,color:#ffffff
    classDef layer5 fill:#5cb85c,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef output fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Functional Block — How P&L Connects to Other Financial Statements

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["TRIAL BALANCE"]:::src
    P2["ADJUSTING ENTRIES Accruals, Prepayments, Depreciation"]:::src
    P3["STATEMENT OF PROFIT AND LOSS"]:::main
    P4["BALANCE SHEET Closing Stock, Profit Transfer"]:::main
    P5["CASH FLOW STATEMENT PAT, Depreciation add-back"]:::main
    P6["STATEMENT OF CHANGES IN EQUITY PAT transferred to Reserves"]:::main

    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P3 --> P6

    classDef src fill:#a9a9a9,stroke:#4a4a4a,color:#000000
    classDef main fill:#4682b4,stroke:#1a3a5a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In KTU exams, examiners often award 1–2 marks for a **neat, well-labeled diagram** that explains the P&L structure. The above Mermaid diagrams are precisely aligned with the **Schedule III vertical format** mandated by the Companies Act, 2013.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]
**Distinguish between EBIT and EBITDA. Why is EBITDA considered a better metric for comparing companies in the same industry?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | EBIT | EBITDA |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Earnings Before Interest and Tax | Earnings Before Interest, Tax, Depreciation & Amortization |
| Includes Non-Cash Items | No (depreciation is deducted) | Yes (depreciation is added back) |
| Measures | Accounting profit | Cash operating profit |

**Why EBITDA is preferred for comparison:**
- (1 mark) Eliminates the effect of different **financing decisions** (debt vs. equity) across companies.
- (1 mark) Removes the impact of varying **accounting policies** for depreciation and amortization.
- (1 mark) Better reflects the **cash-generating ability** of core operations, making it the metric used in **EV/EBITDA valuation multiples** by analysts on Dalal Street.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]
**What is the "Bottom Line" of a Profit & Loss Statement? Explain its significance in fundamental analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- (1 mark) The **Bottom Line** of a P&L Statement refers to the **Net Profit After Tax (PAT)** — the final figure remaining after all expenses (operating, non-operating, and tax) have been deducted from total revenue.
- (1 mark) **Significance in Fundamental Analysis:** PAT is the figure from which **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** is computed. EPS directly drives the **Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio**, which is the most widely used valuation metric in equity markets. A consistent growth in PAT signals **financial health, management efficiency, and earnings quality** to investors.
- (1 mark) PAT determines the **dividend payout capacity** of the company and is also the key input for the **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** valuation model used by institutional investors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

[KTU University Exam – Model Paper aligned to 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** From the following information of **M/s Greenshore Industries Ltd.**, prepare the **Statement of Profit and Loss** for the year ended 31st March 2024 in the **vertical format** as per Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013.

**Trial Balance Extracts (₹ in Lakhs):**

| Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue from Operations (Net Sales) | – | 8,000 |
| Other Income (Interest Received) | – | 300 |
| Cost of Materials Consumed | 1,800 | – |
| Purchases of Stock-in-Trade | 1,200 | – |
| Changes in Inventory (Stock Decrease) | 500 | – |
| Employee Benefits Expense | 1,000 | – |
| Finance Costs | 350 | – |
| Depreciation & Amortization | 450 | – |
| Other Expenses (Admin + Selling) | 900 | – |
| Provision for Tax (Current Year) | – | (To be computed at 30%) |

**(b)** Compute and interpret the **Gross Profit Margin, Operating Margin, and Net Profit Margin**. Also calculate **EPS** assuming 2,00,00,000 weighted average equity shares are outstanding.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — P&L Statement (7 Marks):**

| Line | Particulars | Calculation | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | Revenue from Operations | Given | **8,000** |
| II | Other Income | Given | **300** |
| III | **Total Income (I + II)** | 8,000 + 300 | **8,300** |
| IV (a) | Cost of Materials Consumed | Given | 1,800 |
| IV (b) | Purchases of Stock-in-Trade | Given | 1,200 |
| IV (c) | Changes in Inventory | Given | 500 |
| IV (d) | Employee Benefits Expense | Given | 1,000 |
| IV (e) | Finance Costs | Given | 350 |
| IV (f) | Depreciation & Amortization | Given | 450 |
| IV (g) | Other Expenses | Given | 900 |
| | **Total Expenses** | Sum of (a) to (g) | **6,200** |
| V | **Profit Before Tax (PBT)** | 8,300 − 6,200 | **2,100** |
| VI | Tax Expense (30% of PBT) | 0.30 × 2,100 | **630** |
| VII | **Profit After Tax (PAT / Net Profit)** | 2,100 − 630 | **1,470** |

**Part (b) — Ratio Computation & Interpretation (7 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit} &= 8{,}000 - 1{,}800 - 1{,}200 - 500 = 4{,}500 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{Gross Profit Margin} &= \frac{4{,}500}{8{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{56.25\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Profit (EBIT)} &= \text{GP} - \text{Employee Benefits} - \text{Depreciation} - \text{Other Expenses} \\
&= 4{,}500 - 1{,}000 - 450 - 900 = 2{,}150 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{Operating Margin} &= \frac{2{,}150}{8{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{26.88\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit Margin} &= \frac{1{,}470}{8{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{18.38\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EPS} &= \frac{1{,}470 \times 1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{₹7.35 \text{ per share}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (for full 7 marks):**
- (1 mark) **GPM of 56.25%** indicates strong pricing power and efficient cost-of-production management — a healthy ratio for a manufacturing/trading company.
- (1 mark) **Operating Margin of 26.88%** shows that nearly 27 paise of every rupee of sales is converted into operating profit, reflecting excellent operational efficiency.
- (1 mark) **NPM of 18.38%** is well above the Indian industry average, indicating strong bottom-line profitability after absorbing all costs including interest and tax.
- (1 mark) **EPS of ₹7.35** would be benchmarked against the company's stock price to determine its P/E ratio; consistent year-on-year EPS growth would attract long-term institutional investors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

[KTU University Exam – Model Paper aligned to 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **vertical (cascading) format** of a Statement of Profit and Loss with a clearly labeled diagram. Discuss the **key components** of a P&L Statement under Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013.

**(b)** The following data pertains to **M/s TechVista Solutions Ltd.** (₹ in Crores):

| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| Net Sales | 1,200 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 720 |
| Operating Expenses | 180 |
| Interest Expense | 60 |
| Depreciation | 90 |
| Non-Operating Income | 30 |
| Tax Rate | 25% |
| Outstanding Shares | 6 Crore shares |
| Market Price per Share | ₹125 |

**From the above, calculate:** (i) Gross Profit & GPM (ii) EBIT and Operating Margin (iii) EBITDA and EBITDA Margin (iv) Net Profit and NPM (v) EPS and P/E Ratio. **Provide a one-line interpretation** of whether the company appears overvalued or undervalued relative to a sector P/E of 30.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks):**

- (1 mark) **Definition of Vertical Format:** A P&L in vertical format presents items **sequentially from top to bottom**, starting with revenue and ending with net profit (PAT). Each item is a **subtotal of the previous**.
- (1 mark) **Key Components under Schedule III:**
  1. Revenue from Operations (Net Sales)
  2. Other Income
  3. Total Revenue (1 + 2)
  4. Expenses: (a) Cost of Materials, (b) Purchases of Stock-in-Trade, (c) Changes in Inventory, (d) Employee Benefits, (e) Finance Costs, (f) Depreciation & Amortization, (g) Other Expenses
  5. Profit Before Tax (3 − 4)
  6. Tax Expense (Current + Deferred)
  7. Profit After Tax / Net Profit (5 − 6)
- (2 marks) **Diagram of vertical format** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 above).
- (1 mark) **Why vertical format?** Easier to read, standardized across companies, allows **vertical analysis** (each line as % of revenue), and is the only format accepted by SEBI for listed company filings.
- (1 mark) **Comparison with Horizontal Format:** Horizontal format is not permitted under Schedule III for Indian listed companies; only the vertical (function-of-expense) format is allowed.
- (1 mark) **Real-world utility:** Allows investors to compare profitability of two companies in the same sector regardless of their size.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Gross Profit \& GPM} \\
\text{Gross Profit} &= 1{,}200 - 720 = \mathbf{₹480 \text{ Crores}} \\
\text{GPM} &= \frac{480}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \mathbf{40\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(ii) EBIT \& Operating Margin} \\
\text{EBIT} &= \text{GP} - \text{Operating Expenses} - \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 480 - 180 - 90 = \mathbf{₹210 \text{ Crores}} \\
\text{Operating Margin} &= \frac{210}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \mathbf{17.5\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(iii) EBITDA \& EBITDA Margin} \\
\text{EBITDA} &= \text{EBIT} + \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 210 + 90 = \mathbf{₹300 \text{ Crores}} \\
\text{EBITDA Margin} &= \frac{300}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \mathbf{25\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(iv) Net Profit \& NPM} \\
\text{PBT} &= \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest} + \text{Non-Op Income} \\
&= 210 - 60 + 30 = \mathbf{₹180 \text{ Crores}} \\
\text{Tax} &= 25\% \times 180 = \mathbf{₹45 \text{ Crores}} \\
\text{PAT (Net Profit)} &= 180 - 45 = \mathbf{₹135 \text{ Crores}} \\
\text{Net Profit Margin} &= \frac{135}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \mathbf{11.25\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(v) EPS \& P/E Ratio} \\
\text{EPS} &= \frac{135 \text{ Crore}}{6 \text{ Crore shares}} = \mathbf{₹22.50 \text{ per share}} \\
\text{P/E Ratio} &= \frac{125}{22.50} = \mathbf{5.56 \text{ times}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Interpretation (1 mark):**
The P/E of 5.56 is **significantly lower** than the sector average of 30, indicating that **TechVista Solutions appears undervalued** relative to its peers. This could represent a **value buying opportunity**, or could signal underlying issues (e.g., declining growth, sector headwinds) requiring deeper fundamental investigation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on P&L Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add back Non-Operating Income** when computing PBT. (Loses 1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing Operating Expenses with Total Expenses.** Employee benefits, depreciation, and other expenses are *operating*; only interest paid is *non-operating*. (Loses 1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong treatment of "Changes in Inventory."** A **decrease in inventory** is an *expense* (cost), while an *increase* is a *gain* (income). Students often reverse this. (Loses 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Calculation errors in EPS** — forgetting to convert Lakhs/Crores correctly into per-share rupees. (Loses 1 mark)
> 5. **Not labeling the Statement properly** — Schedule III requires specific line items in a specific order; deviation leads to loss of format marks. (Loses 1 mark)
> 6. **Mixing up EBITDA and EBIT** — EBITDA is EBIT + Depreciation + Amortization. Many students *subtract* depreciation instead of adding it back. (Loses 1–2 marks)
> 7. **Skipping interpretation** — KTU examiners give 1–2 marks for **interpretation of ratios**, not just computation. Students who only compute and do not comment lose easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **P&L Statement** is a **flow statement** (covers a period), unlike the Balance Sheet which is a **stock statement** (point-in-time).
- ✅ P&L is prepared as per **Schedule III** of the Companies Act, 2013, in **vertical format** only.
- ✅ The **Top Line** is **Net Sales (Revenue from Operations)**; the **Bottom Line** is **Net Profit After Tax (PAT)**.
- ✅ **Cascading Order of Deductions:** Net Sales → (less COGS) → Gross Profit → (less Operating Expenses) → EBIT → (less Interest + add Non-Op Income) → PBT → (less Tax) → PAT.
- ✅ **EBITDA = EBIT + Depreciation + Amortization** — used as a proxy for **cash operating profit**.
- ✅ **EPS = (Net Profit − Preferred Dividends) ÷ Weighted Average Number of Equity Shares** — drives the P/E ratio.
- ✅ **Three core margin ratios to always compute:** Gross Profit Margin, Operating Margin, Net Profit Margin.
- ✅ **Vertical (Common-Size) Analysis** converts each line item to a % of Net Sales — allows year-on-year comparison.
- ✅ **Ind AS 1** governs the presentation of P&L; **Ind AS 12** governs tax expense treatment.
- ✅ **"Other Income"** is reported *separately* from "Revenue from Operations" — never combine them before computing GP.
- ✅ For KTU exams, always present the P&L in a **properly labeled table with Note No. columns** (even if "Note No." is left blank).
- ✅ Always compute **both absolute profit AND margin %** — examiners reward both.
- ✅ A **P/E ratio below sector average** typically signals **undervaluation** (but warrants deeper investigation).
- ✅ **Net Profit flows into** the Balance Sheet (as retained earnings) and the Cash Flow Statement (PAT + Depreciation add-back as the starting point).
- ✅ The P&L is the **starting point of fundamental analysis** — it feeds into the Cash Flow Statement, Statement of Changes in Equity, and informs valuation models (DCF, P/E, EV/EBITDA).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cash Flow Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cash Flow Statement (CFS)** is a financial statement that provides a detailed summary of all the **cash inflows** and **cash outflows** of a business entity during a particular accounting period. It is prepared in addition to the Balance Sheet and the Profit & Loss Account as per **Accounting Standard (AS-3)** "Cash Flow Statements" issued by the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** A Cash Flow Statement reports the movement of **cash and cash equivalents** (defined as cash on hand, demand deposits, and short-term highly liquid investments maturing within 3 months) across three specific activities: **Operating, Investing, and Financing**.

The fundamental equation governing the statement is:

$$\text{Opening Cash} + \text{Net Cash Flow} = \text{Closing Cash}$$

Where **Net Cash Flow** is the algebraic sum of cash generated or used in the three activities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Personal Home Budget"

Imagine you are a college student tracking your monthly finances. You have three compartments in a notebook:

1. **Operating (Pocket Money vs Daily Expenses):** Pocket money received, part-time job wages, minus food, transport, books. This is the *core* recurring activity.
2. **Investing (Asset Purchases):** Buying a second-hand laptop, selling old textbooks. These are infrequent but affect your long-term financial position.
3. **Financing (Loans & Transfers):** Taking a loan from a friend, repaying it, or receiving money from parents as a "capital infusion."

The Cash Flow Statement of a company works **identically** — it segregates cash movement into these three logical buckets, making it easy for investors to judge **liquidity, solvency, and operational efficiency**.

## 1.3 Why Cash Flow Statement is Needed

> [!NOTE]
> **The Accrual Fallacy:** A company can report a **huge profit** on paper (using the accrual system) yet **run out of cash** and go bankrupt (e.g., a builder selling flats on 10-year EMIs). The Cash Flow Statement exposes this gap between **paper profit** and **actual cash**.

Key stakeholders who rely on CFS:
- **Equity Investors** — to assess dividend-paying capacity.
- **Lenders/Banks** — to evaluate debt-servicing ability.
- **Management** — for internal liquidity planning.
- **Regulators (SEBI/RBI)** — for systemic risk assessment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Cash Flow

### A. Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)
Cash generated or used in the **core revenue-producing business** of the entity.

- **Inflows:** Cash received from customers, interest received, dividends received.
- **Outflows:** Cash paid to suppliers, employees, operating expenses, income tax.

### B. Cash Flow from Investing Activities (CFI)
Cash flows relating to the **acquisition and disposal of long-term assets** and investments.

- **Inflows:** Sale of fixed assets, sale of investments, interest received on loans given.
- **Outflows:** Purchase of fixed assets, purchase of investments, loans given to third parties.

### C. Cash Flow from Financing Activities (CFF)
Cash flows resulting from changes in the **capital structure** of the entity.

- **Inflows:** Issue of shares, raising of long-term loans/debentures.
- **Outflows:** Repayment of loans, redemption of debentures, dividend paid, share buybacks.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "OIL" + "FISH":**
> **O**perating → core **O**perations
> **I**nvesting → long-term **I**tems/Assets
> **F**inancing → outside **F**unding (capital structure)
> **S**tay **H**ealthy (CFF)!

## 2.2 Methods of Preparation

### Method 1: Direct Method
Lists actual gross cash receipts and gross cash payments (e.g., "Cash received from customers — ₹X"). Simple but **requires extensive data**, rarely used in Indian practice.

### Method 2: Indirect Method (Recommended by KTU/ICAI)
Starts with **Net Profit** and **adjusts** for non-cash items (depreciation), non-operating items (loss on sale of asset), and changes in working capital. Most companies and exam questions prefer this method.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Sign Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net Cash Position | $\text{Opening Cash} + \text{Net Change}$ | Closing > Opening = Inflow |
| 2 | Net Profit → CFO Base | $\text{Net Profit} + \text{Non-Cash Expenses}$ | + (Added Back) |
| 3 | Non-Cash Items | Depreciation, Amortisation, Goodwill written off | + Add |
| 4 | Loss on Sale of Asset | Book Value − Sale Proceeds | + Add to NP, + Full Sale in Investing |
| 5 | Profit on Sale of Asset | Sale Proceeds − Book Value | − Deduct from NP, + Full Sale in Investing |
| 6 | Decrease in Current Assets | (e.g., Debtors, Inventory) | + Add |
| 7 | Increase in Current Assets | (e.g., Debtors, Inventory) | − Deduct |
| 8 | Increase in Current Liabilities | (e.g., Creditors, Bills Payable) | + Add |
| 9 | Decrease in Current Liabilities | (e.g., Creditors) | − Deduct |
| 10 | Proposed Dividend | Treated as **Financing** outflow (in the year of declaration under AS-3) | − Outflow |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

- **Valuation Models:** The **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** model used by analysts to value listed stocks depends entirely on the CFO figures.
- **Credit Rating:** CRISIL, ICRA, and India Ratings use CFS data to assign corporate credit ratings.
- **Project Finance:** Banks sanctioning term loans for engineering projects evaluate **Debt-Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** using CFO.
- **Mergers & Acquisitions:** Acquiring companies (e.g., Tata buying Corus) study target CFS to verify earnings quality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Indirect Method — Derivation Logic

The Indirect Method bridges accrual profit to operating cash by removing **non-cash charges**, removing **non-operating items** (so they can be reported in the correct section), and adjusting for **timing differences** in working capital.

$$\text{CFO} = \text{Net Profit} + \text{Non-Cash} \pm \text{Non-Operating} \pm \text{Working Capital Changes}$$

This can be expanded as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFO} &= \text{Net Profit Before Tax (NPBT)} \\
&\quad + \text{Depreciation \& Amortisation} \\
&\quad + \text{Loss on Sale of Asset} \\
&\quad - \text{Profit on Sale of Asset} \\
&\quad + \text{Goodwill / Preliminary Expenses written off} \\
&\quad - \text{Operating Profit before Working Capital Changes (OPBWC)} \\
&\quad \pm \Delta \text{Working Capital Items} \\
&\quad - \text{Income Tax Paid (net)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (Model Question)

**Question:** From the following particulars of M/s Zenith Engineering Ltd. for the year ended 31-03-2024, prepare the **Cash Flow Statement using the Indirect Method**.

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Net Profit for the year | 5,00,000 |
| Depreciation charged on Machinery | 50,000 |
| Goodwill written off | 20,000 |
| Increase in Trade Debtors | 30,000 |
| Increase in Trade Creditors | 40,000 |
| Profit on sale of Building | 60,000 |
| Sale proceeds of Building (Book Value ₹2,00,000) | 2,60,000 |
| Purchase of Plant & Machinery | 3,00,000 |
| Issue of Equity Shares | 4,00,000 |
| Redemption of 10% Debentures | 2,00,000 |
| Dividend Paid | 1,00,000 |
| Cash and Cash Equivalents (Opening Balance) | 1,50,000 |

### Solution — Step by Step

#### STEP 1: Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Add: Non-Cash Items — Depreciation} &= +50{,}000 \\
\text{Add: Non-Cash Item — Goodwill written off} &= +20{,}000 \\
\text{Less: Non-Operating Item — Profit on sale of Building} &= -60{,}000 \\
\text{Operating Profit before Working Capital Changes (OPBWC)} &= 5{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Working Capital Adjustments:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Add: Increase in Trade Creditors (Current Liability ↑)} &= +40{,}000 \\
\text{Less: Increase in Trade Debtors (Current Asset ↑)} &= -30{,}000 \\
\text{Net Cash Generated from Operating Activities (A)} &= \mathbf{5{,}20{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** When *Creditors increase*, the company has *deferred* cash payment → cash is **retained** inside (Add). When *Debtors increase*, sales have been booked but cash is **not yet collected** (Deduct).

#### STEP 2: Cash Flow from Investing Activities (CFI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sale Proceeds of Building} &= +2{,}60{,}000 \\
\text{Purchase of Plant \& Machinery} &= -3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Cash Used in Investing Activities (B)} &= \mathbf{-40{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Students enter the *Book Value* of the building (₹2,00,000) in investing activities. **Always enter the *actual sale proceeds*** (₹2,60,000). The profit component (₹60,000) was already removed from Net Profit in Step 1 to avoid double-counting.

#### STEP 3: Cash Flow from Financing Activities (CFF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Issue of Equity Shares (capital infusion)} &= +4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Redemption of 10\% Debentures} &= -2{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Dividend Paid} &= -1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Cash Generated from Financing Activities (C)} &= \mathbf{+1{,}00{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### STEP 4: Net Increase / Decrease in Cash & Equivalents

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Change in Cash} &= A + B + C \\
&= 5{,}20{,}000 + (-40{,}000) + 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{6{,}80{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### STEP 5: Closing Cash Balance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing Cash} &= \text{Opening Cash} + \text{Net Change} \\
&= 1{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}80{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{₹\ 8{,}30{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Adjustments Quick Lookup

| Item in P&L | CFO Section | Investing Section | Effect on Cash |
|---|---|---|---|
| Depreciation ₹X | Add ₹X back | No entry | +X to CFO |
| Goodwill written off ₹X | Add ₹X back | No entry | +X to CFO |
| Loss on sale of asset ₹X | Add ₹X back | + Full Sale Proceeds | +X to CFO, +SP to CFI |
| Profit on sale of asset ₹X | Deduct ₹X | + Full Sale Proceeds | -X to CFO, +SP to CFI |
