| Proposed Dividend ₹X | Not in CFO | Not in CFI | -X in CFF (when paid) |
| Tax Paid ₹X | Deduct ₹X | — | -X to CFO |
| Premium on shares issued | — | — | Included in Issue of Shares inflow (CFF) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of the Indirect Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Net Profit as per P and L Account] --> B[Add Non Cash Expenses]
    B --> B1[Depreciation]
    B --> B2[Amortisation]
    B --> B3[Goodwill Written Off]
    B --> C[Add Non Operating Losses]
    C --> C1[Loss on Sale of Asset]
    C --> C2[Loss on Investment]
    B1 --> D[Operating Profit Before Working Capital Changes]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    A --> E[Less Non Operating Gains]
    E --> E1[Profit on Sale of Asset]
    E1 --> D
    D --> F[Adjust for Working Capital Changes]
    F --> F1[Increase in Current Liabilities PLUS]
    F --> F2[Decrease in Current Assets PLUS]
    F --> F3[Decrease in Current Liabilities MINUS]
    F --> F4[Increase in Current Assets MINUS]
    F1 --> G[Less Income Tax Paid]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Net Cash from Operating Activities CFO]
    H --> I[Net Cash from Investing Activities CFI]
    H --> J[Net Cash from Financing Activities CFF]
    I --> K[Net Increase or Decrease in Cash]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Add Opening Cash Balance]
    L --> M[Closing Cash and Cash Equivalents]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Classifying a Cash Item

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Cash Transaction Identified] --> Q1{Is it related to daily revenue generation?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Op[Operating Activities]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Does it involve long term assets or investments?}
    Q2 -- YES --> Inv[Investing Activities]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Does it alter share capital or borrowings?}
    Q3 -- YES --> Fin[Financing Activities]
    Q3 -- NO --> Err[Re examine classification]
    Op --> Out[Record Cash Inflow or Outflow]
    Inv --> Out
    Fin --> Out
    Err --> Out
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Three Pillar Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph OP["OPERATING ACTIVITIES (CFO)"]
        OP1[Cash from Customers]
        OP2[Cash to Suppliers]
        OP3[Cash to Employees]
        OP4[Income Tax Paid]
    end
    subgraph IN["INVESTING ACTIVITIES (CFI)"]
        IN1[Purchase of Fixed Assets]
        IN2[Sale of Fixed Assets]
        IN3[Purchase of Investments]
        IN4[Sale of Investments]
    end
    subgraph FN["FINANCING ACTIVITIES (CFF)"]
        FN1[Issue of Shares]
        FN2[Borrowing Loans]
        FN3[Repayment of Loans]
        FN4[Dividend Payment]
    end
    OP --> NET[Net Change in Cash Position]
    IN --> NET
    FN --> NET
    NET --> CB[Closing Cash Balance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Cash Flow Statement. Mention the three activities into which it classifies cash flows.**

**Model Answer:**
A Cash Flow Statement is a financial statement showing the movement of cash and cash equivalents (inflows and outflows) during an accounting period, as required by **AS-3**. It classifies cash flows into:
1. **Operating Activities** — core revenue-producing transactions.
2. **Investing Activities** — acquisition/disposal of long-term assets and investments.
3. **Financing Activities** — changes in capital structure (equity and borrowings).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for naming the three activities with brief description.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the Direct Method and the Indirect Method of preparing a Cash Flow Statement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Point | Cash receipts and payments | Net Profit |
| Adjustment | None directly | Non-cash + Working capital items adjusted |
| Information | Requires detailed cash records | Easier — uses P&L + Balance Sheet |
| AS-3 Preference | Permitted but not recommended | **Recommended by ICAI/AS-3** |
| Use in India | Rare | Common |

*(3 marks — 1.5 for each method.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the importance of Cash Flow Statement to investors and management of a company. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the following information, prepare a Cash Flow Statement using the **Indirect Method** for M/s Kovalam Steels Ltd. for the year ended 31-03-2024. **(7 marks)**

| Particulars | ₹ |
|---|---|
| Net Profit | 6,00,000 |
| Depreciation | 1,00,000 |
| Provision for Doubtful Debts | 20,000 |
| Profit on Sale of Investments | 30,000 |
| Decrease in Inventory | 50,000 |
| Increase in Trade Receivables | 70,000 |
| Increase in Trade Payables | 60,000 |
| Sale of Investments (Book Value ₹1,50,000) | 1,80,000 |
| Purchase of Equipment | 4,00,000 |
| Issue of 12% Debentures | 3,00,000 |
| Repayment of Bank Loan | 1,50,000 |
| Dividend Paid | 80,000 |
| Opening Cash Balance | 2,00,000 |

#### **Model Solution for Q. A (a): Importance of CFS (7 marks)**

1. **Liquidity Assessment:** Investors use CFS to determine whether the company has **sufficient cash** to meet short-term obligations (wages, suppliers, EMIs). *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Earnings Quality Check:** Reconciles paper profit (Net Profit) with **actual cash** generated — exposing companies that show profit but no cash. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Solvency & Debt Servicing:** Lenders examine **CFO** to verify the company's ability to service interest and principal on debt. *(1 mark)*
4. **Future Planning:** Management uses CFS to plan capital expenditure, dividend policy, and borrowing strategy. *(1 mark)*
5. **Comparison Across Companies:** Since cash is not affected by accounting policies, CFS enables **apples-to-apples comparison** between firms. *(1 mark)*
6. **Statutory Compliance:** Mandatory under **Companies Act, 2013 (Section 129)** and **AS-3** for listed and public companies. *(1 mark)*

#### **Model Solution for Q. A (b): Numerical (7 marks)**

**A. Cash Flow from Operating Activities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= 6{,}00{,}000 \\
+ \text{Depreciation} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
+ \text{Provision for Doubtful Debts} &= 20{,}000 \\
- \text{Profit on Sale of Investments} &= -30{,}000 \\
\hline
\text{Operating Profit before WC Changes} &= 6{,}90{,}000 \\
+ \text{Decrease in Inventory} &= 50{,}000 \\
+ \text{Increase in Trade Payables} &= 60{,}000 \\
- \text{Increase in Trade Receivables} &= -70{,}000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Net Cash from Operating (A)} &= \mathbf{7{,}30{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**B. Cash Flow from Investing Activities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
+ \text{Sale of Investments} &= 1{,}80{,}000 \\
- \text{Purchase of Equipment} &= -4{,}00{,}000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Net Cash used in Investing (B)} &= \mathbf{-2{,}20{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**C. Cash Flow from Financing Activities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
+ \text{Issue of 12\% Debentures} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \\
- \text{Repayment of Bank Loan} &= -1{,}50{,}000 \\
- \text{Dividend Paid} &= -80{,}000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Net Cash from Financing (C)} &= \mathbf{70{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net Change in Cash and Closing Balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Increase} &= 7{,}30{,}000 - 2{,}20{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 5{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{Closing Cash} &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}80{,}000 = \mathbf{₹\ 7{,}80{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is meant by "Cash and Cash Equivalents" as per AS-3? List any four items that qualify and three items that do not qualify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Cash Flow Statement** and **Fund Flow Statement**. Why is the Cash Flow Statement considered more useful in modern financial analysis? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q. B (a): Cash & Cash Equivalents (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** As per AS-3, *Cash* comprises cash on hand and demand deposits with banks. *Cash Equivalents* are short-term, highly liquid investments that are:
- Readily convertible into a known amount of cash, and
- Subject to an **insignificant risk of change in value**, with original maturity of **3 months or less** from the date of acquisition.

**Items that Qualify (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
1. Cash on hand
2. Balance with bank in current account
3. Bank deposits with maturity ≤ 3 months
4. Treasury bills (with maturity ≤ 3 months)
5. Money market funds / commercial paper (very short maturity)

**Items that do NOT Qualify (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**
1. Bank deposits with maturity **exceeding 3 months**.
2. Equity preference shares (not readily convertible into fixed cash).
3. Long-term government bonds.
4. Bills of exchange with maturity > 3 months.
5. Restricted cash (e.g., margin money against guarantees).

#### **Model Solution for Q. B (b): CFS vs. FFS (7 marks)**

| Basis | Cash Flow Statement | Fund Flow Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Concept | Movement of **Cash & Equivalents** | Movement of **Working Capital / Funds** |
| Source | Mandatory under **AS-3** | Not statutorily mandatory |
| Frequency | Prepared for **actual cash** items | Adjusts both cash and non-cash transactions |
| Format | Standardised (3 sections) | No prescribed format |
| Modern Usage | **Widely used** by analysts, SEBI, banks | Largely **outdated**, rarely prepared |
| Working Capital | Focuses on **cash**, not on working capital directly | Calculates change in working capital as a summary |

**Why CFS is More Useful (3 marks):**
1. **Real & Verifiable:** Cash cannot be manipulated; accruals can. Hence CFS shows **true liquidity**.
2. **Standardised:** Globally comparable (AS-3 / IFRS equivalent).
3. **Decision-Relevant:** Directly feeds **DCF valuation models** and **DSCR** for lenders.
4. **Statutory Weight:** Mandated under the **Companies Act, 2013**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong Section Placement (–2 marks):** Students often place dividend paid in *Operating* activities. It belongs in **Financing**.
> 2. **Sale of Asset — Book Value Confusion (–2 marks):** Always enter the *actual sale proceeds* in Investing, not book value. The profit/loss on sale is shown separately because it was reversed in CFO.
> 3. **Forgetting to Adjust Non-Cash Items (–2 marks):** Depreciation, Goodwill written off, and Provision for Doubtful Debts must be **added back** to Net Profit.
> 4. **Working Capital Sign Error (–1 mark):** Increase in current asset = **Deduct**; Increase in current liability = **Add**.
> 5. **Missing Closing Cash Reconciliation (–1 mark):** Always show Opening Cash + Net Change = Closing Cash as the final line.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CFS = Statement of Cash Movement** for the period, classifying flows into **Operating, Investing, and Financing** activities.
- ✅ **AS-3** governs the preparation; **Indirect Method** is the most commonly tested format in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Starting Point (Indirect):** Net Profit → add back non-cash items → reverse non-operating items → adjust working capital.
- ✅ **Direct Method:** Begins with gross cash receipts and gross cash payments (rarely tested numerically).
- ✅ **Investing Activity** = Long-term assets and investments (purchase/sale of fixed assets, investments, loans given).
- ✅ **Financing Activity** = Capital structure changes (issue/redemption of shares, debentures, loans, dividend).
- ✅ **Cash Equivalents:** Short-term, highly liquid, original maturity **≤ 3 months**, insignificant risk.
- ✅ **Two Sign Rules to Memorize:** ↓ Current Asset / ↑ Current Liability = **Cash Inflow Indicator**; vice-versa = Outflow Indicator.
- ✅ **Final Reconciliation:** Opening Cash + Net Change = Closing Cash — always show this line.
- ✅ **Key Use Case:** **DCF valuation**, **DSCR analysis**, **credit rating**, and **SEBI-mandated financial reporting** under the **Companies Act, 2013**.
- ✅ **Common MCQ Traps:** "Dividend paid" is financing (not operating); "Interest paid on loan" is operating (in India per AS-3); "Purchase of patent" is investing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Ratios - Leverage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Financial Ratios - Leverage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leverage Ratios** are a class of **solvency and capital structure metrics** that quantify the extent to which a firm finances its assets and operations through **debt (fixed-cost financing) versus equity (variable-cost financing)**. They measure the degree of financial risk arising from the use of borrowed funds and the firm's ability to meet its long-term fixed financial obligations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEIET745 – Introduction to Stock Markets and Trading**, leverage ratios fall under the broader umbrella of **Solvency Ratios** within the financial statement analysis toolkit. They are central to **equity research, credit analysis, and investment decision-making** because they expose the underlying risk profile of a company that may not be visible from profitability ratios alone.

### 1.2 The Two Broad Categories of Leverage

| Category | What It Measures | Core Question Answered |
|:---------|:-----------------|:------------------------|
| **Capital Structure Leverage (Balance Sheet)** | Mix of debt vs. equity in the firm's capital | How much of the firm is financed by debt? |
| **Operating & Combined Leverage (Income Statement)** | Sensitivity of earnings to changes in sales | How do profits fluctuate with revenue changes? |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Both categories are tested in the university examination, often through numerical problems involving **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)**, **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)**, and **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Crowbar Principle

Imagine you are trying to lift a heavy rock. You place a crowbar (the **lever**) under it and push down on the long end. A small push produces a large upward force. However, if the rock is too heavy or the fulcrum slips, the crowbar can snap back and hurt you.

**The Crowbar = Debt Capital**
**Your Hands = Equity Capital**
**The Rock = Total Assets / Business Operations**

When a firm uses **debt** as a financial crowbar:
- **On the upside:** A small increase in Return on Assets (ROA) above the cost of debt produces a magnified gain for equity holders (positive leverage / financial gearing).
- **On the downside:** The same small decrease in ROA below the cost of debt produces a magnified loss (negative leverage).

This is the **double-edged sword of leverage** — and the reason leverage ratios are the most closely watched indicators by bankers, rating agencies, and stock market investors.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Declared

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard leverage metrics** in capital market analysis:
> - **Debt-to-Equity Ratio (D/E)** — expressed as a ratio (e.g., 1.5 : 1)
> - **Debt-to-Assets Ratio (D/A)** — expressed as a decimal or percentage
> - **Equity Multiplier (EM)** — expressed as a ratio
> - **Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR)** — expressed as a multiple (times)
> - **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** — expressed as a multiple (times)
> - **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)** — dimensionless coefficient
> - **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** — dimensionless coefficient
> - **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)** — dimensionless coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Capital Structure (Balance Sheet) Leverage Ratios

#### 2.1.1 Debt-to-Equity Ratio (D/E)

The **most widely used leverage indicator** in fundamental analysis. It compares the **total liabilities of the firm** to the **shareholders' equity**.

**Logic Steps:**

1. Identify the total interest-bearing debt of the firm (long-term borrowings + short-term borrowings + bank loans + bonds).
2. Identify the book value of shareholders' equity (share capital + reserves & surplus – accumulated losses).
3. Form the ratio to express how many rupees of debt are used for every ₹1 of equity.
4. Interpret: A higher ratio signals **greater financial risk**; a lower ratio signals a **more conservative capital structure**.

#### 2.1.2 Debt-to-Assets Ratio (D/A)

This expresses what **proportion of total assets** has been financed by debt.

**Logic Steps:**

1. Take total debt (numerator).
2. Take total assets (denominator) — both fixed and current.
3. The ratio is bounded between **0 and 1** (or 0% to 100%).
4. A higher ratio indicates greater solvency risk and lower cushion of unencumbered assets.

#### 2.1.3 Equity Multiplier (EM)

This is the **DuPont Identity** building block that links the **balance sheet** to the **Return on Equity (ROE)**.

**Logic Steps:**

1. Take total assets (numerator).
2. Take total equity (denominator).
3. EM > 1 always (because assets = debt + equity).
4. It is the **inverse** of the equity-to-assets ratio.

#### 2.1.4 Debt-to-Capitalization (Debt-to-Capital) Ratio

Often used by **rating agencies** (CRISIL, ICRA, Moody's, S&P). Normalizes the debt against the **total capitalization** (debt + equity) rather than against equity alone.

#### 2.1.5 Fixed Assets to Long-Term Funds Ratio

Evaluates whether the firm has **adequate long-term funds** to cover its **fixed (non-current) assets**.

### 2.2 Coverage (Income Statement) Leverage Ratios

#### 2.2.1 Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR) / Times Interest Earned (TIE)

Measures how many times the firm's **operating profit (EBIT)** can cover its **interest expense**.

**Logic Steps:**

1. Take EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes) as the numerator.
2. Take total interest expense for the period as the denominator.
3. A higher ICR means the firm has a **larger cushion** to service interest obligations.
4. A bank/lender typically expects ICR ≥ 2 or 3 before approving additional debt.

#### 2.2.2 Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)

A more comprehensive coverage measure that includes the **principal repayment obligation** in addition to interest.

#### 2.2.3 Cash Flow to Debt Ratio

Compares **operating cash flow** to total debt — useful because accrual profits can overstate debt-servicing capacity.

### 2.3 Operating, Financial, and Combined Leverage

This is the **KTU-favoured** advanced segment and frequently appears in 14-mark numerical problems.

#### 2.3.1 Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)

Measures the **percentage change in EBIT** for a given **percentage change in Sales**. It captures the effect of **fixed operating costs** (depreciation, rent, salaries) on profit volatility.

#### 2.3.2 Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)

Measures the **percentage change in EPS (Earnings Per Share)** for a given **percentage change in EBIT**. It captures the effect of **fixed financing costs** (interest) on shareholder earnings.

#### 2.3.3 Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)

The product of DOL and DFL, expressing the **total risk exposure** of the firm — both operating and financial — in a single coefficient.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master formula reference** for the Leverage Ratios topic. Memorize each row along with the units/range.

| # | Ratio Name | Formula | Standard Interpretation |
|:-:|:-----------|:--------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Debt-to-Equity Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Equity}}$ | $\lt 1$ = conservative; $\gt 2$ = aggressive |
| 2 | **Debt-to-Assets Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | $0 \le \text{ratio} \le 1$ |
| 3 | **Equity Multiplier** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Total Equity}}$ | Always $\ge 1$ |
| 4 | **Debt-to-Capital Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Debt} + \text{Total Equity}}$ | $0 \le \text{ratio} \le 1$ |
| 5 | **Long-Term Debt-to-Equity** | $\dfrac{\text{Long-Term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$ | Lower is safer |
| 6 | **Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR)** | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Expense}}$ | $\ge 3$ is healthy |
| 7 | **Times Interest Earned (TIE)** | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Expense}}$ | Same as ICR |
| 8 | **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Income} + \text{Depreciation} + \text{Interest}}{\text{Principal} + \text{Interest}}$ | $\ge 1.5$ preferred |
| 9 | **Cash Flow to Debt** | $\dfrac{\text{Operating Cash Flow}}{\text{Total Debt}}$ | Higher is better |
| 10 | **Fixed Assets to Long-Term Funds** | $\dfrac{\text{Fixed Assets}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity} + \text{Long-Term Debt}}$ | Should be $\le 1$ |
| 11 | **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)** | $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}}{\text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} - \text{Fixed Cost}}$ | $\gt 1$ implies operating risk |
| 12 | **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBIT} - \text{Interest}}$ | $\gt 1$ implies financial risk |
| 13 | **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)** | $\text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}}$ | Captures total business risk |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Stock Markets

Leverage ratios are deployed in the following market functions:

1. **Equity Research / Fundamental Analysis** — Analysts compare a company's D/E with industry peers to judge whether the stock is **over-leveraged or under-leveraged**.
2. **Credit Rating** — CRISIL, ICRA, CARE, and global agencies (Moody's, S&P, Fitch) use **DSCR, ICR, and Debt-to-Capital** as primary inputs.
3. **Bank Loan Sanction** — Banks insist on minimum ICR and D/E thresholds before approving term loans and working capital limits.
4. **Portfolio Construction** — Aggressive investors may favour high-D/E firms for amplified returns; conservative investors prefer low-D/E defensive stocks.
5. **Trading Signals (Combining with Technicals)** — Sudden spikes in leverage may coincide with increased **stock price volatility (beta)** and are flagged in event-driven trading strategies.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)

The **Degree of Operating Leverage** is derived from the sensitivity of EBIT to changes in Sales.

**Step 1:** Define the **Contribution Margin (CM)**.

$$
CM \;=\; \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Costs}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Contribution is the revenue left over after covering only those costs that vary with production volume.

**Step 2:** Define **Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT)**.

$$
EBIT \;=\; \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Costs} - \text{Fixed Costs}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* EBIT subtracts the fixed operating expenses (depreciation, rent, permanent salaries) from Contribution.

**Step 3:** Take the **% change in EBIT** for a given **% change in Sales**.

$$
\% \Delta EBIT \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta EBIT}{EBIT}
$$

$$
\% \Delta \text{Sales} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta \text{Sales}}{\text{Sales}}
$$

**Step 4:** Express DOL as the ratio of these two percentages.

$$
DOL \;=\; \dfrac{\% \Delta EBIT}{\% \Delta \text{Sales}} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta EBIT / EBIT}{\Delta \text{Sales} / \text{Sales}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the algebraic expressions and simplify. Because Variable Cost and Fixed Cost are independent of Sales in the short run, the ratio collapses to:

$$
DOL \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}}{\text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} - \text{Fixed Cost}}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* A 1% change in Sales produces a `DOL × 1%` change in EBIT. The higher the fixed cost base, the larger the multiplier effect.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)

**Step 1:** Define **Earnings Before Tax (EBT)**.

$$
EBT \;=\; EBIT - \text{Interest Expense}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* After deducting interest on debt, the remaining earnings constitute the taxable profit.

**Step 2:** Express DFL as the sensitivity of **EPS** (or EBT) to a change in **EBIT**.

$$
DFL \;=\; \dfrac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta EBIT} \;=\; \dfrac{\% \Delta EBT}{\% \Delta EBIT}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and simplify:

$$
DFL \;=\; \dfrac{EBIT}{EBT} \;=\; \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}
$$

where $I$ = Interest Expense.

*Conversion Logic:* The presence of fixed interest in the denominator magnifies the effect of any change in EBIT on the residual profit available to shareholders.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)

**Step 1:** Recognize that the chain of sensitivities is multiplicative.

$$
DCL \;=\; \dfrac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta \text{Sales}}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule:

$$
DCL \;=\; \dfrac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta \text{Sales}} \;=\; \dfrac{\% \Delta EPS}{\% \Delta EBIT} \times \dfrac{\% \Delta EBIT}{\% \Delta \text{Sales}}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the two factors:

$$
DCL \;=\; DFL \times DOL
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the closed-form expressions:

$$
DCL \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{EBT} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}}{EBIT - \text{Interest}}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* DCL is the **total magnification coefficient** that translates a 1% change in Sales into the corresponding percentage change in EPS.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
A firm reports the following data for the year ending 31 March 2024:

- Sales = ₹10,00,000
- Variable Costs = ₹6,00,000
- Fixed Operating Costs = ₹2,00,000
- Interest Expense = ₹1,00,000

**Required:** Compute the **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)**, **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)**, and **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)**. Also, calculate the new EPS if Sales rise by 10% (assume 1,00,000 shares outstanding and tax rate of 30%).

**Step 1 — Compute Contribution:**

$$
CM \;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Sales minus variable cost gives the contribution margin.

**Step 2 — Compute EBIT:**

$$
EBIT \;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Subtract fixed operating costs from contribution.

**Step 3 — Compute EBT:**

$$
EBT \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 1{,}00{,}000
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Subtract interest from EBIT.

**Step 4 — Compute DOL:**

$$
DOL \;=\; \dfrac{CM}{EBIT} \;=\; \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \;=\; 2.0
$$

*Conversion Logic:* A 1% rise in sales will raise EBIT by 2%.

**Step 5 — Compute DFL:**

$$
DFL \;=\; \dfrac{EBIT}{EBT} \;=\; \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \;=\; 2.0
$$

*Conversion Logic:* A 1% rise in EBIT will raise EPS by 2%.

**Step 6 — Compute DCL:**

$$
DCL \;=\; DOL \times DFL \;=\; 2.0 \times 2.0 \;=\; 4.0
$$

*Conversion Logic:* A 1% rise in sales will raise EPS by 4%.

**Step 7 — Compute New EPS after 10% Sales Rise:**

New Sales = ₹11,00,000.
New CM = $11{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}60{,}000 = 4{,}40{,}000$ (assuming variable cost ratio stays at 60%).
New EBIT = $4{,}40{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000$.
New EBT = $2{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}40{,}000$.
New PAT = $1{,}40{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 98{,}000$.
New EPS = $\dfrac{98{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 0.98$.

**Verification using DCL shortcut:**

Old PAT = $1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 = 70{,}000$.
Old EPS = $\dfrac{70{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 0.70$.

$$
\% \Delta EPS \;=\; DCL \times \% \Delta \text{Sales} \;=\; 4.0 \times 10\% \;=\; 40\%
$$

$$
\text{New EPS} \;=\; 0.70 \times (1 + 0.40) \;=\; 0.98 \quad \checkmark
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The two methods match — this confirms the leverage formulas are correctly applied.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation Thresholds

| Ratio | Low Risk (Conservative) | Moderate Risk | High Risk (Aggressive) |
|:------|:-------------------------|:--------------|:------------------------|
| **D/E** | $\lt 0.5$ | $0.5 - 1.5$ | $\gt 1.5$ |
| **D/A** | $\lt 0.3$ | $0.3 - 0.6$ | $\gt 0.6$ |
| **ICR** | $\ge 5$ | $2 - 5$ | $\lt 2$ |
| **DSCR** | $\ge 2$ | $1.25 - 2$ | $\lt 1.25$ |
| **DOL** | $\lt 1.5$ | $1.5 - 3$ | $\gt 3$ |
| **DFL** | $\lt 1.5$ | $1.5 - 2.5$ | $\gt 2.5$ |

```mermaid
    nodeC --> nodeC3
    nodeD --> nodeD1
    nodeD --> nodeD2
    nodeD --> nodeD3
```

### 4.2 Flow of Risk Magnification (DCL Chain)

The following diagram illustrates the **risk transmission chain** from Sales to EPS via the leverage coefficients.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["SALES VOLUME"]
    node2["CHANGE IN SALES DELTA S"]
    node3["CONTRIBUTION MARGIN CM"]
    node4["EBIT"]
    node5["EBT"]
    node6["PAT AND EPS"]
    node7["DOL OPERATING MAGNIFIER"]
    node8["DFL FINANCING MAGNIFIER"]
    node9["DCL TOTAL MAGNIFIER"]
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node2 -. "DOL" .-> node4
    node4 -. "DFL" .-> node5
    node2 -. "DCL" .-> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node9
```

### 4.3 DuPont Identity Integration (Linking Leverage to ROE)

The following block diagram maps how **leverage ratios** integrate into the **DuPont ROE decomposition** — a key KTU high-yield topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["RETURN ON EQUITY ROE"]
    nodeB["NET PROFIT MARGIN NPM"]
    nodeC["ASSET TURNOVER AT"]
    nodeD["EQUITY MULTIPLIER EM LEVERAGE COMPONENT"]
    nodeE["ROA RETURN ON ASSETS"]
    nodeF["TOTAL ASSETS"]
    nodeG["SHAREHOLDERS EQUITY"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
```

*Conversion Logic:* The **Equity Multiplier (EM)** is the leverage component in the DuPont formula:

$$
ROE \;=\; NPM \times AT \times EM
$$

A higher EM (i.e., more debt) mechanically boosts ROE — but only if the firm earns a return on assets **above** the cost of debt.
