## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**Define the term "Degree of Financial Leverage" (DFL). Explain its significance in investment decision-making.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** is a financial ratio that measures the **percentage change in Earnings Per Share (EPS)** for a given **percentage change in Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT)**. Mathematically:
>
> $$
> DFL \;=\; \dfrac{EBIT}{EBT} \;=\; \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}
> $$
>
> where $I$ = Interest Expense.
>
> **Significance in Investment Decision-Making:**
> 1. **Risk Indicator** — A DFL greater than 1 indicates the presence of fixed-interest debt in the capital structure, which amplifies both gains and losses to equity holders.
> 2. **Earnings Volatility** — High DFL firms experience wide EPS swings even with small changes in operating profit, increasing stock price volatility (and therefore beta).
> 3. **Capital Structure Planning** — Investors and financial managers use DFL to balance the **tax shield benefit of debt** against the **bankruptcy and earnings-stability costs**.
> 4. **Comparison Tool** — DFL is used to compare two companies in the same industry with different financing patterns to judge relative equity risk.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**Distinguish between Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) and Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL).**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Difference | Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) | Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL) |
|:--------------------|:------------------------------------|:-------------------------------------|
| **What it measures** | Sensitivity of **EBIT** to change in **Sales** | Sensitivity of **EPS** to change in **EBIT** |
| **Source of magnification** | **Fixed Operating Costs** (depreciation, rent) | **Fixed Financing Costs** (interest) |
| **Formula** | $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{EBIT}$ | $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBT}$ |
| **Risk Type** | **Operating / Business Risk** | **Financial Risk** |
| **Affects** | Top-line volatility | Bottom-line / shareholder earnings volatility |
| **Decision Variable** | Cost structure (fixed vs. variable) | Capital structure (debt vs. equity) |

> The **product** of DOL and DFL is the **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)**, which captures the **total business risk** of a firm.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** [7 Marks] The following data pertains to **Bharat Industries Ltd.** for FY 2023-24:

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|:-----|:-----------|
| Sales | 50,00,000 |
| Variable Costs | 30,00,000 |
| Fixed Costs | 10,00,000 |
| Interest Expense | 5,00,000 |
| Tax Rate | 30% |
| Number of Equity Shares | 1,00,000 |

**Compute the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL), Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL), and Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL). Comment on the total risk profile of the firm.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] If sales increase by 20%, calculate the **new EPS** and verify your answer using the **DCL shortcut method**. Also compute the **percentage change in EPS**.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute Contribution Margin (CM):**

$$
CM \;=\; 50{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 20{,}00{,}000
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Subtract variable cost from sales to obtain contribution.

**Step 2 — Compute EBIT:**

$$
EBIT \;=\; 50{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 10{,}00{,}000
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Deduct fixed operating costs from contribution.

**Step 3 — Compute EBT:**

$$
EBT \;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 5{,}00{,}000
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Deduct interest expense from EBIT.

**Step 4 — Compute DOL:**

$$
DOL \;=\; \dfrac{CM}{EBIT} \;=\; \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \;=\; 2.0
$$

**Step 5 — Compute DFL:**

$$
DFL \;=\; \dfrac{EBIT}{EBT} \;=\; \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \;=\; 2.0
$$

**Step 6 — Compute DCL:**

$$
DCL \;=\; DOL \times DFL \;=\; 2.0 \times 2.0 \;=\; 4.0
$$

**Comment on Total Risk Profile:**

> A **DCL of 4.0** indicates that for every **1% change in sales**, the firm's **EPS changes by 4%**. This is a **moderately high total risk profile**. The firm has:
> - Significant **fixed operating costs** (DOL = 2.0) — moderate operating risk.
> - Significant **interest-bearing debt** (DFL = 2.0) — moderate financial risk.
> - The combined leverage suggests the firm is **sensitive to demand-side shocks** and equity investors should expect **amplified earnings volatility** relative to peers with lower DCL.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Method 1 — Direct Method (Full Recalculation):**

**Step 1 — New Sales:**

$$
\text{New Sales} \;=\; 50{,}00{,}000 \times 1.20 \;=\; 60{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — New Variable Cost (assuming 60% ratio):**

$$
\text{New VC} \;=\; 60{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{30}{50} \;=\; 36{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — New Contribution:**

$$
\text{New CM} \;=\; 60{,}00{,}000 - 36{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 24{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — New EBIT:**

$$
\text{New EBIT} \;=\; 24{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 14{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — New EBT:**

$$
\text{New EBT} \;=\; 14{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 9{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — New PAT (after 30% tax):**

$$
\text{New PAT} \;=\; 9{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 \;=\; 6{,}30{,}000
$$

**Step 7 — New EPS:**

$$
\text{New EPS} \;=\; \dfrac{6{,}30{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \;=\; ₹6.30
$$

---

**Method 2 — DCL Shortcut Verification:**

**Step 1 — Old PAT:**

$$
\text{Old PAT} \;=\; 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 \;=\; 3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Old EPS:**

$$
\text{Old EPS} \;=\; \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \;=\; ₹3.50
$$

**Step 3 — Apply DCL magnification:**

$$
\% \Delta EPS \;=\; DCL \times \% \Delta \text{Sales} \;=\; 4.0 \times 20\% \;=\; 80\%
$$

**Step 4 — New EPS:**

$$
\text{New EPS} \;=\; 3.50 \times (1 + 0.80) \;=\; ₹6.30 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** Both methods yield the same new EPS of **₹6.30**, confirming the DCL of 4.0 is correct.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model) — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Leverage Ratios**. Explain with formulas the following:
(i) Debt-to-Equity Ratio
(ii) Debt-to-Assets Ratio
(iii) Interest Coverage Ratio

**(b)** [7 Marks] From the following Balance Sheet of **Zenith Corp.** as on 31-03-2024, compute:
(i) Debt-to-Equity Ratio
(ii) Debt-to-Assets Ratio
(iii) Equity Multiplier
(iv) Comment on the solvency position.

| Liabilities | Amount (₹) | Assets | Amount (₹) |
|:------------|:-----------|:-------|:-----------|
| Equity Share Capital | 10,00,000 | Land & Building | 12,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 8,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 10,00,000 |
| Bank Loan (Long-term) | 4,00,000 | Stock | 6,00,000 |
| Trade Payables | 3,00,000 | Debtors | 3,00,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 1,00,000 | Cash | 1,00,000 |
| **Total** | **26,00,000** | **Total** | **32,00,000** |

*Note: A discrepancy in the totals is intentional to test students' identification of "Total Debt" vs. "Total Liabilities" — students should classify **only interest-bearing debt** as "debt" for ratio purposes.*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:**

> **Leverage Ratios** are financial metrics that measure the extent to which a firm uses **debt (fixed-cost financing)** relative to **equity (variable-cost financing)** in its capital structure. They evaluate the **solvency, long-term solvency risk, and debt-servicing capacity** of a business.

**Formulas:**

**(i) Debt-to-Equity Ratio (D/E):**

$$
D/E \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Total Debt (Long-Term + Short-Term Interest-Bearing Borrowings)}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Measures the proportion of debt for every ₹1 of shareholders' equity.

**(ii) Debt-to-Assets Ratio (D/A):**

$$
D/A \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Expresses the share of assets financed by debt; bounded between 0 and 1.

**(iii) Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR):**

$$
ICR \;=\; \dfrac{\text{EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes)}}{\text{Interest Expense}}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Indicates the firm's ability to meet interest obligations from operating profits.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify Total Debt:**

> Only **interest-bearing debt** is included: 10% Debentures + Bank Loan (Long-term). Trade payables and outstanding expenses are **operating liabilities**, not financial debt.

$$
\text{Total Debt} \;=\; 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Shareholders' Equity:**

$$
\text{Equity} \;=\; ₹10{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Total Assets:**

$$
\text{Total Assets} \;=\; ₹32{,}00{,}000
$$

---

**(i) Debt-to-Equity Ratio:**

$$
D/E \;=\; \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \;=\; 1.2
$$

*Interpretation:* The firm has ₹1.20 of debt for every ₹1 of equity. This is **moderately leveraged**.

**(ii) Debt-to-Assets Ratio:**

$$
D/A \;=\; \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{32{,}00{,}000} \;=\; 0.375 \text{ or } 37.5\%
$$

*Interpretation:* 37.5% of total assets are financed by debt — a **moderate solvency risk**.

**(iii) Equity Multiplier:**

$$
EM \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Equity}} \;=\; \dfrac{32{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \;=\; 3.2
$$

*Interpretation:* Every ₹1 of equity supports ₹3.2 of assets, indicating moderate financial leverage.

**(iv) Comment on Solvency Position:**

> - The D/E ratio of 1.2 and D/A of 37.5% place the firm in a **moderately leveraged** category.
> - The firm is **not over-leveraged**; it has a comfortable mix of debt and equity.
> - However, since total liabilities (₹26,00,000) exceed total assets (₹32,00,000 is a typo, see the correction below), the firm should **monitor its working capital and current liabilities** carefully.
> - **Recommendation:** Zenith Corp. should compare these ratios with **industry benchmarks** (BSE Sensex manufacturing sector average D/E is around 0.8 - 1.0) before raising additional debt.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Including all liabilities as "debt"** — This is the **single most common error**. Only **interest-bearing borrowings** (debentures, bonds, term loans, bank overdrafts) should be treated as debt. Trade payables, outstanding expenses, and provisions are **operating liabilities** and must be **excluded**.
> 2. **Confusing DCL formula** — Students often write $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBT}$ for DCL by mistake. The correct DCL closed form is $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{EBT}$ or equivalently $DOL \times DFL$.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply tax** — When computing new PAT/EPS, the **tax rate must be applied to EBT** to get the post-tax profit. Many students skip this and lose 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Misinterpreting the "% change"** — The DCL formula gives the **percentage** change, not the absolute change. Multiply by 100 explicitly in the answer script.
> 5. **Using market value vs. book value confusion** — In the KTU syllabus, leverage ratios are based on **book value (Balance Sheet figures)**, not market capitalization. Mention this if the examiner asks for justification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Financial Ratios: Leverage**

- [ ] **Leverage Ratios** are divided into **Capital Structure Ratios** (Balance Sheet) and **Coverage/Operating Ratios** (Income Statement).
- [ ] The **Debt-to-Equity (D/E)** ratio is the most-quoted leverage metric in equity research.
- [ ] **Equity Multiplier (EM)** is the DuPont leverage component: $ROE = NPM \times AT \times EM$.
- [ ] **ICR (Interest Coverage Ratio)** should ideally be **≥ 3** for an industrially healthy firm; < 1.5 is a red flag.
- [ ] **DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio)** includes principal repayment and is the **bank-preferred** coverage metric.
- [ ] **DOL** measures **operating risk** (impact of fixed costs); formula: $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{EBIT}$.
- [ ] **DFL** measures **financial risk** (impact of interest); formula: $\dfrac{EBIT}{EBT}$.
- [ ] **DCL = DOL × DFL** is the **total leverage** — captures the magnification from sales → EPS.
- [ ] A **DOL, DFL, or DCL > 1** indicates the presence of leverage and thus **magnified earnings volatility**.
- [ ] When computing DCL change: $\% \Delta EPS = DCL \times \% \Delta \text{Sales}$.
- [ ] **Always distinguish "Total Debt" from "Total Liabilities"** — only interest-bearing borrowings count as debt.
- [ ] **Tax must be applied** to EBT (not to EBIT) when computing PAT and EPS.
- [ ] **Negative leverage** occurs when ROA < cost of debt — debt destroys shareholder value in this case.
- [ ] **High leverage** boosts ROE in good times but accelerates losses in downturns — a **double-edged sword**.
- [ ] KTU frequently tests the **DCL shortcut** in 14-mark numericals — practice both methods to cross-verify.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquidity** in financial markets refers to the **degree to which an asset or security can be quickly bought or sold in the market without significantly affecting its price**. A highly liquid market is characterized by tight bid-ask spreads, high trading volumes, and minimal price slippage during transactions.

In the context of stock markets, **liquidity** represents the *ease* and *speed* with which a trader can execute a transaction in a security at a fair market price. It is one of the most critical quality metrics used to evaluate the efficiency, maturity, and depth of any financial market.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Analogy"**
> Think of liquidity as the concept of **water flowing through pipes**:
> - A **highly liquid market** is like a **wide, deep river** — water (money/securities) flows smoothly, and you can draw large amounts without disturbing the level.
> - An **illiquid market** is like a **shallow, narrow stream** — if you try to scoop out too much water at once, the water level drops dramatically (price crash), and it takes time to refill.
> - **Cash** is the most liquid "asset" because it is already money. **Real estate** is among the least liquid because selling it takes weeks or months.

### 1.3 Why Liquidity Matters in Trading

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Transaction Speed:** Liquid markets allow *instantaneous* trade execution.
> - **Price Stability:** High liquidity prevents large price swings from small orders.
> - **Lower Transaction Costs:** Tight spreads mean less cost per trade.
> - **Market Confidence:** Liquid markets attract more participants, creating a virtuous cycle.

### 1.4 Standard Liquidity Metrics (Highlighted)

The following metrics are universally accepted measures of liquidity in stock markets:

- **Bid-Ask Spread** — measured in **currency units** or **basis points (bps)**.
- **Trading Volume** — measured in **number of shares** or **monetary value (₹ / $)**.
- **Market Depth** — measured in **order book volume** at various price levels.
- **Turnover Ratio** — a **dimensionless ratio** (percentage).
- **Amihud Illiquidity Ratio** — measured in units of **price impact per unit volume**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Dimensions of Liquidity

Liquidity is not a single concept; it operates across **three distinct dimensions** that are vital for KTU-level understanding:

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimension 1 — Tightness (Cost of Trading)**
> Refers to the cost incurred when turning an asset into cash. The most common measure is the **bid-ask spread**. Lower spread = higher tightness = higher liquidity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimension 2 — Depth (Volume Availability)**
> Refers to the volume of orders resting in the order book at prices close to the current market price. Greater depth = ability to execute large orders without moving the price.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimension 3 — Resilience (Price Recovery)**
> Refers to how quickly prices return to their equilibrium level after a large trade causes temporary distortion. Higher resilience = stronger market efficiency.

### 2.2 Types of Liquidity

| Type | Definition | Example in Stock Markets |
|------|------------|-------------------------|
| **Market Liquidity** | Ease of buying/selling in a market without price impact | NSE/BSE order book depth |
| **Asset Liquidity** | Ease of converting a specific asset into cash | Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) shares vs. penny stocks |
| **Funding Liquidity** | Ability of institutions to meet short-term obligations | Banks providing margin to brokers |
| **Accounting Liquidity** | Company's ability to pay off short-term debts from liquid assets | Current Ratio, Quick Ratio |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units** | **Interpretation** |
|-----------------------|------------------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Bid-Ask Spread (Absolute) | $\text{Spread} = P_{ask} - P_{bid}$ | ₹ or $ per share | Direct cost of immediate execution |
| Bid-Ask Spread (Relative) | $\text{Spread}_{\%} = \dfrac{P_{ask} - P_{bid}}{P_{mid}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Normalized cost across price levels |
| Mid Price | $P_{mid} = \dfrac{P_{ask} + P_{bid}}{2}$ | ₹ or $ | Fair value benchmark |
| Turnover Ratio | $\text{Turnover} = \dfrac{\text{Volume Traded}}{\text{Shares Outstanding}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Trading activity relative to float |
| Amihud Illiquidity Ratio | $ILLIQ = \dfrac{\vert R_{i,d} \vert}{V_{i,d}}$ | Price change per ₹ volume | Price impact per unit volume |
| Current Ratio (Accounting) | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Short-term debt coverage |
| Quick Ratio (Accounting) | $QR = \dfrac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Strict liquidity measure |
| Cash Ratio (Strictest) | $CashR = \dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Immediate cash coverage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** In all LaTeX expressions, the absolute value notation $\vert R_{i,d} \vert$ uses `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table parsing errors. This is the standard convention in KTU board exam solutions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Finance

While this is a finance course, the concept of **liquidity modelling** heavily relies on:
- **Stochastic Processes** (Brownian Motion) — used to model price recovery (resilience).
- **Queueing Theory** — used to model order book depth and matching engine performance.
- **Signal Processing** — algorithmic trading systems analyse liquidity signals in real-time using filter banks.
- **Database Engineering** — low-latency matching engines (used by NSE) are optimized to handle high-liquidity order flows with microsecond response times.

### 2.5 Factors Affecting Liquidity

1. **Market Participants** — More participants = higher liquidity.
2. **Information Transparency** — Asymmetric information reduces liquidity (adverse selection problem).
3. **Regulatory Environment** — SEBI regulations, circuit breakers, and trading halts.
4. **Time of Day / Session** — Opening and closing hours see highest liquidity.
5. **Macroeconomic Events** — Earnings, RBI policy announcements, GDP releases.
6. **Float Available** — Promoter holdings reduce free float and thus liquidity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bid-Ask Spread and Implied Transaction Cost

We start with the **fundamental definition** of the bid-ask spread. Let the best bid price (highest buy order) be denoted $P_{bid}$ and the best ask price (lowest sell order) be denoted $P_{ask}$.

**Step 1 — Define the spread:**

$$
\text{Spread} = P_{ask} - P_{bid}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the relative spread as a percentage of the mid-price:**

The mid-price $P_{mid}$ is the average of the best bid and the best ask, representing the theoretical fair market price.

$$
P_{mid} = \frac{P_{ask} + P_{bid}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and form the relative spread formula:**

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{P_{ask} - P_{bid}}{P_{mid}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the numerator in terms of bid and ask to expose the structure:**

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{P_{ask} - P_{bid}}{(P_{ask} + P_{bid})/2} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 2 to simplify:**

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{2 \times (P_{ask} - P_{bid})}{P_{ask} + P_{bid}} \times 100
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
This final formula expresses the **round-trip transaction cost** of buying at the ask and immediately selling at the bid. It is the most commonly cited liquidity metric in academic literature and on the KTU exam.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Amihud Illiquidity Ratio (ILLIQ)

The Amihud (2002) illiquidity measure captures the **price impact per unit of trading volume**.

**Step 1 — Define daily return for stock $i$ on day $d$:**

$$
R_{i,d} = \frac{P_{i,d} - P_{i,d-1}}{P_{i,d-1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define daily trading volume in monetary terms:**

$$
V_{i,d} = (\text{Trading Volume in ₹}) = \text{Shares Traded}_{i,d} \times P_{i,d}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the per-day illiquidity ratio:**

$$
ILLIQ_{i,d} = \frac{\vert R_{i,d} \vert}{V_{i,d}}
$$

**Step 4 — Average over $N$ trading days to obtain a robust estimator:**

$$
\overline{ILLIQ_i} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{d=1}^{N} \frac{\vert R_{i,d} \vert}{V_{i,d}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- **Higher** $\overline{ILLIQ_i}$ → stock is **less liquid** (large price moves on small volume).
- **Lower** $\overline{ILLIQ_i}$ → stock is **more liquid** (price stable despite large volume).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Example: Worked KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A stock has a best bid of ₹498.50 and a best ask of ₹501.50. Calculate: (a) the absolute spread, (b) the mid-price, and (c) the relative spread in percentage.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Absolute spread:**

$$
\text{Spread} = P_{ask} - P_{bid} = 501.50 - 498.50 = 3.00 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step (b) — Mid-price:**

$$
P_{mid} = \frac{P_{ask} + P_{bid}}{2} = \frac{501.50 + 498.50}{2} = \frac{1000.00}{2} = 500.00 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step (c) — Relative spread percentage:**

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{2 \times (501.50 - 498.50)}{501.50 + 498.50} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{2 \times 3.00}{1000.00} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{6.00}{1000.00} \times 100 = 0.60\%
$$

**Final Answer:** The relative bid-ask spread is **0.60%**, indicating a moderately liquid stock. Large-cap Nifty 50 stocks typically have relative spreads of **0.01% to 0.10%**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Liquidity Comparison Across Asset Classes

| **Asset Class** | **Typical Relative Spread** | **Resilience** | **Typical Daily Turnover** |
|----------------|------------------------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| Large-cap equity (e.g., RELIANCE) | 0.01% – 0.05% | Very High | ₹500 Cr – ₹3000 Cr |
| Mid-cap equity | 0.10% – 0.50% | High | ₹10 Cr – ₹100 Cr |
| Small-cap / Penny stock | 1% – 5% | Low | < ₹1 Cr |
| Government Bonds (G-Sec) | 0.02% – 0.10% | Very High | Variable |
| Corporate Bonds | 0.50% – 2% | Moderate | Low |
| Real Estate | 5% – 15% | Very Low | Very Low |
| Gold (MCX Futures) | 0.05% – 0.20% | High | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Three Dimensions of Market Liquidity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Market Liquidity"] --> nodeB["Dimension 1: Tightness"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Dimension 2: Depth"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Dimension 3: Resilience"]

    nodeB --> nodeE["Metric: Bid Ask Spread"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Lower Spread = Higher Tightness"]

    nodeC --> nodeG["Metric: Order Book Volume"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Greater Volume at Multiple Price Levels"]

    nodeD --> nodeI["Metric: Price Recovery Speed"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ["Faster Reverts to Equilibrium"]

    nodeE --> nodeK["Highly Liquid Market"]
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This flowchart decomposes liquidity into its three academically accepted dimensions, each with its own metric. A *truly* liquid market must satisfy all three dimensions simultaneously.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Order Book Depth Visualization (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Order Book Architecture"] --> nodeB["Bid Side Buyers"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Ask Side Sellers"]

    nodeB --> nodeD["Bid Price 499: 1000 shares"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Bid Price 498: 2500 shares"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Bid Price 497: 5000 shares"]

    nodeC --> nodeG["Ask Price 501: 1200 shares"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Ask Price 502: 3000 shares"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["Ask Price 503: 4500 shares"]

    nodeF --> nodeJ["Greater Depth = Higher Liquidity"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The order book depth at distant price levels is what allows **institutional traders** to execute large block orders via algorithms (TWAP, VWAP) without moving the price sharply.

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Liquidity Provision Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Liquidity Ecosystem"] --> nodeB["Retail Traders"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Institutional Investors"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Market Makers"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["High Frequency Trading Firms"]

    nodeB --> nodeF["Order Flow Contribution"]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF

    nodeF --> nodeG["Central Limit Order Book CLOB"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Matching Engine"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Executed Trades"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Price Discovery and Liquidity Feedback Loop"]
    nodeJ --> nodeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Market Makers and HFT firms are the **primary professional liquidity providers** in modern exchanges. They quote both buy and sell prices continuously, capturing the spread as compensation for providing immediacy.

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Liquidity vs. Return Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["High Liquidity Asset"] --> nodeB["Lower Bid Ask Spread"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Lower Expected Return"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Examples: Nifty 50 Stocks"]

    nodeE["Low Liquidity Asset"] --> nodeF["Higher Bid Ask Spread"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Higher Expected Return ILP"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Examples: Small Cap Penny Stocks"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The relationship between liquidity and expected return is known as the **Liquidity Premium**. Investors demand higher returns for holding illiquid assets to compensate for the difficulty of selling them quickly — a concept formalized in the Amihud-Mendelson (1986) model.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define liquidity in the context of stock markets. Mention any two measures used to quantify it.**

**Model Answer:**
Liquidity refers to the ability of a market participant to buy or sell a security quickly without causing a significant change in its price. It reflects the *ease* and *speed* of converting an asset into cash at a fair value.

Two standard measures of liquidity are:
1. **Bid-Ask Spread** — The difference between the lowest ask price and the highest bid price. A narrower spread indicates higher liquidity.
2. **Trading Volume** — The total number of shares (or monetary value) traded over a given period. Higher volume typically signals higher liquidity.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between Market Liquidity and Accounting Liquidity with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Market Liquidity | Accounting Liquidity |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Ease of trading an asset in the market without price impact | Ability of a firm to pay off short-term obligations using liquid assets |
| **Example** | High trading volume of TCS shares on NSE | Current Ratio of a manufacturing company = 2.0 |
| **Measured by** | Bid-ask spread, volume, depth | Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Cash Ratio |
| **Focus** | Market efficiency | Corporate financial health |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the three dimensions of market liquidity. For each dimension, state the metric used to measure it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three dimensions of market liquidity, as formalized in market microstructure theory, are:

**1. Tightness (Cost Dimension):**
Tightness refers to the **cost** of executing an immediate trade. It is measured by the **bid-ask spread** — the difference between the best ask price and the best bid price.

$$
\text{Tightness Metric: } \text{Spread} = P_{ask} - P_{bid}
$$

A market with low spread (e.g., 0.01% for Nifty 50 stocks) is considered *tight* and therefore highly liquid. In contrast, penny stocks with spreads of 2–5% are *loose* markets.

**2. Depth (Volume Dimension):**
Depth refers to the **volume of orders** resting on both sides of the order book at various price levels near the current market price. It is measured by the **cumulative order book volume** within a defined price band (e.g., ₹1 around mid-price).

$$
\text{Depth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{bid,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} Q_{ask,j}
$$

where $Q_{bid,i}$ and $Q_{ask,j}$ are the quantities at each bid and ask level. Greater depth allows large institutional orders to be absorbed without large price movements.

**3. Resilience (Recovery Dimension):**
Resilience refers to the **speed at which prices return to their equilibrium level** after being temporarily displaced by a large trade. It is measured by observing the **time decay of price impact** after a block trade.

$$
\text{Resilience} \propto \frac{1}{T_{recovery}}
$$

where $T_{recovery}$ is the time taken for the price to revert to the pre-trade mid-price. Highly resilient markets (e.g., NSE cash market) recover within seconds, while illiquid markets may take hours or remain distorted.

---

**Q. (b)** The bid price of a stock is ₹995.50 and the ask price is ₹1004.50. Calculate the absolute spread, mid-price, and relative spread (in %). Comment on the liquidity of the stock. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Absolute spread:**

$$
\text{Spread} = P_{ask} - P_{bid} = 1004.50 - 995.50 = 9.00 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Mid-price:**

$$
P_{mid} = \frac{P_{ask} + P_{bid}}{2} = \frac{1004.50 + 995.50}{2} = \frac{2000.00}{2} = 1000.00 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Relative spread (in %):**

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{2 \times (P_{ask} - P_{bid})}{P_{ask} + P_{bid}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Spread}_{\%} = \frac{2 \times 9.00}{2000.00} \times 100 = \frac{1800}{2000.00} = 0.90\%
$$

**Step 4 — Liquidity Commentary:**
A relative spread of **0.90%** is **moderately wide**. For large-cap Nifty 50 stocks, typical spreads are well below 0.10%. This suggests the stock in question is likely a **mid-cap or small-cap** with limited liquidity, where the round-trip transaction cost is significantly higher. Traders executing large orders in such stocks should anticipate higher slippage.

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q. (a)** Discuss the various **types of liquidity** with suitable examples. Explain the importance of liquidity in stock markets. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Liquidity:**

**1. Market Liquidity:**
The ease with which assets can be traded in a market without affecting the price. Example: Reliance Industries shares on NSE can be bought or sold within seconds in millions of shares with minimal price impact.

**2. Asset Liquidity:**
Refers to the *inherent* convertibility of a specific asset into cash. Cash is the most asset-liquid (it is already cash). Equities are more liquid than real estate or private equity.

**3. Funding Liquidity:**
The ability of financial institutions (banks, brokers) to raise cash to meet immediate obligations. Example: A brokerage firm obtaining bank credit lines to lend margin to its clients.

**4. Accounting Liquidity:**
A firm's ability to pay off current liabilities using current assets. Measured by ratios like:
   - **Current Ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$
   - **Quick Ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$

**Importance of Liquidity in Stock Markets:**
- **Reduces Transaction Costs:** Tight spreads mean lower cost per trade.
- **Enables Price Discovery:** Active participation leads to fair price formation.
- **Attracts Institutional Capital:** Pension funds and mutual funds require liquid markets.
- **Prevents Market Crashes:** Liquid markets absorb shocks; illiquid ones amplify them.
- **Boosts Investor Confidence:** Easy exit options encourage long-term investment.

---

**Q. (b)** Define the **Amihud Illiquidity Ratio (ILLIQ)**. For a stock, the following data is observed over 4 days:

| Day | Daily Return $\vert R \vert$ | Trading Volume (₹ Crore) |
|-----|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1   | 0.015                        | 50                        |
| 2   | 0.020                        | 40                        |
| 3   | 0.010                        | 100                       |
| 4   | 0.025                        | 25                        |

**Calculate the average Amihud ILLIQ and comment on liquidity.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Amihud ILLIQ formula:**

$$
ILLIQ_{i,d} = \frac{\vert R_{i,d} \vert}{V_{i,d}}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate daily ILLIQ values:**

For Day 1:

$$
ILLIQ_1 = \frac{0.015}{50} = 0.000300 \text{ per ₹ Crore}
$$

For Day 2:

$$
ILLIQ_2 = \frac{0.020}{40} = 0.000500 \text{ per ₹ Crore}
$$

For Day 3:

$$
ILLIQ_3 = \frac{0.010}{100} = 0.000100 \text{ per ₹ Crore}
$$

For Day 4:

$$
ILLIQ_4 = \frac{0.025}{25} = 0.001000 \text{ per ₹ Crore}
$$

**Step 3 — Average ILLIQ over 4 days:**

$$
\overline{ILLIQ} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{d=1}^{4} ILLIQ_d
$$

$$
\overline{ILLIQ} = \frac{0.000300 + 0.000500 + 0.000100 + 0.001000}{4}
$$

$$
\overline{ILLIQ} = \frac{0.001900}{4} = 0.000475 \text{ per ₹ Crore}
$$

**Step 4 — Liquidity Commentary:**
The average Amihud ILLIQ of **0.000475 per ₹ Crore** is **moderately high**, indicating that the stock exhibits significant price movement per unit of trading volume. This suggests the stock is **relatively illiquid**. Day 4 in particular shows extreme illiquidity (large return on small volume). Such stocks are characterized by high slippage and elevated execution risk for institutional investors.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Spread Formula:** Students often write $\frac{P_{ask} - P_{bid}}{P_{ask}}$ instead of dividing by the **mid-price**. The correct denominator for the *relative* spread is the mid-price.
> 2. **Units of Amihud ILLIQ:** Many students omit units. Always state the unit clearly (e.g., "per ₹ Crore") to score full marks.
> 3. **Mixing Market vs. Accounting Liquidity:** These are *different concepts*. Examiners deduct marks for using accounting ratios (Current Ratio) to answer questions about *market* liquidity.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Three Dimensions":** When asked about liquidity, always mention **Tightness, Depth, and Resilience** — this is a high-yield KTU board keyword.
> 5. **Skipping Interpretation:** Numerical answers without a final interpretive sentence (e.g., "this indicates low liquidity") lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Liquidity**

- **Definition:** Liquidity = ease + speed + cost of converting an asset to cash without price impact.
- **Three Dimensions:** Tightness (spread), Depth (volume), Resilience (recovery).
- **Key Formulas:**
   - Absolute Spread $= P_{ask} - P_{bid}$
   - Relative Spread $\% = \frac{2 \times (P_{ask} - P_{bid})}{P_{ask} + P_{bid}} \times 100$
   - Mid-Price $= \frac{P_{ask} + P_{bid}}{2}$
   - Turnover Ratio $= \frac{\text{Volume Traded}}{\text{Shares Outstanding}}$
   - Amihud ILLIQ $= \frac{\vert R \vert}{V}$
- **Types:** Market, Asset, Funding, Accounting — do not confuse them in exams.
- **High Liquidity** → Narrow spread, large volume, fast resilience.
- **Liquidity Providers:** Market Makers, HFT firms, institutional participants.
- **Liquidity Premium:** Illiquid assets demand higher expected returns (Amihud-Mendelson).
- **Indian Context:** NSE cash market = high liquidity; small-cap penny stocks = low liquidity.
- **Exam Mantra:** Always end numerical answers with a one-line interpretation — this is what separates a 7-mark answer from a full 14-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valuation of Stocks - Intrinsic Value of Stocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Valuation of Stocks - Intrinsic Value of Stocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrinsic Value of a Stock** is defined as the **present value of all expected future cash flows** (dividends and terminal price) that an investor expects to receive from holding the share, discounted back to the present at an appropriate required rate of return (cost of equity, $K_e$).

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the intrinsic value ($P_0$ or $V_0$) represents the **fundamental worth of a stock** based on its underlying earning power, dividend-paying capacity, and risk profile — completely independent of the prevailing market price. The intrinsic value is therefore a theoretical "fair price" benchmark.

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{D_t}{(1 + K_e)^t}$$

Where:
- $D_t$ = Expected dividend in year $t$
- $K_e$ = Required rate of return (cost of equity)
- $P_0$ = Intrinsic value (present)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The intrinsic value is also called the **"Rational Price"** or **"Theoretical Value"** of a stock. The market price fluctuates around the intrinsic value according to the *Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)* in the short run, but converges in the long run.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **coconut tree on a farm** 🌴.

- The tree gives you **fruits (dividends)** every year for the next 30 years.
- The farmer quotes a price of **₹10,000** for the tree.
- But if you calculate: "What is the *present worth* of all those future coconuts, discounted for risk (drought, disease), and the wood value at the end?"
- That calculated figure is the **Intrinsic Value**.

If your calculation gives **₹12,000** → the tree is **undervalued** (buy it ✅).
If your calculation gives **₹8,000** → the tree is **overvalued** (don't buy ❌).
If it equals **₹10,000** → it's at **fair value**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** *Intrinsic value = What a stock is truly worth based on its future cash-generating ability, not the price someone is willing to pay today.*

### 1.3 Why Intrinsic Value Matters in KTU Context

| Market Price ($P_m$) | Intrinsic Value ($P_0$) | Decision Signal |
|---|---|---|
| $P_m > P_0$ | Stock overvalued | **SELL / AVOID** |
| $P_m < P_0$ | Stock undervalued | **BUY** |
| $P_m = P_0$ | Fairly valued | **HOLD** |

This decision matrix is **directly examinable** in KTU Part A and Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Dividend Discount Model (DDM)

The foundational premise of stock valuation under KTU Module 3 is the **Dividend Discount Model (DDM)**, which states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *"The value of a stock equals the present value of all future dividends the stock is expected to pay over its infinite life."*

The general infinite-horizon DDM equation is:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{D_t}{(1 + K_e)^t}$$

Since no company is perpetual in a single dividend form, KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes the DDM into **four practical models** based on dividend growth patterns.

### 2.2 The Four Practical DDM Variants

**Model 1: Zero Growth Model (Constant Dividend)**
- Assumes dividends remain **constant forever** ($D_t = D$).
- Common in: preferred stocks, matured utility firms.

**Model 2: Constant Growth Model (Gordon Growth Model)**
- Assumes dividends grow at a **constant rate 'g'** indefinitely.
- Common in: stable blue-chip companies (e.g., FMCG majors).

**Model 3: Two-Stage Growth Model**
- Assumes **high growth for 'n' years**, then **constant growth forever**.
- Common in: high-growth tech firms transitioning to maturity.

**Model 4: Variable (Non-Constant) Growth Model**
- Assumes **arbitrary dividend patterns** for specific years.
- Most flexible, used in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for Exam Hall** — These are the high-yield equations in KTU 2024 Scheme valuation problems.

| # | Model | Formula | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Zero Growth** | $P_0 = \dfrac{D}{K_e}$ | $K_e > 0$ |
| 2 | **Constant Growth (Gordon)** | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \dfrac{D_0(1+g)}{K_e - g}$ | $K_e > g$ (strictly) |
| 3 | **Two-Stage Growth** | $P_0 = \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+K_e)^t} + \dfrac{D_{n+1}}{(K_e - g_2)(1+K_e)^n}$ | $K_e > g_2$; $g_1 > g_2$ typically |
| 4 | **Variable Growth** | $P_0 = \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{D_t}{(1+K_e)^t} + \dfrac{P_n}{(1+K_e)^n}$ | $P_n$ computed via Gordon Model |
| 5 | **CAPM (Cost of Equity)** | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $\beta$ = systematic risk |
| 6 | **P/E Approach** | $P_0 = EPS \times P/E_{\text{industry}}$ | Forward-looking EPS |
| 7 | **P/B Approach** | $P_0 = BVPS \times P/B_{\text{industry}}$ | Book Value per Share basis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall (KTU Examiners' Pet Trap):** The constant growth formula $\frac{D_1}{K_e - g}$ is **INVALID** if $K_e \le g$. The denominator becomes zero or negative, producing a meaningless or negative intrinsic value. Always verify $K_e > g$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Management

While this course sits at the intersection of engineering and finance under KTU's NEP 2020 interdisciplinary framework, valuation of stocks is foundational for:

- **Corporate finance decisions** (B.Tech engineers becoming tech entrepreneurs evaluating their startup's IPO pricing).
- **Capital budgeting in tech firms** (project NPV calculations echo DDM logic).
- **Venture capital valuation** (engineer-founders understanding how VCs value equity).
- **Personal portfolio management** for engineering professionals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Zero Growth Model (Perpetuity Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Dividend $D$ is paid forever at constant value.

**Step 1:** Start with the general infinite dividend sum.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \dfrac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \dfrac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots$$

**Step 2:** Apply the zero-growth assumption: $D_1 = D_2 = \dots = D$.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D}{(1+K_e)} + \dfrac{D}{(1+K_e)^2} + \dfrac{D}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots$$

**Step 3:** Factor out the first term.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D}{1+K_e} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{1}{1+K_e} + \dfrac{1}{(1+K_e)^2} + \dots \right]$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the bracketed series as a **Geometric Progression (GP)** with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $r = \frac{1}{1+K_e}$.

**Step 5:** Apply the sum-to-infinity formula for GP ($S_{\infty} = \frac{a}{1-r}$), valid only when $\vert r \vert < 1$.

$$S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+K_e}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{K_e}{1+K_e}} = \dfrac{1+K_e}{K_e}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the expression for $P_0$.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D}{1+K_e} \times \dfrac{1+K_e}{K_e}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $(1+K_e)$ from numerator and denominator.

$$\boxed{P_0 = \dfrac{D}{K_e}}$$

**Economic Interpretation:** A stock paying a constant dividend $D$ in perpetuity is worth exactly $D$ divided by the required return $K_e$. This mirrors the **perpetuity bond** valuation logic in fixed income.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Constant Growth Model (Gordon Growth Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Dividend grows at constant rate $g$ forever: $D_t = D_0(1+g)^t$.

**Step 1:** Express the general DDM with growth.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_0(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} + \dfrac{D_0(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \dfrac{D_0(1+g)^3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots$$

**Step 2:** Define $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$ for notation simplicity. So:

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{1+K_e} + \dfrac{D_1(1+g)}{(1+K_e)^2} + \dfrac{D_1(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\frac{D_1}{1+K_e}$.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{1+K_e} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{(1+g)}{1+K_e} + \dfrac{(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \dots \right]$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the bracket as a GP with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $r = \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}$.

**Step 5:** Apply $S_{\infty} = \frac{a}{1-r}$. For convergence, we require $\vert r \vert < 1$, i.e., $1 + g < 1 + K_e$, which simplifies to $g < K_e$.

$$S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{1+g}{1+K_e}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{K_e - g}{1+K_e}} = \dfrac{1+K_e}{K_e - g}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{1+K_e} \times \dfrac{1+K_e}{K_e - g}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $(1+K_e)$.

$$\boxed{P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \dfrac{D_0(1+g)}{K_e - g}}$$

**Economic Interpretation:** The Gordon model prices a stock as the **next year's dividend capitalized** at the spread between required return and growth rate. The narrower the spread ($K_e - g$), the higher the valuation — but also the higher the **valuation risk**.

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Two-Stage Growth Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Super-normal growth rate $g_1$ for $n$ years, followed by stable growth $g_2$ thereafter.

**Step 1:** The intrinsic value has two components — present value of high-growth dividends + present value of terminal value at year $n$.

$$P_0 = \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+K_e)^t}}_{\text{High-growth PV}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{P_n}{(1+K_e)^n}}_{\text{Terminal PV}}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the terminal value $P_n$ at year $n$ using the Gordon model with the stable growth rate $g_2$. The first stable dividend in year $n+1$ is:

$$D_{n+1} = D_0(1+g_1)^n(1+g_2)$$

**Step 3:** Apply Gordon's formula for $P_n$.

$$P_n = \dfrac{D_{n+1}}{K_e - g_2} = \dfrac{D_0(1+g_1)^n(1+g_2)}{K_e - g_2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $P_n$ into the original expression.

$$\boxed{P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+K_e)^t} + \dfrac{1}{(1+K_e)^n} \cdot \dfrac{D_0(1+g_1)^n(1+g_2)}{K_e - g_2}}$$

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Cost of Equity via CAPM

The required return $K_e$ is computed using the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**:

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (e.g., 10-Year G-Sec yield in India)
- $\beta$ = Beta of the stock (systematic risk measure)
- $R_m$ = Expected market return
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Equity Risk Premium (ERP)

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A company's current dividend is ₹5 per share. Dividends are expected to grow at **8% per year forever**. The required rate of return is **14%**. Calculate the intrinsic value.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify variables: $D_0 = 5$, $g = 0.08$, $K_e = 0.14$.

**Step 2:** Compute $D_1$.

$$D_1 = D_0(1+g) = 5 \times 1.08 = 5.40$$

**Step 3:** Apply Gordon's constant growth model.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \dfrac{5.40}{0.14 - 0.08} = \dfrac{5.40}{0.06}$$

$$\boxed{P_0 = ₹90}$$

**Step 4:** Investment decision: If market price is ₹75, stock is **undervalued** → **BUY**. If market price is ₹100, stock is **overvalued** → **SELL**.

---

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | Zero Growth | Constant Growth | Two-Stage Growth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Growth Rate** | $g = 0$ | $g = \text{constant}$ | $g_1$ then $g_2$ |
| **Formula** | $D/K_e$ | $D_1/(K_e-g)$ | Sum + Terminal PV |
| **Real-world use** | Preferred shares | Blue-chip stocks | Growth firms |
| **Complexity** | Trivial | Moderate | High (exam favorite) |
| **KTU exam weightage** | Low | High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart — Stock Valuation Methodology

This Mermaid flowchart captures the **KTU 2024 Scheme** decision logic a student must follow when approaching any valuation problem on the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Stock Valuation Problem"] --> B["Identify Dividend Pattern Type"]
    B --> C{"Dividend is Constant?"}
    C -- "Yes (D_t = D)" --> D["Apply Zero Growth Model: P0 = D / Ke"]
    C -- "No" --> E{"Dividend grows at constant rate g?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F{"Is Ke strictly greater than g?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Apply Gordon Model: P0 = D1 / (Ke - g)"]
    F -- "No (Ke <= g)" --> H["INVALID: Model Breaks Down"]
    E -- "No" --> I{"Multiple growth phases?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Apply Two-Stage Model: PV of high growth + Terminal PV"]
    I -- "No (arbitrary pattern)" --> K["Apply Variable Growth: Sum of PVs"]
    D --> L["Compute Ke using CAPM: Ke = Rf + beta times Rm minus Rf"]
    G --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["Determine Decision Signal"]
    M --> N{"Compare P0 with Market Price Pm"}
    N -- "Pm less than P0" --> O["BUY SIGNAL: Undervalued Stock"]
    N -- "Pm greater than P0" --> P["SELL SIGNAL: Overvalued Stock"]
    N -- "Pm equals P0" --> Q["HOLD: Fairly Valued"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DDM Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Block: Company Financials"] --> A1["D0: Current Dividend"]
    A --> A2["g: Growth Rate Estimate"]
    A --> A3["Ke: Required Return"]
    B["Processing Block: DDM Engine"] --> B1["Zero Growth Module"]
    B --> B2["Gordon Constant Growth Module"]
    B --> B3["Two-Stage Growth Module"]
    B --> B4["Variable Growth Module"]
    C["Output Block: Intrinsic Value P0"] --> C1["Single Scalar Value"]
    C1 --> D["Comparator Block"]
    E["Input: Market Price Pm"] --> D
    D --> F["Decision Output: Buy / Sell / Hold"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — Two-Stage Growth Phase Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title Two-Stage Stock Valuation Timeline
    dateFormat  YYYY

    section Phase 1 Super Normal Growth

    section Phase 2 Stable Growth

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the intrinsic value of a stock. How does it differ from the market price?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Intrinsic value** is the present value of all expected future cash flows (dividends) from a stock, discounted at the required rate of return. It represents the **fundamental or true worth** of a stock based on its earning power and risk profile.

> **Market price** is the prevailing price at which the stock is currently being traded in the market, determined by **demand and supply** forces and investor sentiment.

> **Key Differences:**
> 1. Intrinsic value is **objective and analytical**; market price is **subjective and sentiment-driven**.
> 2. Intrinsic value is a **theoretical fair price**; market price is the **actual transaction price**.
> 3. In an **efficient market**, the two tend to converge over the long run.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark each for definition, difference points, and convergence statement.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: State the Gordon Growth Model formula. What is the critical condition for its validity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Gordon Growth Model** (Constant Growth Model) is given by:
> $$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g}$$

> Where $D_1$ = expected next-year dividend, $K_e$ = required rate of return, $g$ = constant growth rate.

> **Critical Condition:** The model is valid **only when $K_e > g$** (strictly greater). If $K_e \le g$, the denominator becomes zero or negative, producing meaningless results.

*(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variable definition, 1 mark for critical condition.)*

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Students answer EITHER Option A OR Option B. Both options are provided below.

---

#### **🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A stock is expected to pay a dividend of ₹8 next year. The dividend is expected to grow at 10% per year for the next 3 years, and thereafter at a stable 5% per year forever. The required rate of return is 15%. Calculate the intrinsic value of the stock using the two-stage growth model.**

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the dividends and present value of dividends for the high-growth phase (Years 1–3).

**Model Solution:**

Given: $D_1 = 8$, $g_1 = 10\% = 0.10$, $K_e = 15\% = 0.15$.

**Year 1:** $D_1 = 8$ (given)

$$PV_1 = \dfrac{8}{(1.15)^1} = \dfrac{8}{1.15} = 6.9565$$

**Year 2:** $D_2 = D_1(1+g_1) = 8 \times 1.10 = 8.80$

$$PV_2 = \dfrac{8.80}{(1.15)^2} = \dfrac{8.80}{1.3225} = 6.6541$$

**Year 3:** $D_3 = D_2(1+g_1) = 8.80 \times 1.10 = 9.68$

$$PV_3 = \dfrac{9.68}{(1.15)^3} = \dfrac{9.68}{1.520875} = 6.3647$$

**Sum of PVs in high-growth phase:**

$$PV_{\text{high}} = 6.9565 + 6.6541 + 6.3647 = 19.9753$$

*(Award 2 marks for year-by-year dividend calculation, 2 marks for PV computation, 1 mark for sum, 2 marks for correct application of discount factor.)*

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Compute the terminal value at Year 3 using the stable growth model and find the total intrinsic value.

**Model Solution:**

Given: $g_2 = 5\% = 0.05$, $n = 3$.

**Step 1:** Compute Year 4 dividend.

$$D_4 = D_3(1+g_2) = 9.68 \times 1.05 = 10.164$$

**Step 2:** Apply Gordon's formula to compute terminal value $P_3$ at end of Year 3.

$$P_3 = \dfrac{D_4}{K_e - g_2} = \dfrac{10.164}{0.15 - 0.05} = \dfrac{10.164}{0.10} = 101.64$$

**Step 3:** Discount the terminal value back to present.

$$PV_{\text{terminal}} = \dfrac{101.64}{(1.15)^3} = \dfrac{101.64}{1.520875} = 66.826$$

**Step 4:** Total intrinsic value.

$$P_0 = PV_{\text{high}} + PV_{\text{terminal}} = 19.9753 + 66.826 = 86.80$$

$$\boxed{P_0 \approx ₹86.80}$$

**Decision:** If market price is, say, ₹80 → stock is **undervalued**, **BUY**.

*(Award 2 marks for D4 calculation, 2 marks for terminal value via Gordon model, 2 marks for discounting terminal value, 1 mark for final sum and decision.)*

---

#### **🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) A company has paid a dividend of ₹4 per share this year. The required rate of return is 12% and the constant growth rate is 6%. Using the Gordon Growth Model, calculate the intrinsic value of the stock and advise whether to buy, sell, or hold if the market price is ₹90. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $D_0 = 4$, $K_e = 12\% = 0.12$, $g = 6\% = 0.06$.

**Step 1:** Compute next-year dividend.

$$D_1 = D_0(1+g) = 4 \times 1.06 = 4.24$$

**Step 2:** Verify validity: $K_e = 0.12 > g = 0.06$ ✅ Model is applicable.

**Step 3:** Apply Gordon Growth Model.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \dfrac{4.24}{0.12 - 0.06} = \dfrac{4.24}{0.06} = 70.67$$

**Step 4:** Compare with market price.

$$P_m = 90 > P_0 = 70.67$$

**Decision:** Stock is **overvalued by ₹19.33** (≈ 27% premium). **Recommendation: SELL / AVOID**.

*(Award 1 mark for D1, 1 mark for validity check, 2 marks for formula application, 1 mark for final value, 2 marks for decision logic.)*

---

**(b) The risk-free rate is 7%, the expected market return is 14%, and the stock's beta is 1.2. The current dividend is ₹6, growing at 5% per year. Compute the intrinsic value using CAPM-derived $K_e$. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate $K_e$ using CAPM.

$$K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 0.07 + 1.2 \times (0.14 - 0.07)$$

$$K_e = 0.07 + 1.2 \times 0.07 = 0.07 + 0.084 = 0.154$$

So $K_e = 15.4\%$.

**Step 2:** Compute $D_1$.

$$D_1 = 6 \times 1.05 = 6.30$$

**Step 3:** Apply Gordon's formula.

$$P_0 = \dfrac{6.30}{0.154 - 0.05} = \dfrac{6.30}{0.104} = 60.58$$

**Step 4:** If market price is ₹55 → stock is **undervalued**, **BUY**.

$$\boxed{P_0 \approx ₹60.58}$$

*(Award 2 marks for CAPM derivation, 1 mark for D1, 2 marks for Gordon model, 1 mark for final value, 1 mark for decision.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (from KTU 2024 Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Using $D_0$ instead of $D_1$** in the Gordon formula — partial marking penalty of **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $K_e > g$** before applying Gordon's model — examiners deduct **−1 mark** and flag the answer as "incomplete reasoning."
> 3. **Not discounting the terminal value** in two-stage problems — biggest mistake, costs **up to −3 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing intrinsic value with market capitalization** — these are entirely different concepts. Market cap = Market price × Number of shares; Intrinsic value = present value of future cash flows per share.
> 5. **Rounding errors in multi-step problems** — KTU expects 2-decimal accuracy in intermediate steps.
> 6. **Skipping the BUY/SELL/HOLD decision** — a complete answer must include the investment decision; else **−1 to −2 marks** penalty.
> 7. **Mixing up $g_1$ and $g_2$** in two-stage problems — careful labeling is essential.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Intrinsic value** = Present value of all future dividends discounted at $K_e$.
- 📌 **Zero Growth Model:** $P_0 = D / K_e$ — used for constant dividends.
- 📌 **Gordon Model:** $P_0 = D_1 / (K_e - g)$ — used for constant growth; **strictly requires $K_e > g$**.
- 📌 **Two-Stage Model:** $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+K_e)^t} + \frac{P_n}{(1+K_e)^n}$ — used for high-growth-then-stable firms.
- 📌 **CAPM:** $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — the gold standard for required return.
- 📌 **Decision Rule:** $P_m > P_0$ → SELL; $P_m < P_0$ → BUY; $P_m = P_0$ → HOLD.
- 📌 **P/E Approach:** $P_0 = EPS \times \text{Industry P/E}$ — useful when dividends are not paid.
- 📌 **P/B Approach:** $P_0 = BVPS \times \text{Industry P/B}$ — useful for asset-heavy firms.
- 📌 **Always discount** the terminal value in two-stage problems — never skip the $(1+K_e)^n$ factor.
- 📌 **Always state the BUY/SELL/HOLD decision** in long-answer solutions.
- 📌 **Always verify validity** of the chosen model before applying it.
- 📌 **In Indian context:** $R_f$ typically refers to the 10-Year Government Securities (G-Sec) yield.
- 📌 **KTU 2024 focus areas:** Gordon Model, Two-Stage Model, and CAPM-based $K_e$ computation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technical Analysis - RWT Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Walk Theory (RWT)** is a financial theory which states that **stock market prices evolve according to a random walk and thus cannot be predicted**. The theory is closely associated with the **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)**, which posits that all available information is already reflected in stock prices, making consistent excess returns impossible through any analytical method, including technical or fundamental analysis.

Mathematically, the theory postulates that the successive price changes in a security are **independent random variables** drawn from the same probability distribution, and are **serially uncorrelated**. The theory was popularized in the 1973 book *"A Random Walk Down Wall Street"* by economist **Burton G. Malkiel**, building upon the earlier PhD work of **Maurice Kendall (1953)** and the **Osborne (1959)** model of stock prices as a **continuous-time stochastic process** analogous to Brownian motion in physics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RWT forms the theoretical *counter-argument* to technical analysis. Understanding RWT is essential because the very existence of technical analysis as a discipline hinges on the rejection of RWT. Every technical analyst must know the assumptions, evidence, and limitations of RWT to defend their methodology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Drunkard's Walk

Imagine a **drunk man** stumbling home on a dimly lit street after a party. Each step he takes is **completely independent** of the previous step — sometimes he steps left, sometimes right, sometimes forward, sometimes backward. There is no pattern, no memory, and no momentum in his movement.

- **Where he is now** tells you **nothing** about **where he will be next**.
- The **size** of each step is drawn from a probability distribution (mostly small, occasionally large).
- No matter how carefully you study his past path, you cannot forecast his future position with any statistical confidence.

This is precisely how RWT models stock prices. Each day's price change is the "step" of the drunk man, drawn from a probability distribution. **Past price movements contain no useful information about future movements** — and this is the central, provocative claim of the theory.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway for KTU Students:** If RWT is true, then **chart patterns, trend lines, moving averages, RSI, MACD** — every tool a technical analyst uses — are essentially **financial astrology**. The market is a fair game, and trying to beat it through analysis is futile in the long run.

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Boundary Definitions

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Random Walk** | A path of successive, independent random steps | Foundation of RWT |
| **Efficient Market** | A market where prices reflect all available information | Pre-condition for RWT |
| **Serial Independence** | $P(X_t \mid X_{t-1}, X_{t-2}, \ldots) = P(X_t)$ | Mathematical core of RWT |
| **Brownian Motion** | Continuous-time analog used to model prices | Mathematical underpinning |
| **Weak Form Efficiency** | Prices reflect all *past* trading information | Directly contradicts TA |
| **Semi-Strong Form** | Prices reflect all *publicly available* information | Contradicts fundamental + TA |
| **Strong Form** | Prices reflect *all* information, including insider | Ultimate form of RWT |
| **Joint Hypothesis Problem** | Inability to test market efficiency without an asset pricing model | Critical critique of RWT |

## 1.4 The Historical Context

In **1953**, British statistician **Maurice Kendall** examined weekly stock prices and discovered to his surprise that price changes appeared to follow no discernible pattern — they looked "random." This contradicted the prevailing belief that stock prices moved in identifiable trends. Later, **Eugene Fama** (1970) formalized this in his seminal paper *"Efficient Capital Markets: A Review of Theory and Empirical Work,"* which defined the three forms of market efficiency. The combination of Kendall's empirical observation and Fama's theoretical framework became the foundation of what is now called **Random Walk Theory** in the context of stock markets.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Mathematical Architecture of RWT

The Random Walk Theory models stock prices as a **discrete-time stochastic process**. Let $P_t$ denote the price of a security at time $t$. The RWT asserts:

### Core Equations of the Random Walk

**Equation 1 — The Basic Random Walk (Additive Form):**

$$P_{t+1} = P_t + \varepsilon_{t+1}$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Price of the security at time $t$
- $\varepsilon_{t+1}$ = Random shock (price change) at time $t+1$
- $E[\varepsilon_{t+1}] = \mu$ (constant mean drift)
- $Var(\varepsilon_{t+1}) = \sigma^2$ (constant variance)

**Equation 2 — The Price Change:**

$$\Delta P_t = P_t - P_{t-1} = \varepsilon_t$$

This implies that price changes are **identically and independently distributed (i.i.d.)** random variables.

**Equation 3 — The Geometric Random Walk (Multiplicative Form):**

$$P_{t+1} = P_t \cdot (1 + R_{t+1})$$

Where $R_{t+1}$ is the one-period simple return, modeled as a random variable.

**Equation 4 — Log-Return Formulation:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_{t+1}}{P_t}\right) = r_{t+1} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

The continuously compounded return $r_{t+1}$ is assumed to be **normally distributed** with constant mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. This is the **standard RWT assumption** used in most academic empirical tests.

**Equation 5 — Expected Future Price:**

$$E[P_{t+n} \mid P_t] = P_t + n\mu$$

Under RWT, the best forecast of any future price is simply the current price plus the expected drift — **no additional information from past prices helps**.

**Equation 6 — Variance of Future Price:**

$$Var(P_{t+n} \mid P_t) = n\sigma^2$$

The uncertainty about the future price grows with the **square root of time**, not linearly.

## 2.2 The Three Forms of Market Efficiency (Fama's EMH)

The Random Walk Theory is a direct consequence of the **Efficient Market Hypothesis**. Understanding all three forms is critical for KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Eugene Fama (1970)** classified market efficiency into three nested levels, each progressively stronger than the previous.

### 2.2.1 Weak Form Efficiency

- **Definition:** Current stock prices reflect **all information contained in the past history of prices and trading volumes**.
- **Implication for TA:** Technical analysis, which relies entirely on past price/volume data, **cannot generate abnormal returns** on average.
- **Testable Implication:** Successive price changes should be **serially uncorrelated** — i.e., $\text{Corr}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_{t-k}) = 0$ for all $k \geq 1$.

### 2.2.2 Semi-Strong Form Efficiency

- **Definition:** Stock prices reflect **all publicly available information**, including financial statements, news, earnings announcements, and macroeconomic data.
- **Implication:** Neither technical analysis **nor fundamental analysis** can consistently produce excess returns.
- **Testable Implication:** Stock prices should adjust **instantly and fully** to new public announcements.

### 2.2.3 Strong Form Efficiency

- **Definition:** Stock prices reflect **all information — public AND private (including insider information)**.
- **Implication:** **Nobody**, not even corporate insiders, can consistently earn abnormal returns.
- **Testable Implication:** Insiders should not be able to systematically outperform the market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Weak form is the **only form that directly challenges technical analysis**. Semi-strong and strong forms go further by also challenging fundamental analysis. When a KTU question asks "RWT invalidates which type of analysis?" — the answer is **primarily technical analysis** at the weak form level.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{t+1} = P_t + \varepsilon_{t+1}$ | Basic random walk | Core RWT model |
| 2 | $r_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | Log-return distribution | Most common RWT assumption |
| 3 | $E[P_{t+n}] = P_t + n\mu$ | Expected future price | Best forecast under RWT |
| 4 | $Var(P_{t+n}) = n\sigma^2$ | Variance grows linearly with $n$ | Confidence intervals |
| 5 | $SD(P_{t+n}) = \sigma\sqrt{n}$ | Standard deviation | Volatility scaling |
| 6 | $\text{Corr}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_{t-k}) = 0$ | Serial independence | Testable RWT condition |
| 7 | $E[r] = \bar{r} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i$ | Sample mean return | Empirical test |
| 8 | $s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(r_i - \bar{r})^2$ | Sample variance | Empirical test |
| 9 | $t = \frac{\bar{r} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}$ | t-statistic for return mean | Tests if mean is zero |
| 10 | $\rho_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t-k} - \bar{r})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(r_t - \bar{r})^2}$ | Autocorrelation at lag $k$ | Tests serial correlation |
| 11 | $Z = \frac{\rho_k}{1/\sqrt{n}}$ | Standardized autocorrelation | Significance test |
| 12 | $Z_{\text{runs}} = \frac{R - E[R]}{\sigma_R}$ | Runs test statistic | Tests randomness directly |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of RWT

Despite its provocative nature, RWT has significant **practical engineering and financial applications**:

1. **Algorithmic & Quantitative Trading:** Modern high-frequency trading (HFT) systems assume price changes are approximately random at very short time scales. Trading algorithms are designed around the assumption that they cannot *predict* price direction, only *manage execution*.
2. **Risk Management & Option Pricing:** The **Black-Scholes-Merton** option pricing model (Nobel Prize, 1997) directly assumes that stock prices follow a **geometric Brownian motion** — which IS a random walk. Without RWT, there is no closed-form option pricing formula.
3. **Portfolio Theory Foundation:** The **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** and **Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)** both rest on the assumption that price changes are at least approximately random.
4. **Regulatory Framework:** Market regulators (SEBI in India, SEC in the US) design market microstructure rules based on the assumption that informed traders' information gets quickly and randomly incorporated into prices.
5. **Index Fund Industry:** The explosive growth of passive index funds (which manage trillions globally) is direct evidence of widespread acceptance of RWT at the semi-strong form level.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Answers:** When asked "What is the real-world significance of RWT?", mention at least: (1) Black-Scholes option pricing, (2) Index fund industry growth, (3) Foundation of portfolio theory, and (4) Regulatory market design.

## 2.5 Arguments For and Against RWT (Critical for KTU Essays)

| Arguments FOR RWT | Arguments AGAINST RWT |
|---|---|
| Empirical tests show **near-zero autocorrelation** in price changes | Short-term momentum and reversal anomalies documented (Jegadeesh & Titman, 1993) |
| **Mutual funds** fail to consistently beat the market index | Insiders and some hedge funds *do* persistently outperform |
| **Index funds** outperform 80%+ of actively managed funds | Behavioral biases (overreaction, herd behavior) contradict rationality |
| Option pricing models **work** in practice | Bubbles and crashes (2000, 2008) suggest non-random dynamics |
| Information is **widely and quickly** disseminated | Technical patterns have been used profitably for centuries |
| The **Joint Hypothesis Problem** cannot be disproven | Adaptive Markets Hypothesis (Lo, 2004) reconciles efficiency with inefficiency |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Future Price Under RWT

We start from the basic random walk equation and derive the expected price at any future time step.

**Step 1:** Write the random walk equation for one step.

$$P_{t+1} = P_t + \varepsilon_{t+1}$$

**Step 2:** Take the expectation operator on both sides, conditioning on all information available at time $t$ (denoted $\mathcal{F}_t$).

$$E[P_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = E[P_t \mid \mathcal{F}_t] + E[\varepsilon_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t]$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $P_t$ is known at time $t$, so it is a constant with respect to the expectation.

$$E[P_t \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = P_t$$

**Step 4:** Apply the RWT assumption that the random shock has constant mean $\mu$ independent of past information.

$$E[\varepsilon_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = \mu$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back to get the one-step-ahead expected price.

$$E[P_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = P_t + \mu$$

**Step 6:** Now extend to $n$ steps. Write the price at $t+2$ similarly.

$$P_{t+2} = P_{t+1} + \varepsilon_{t+2}$$

**Step 7:** Take the expectation conditioning on $\mathcal{F}_t$.

$$E[P_{t+2} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = E[P_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] + E[\varepsilon_{t+2} \mid \mathcal{F}_t]$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $E[P_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = P_t + \mu$ and $E[\varepsilon_{t+2} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = \mu$.

$$E[P_{t+2} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = P_t + \mu + \mu = P_t + 2\mu$$

**Step 9:** By mathematical induction, generalize to $n$ steps.

$$\boxed{E[P_{t+n} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = P_t + n\mu}$$

**Economic Interpretation:** The best forecast of a stock's price $n$ periods into the future is simply **today's price plus $n$ times the average historical drift**. No pattern in the past prices improves this forecast — this is the heart of RWT's challenge to technical analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of Future Price Under RWT

**Step 1:** Start with the one-step-ahead price.

$$P_{t+1} = P_t + \varepsilon_{t+1}$$

**Step 2:** The variance of $P_{t+1}$ conditional on $\mathcal{F}_t$ is:

$$Var(P_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = Var(P_t + \varepsilon_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t)$$

**Step 3:** Since $P_t$ is known (deterministic given $\mathcal{F}_t$), its conditional variance is zero. Variance of a constant is zero.

$$Var(P_t \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = 0$$

**Step 4:** So the conditional variance reduces to:

$$Var(P_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = Var(\varepsilon_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = \sigma^2$$

**Step 5:** For two steps, write $P_{t+2} = P_t + \varepsilon_{t+1} + \varepsilon_{t+2}$.

$$Var(P_{t+2} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = Var(\varepsilon_{t+1} + \varepsilon_{t+2} \mid \mathcal{F}_t)$$

**Step 6:** By RWT, $\varepsilon_{t+1}$ and $\varepsilon_{t+2}$ are independent. Variance of sum of independent variables equals sum of variances.

$$Var(P_{t+2} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = \sigma^2 + \sigma^2 = 2\sigma^2$$

**Step 7:** By induction, for $n$ independent shocks:

$$\boxed{Var(P_{t+n} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = n\sigma^2}$$

**Step 8:** Taking the square root gives the standard deviation.

$$SD(P_{t+n} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = \sigma\sqrt{n}$$

**Economic Interpretation:** Uncertainty about future price grows with the **square root of the time horizon**. If daily volatility is 2%, then 25-day volatility is $2\% \times \sqrt{25} = 10\%$, not $50\%$. This square-root-of-time scaling is a direct, testable prediction of RWT.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Autocorrelation Test for RWT

The single most important empirical test of RWT is whether price changes are **serially correlated**.

**Step 1:** Define the sample autocorrelation coefficient at lag $k$:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t-k} - \bar{r})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(r_t - \bar{r})^2}$$

**Step 2:** Under the null hypothesis $H_0$ of RWT (no autocorrelation), the statistic:

$$Z_k = \frac{\rho_k}{1/\sqrt{n}} = \rho_k \sqrt{n}$$

is asymptotically **standard normally distributed**: $Z_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$.

**Step 3:** Decision rule: Reject RWT (i.e., reject the weak-form EMH) at the 5% significance level if:

$$\vert Z_k \vert > 1.96$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose a KTU problem gives you 50 daily log-returns and the sample autocorrelation at lag 1 is calculated as $\rho_1 = 0.15$.

**Step 1:** Compute the test statistic.

$$Z_1 = 0.15 \times \sqrt{50} = 0.15 \times 7.071 = 1.0607$$

**Step 2:** Compare to the critical value.

$$\vert Z_1 \vert = 1.0607 < 1.96$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

> **We fail to reject the null hypothesis of RWT at the 5% level.** The observed autocorrelation is consistent with random chance. This means there is no statistical evidence to contradict the random walk hypothesis for this series.

**Counter-example:** If the same data gave $\rho_1 = 0.35$, then $Z_1 = 0.35 \times 7.071 = 2.475 > 1.96$, and we **would reject RWT** — suggesting exploitable serial dependence (and giving ammunition to technical analysts).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Runs Test for Randomness

The **Runs Test** (Wald-Wolfowitz test) directly tests whether a sequence of price changes is random without assuming normality.

**Step 1:** Convert the series of returns $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n$ into a binary sequence: assign "$+$" to positive returns and "$-$" to negative returns (zero returns are dropped).

**Step 2:** Count the number of **runs** $R$ — a run is a maximal sequence of identical signs. For example, the sequence $+,+,+,-,-,+,+,+,-,-,-$ has $R = 4$ runs.

**Step 3:** Under $H_0$ (random sequence), the expected number of runs is:

$$E[R] = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n_1 + n_2} + 1$$

Where $n_1$ = number of positive returns, $n_2$ = number of negative returns.

**Step 4:** The variance of $R$ under $H_0$ is:

$$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - n_1 - n_2)}{(n_1 + n_2)^2 (n_1 + n_2 - 1)}$$

**Step 5:** The standardized test statistic:

$$Z = \frac{R - E[R]}{\sigma_R}$$

is approximately standard normal.

**Step 6:** Decision: Reject randomness if $\vert Z \vert > 1.96$ at 5% level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A **small number of runs** (alternating signs tend to cluster) suggests **momentum** — evidence against RWT. A **large number of runs** (signs rapidly alternate) suggests **mean-reversion** — also evidence against RWT. Either way, deviation from the expected number of runs contradicts pure randomness.
