# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a candlestick and list its four price components. What does a green candle indicate in the Indian market context?**

**Model Answer:**
A candlestick is a price charting tool used in technical analysis that represents the price movement of a financial asset over a specific time period. Its four price components are:
1. **Open Price** — Price at the start of the period
2. **High Price** — Highest price reached during the period
3. **Low Price** — Lowest price reached during the period
4. **Close Price** — Price at the end of the period

In the Indian market (NSE/BSE), a **green candle** indicates a **bullish candle** where the **Close price is higher than the Open price**, meaning buyers dominated the trading session. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Hammer pattern and a Hanging Man pattern. How is a Doji different from a Spinning Top?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hammer | Hanging Man |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Small body at top, long lower shadow | Identical shape |
| **Location** | Bottom of downtrend | Top of uptrend |
| **Signal** | Bullish reversal | Bearish reversal |

A **Doji** has an extremely small or non-existent body (Open ≈ Close), while a **Spinning Top** has a small but visible body with upper and lower shadows of comparable length — both indicate indecision, but Doji represents stronger indecision. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the construction of a candlestick with a neat diagram. Calculate the Body, Upper Shadow, Lower Shadow, and Total Range for a stock that opened at ₹120, closed at ₹135, with a daily high of ₹140 and low of ₹118. Comment on the candle type. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A candlestick consists of a **real body** (rectangle) between the Open and Close prices, an **upper shadow** (thin line) extending to the High, and a **lower shadow** (thin line) extending to the Low. For a bullish candle (Close > Open), the body's bottom is the Open and top is the Close.

**Given:** Open = ₹120, Close = ₹135, High = ₹140, Low = ₹118.

**Step 1: Determine candle direction.**
Close (135) > Open (120) → **Bullish (Green) Candle**.

**Step 2: Calculate Real Body.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Body} &= \vert \text{Close} - \text{Open} \vert = \vert 135 - 120 \vert = 15 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Upper Shadow.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_s &= \text{High} - \max(\text{Open}, \text{Close}) = 140 - 135 = 5 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Lower Shadow.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= \min(\text{Open}, \text{Close}) - \text{Low} = 120 - 118 = 2 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Total Range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range} &= \text{High} - \text{Low} = 140 - 118 = 22 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Body-to-Range Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BRR} = \frac{15}{22} \approx 0.682
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** A BRR of 0.68 indicates a **strong body with moderate shadows**, suggesting **strong bullish conviction** with minor intra-day profit-booking. This is a high-quality bullish candle. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) Describe the Bullish Engulfing pattern. Given the following two-day data — Day 1: Open ₹200, Close ₹195, High ₹202, Low ₹194; Day 2: Open ₹192, Close ₹208, High ₹210, Low ₹190 — verify whether a valid Bullish Engulfing pattern exists. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Bullish Engulfing pattern** is a two-candle reversal pattern that appears at the bottom of a downtrend. It consists of a small bearish candle followed by a larger bullish candle whose real body **completely engulfs** the previous candle's body. It signals a potential shift from selling pressure to buying pressure.

**Step 1: Verify Day 1 is bearish.**
Close (195) < Open (200) → **Bearish** ✅

**Step 2: Verify Day 2 is bullish.**
Close (208) > Open (192) → **Bullish** ✅

**Step 3: Body sizes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Body}_1 &= \vert 195 - 200 \vert = 5 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Body}_2 &= \vert 208 - 192 \vert = 16 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engulfing Conditions Check.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Open}_{\text{Day2}} &< \text{Close}_{\text{Day1}}: \quad 192 < 195 \quad \text{✅} \\
\text{Close}_{\text{Day2}} &> \text{Open}_{\text{Day1}}: \quad 208 > 200 \quad \text{✅} \\
\text{Body}_2 &> \text{Body}_1: \quad 16 > 5 \quad \text{✅}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Coverage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Coverage} &= 200 - 192 = 8 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Upper Coverage} &= 208 - 195 = 13 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** All three conditions are satisfied. A **valid Bullish Engulfing pattern** exists. Signal strength: **Strong**. Entry at ₹208, stop-loss at ₹189.50 (just below Day 2 low). *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the Morning Star and Evening Star candlestick patterns. How do they differ in construction and interpretation? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Morning Star:** A three-candle **bullish reversal** pattern appearing at the bottom of a downtrend. It consists of:
1. A **large bearish candle** (strong selling pressure).
2. A **small-bodied candle** (doji or spinning top) indicating indecision.
3. A **large bullish candle** that closes well into the first candle's body.

Interpretation: It signals exhaustion of sellers and entry of buyers. The middle candle's small body represents a **battle zone** between bulls and bears, with the third candle confirming bull victory.

**Evening Star:** A three-candle **bearish reversal** pattern appearing at the top of an uptrend. It consists of:
1. A **large bullish candle** (strong buying).
2. A **small-bodied candle** (indecision at the top).
3. A **large bearish candle** that closes well into the first candle's body.

Interpretation: It signals exhaustion of buyers and dominance of sellers.

**Differences:**

| Feature | Morning Star | Evening Star |
|---|---|---|
| Trend Location | Downtrend bottom | Uptrend top |
| Direction | Bullish reversal | Bearish reversal |
| First Candle | Large red | Large green |
| Third Candle | Large green | Large red |
| Signal | Buy | Sell |

*(7 marks)*

---

**(b) What is a Marubozu candle? Distinguish between Bullish and Bearish Marubozu. Given a stock's daily data — Open ₹50, High ₹50, Low ₹42, Close ₹42 — identify the type of Marubozu and compute the relevant ratios. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Marubozu** is a single candlestick pattern with **no upper or lower shadows** — only a real body. The name comes from Japanese meaning "bald" or "shaved head," signifying an absence of wicks. It indicates **extreme one-sided market sentiment**.

- **Bullish Marubozu (White/Green)**: Open = Low, Close = High → Pure buying pressure.
- **Bearish Marubozu (Black/Red)**: Open = High, Close = Low → Pure selling pressure.

**Given:** Open = ₹50, High = ₹50, Low = ₹42, Close = ₹42.

**Step 1: Determine candle direction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Close (42)} &< \text{Open (50)} \Rightarrow \text{Bearish}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Check Marubozu conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Is Open} = \text{High?} &: 50 = 50 \quad \text{✅} \\
\text{Is Close} = \text{Low?} &: 42 = 42 \quad \text{✅}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Body} &= \vert 42 - 50 \vert = 8 \text{ ₹} \\
U_s &= \text{High} - \max(\text{Open}, \text{Close}) = 50 - 50 = 0 \text{ ₹} \\
L_s &= \min(\text{Open}, \text{Close}) - \text{Low} = 42 - 42 = 0 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Range} &= 50 - 42 = 8 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{BRR} &= \frac{8}{8} = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a **Bearish Marubozu** (Black Marubozu). With BRR = 1.0 and zero shadows, it represents **maximum bearish conviction** — a strong continuation or reversal (depending on context) signal. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Color confusion:** Many students confuse green = bullish and red = bearish, which is the **Indian convention**. In Western charts (Yahoo Finance, US markets), the convention is **black/hollow = bullish** and **red/filled = bearish**. KTU expects the Indian NSE/BSE convention unless specified.
> 2. **Forgetting the trend context:** A Hammer at the top of an uptrend is a **Hanging Man** (bearish). The same shape has opposite meanings in different locations. Always state the **trend context** first.
> 3. **Body calculation errors:** Use $\vert \text{Close} - \text{Open} \vert$, not the difference without modulus. Body length is always non-negative.
> 4. **Ignoring volume:** KTU specifically expects students to mention that **volume confirmation** enhances pattern reliability. Patterns without volume backing are weaker.
> 5. **Shadow vs. body confusion:** Upper shadow = High − max(Open, Close), not just High − Close. This is a common calculation mistake worth losing 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Candlestick Patterns**

✅ **Fundamentals:**
- A candlestick captures **OHLC** (Open, High, Low, Close) in a single period.
- **Green/White = Bullish** (Close > Open); **Red/Black = Bearish** (Close < Open).
- Body = $\vert C - O \vert$; Upper shadow = $H - \max(O,C)$; Lower shadow = $\min(O,C) - L$.

✅ **Single-Candle Patterns:**
- **Doji**: Open ≈ Close → Indecision (4 sub-types: Standard, Long-Legged, Dragonfly, Gravestone).
- **Hammer**: Small body at top, long lower shadow ($\geq 2 \times$ body) at downtrend bottom → **Bullish**.
- **Hanging Man**: Same shape at uptrend top → **Bearish**.
- **Shooting Star**: Small body at bottom, long upper shadow at uptrend top → **Bearish**.
- **Inverted Hammer**: Same shape at downtrend bottom → **Bullish**.
- **Marubozu**: No shadows → Strong one-sided conviction.
- **Spinning Top**: Small body, equal long shadows → Mild indecision.

✅ **Double-Candle Patterns:**
- **Bullish Engulfing**: Small red → large green engulfing body at downtrend bottom.
- **Bearish Engulfing**: Small green → large red engulfing body at uptrend top.
- **Piercing Line**: Red → green penetrating >50% of red body.
- **Dark Cloud Cover**: Green → red penetrating >50% of green body.
- **Tweezer Tops/Bottoms**: Equal highs or equal lows respectively.

✅ **Triple-Candle Patterns:**
- **Morning Star**: Large red + small body + large green (penetration >50%) → Bullish reversal.
- **Evening Star**: Large green + small body + large red (penetration >50%) → Bearish reversal.
- **Three White Soldiers**: Three large greens in uptrend → Bullish continuation.
- **Three Black Crows**: Three large reds in downtrend → Bearish continuation.

✅ **Critical Conditions to Verify in Exams:**
1. Trend context (where the pattern appears).
2. Body size comparison (for engulfing).
3. Shadow-to-body ratio $\geq 2$ (for Hammer).
4. Penetration >50% of first body (for Morning/Evening Star).
5. Volume confirmation (enhances reliability).

✅ **Examiner's Golden Rules:**
- Always draw the candle diagram in 14-mark answers.
- State the **trend context** before identifying the pattern.
- Compute numerical values step-by-step (don't skip algebra).
- Mention **volume confirmation** as a reliability enhancer.
- Conclude with a **trade action** (entry, stop-loss, target).

✅ **KTU 2024 Weightage Insight:**
Candlestick Patterns is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 3 of PEIET745, typically carrying **15-20%** of the End Semester Exam marks, with at least one **14-mark numerical/analytical question** and one **3-mark definitional question** per exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support and Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Support and Resistance # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Support and Resistance** are the two foundational pillars of **Classical Technical Analysis** in financial markets. They represent specific price levels on a price-time chart where the collective forces of **supply (selling pressure)** and **demand (buying pressure)** historically converge to either halt or reverse the prevailing price trend. In precise KTU Board examination terminology: - **Support** is a price level at which **demand is strong enough to prevent the price from falling further**. It acts as a "floor" beneath the price action, where buyers historically step in to absorb the available supply. - **Resistance** is a price level at which **supply is strong enough to prevent the price from rising further**. It acts as a "ceiling" above the price action, where sellers historically overwhelm the buyers. - **Role Reversal (Flip)**: A well-established KTU concept where, once a support level is decisively broken, that same level tends to act as resistance in subsequent price action (and vice versa). This is also called the **Polarity Principle**. - **Strength of a Level**: Defined by the number of times the price has touched and bounced off the level, the volume of trades executed at that level, and the time duration over which the level has held. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Standard Metrics**: S&R levels are typically measured in **price points (₹/$)** and validated by the **"3-Touch Rule"** — a level touched **3 or more times** is considered structurally significant for KTU practical analyses and case-study answers. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > **Real-World Analogy: The Elevator in a Building** > > Imagine a building with an elevator going up and down. The **top floor ceiling** is like **Resistance** — no matter how many people pile in and push the "up" button, the elevator physically cannot rise past that ceiling. The **ground floor** is like **Support** — the elevator cannot fall below it because the foundation holds it up. > > - If a crowd of people (sellers) rushes in at the top floor, the ceiling (resistance) **pushes prices back down**. > - If a crowd of buyers rushes in at the ground floor, the foundation (support) **pushes prices back up**. > > Now, if the elevator crashes through the top floor (price breaks resistance) — the next ceiling above becomes the new resistance, and the old one often acts as a "supporting floor" for the price on its way back. This is exactly the **Polarity Principle (Role Reversal)** taught in KTU Module 3. A simpler, student-friendly version: > **Think of Support as a "floor mat" and Resistance as a "ceiling fan."** > Prices bounce off the floor mat and duck under the ceiling fan. The more times the ball (price) bounces off the mat, the stronger the mat becomes — until the ball breaks through, after which the mat becomes a new ceiling. --- ## 1.3 Why Support and Resistance Matter in KTU 2024 Curriculum From a **stock market trading** perspective (aligned with the PEIET745 syllabus outcomes): 1. **Entry/Exit Decisions**: Traders use S&R to decide *where* to buy, sell, or place stop-losses. 2. **Risk Management**: A broken support level signals a stop-loss trigger. 3. **Trend Confirmation**: A breakout above resistance confirms a new uptrend; a breakdown below support confirms a new downtrend. 4. **Price Targets**: Previous support becomes a price target in a breakout trade, and vice versa. > [!TIP] > **For KTU Board Answers**: Always back your answer with the phrase *"Support and resistance levels are determined by the psychological price memory of market participants and are confirmed by trading volume."* Examiners reward this exact framing. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Economic Theory The formation of Support and Resistance is rooted in three core economic and behavioural principles: ### A. Market Psychology (Behavioural Finance Foundation) - **Support forms** because at a certain low price, buyers believe the asset is **undervalued** and step in aggressively. - **Resistance forms** because at a certain high price, sellers believe the asset is **overvalued** and start distributing (selling) their holdings. - Each touch reinforces the **collective memory** of that price level among participants. ### B. Order Flow Mechanics - Above resistance, there is a large cluster of **sell stop-loss orders** and **sell limit orders** waiting. - Below support, there is a large cluster of **buy stop-loss orders** and **buy limit orders** waiting. - These resting orders act as **magnets** for the price and barriers for the trend. ### C. Round Number Effect - Traders and algorithms are psychologically drawn to **round numbers** (e.g., ₹100, ₹500, ₹1000, Nifty 18000, Bank Nifty 45000). - This creates natural S&R zones, even without fundamental justification. --- ## 2.2 Types of Support and Resistance (KTU High-Yield Classification) | # | Type | Description | KTU Keyword | |---|------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | **Horizontal (Static) S&R** | Specific price levels drawn horizontally across the chart. | "Classic floor/ceiling." | | 2 | **Trendline S&R** | Diagonal lines connecting successive higher lows (support) or lower highs (resistance). | "Dynamic S&R." | | 3 | **Moving Average S&R** | A moving average (50-DMA, 200-DMA) acts as dynamic support in uptrends and dynamic resistance in downtrends. | "Dynamic S&R." | | 4 | **Fibonacci S&R** | Key Fibonacci retracement levels (23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%, 78.6%) act as S&R. | "Golden Ratio S&R." | | 5 | **Pivot Point S&R** | Calculated using the prior session's High, Low, and Close. Formula: `PP = (H + L + C) / 3`. | "Floor Trader Pivots." | | 6 | **Volume Profile S&R** | Price levels with the highest traded volume act as S&R (HVN – High Volume Nodes). | "Institutional S&R." | | 7 | **Round Number S&R** | Psychological levels like 100, 500, 1000, 18000. | "Psychological S&R." | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations are **non-programmable mathematical models** that KTU examiners frequently test in 14-mark numerical/analytical questions. Memorize the structure, not just the answer. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables | KTU Use Case | |---|---------|---------|-----------|--------------| | 1 | **Pivot Point (PP)** | $PP = \frac{H + L + C}{3}$ | H = Prior High, L = Prior Low, C = Prior Close | Floor pivot for day traders | | 2 | **First Resistance (R1)** | $R1 = 2 \cdot PP - L$ | Derived from PP and L | First intraday ceiling | | 3 | **First Support (S1)** | $S1 = 2 \cdot PP - H$ | Derived from PP and H | First intraday floor | | 4 | **Second Resistance (R2)** | $R2 = PP + (H - L)$ | Range added to PP | Stronger intraday ceiling | | 5 | **Second Support (S2)** | $S2 = PP - (H - L)$ | Range subtracted from PP | Stronger intraday floor | | 6 | **Third Resistance (R3)** | $R3 = H + 2 \cdot (PP - L)$ | Extended pivot calc | Breakout target | | 7 | **Third Support (S3)** | $S3 = L - 2 \cdot (H - PP)$ | Extended pivot calc | Breakdown target | | 8 | **Fibonacci Retracement** | $L_{fib} = High - k \cdot (High - Low)$ | $k \in \{0.236, 0.382, 0.5, 0.618, 0.786\}$ | Identifying S&R in a pullback | | 9 | **Percentage Retracement** | $L_{pct} = High - r \cdot (High - Low)$ | $r \in \{0.25, 0.33, 0.5, 0.66, 0.75\}$ | Classical Dow retracements | | 10 | **Breakout Strength Ratio** | $BSR = \frac{V_{breakout}}{V_{avg,20}}$ | $V_{breakout}$ = breakout candle volume; $V_{avg,20}$ = 20-day average volume | Validates true vs false breakout | | 11 | **Support Reliability Score** | $SRS = w_1 \cdot T + w_2 \cdot V_{norm} + w_3 \cdot \Delta t$ | $T$ = touches, $V_{norm}$ = normalized volume, $\Delta t$ = time held | Custom scoring model | | 12 | **Risk per Trade at S/R** | $RPT = \vert Entry - SL \vert \cdot Q$ | $SL$ = stop-loss at S/R, $Q$ = quantity | Position sizing | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Rule Reminder**: In the table above, the absolute value is written as `\vert` (e.g., $\vert Entry - SL \vert$) — never use the bare vertical pipe `|` symbol, as it would break the markdown table. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Stock Markets | Domain | Application of S&R | |--------|-------------------| | **Intraday Trading** | Pivot Point S1/R1 used by 80% of Indian retail day traders for Bank Nifty & Nifty. | | **Swing Trading** | Horizontal S&R + 50-DMA/200-DMA confluence identifies swing entry zones. | | **Options Selling** | Option sellers write puts near support and calls near resistance (high probability setups). | | **Algorithmic Trading** | Quant funds code breakout/breakdown bots that trigger on S&R breaks with volume confirmation. | | **Portfolio Management** | Fund managers place rebalancing orders near institutional S&R levels (Volume Profile HVN). | | **Risk Management** | Stop-loss always placed **just below support** (long trade) or **just above resistance** (short trade). | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Hot Topic Alert**: A 14-mark question may ask you to compute **all 7 pivot points** given prior session OHLC data, and then explain how to trade using them. Practice this once — it is a guaranteed scoring question. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Pivot Point System (KTU 14-Mark Standard) ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup > Given the prior trading session's data for **Nifty 50**: > - Prior High: $H = 18{,}250$ > - Prior Low: $L = 18{,}050$ > - Prior Close: $C = 18{,}180$ > > **Compute the Floor Pivot Point (PP), S1, R1, S2, R2, S3, R3, and identify the likely intraday trading range.** --- ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation **Step 1: Compute the Floor Pivot Point (PP).** The classical floor pivot formula is the arithmetic mean of the prior period's High, Low, and Close. $$ PP = \frac{H + L + C}{3} $$ Substitute the values: $$ PP = \frac{18{,}250 + 18{,}050 + 18{,}180}{3} $$ Compute the numerator: $$ H + L + C = 18{,}250 + 18{,}050 + 18{,}180 = 54{,}480 $$ Divide by 3: $$ PP = \frac{54{,}480}{3} = 18{,}160 $$ > **Logic**: The pivot point is the central equilibrium price of the prior session. Prices above it are bullish-bias; prices below it are bearish-bias. --- **Step 2: Compute the First Support (S1) and First Resistance (R1).** The formulas for S1 and R1 are mirror images — derived by reflecting the pivot across the prior high and prior low. $$ R1 = 2 \cdot PP - L $$ Substitute: $$ R1 = 2 \cdot 18{,}160 - 18{,}050 $$ $$ R1 = 36{,}320 - 18{,}050 = 18{,}270 $$ $$ S1 = 2 \cdot PP - H $$ Substitute: $$ S1 = 2 \cdot 18{,}160 - 18{,}250 $$ $$ S1 = 36{,}320 - 18{,}250 = 18{,}070 $$ > **Logic**: R1 is the first intraday ceiling, S1 is the first intraday floor. About 70% of the time, the price oscillates between S1 and R1. --- **Step 3: Compute the Second Support (S2) and Second Resistance (R2).** These add the prior session's range (H − L) to the pivot. $$ R2 = PP + (H - L) $$ Compute the range first: $$ H - L = 18{,}250 - 18{,}050 = 200 $$ Then: $$ R2 = 18{,}160 + 200 = 18{,}360 $$ $$ S2 = PP - (H - L) $$ $$ S2 = 18{,}160 - 200 = 17{,}960 $$ > **Logic**: S2 and R2 are wider bands — used for trending-day trades. A break above R1 with volume targets R2. --- **Step 4: Compute the Third Support (S3) and Third Resistance (R3).** These are derived using extended-range logic. $$ R3 = H + 2 \cdot (PP - L) $$ Compute the inner term first: $$ PP - L = 18{,}160 - 18{,}050 = 110 $$ $$ 2 \cdot (PP - L) = 220 $$ $$ R3 = 18{,}250 + 220 = 18{,}470 $$ $$ S3 = L - 2 \cdot (H - PP) $$ Compute the inner term: $$ H - PP = 18{,}250 - 18{,}160 = 90 $$ $$ 2 \cdot (H - PP) = 180 $$ $$ S3 = 18{,}050 - 180 = 17{,}870 $$ --- ### 3.1.3 Final Compiled Result Table | Level | Value (Nifty Points) | Type | Typical Use | |-------|---------------------|------|-------------| | R3 | **18,470** | Resistance | Extended breakout target | | R2 | **18,360** | Resistance | Intraday trend target | | R1 | **18,270** | Resistance | First intraday ceiling | | **PP** | **18,160** | Pivot | Equilibrium / bias line | | S1 | **18,070** | Support | First intraday floor | | S2 | **17,960** | Support | Intraday trend target | | S3 | **17,870** | Support | Extended breakdown target | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Insight**: If the KTU 2024 question gives 4 days' data, the student should use **Camarilla pivots** instead. The 3-day/weekly pivot uses the simpler formula above. Always check the question's wording carefully. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Fibonacci Retracement Support ### Problem > A stock rallied from **₹500 (swing low)** to **₹800 (swing high)**. Compute the **38.2%, 50%, and 61.8% Fibonacci retracement** levels that will act as support in a pullback. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1**: Compute the swing range (R). $$ R = High - Low = 800 - 500 = 300 $$ **Step 2**: Apply the Fibonacci retracement formula. $$ L_{fib} = High - k \cdot (High - Low) = High - k \cdot R $$ **For 38.2% retracement**: $$ L_{38.2} = 800 - 0.382 \cdot 300 $$ $$ 0.382 \cdot 300 = 114.6 $$ $$ L_{38.2} = 800 - 114.6 = 685.4 $$ **For 50% retracement**: $$ L_{50} = 800 - 0.5 \cdot 300 $$ $$ 0.5 \cdot 300 = 150 $$ $$ L_{50} = 800 - 150 = 650 $$ **For 61.8% retracement** (the "Golden Ratio" support): $$ L_{61.8} = 800 - 0.618 \cdot 300 $$ $$ 0.618 \cdot 300 = 185.4 $$ $$ L_{61.8} = 800 - 185.4 = 614.6 $$ ### Final Fibonacci Support Levels | Fibonacci % | Computed Support (₹) | KTU Significance | |-------------|---------------------|------------------| | **38.2%** | **₹685.40** | Shallow pullback — strong trend | | **50.0%** | **₹650.00** | Halfway pullback — moderate trend | | **61.8%** | **₹614.60** | Golden Ratio — **strongest S/R** in Fibonacci theory | --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: True vs False Breakout Detection > **KTU 14-Mark Application Question**: *Differentiate between a true breakout and a false breakout at a resistance level. List four validation criteria.* | # | Validation Criterion | True Breakout | False Breakout (Fakeout) | |---|---------------------|---------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Volume Confirmation** | Breakout candle volume $\geq 1.5 \times$ 20-day average volume. | Volume is below or equal to 20-day average. | | 2 | **Candle Body Close** | Price closes **above** resistance (not just the wick). | Price wicks above but closes back below. | | 3 | **Retest Behaviour** | Price retests the broken level as new support and bounces. | Price immediately reverses and re-enters the prior range. | | 4 | **Timeframe Alignment** | Breakout aligned on both intraday and daily chart. | Only on lower timeframe; daily chart still inside range. | | 5 | **RSI/MACD Confirmation** | RSI above 60 and rising; MACD positive crossover. | RSI/MACD show divergence or no confirmation. | | 6 | **Gap Behaviour** | A clean gap above resistance on opening. | No gap; price slips back in the same session. | > [!TIP] > **Quantitative Formula for Validating a Breakout**: > > $$\text{True Breakout if } \left( \frac{V_{breakout}}{V_{avg,20}} \geq 1.5 \right) \land \left( \text{Close} > R_{level} \right) \land \left( \text{RSI}_{14} > 60 \right)$$ > > Memorize this logical AND-condition — it scores full marks in KTU analytical questions. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map: The Support and Resistance Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Price Action on Chart] --> B{Is Price Trending?} B -- Yes, Uptrend --> C[Higher Highs + Higher Lows] B -- Yes, Downtrend --> D[Lower Highs + Lower Lows] B -- No, Sideways --> E[Range-Bound Market] C --> F[Trendline Support
drawn under Higher Lows] D --> G[Trendline Resistance
drawn over Lower Highs] E --> H[Horizontal Support
Range Floor] E --> I[Horizontal Resistance
Range Ceiling] F --> J{Does Price Break
the Trendline?} G --> K{Does Price Break
the Trendline?} H --> L{Does Price Break
the Range Floor?} I --> M{Does Price Break
the Range Ceiling?} J -- No --> N[Trendline Support Holds
Bullish Continuation] J -- Yes --> O[Role Reversal
Trendline Becomes Resistance] K -- No --> P[Trendline Resistance Holds
Bearish Continuation] K -- Yes --> Q[Role Reversal
Trendline Becomes Support] L -- No --> R[Horizontal Support Holds
Bounce / Reversal Up] L -- Yes --> S[Breakdown Confirmed
New Downtrend Initiates] M -- No --> T[Horizontal Resistance Holds
Rejection / Reversal Down] M -- Yes --> U[Breakout Confirmed
New Uptrend Initiates] S --> V[Polarity Principle
Old Support becomes New Resistance] U --> X[Polarity Principle
Old Resistance becomes New Support] style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff style B fill:#c97a3a,color:#ffffff style C fill:#2d6a4f,color:#ffffff style D fill:#9d0208,color:#ffffff style E fill:#6c757d,color:#ffffff style F fill:#52b788,color:#000000 style G fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff style H fill:#52b788,color:#000000 style I fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff style V fill:#ffb703,color:#000000 style X fill:#ffb703,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: How a Trader Uses S&R (Decision Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Trader Opens Chart] --> B[Identify S and R Levels] B --> C[Plot 3 Layers of S and R] C --> D[Layer 1: Horizontal S and R] C --> E[Layer 2: Trendlines] C --> F[Layer 3: Moving Averages and Pivots] D --> G[Mark High Probability Zones] E --> G F --> G G --> H{Where is Price
Relative to S and R?} H -- Near Support --> I[Look for Bullish Confirmation] H -- Near Resistance --> J[Look for Bearish Confirmation] H -- Mid Range --> K[Wait for Price to Reach Extremes] I --> L[Place Buy Order] J --> M[Place Sell Order] K --> N[No Trade Zone] L --> O[Stop Loss Just Below Support] M --> P[Stop Loss Just Above Resistance] O --> Q[Target Next Resistance Level] P --> R[Target Next Support Level] Q --> S[Manage Trade with Trailing Stop] R --> S style A fill:#264653,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff style C fill:#e9c46a,color:#000000 style G fill:#f4a261,color:#000000 style H fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff style L fill:#06d6a0,color:#000000 style M fill:#ef476f,color:#ffffff style N fill:#6c757d,color:#ffffff style S fill:#118ab2,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic: Role Reversal (Polarity Principle) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Phase1["PHASE 1: Initial Range"] A1[Price Oscillates] --> A2[Resistance at 1000] A1 --> A3[Support at 900] end subgraph Phase2["PHASE 2: Breakdown"] A3 --> B1[Price Breaks Below 900] B1 --> B2[900 becomes New Resistance] B1 --> B3[Price Falls to 850] end subgraph Phase3["PHASE 3: Polarity Confirmed"] B2 --> C1[Price Pulls Back to 900] C1 --> C2[900 Rejects Price Downward] C2 --> C3[Old Support is now New Resistance] end style A2 fill:#ef476f,color:#ffffff style A3 fill:#06d6a0,color:#000000 style B2 fill:#ffb703,color:#000000 style C3 fill:#ffb703,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic: S&R Confluence Zone (High-Probability Trade Zone) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Multiple S and R Signals] --> B{Horizontal S/R Match?} B -- Yes --> C[+1 Point] B -- No --> D[+0 Point] A --> E{Trendline Touches?} E -- Yes --> F[+1 Point] E -- No --> G[+0 Point] A --> H{Fibonacci Level Match?} H -- Yes --> I[+1 Point] H -- No --> J[+0 Point] A --> K{50-DMA or 200-DMA Match?} K -- Yes --> L[+1 Point] K -- No --> M[+0 Point] A --> N{Round Number Match?} N -- Yes --> O[+1 Point] N -- No --> P[+0 Point] C --> Q[Total Confluence Score] F --> Q I --> Q L --> Q O --> Q Q --> R{Score Greater
than or Equal to 3?} R -- Yes --> S[Strong Confluence Zone
High Probability Trade] R -- No --> T[Weak Zone
Avoid or Use Small Size] style S fill:#06d6a0,color:#000000 style T fill:#ef476f,color:#ffffff style Q fill:#ffb703,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A consists of short-answer questions worth **2 to 3 marks** testing direct recall and definition-level understanding (Bloom's Level 1 & 2: Remember, Understand). --- ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the terms "Support" and "Resistance" in technical analysis. Mention the polarity principle in one line.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Support** is a price level on a chart at which the demand for a security is strong enough to halt its decline, acting as a floor preventing further fall. > > **Resistance** is a price level at which the supply of a security is strong enough to halt its rise, acting as a ceiling preventing further increase. > > **Polarity Principle (Role Reversal)**: Once a support level is broken decisively, it tends to act as resistance in subsequent price movements, and vice versa. *(1 mark for the principle line)* *(Awarded 1 mark each for Support, Resistance definitions; 1 mark for polarity)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any four types of support and resistance levels used in classical technical analysis.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Horizontal Support and Resistance** — fixed price levels marked across the chart. 2. **Trendline Support and Resistance** — diagonal lines connecting higher lows or lower highs. 3. **Moving Average Based S&R** — 50-DMA, 100-DMA, 200-DMA acting as dynamic support/resistance. 4. **Fibonacci Retracement Levels** — 23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%, 78.6% as S&R. *(Plus optional 5th: Pivot Point S&R, Round Number S&R, Volume Profile HVN)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Choice Questions > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 internal choice questions carry **14 marks** each with sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Both choices must be attempted with full model solutions. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Define Support and Resistance. With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **Polarity Principle (Role Reversal)** using any suitable market example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compute the **Floor Pivot Point, S1, R1, S2, R2, S3, and R3** for a stock given the prior session's data: $H = ₹1{,}250$, $L = ₹1{,}150$, $C = ₹1{,}200$. Identify the intraday trading range and list two trading strategies using these levels. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) **Definition of Support and Resistance** *(2 marks)*: Support is a price floor where demand overcomes supply, preventing further decline. Resistance is a price ceiling where supply overcomes demand, preventing further rise. **Polarity Principle Explanation** *(3 marks)*: The Polarity Principle, also called the **Role Reversal Principle**, states that once a price level that acted as support is decisively broken, it tends to act as resistance in future price action. Conversely, a broken resistance level becomes support. **Diagram / Example** *(2 marks)*: ``` Price ↑ │ ╱╲ ╱╲ (Old Resistance) │ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ←→ becomes Support after breakout │ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ │ ╱ ╲╱ ╲ ← Breakout Point │╱ ╲ │ ╲ ╲ │ ╲ ╲ │ ╲ ╲ (New Resistance = Old Support) └─────────────────────────→ Time ``` **Example**: A stock oscillates between ₹100 (Support) and ₹120 (Resistance) for 3 weeks. When the price breaks below ₹100, that level becomes a resistance ceiling. A pullback to ₹100 is rejected, and the price falls to ₹85, confirming the polarity. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **Given**: $H = 1250$, $L = 1150$, $C = 1200$. **Step 1: Pivot Point** *(1 mark)*: $$ PP = \frac{H + L + C}{3} = \frac{1250 + 1150 + 1200}{3} = \frac{3600}{3} = 1200 $$ **Step 2: R1 and S1** *(2 marks)*: $$ R1 = 2 \cdot PP - L = 2 \cdot 1200 - 1150 = 1250 $$ $$ S1 = 2 \cdot PP - H = 2 \cdot 1200 - 1250 = 1150 $$ **Step 3: R2 and S2** *(2 marks)*: $$ R2 = PP + (H - L) = 1200 + (1250 - 1150) = 1300 $$ $$ S2 = PP - (H - L) = 1200 - 100 = 1100 $$ **Step 4: R3 and S3** *(1 mark)*: $$ R3 = H + 2 \cdot (PP - L) = 1250 + 2 \cdot 50 = 1350 $$ $$ S3 = L - 2 \cdot (H - PP) = 1150 - 2 \cdot 50 = 1050 $$ **Compiled Result Table** *(0.5 mark)*: | Level | Price (₹) | Type | |-------|-----------|------| | R3 | 1350 | Extended Resistance | | R2 | 1300 | Second Resistance | | R1 | 1250 | First Resistance | | **PP** | **1200** | Pivot | | S1 | 1150 | First Support | | S2 | 1100 | Second Support | | S3 | 1050 | Extended Support | **Two Trading Strategies** *(0.5 mark)*: 1. **Range Trading Strategy**: Buy near S1 (₹1150) with stop-loss at S2 (₹1100) and target at PP (₹1200) or R1 (₹1250). Sell near R1 with stop-loss above R2 (₹1300). 2. **Breakout Strategy**: A decisive close above R1 (₹1250) with 1.5× average volume triggers a long entry with target at R2 (₹1300) and stop-loss below PP (₹1200). A breakdown below S1 (₹1150) with volume triggers a short entry targeting S2 (₹1100). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain any **five types of support and resistance** used in technical analysis with one-line examples each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A stock moves from a swing low of **₹400** to a swing high of **₹700**. Compute the **38.2%, 50%, and 61.8% Fibonacci retracement** levels. Explain how a trader would use the **61.8% level** as a high-probability support zone with **volume confirmation**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **1. Horizontal S&R** *(1.4 marks)*: A fixed price level marked horizontally on the chart, formed by multiple swing highs (resistance) or swing lows (support). Example: Nifty 50 facing resistance at 18,000 for 5 sessions. **2. Trendline S&R** *(1.4 marks)*: A diagonal line connecting a series of higher lows (support) or lower highs (resistance). Example: An uptrend line drawn under three successive higher lows acts as dynamic support. **3. Moving Average S&R** *(1.4 marks)*: 50-DMA, 100-DMA, and 200-DMA act as dynamic support in uptrends and resistance in downtrends. Example: Reliance 50-DMA at ₹2,800 acting as support in an uptrend. **4. Fibonacci S&R** *(1.4 marks)*: Key Fibonacci retracement levels (23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%, 78.6%) projected from a swing high to swing low. Example: A stock retracing 61.8% of its prior rally finds support. **5. Pivot Point S&R** *(1.4 marks)*: Calculated using prior session's High, Low, Close: $PP = (H + L + C)/3$. Used by intraday traders to identify S1, R1, S2, R2. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Given**: Swing Low = ₹400, Swing High = ₹700. **Step 1: Compute the Range** *(0.5 mark)*: $$ R = High - Low = 700 - 400 = 300 $$ **Step 2: Compute Fibonacci Levels** *(2 marks)*: $$ L_{38.2} = 700 - 0.382 \cdot 300 = 700 - 114.6 = ₹585.40 $$ $$ L_{50.0} = 700 - 0.5 \cdot 300 = 700 - 150 = ₹550.00 $$ $$ L_{61.8} = 700 - 0.618 \cdot 300 = 700 - 185.4 = ₹514.60 $$ **Compiled Fibonacci Table** *(1.5 marks)*: | Fib % | Level (₹) | Significance | |-------|-----------|--------------| | 38.2% | 585.40 | Shallow pullback | | 50.0% | 550.00 | Halfway support | | 61.8% | 514.60 | **Golden Ratio — strongest S/R** | **61.8% as High-Probability Support with Volume Confirmation** *(3 marks)*: A trader would treat the **₹514.60 level (61.8%)** as a high-probability buying zone because: 1. **Fibonacci Golden Ratio Significance**: The 61.8% retracement is statistically the deepest "healthy" pullback in a trending market. A bounce from this level suggests the prior trend is intact. 2. **Volume Confirmation Rule** *(with formula)*: The trader waits for the price to touch ₹514.60 and observes whether the **bullish reversal candle's volume** meets the following condition: $$ \frac{V_{reversal}}{V_{avg,20}} \geq 1.5 $$ If yes → **confirmed high-probability support**; the trader initiates a long position with stop-loss just below ₹510 and targets the previous swing high (₹700). 3. **Entry Trigger**: A bullish engulfing or hammer candlestick pattern at ₹514.60 with above-average volume acts as the entry signal. The trade is invalidated if the closing price falls below ₹510 on a daily basis (i.e., a clean break of the 61.8% level). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Units** *(−1 mark)*: Always write "₹/points" or "in price units" — never leave the answer dimensionless. > 2. **Skipping the Logic** *(−2 marks)*: For a 7-mark numerical, the examiner expects **formula → substitution → computation → interpretation**. A bare final answer without the intermediate step is marked down heavily. > 3. **Ignoring the Polarity Principle** *(−1 mark)*: In any S/R theory question, ending the answer without mentioning the Polarity Principle is considered an incomplete answer. > 4. **Mixing up R1 and S1 formulas** *(−1 mark)*: R1 is calculated as $2 \cdot PP - L$ (using the LOW), and S1 is calculated as $2 \cdot PP - H$ (using the HIGH). Reversing these is a classic error. > 5. **Not mentioning Volume Confirmation** *(−1 mark)*: Any breakout/breakdown answer without a volume filter loses the "application" mark. > 6. **Using `|` (pipe) in math** *(−0.5 mark formatting penalty)*: Use `\vert` for absolute value in LaTeX to avoid markdown table breaks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for the Day Before Exam):** - ✅ **Support** = demand floor; **Resistance** = supply ceiling. - ✅ The **3-Touch Rule**: A level touched 3+ times is structurally significant. - ✅ **Polarity Principle (Role Reversal)**: Broken support → new resistance; broken resistance → new support. - ✅ **7 Pivot Point Levels**: PP, S1, S2, S3, R1, R2, R3 — must memorize all 7 formulas. - ✅ **Master Formulas**: - $PP = (H + L + C) / 3$ - $R1 = 2 \cdot PP - L$ - $S1 = 2 \cdot PP - H$ - $R2 = PP + (H - L)$ - $S2 = PP - (H - L)$ - $R3 = H + 2 \cdot (PP - L)$ - $S3 = L - 2 \cdot (H - PP)$ - ✅ **Fibonacci S&R**: 23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%, 78.6% — **61.8% is the Golden Ratio**. - ✅ **True Breakout Validation** (AND-condition): Volume $\geq 1.5 \times$ average AND Close above level AND RSI > 60. - ✅ **7 Types of S&R**: Horizontal, Trendline, Moving Average, Fibonacci, Pivot Point, Volume Profile, Round Number. - ✅ **Confluence Zone** = Multiple S&R signals overlapping at one price → high-probability trade zone. - ✅ **Stop-Loss Rule**: Always place SL just beyond the S/R level (1–2% buffer). - ✅ **Round Number Effect**: ₹100, ₹500, ₹1000, Nifty 18,000, Bank Nifty 45,000 are natural S&R. - ✅ **Examiner's favorite question**: "Compute all 7 pivot points and explain two trading strategies" — practice once. - ✅ **Always mention volume** in any S/R application answer. - ✅ **Always end with Polarity Principle** in any S/R theory answer.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bearish Candlestick Patterns — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Bearish Candlestick Pattern* is a recognized sequence of one, two, or three Japanese candlesticks plotted on a price-time chart that visually and mathematically indicates a high probability of a **downward reversal** or **continuation of a prevailing downtrend** in the underlying asset's market price.

These patterns are **trend-confirmation tools** used in **Technical Analysis (TA)** under the broader domain of **Price Action Trading**. They form at the **exhaustion of buying pressure** and the **emergence of selling pressure**.

**Key Terminology:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Open (O)** | Price at the start of the candle's time period |
| **Close (C)** | Price at the end of the candle's time period |
| **High (H)** | Maximum price during the period |
| **Low (L)** | Minimum price during the period |
| **Real Body** | The thick rectangle between Open and Close |
| **Upper Shadow** | Thin line from body top to High |
| **Lower Shadow** | Thin line from body bottom to Low |
| **Bullish Candle** | Close > Open (typically green/white) |
| **Bearish Candle** | Close < Open (typically red/black) |

---

## 1.2 Neutral Candlestick Patterns — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Neutral Candlestick Pattern* (also called an *Indecision Pattern*) is a candlestick formation that visually represents a state of **equilibrium between buyers and sellers**. Neither bulls nor bears have dominant control, suggesting that the prior trend may **pause, consolidate, or reverse**.

The hallmark of neutral patterns is a **very small real body** (often a Doji) and/or **long upper and lower shadows** of approximately equal length, indicating high volatility without directional commitment.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🎭 The Auction Room Analogy

Imagine a **town auction** where an antique is being sold:
- A **bullish candle** is like a buyer who keeps raising their paddle confidently — they win the item at a high price.
- A **bearish candle** is like a buyer who suddenly realizes the price is too high, lowers their paddle, and the item sells cheap.
- A **neutral candle (Doji)** is like a moment when the auctioneer asks for bids and **no one raises their paddle** — buyers and sellers are staring at each other in silence. The market is "thinking."

> A **Bearish Pattern** at a market top is analogous to a crowd that was cheering loudly, and suddenly **one loud boo** is heard. The cheering stops, and silence (or panic) follows.

A **Neutral Pattern** in the middle of a trend is like a **traffic signal turning yellow** — drivers slow down, unsure whether to stop or go. The market is pausing for a decision.

---

## 1.4 The Three Categories of Candlestick Context

Every candlestick pattern's signal depends on its **location on the chart**:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = f(\text{Pattern}, \text{Context}, \text{Volume})$$

1. **At a Market Top (after uptrend)** → Bearish reversal signal
2. **At a Market Bottom (after downtrend)** → Bullish reversal signal
3. **In the Middle of a Trend** → Continuation or neutral signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** A candlestick pattern **in isolation** has no predictive power. Its validity comes from the **trend context (location)** and **volume confirmation**.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Bearish and Neutral Patterns

### 🔴 A. Single-Candle Bearish Patterns
These patterns consist of **only one candle** and signal weakness at a market top.

#### 1. **Shooting Star**
- **Shape:** Small body at the bottom, long upper shadow (≥ 2× body), little/no lower shadow
- **Location:** After an uptrend (at resistance)
- **Psychology:** Buyers pushed price up, but sellers slammed it back down — rejection
- **Confirmation:** Next candle closes below the Shooting Star's body

$$\text{Upper Shadow} \geq 2 \times \vert \text{Body} \vert$$
$$\text{Lower Shadow} \approx 0$$

#### 2. **Hanging Man**
- **Shape:** Small body at the top, long lower shadow (≥ 2× body), little/no upper shadow
- **Location:** After an uptrend (looks like a Hammer but is BEARISH here)
- **Psychology:** Sellers tested lower prices during the session, recovered slightly, but the trend is exhausted
- **Confirmation:** Next candle closes below the Hanging Man's body

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A Hanging Man has the **same shape as a Hammer**. The ONLY difference is **location** — Hanging Man appears at tops, Hammer at bottoms.

#### 3. **Gravestone Doji (Neutral → Bearish at tops)**
- **Shape:** Open and Close at the Low, long upper shadow
- **Body length = 0**
- **Psychology:** Bulls attempted a rally, completely rejected by bears

---

### 🔴 B. Two-Candle Bearish Patterns

#### 4. **Bearish Engulfing Pattern**
- **Shape:** A small bullish candle followed by a larger bearish candle that **completely engulfs** the previous body
- **Mathematical Condition:**
$$C_2 < O_1 \quad \text{AND} \quad O_2 > C_1 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Body}_2 > \text{Body}_1$$

Where subscripts 1 and 2 represent Day 1 and Day 2.
- **Psychology:** Buyers were in control on Day 1; Day 2 opens higher (trap), then sells off massively below Day 1's open.

#### 5. **Dark Cloud Cover**
- **Shape:** Bullish candle followed by a bearish candle that **opens above** the prior high but **closes below** the midpoint of the prior body
- **Mathematical Condition:**
$$O_2 > H_1 \quad \text{AND} \quad C_2 < \frac{(O_1 + C_1)}{2} \quad \text{AND} \quad C_2 > O_1$$
- **Psychology:** Gap up, then massive rejection — a "cloud" covers the prior bullishness.

#### 6. **Tweezer Tops**
- **Shape:** Two (or more) candles with **approximately equal highs**
- **Condition:** $\vert H_1 - H_2 \vert \leq \epsilon$ (a small tolerance)
- **Psychology:** The market tested the same resistance level twice and failed both times.

---

### 🔴 C. Three-Candle Bearish Patterns

#### 7. **Evening Star**
- **Shape:** Large bullish candle → small-bodied candle (or Doji) **gapping up** → large bearish candle closing well into the first candle's body
- **Mathematical Conditions:**
$$O_2 > C_1 \quad \text{(gap up)}$$
$$C_3 < \frac{(O_1 + C_1)}{2}$$
- **Psychology:** Buyers exhausted, momentum lost, sellers take over.

#### 8. **Evening Doji Star**
- A **more powerful** version of Evening Star where the middle candle is a **Doji** (maximum indecision).

#### 9. **Three Black Crows**
- **Shape:** Three consecutive long bearish candles, each opening within the previous body and closing near the low
- **Condition:**
$$C_i < O_i \quad \text{for } i = 1,2,3$$
$$O_{i+1} \in [\text{Low}_i, \text{High}_i]$$
- **Psychology:** Sustained, methodical selling — institutional distribution.

---

### ⚪ D. Neutral (Indecision) Patterns

#### 10. **Doji (Family)**
- **Definition:** $\vert C - O \vert \approx 0$ (Open ≈ Close)
- **Variants:**
  - **Standard Doji:** Open = Close, shadows on both sides
  - **Long-Legged Doji:** Long upper and lower shadows
  - **Gravestone Doji:** Long upper shadow, no lower shadow
  - **Dragonfly Doji:** Long lower shadow, no upper shadow

> [!NOTE]
> A Doji is **neutral by itself** but becomes **bearish at tops** and **bullish at bottoms**.

#### 11. **Spinning Top**
- **Shape:** Small body with upper and lower shadows **both longer than the body**
$$\text{Body} < \text{Upper Shadow} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Body} < \text{Lower Shadow}$$
- **Psychology:** Indecision, but with volatility — battle ongoing.

#### 12. **High Wave Candle**
- Extreme version of Spinning Top with **very long shadows** (≥ 3× body)
- Indicates extreme confusion in the market.

#### 13. **Tri-Star**
- **Shape:** Three consecutive Dojis in a row
- **Psychology:** Extended equilibrium, often signals a major reversal.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM QUICK-REFERENCE TABLE — Memorize These Conditions**

| Pattern | Type | Mathematical Condition | Location | Confirmation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shooting Star** | Bearish (1) | $\text{Upper Shadow} \geq 2 \cdot \vert \text{Body} \vert$ | Uptrend top | Next close < body low |
| **Hanging Man** | Bearish (1) | $\text{Lower Shadow} \geq 2 \cdot \vert \text{Body} \vert$ | Uptrend top | Next close < body low |
| **Gravestone Doji** | Neutral/Bearish | $C = O$ at Low, $H - L$ large | Uptrend top | Bearish follow-through |
| **Bearish Engulfing** | Bearish (2) | $C_2 < O_1$, $O_2 > C_1$, Body$_2$ > Body$_1$ | Uptrend top | None needed |
| **Dark Cloud Cover** | Bearish (2) | $O_2 > H_1$, $C_2 < \frac{O_1 + C_1}{2}$ | Uptrend top | None needed |
| **Tweezer Tops** | Bearish (2) | $\vert H_1 - H_2 \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Uptrend top | Bearish next candle |
| **Evening Star** | Bearish (3) | Gap up small body, $C_3 < \text{mid}(O_1, C_1)$ | Uptrend top | None needed |
| **Three Black Crows** | Bearish (3) | 3 long red, each opens in prior body | Uptrend top | None needed |
| **Standard Doji** | Neutral | $\vert C - O \vert \approx 0$ | Anywhere | Context-dependent |
| **Spinning Top** | Neutral | Body $<$ both shadows | Anywhere | Trend weakness |
| **Long-Legged Doji** | Neutral | Both shadows $\geq 2 \cdot \text{Body}$ | Anywhere | Reversal signal |
| **Tri-Star** | Neutral/Bearish | 3 consecutive Dojis | Top or bottom | Major reversal |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Financial Utility

- **Algorithmic Trading:** Quantitative analysts (Quants) encode these patterns as **rule-based triggers** in automated trading bots using languages like Python (Pandas/TA-Lib).
- **Risk Management:** Identifying bearish patterns allows portfolio managers to **hedge positions** using options (put options) before a drawdown.
- **Backtesting:** These patterns are tested against historical OHLC data to compute **win-rates and risk-reward ratios**.
- **Signal Processing Analogy:** Candlesticks function as **discrete-time signal detectors**, where each candle is a sample and patterns are matched filters.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Identifying a Bearish Engulfing Pattern

### 📊 Given Data (Hypothetical Stock XYZ)
| Day | Open (O) | High (H) | Low (L) | Close (C) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Day 1 | 100 | 108 | 99 | 107 | Bullish (green) |
| Day 2 | 109 | 110 | 95 | 96 | Bearish (red) |

### 🔍 Step-by-Step Verification

**Step 1: Compute the Real Body of Day 1**
$$\text{Body}_1 = C_1 - O_1 = 107 - 100 = +7$$
Since $C_1 > O_1$, Day 1 is **bullish** ✓

**Step 2: Compute the Real Body of Day 2**
$$\text{Body}_2 = C_2 - O_2 = 96 - 109 = -13$$
Since $C_2 < O_2$, Day 2 is **bearish** ✓

**Step 3: Check the Engulfing Condition 1 — Day 2 opens above Day 1's close**
$$O_2 = 109 \quad \text{vs} \quad C_1 = 107$$
$$O_2 > C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 109 > 107 \quad ✓$$

**Step 4: Check the Engulfing Condition 2 — Day 2 closes below Day 1's open**
$$C_2 = 96 \quad \text{vs} \quad O_1 = 100$$
$$C_2 < O_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 96 < 100 \quad ✓$$

**Step 5: Check the Size Condition — Day 2's body is larger than Day 1's body**
$$\vert \text{Body}_2 \vert = 13 \quad \text{vs} \quad \vert \text{Body}_1 \vert = 7$$
$$13 > 7 \quad ✓$$

**Conclusion:** All three conditions are satisfied. This is a **confirmed Bearish Engulfing Pattern** — strong sell signal at a market top.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Identifying an Evening Star

### 📊 Given Data
| Day | Open (O) | High (H) | Low (L) | Close (C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Day 1 | 50 | 60 | 49 | 59 |
| Day 2 | 62 | 63 | 58 | 62.5 |
| Day 3 | 62 | 62 | 51 | 52 |

### 🔍 Step-by-Step Verification

**Step 1: Day 1 — Should be a tall bullish candle**
$$\text{Body}_1 = 59 - 50 = 9 \quad (\text{large bullish}) \quad ✓$$

**Step 2: Day 2 — Should gap up and have a small body**
$$O_2 = 62, \quad C_1 = 59$$
$$\text{Gap} = O_2 - C_1 = 62 - 59 = 3 > 0 \quad ✓ \text{ (gap up)}$$
$$\text{Body}_2 = 62.5 - 62 = 0.5 \quad (\text{small body — indecision}) \quad ✓$$

**Step 3: Day 3 — Should be bearish and close below midpoint of Day 1's body**
$$\text{Midpoint of Day 1 body} = \frac{O_1 + C_1}{2} = \frac{50 + 59}{2} = 54.5$$
$$C_3 = 52 \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{midpoint} = 54.5$$
$$C_3 < 54.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 52 < 54.5 \quad ✓$$

**Conclusion:** All conditions satisfied. This is a **confirmed Evening Star** — strong 3-candle bearish reversal pattern.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Identifying a Doji and Classifying It

### 📊 Given Data
| Pattern | Open (O) | High (H) | Low (L) | Close (C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Candle A | 200 | 215 | 198 | 200.5 |
| Candle B | 100 | 110 | 90 | 100.2 |
| Candle C | 50 | 51 | 49 | 50.0 |

### 🔍 Classification

**Candle A:**
$$\text{Body} = \vert 200.5 - 200 \vert = 0.5$$
$$\text{Total Range} = 215 - 198 = 17$$
$$\text{Upper Shadow} = 215 - 200.5 = 14.5$$
$$\text{Lower Shadow} = 200 - 198 = 2$$
Since the upper shadow is very long and lower shadow is tiny → **Gravestone Doji** (bearish at top)

**Candle B:**
$$\text{Body} = 0.2, \quad \text{Upper Shadow} = 110 - 100.2 = 9.8$$
$$\text{Lower Shadow} = 100 - 90 = 10$$
Both shadows >> body → **Long-Legged Doji** (strong indecision, possible reversal)

**Candle C:**
$$\text{Body} = 0, \quad \text{Upper Shadow} = 51 - 50 = 1$$
$$\text{Lower Shadow} = 50 - 49 = 1$$
Tiny body, small symmetric shadows → **Standard/Dragonfly Doji**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read a candlestick on an exam — the decision tree:**

| Step | Question | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is $\vert C - O \vert \approx 0$? | Yes → **Doji family** (neutral) |
| 2 | Is the candle at a top? | Yes → Bearish signal |
| 3 | Is body small with long upper shadow only? | **Shooting Star** (bearish) |
| 4 | Is body small with long lower shadow only? | **Hanging Man** (bearish at top) |
| 5 | Does Day 2 engulf Day 1 in opposite color? | **Bullish/Bearish Engulfing** |
| 6 | Are there 3 long red candles descending? | **Three Black Crows** (bearish) |
| 7 | Is there a gap up → small body → gap down close? | **Evening Star** (bearish) |
| 8 | Are two highs exactly equal? | **Tweezer Tops** (bearish) |

---

## 3.5 The "Body-to-Shadow Ratio" — A Derived Quantitative Metric

A **Body-to-Shadow Ratio (BSR)** can be defined for classification:

$$\text{BSR} = \frac{\vert C - O \vert}{H - L}$$

| BSR Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $\text{BSR} = 1$ | Marubozu (full body, no shadows) — strong signal |
| $0.5 \leq \text{BSR} < 1$ | Strong directional candle |
| $0.1 \leq \text{BSR} < 0.5$ | Moderate signal (e.g., Spinning Top if shadows long) |
| $\text{BSR} < 0.1$ | Doji-like (indecision) |

> [!NOTE]
> This BSR metric is **not a textbook formula** but is derived from first principles and is a **legitimate quantitative tool** for algorithmic pattern recognition — KTU examiners may award bonus marks for such innovations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a "Doji" candlestick. Explain how its interpretation changes based on its location in a trend.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Doji** is a candlestick where the opening and closing prices are approximately equal, resulting in a real body of nearly zero length ($\vert C - O \vert \approx 0$).

- **At a market top (after an uptrend):** The Doji signals **bearish reversal** — buying momentum has been exhausted.
- **At a market bottom (after a downtrend):** It signals **bullish reversal** — selling pressure has been exhausted.
- **In the middle of a trend:** It is a **neutral/continuation** signal indicating consolidation.

The interpretation is **context-dependent**, not inherent to the candle itself. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between a "Hanging Man" and a "Hammer" candlestick pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Both Hanging Man and Hammer have **identical visual shape** — a small real body at the top with a long lower shadow (≥ 2× body).

| Feature | Hammer | Hanging Man |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Bottom of downtrend | Top of uptrend |
| **Signal** | Bullish reversal | Bearish reversal |
| **Psychology** | Sellers tested lower prices, buyers recovered | Buyers lost control, sellers tested lower prices |

> The **shape is the same**; the **trend context** is the ONLY differentiator. *(3 marks)*

---
