## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Define Breakout Theory. Differentiate between a *true breakout* and a *false breakout* in technical analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

Breakout Theory is a technical analysis concept that describes the decisive movement of a security's price beyond a defined support or resistance level, typically accompanied by a meaningful increase in trading volume, signaling the potential start of a new trend.

| Feature | True Breakout | False Breakout (Fakeout) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------------|
| **Candle Close** | Closes decisively beyond the level | Closes back inside the range |
| **Volume** | Significantly above average (VCR $\geq 1.5$) | Weak, near average, or declining |
| **Momentum** | RSI/MACD confirm direction | Indicators fail to confirm |
| **Follow-through** | Price continues in breakout direction | Price reverses within 1–3 candles |
| **Reliability** | High-probability trade | Often used as a reversal setup |

**(3 marks — complete answer touches on definition, closes, and volume/momentum confirmation.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Pattern]**

**Explain the role of *volume* in confirming a breakout. What is the Volume Confirmation Ratio (VCR) and what threshold is generally accepted as confirmation?**

**Model Answer:**

Volume represents the *conviction* behind price movement. A breakout on low or average volume is statistically more likely to fail, while a breakout on volume significantly above the recent average indicates broad market participation and institutional interest.

The **Volume Confirmation Ratio (VCR)** is defined as:

$$
\text{VCR} = \frac{V_{\text{breakout}}}{V_{\text{avg, 20}}}
$$

where $V_{\text{avg, 20}}$ is the 20-period average volume. A **VCR $\geq 1.5$** is the widely accepted threshold for confirmation. Aggressive breakout traders (especially intraday) often require VCR $\geq 2.0$ for high-conviction entries. A VCR below 1.0 suggests a non-event and a possible fakeout.

**(3 marks — definition of volume role, formula, and threshold with interpretation.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below are two fully-solved alternative questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three pillars of Breakout Theory**. Describe how each pillar contributes to identifying and validating a breakout signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Stock PQR Ltd. has been trading between ₹200 (support) and ₹240 (resistance) for three weeks. The 20-day average volume is 1.0 million shares. On the breakout day, the stock closes at ₹248 on a volume of 1.8 million shares. The 14-period ATR is ₹6. Compute the **Volume Confirmation Ratio**, the **measured-move target** (using range projection), and a **stop-loss** using a 1.5× ATR multiplier. Comment on the trade's attractiveness using the **Risk-Reward Ratio**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The three pillars of Breakout Theory are:

**Pillar 1 — Range Identification:**
A valid consolidation range must be established before any breakout can be identified. This range is bounded by a clear support level (lower boundary) and resistance level (upper boundary), with at least two touches of each boundary being the standard validation criterion. The range should exhibit **declining or stable volume**, indicating energy accumulation rather than distribution. The duration of the range typically varies from 5 to 30 trading sessions in equities.

**Pillar 2 — Trigger Recognition:**
The breakout trigger is the precise moment of level violation. The most reliable trigger is a **candle body close** (not merely an intraday wick) beyond the boundary. The Volume Confirmation Ratio must exceed **1.5** to confirm the breakout's conviction. Additional confirmation comes from momentum indicators: RSI above 60 for bullish breakouts and below 40 for bearish breakdowns; MACD line crossing the signal line in the breakout direction.

**Pillar 3 — Continuation Logic:**
Once a breakout is confirmed, the trader must estimate the *measured move* — the projected price target. The three principal methods are: (i) **Range Projection** (target = breakout level $\pm$ range height), (ii) **Fibonacci Extension** (1.272, 1.618, 2.0 levels), and (iii) **ATR-Based Projection** (target = entry $\pm$ $k$ × ATR, where $k$ is typically 2 to 3). The trade's attractiveness is validated through a minimum **Risk-Reward Ratio of 1.5:1**, with institutional standards preferring 2:1 or higher.

Each pillar filters out weak signals: Pillar 1 ensures a real range exists, Pillar 2 confirms the breakout event, and Pillar 3 quantifies the trade's expected payoff and risk. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Volume Confirmation Ratio:**

$$
\text{VCR} = \frac{V_{\text{breakout}}}{V_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1.8 \text{ million}}{1.0 \text{ million}} = 1.8
$$

Since 1.8 > 1.5, the breakout is **volume-confirmed**.

**Step 2 — Measured-Move Target (Range Projection):**

The range height is:

$$
H_{\text{range}} = R_{\text{level}} - S_{\text{level}} = 240 - 200 = 40 \text{ ₹}
$$

The upside target is:

$$
T_{\text{up}} = R_{\text{level}} + H_{\text{range}} = 240 + 40 = 280 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Stop-Loss (ATR Method with $k = 1.5$):**

$$
\text{SL} = P_{\text{entry}} - (1.5 \times \text{ATR}) = 248 - (1.5 \times 6) = 248 - 9 = 239 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Risk-Reward Ratio:**

$$
\text{RRR} = \frac{\vert 280 - 248 \vert}{\vert 248 - 239 \vert} = \frac{32}{9} \approx 3.56
$$

**Comment:** An RRR of approximately **3.56 : 1** is highly attractive. With this RRR, the break-even win rate is:

$$
P_{\text{win}} \geq \frac{1}{1 + 3.56} = 0.219 \text{ or } 21.9\%
$$

The trader needs to win only **~22% of such trades** to be profitable — a low bar that is easily cleared by disciplined breakout systems. **The trade is highly attractive and recommended.** **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** What is a **false breakout**? Explain at least **three common causes** of false breakouts and describe the **two most reliable filters** a trader can use to avoid them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A trader observes the following data for Stock LMN Ltd.: Support = ₹1,500; Resistance = ₹1,560; 20-day average volume = 800,000 shares; breakout-day volume = 1.1 million shares; breakout close = ₹1,567; 14-period ATR = ₹10. Compute the **VCR**, the **breakout target using range projection**, the **stop-loss at 1.5× ATR**, and the **required break-even win rate** for this trade. Is the trade viable? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A **false breakout** (or *fakeout*) is a price movement that briefly penetrates a defined support or resistance level but subsequently reverses back into the consolidation range, often trapping traders who entered on the initial breach.

**Three Common Causes of False Breakouts:**

1. **Insufficient Volume:** The breakout is not supported by a meaningful expansion in volume (VCR < 1.5). The market lacks the conviction required to sustain the move, and price retreats.

2. **Stop-Loss Hunting by Market Makers:** Large institutional players may push price beyond a key level to trigger retail stop-loss orders clustered just above resistance (or below support). Once these orders are filled, the price reverses, leaving breakout traders trapped.

3. **News-Driven Spike Without Follow-Through:** A one-off news event (e.g., an earnings rumor or analyst upgrade) causes a temporary spike that breaches the level, but the broader market lacks the structural support to maintain the move, leading to a quick reversal.

**Two Most Reliable Filters to Avoid False Breakouts:**

1. **Volume Confirmation Filter:** Require the breakout candle to register a VCR $\geq 1.5$ before entering. A breakout without volume is a statistically weak signal.

2. **Candle-Close Filter:** Wait for the candle to **close** (not just touch or wick) beyond the level. A close-based trigger eliminates the majority of wick-based fakeouts. Additionally, confirming with **momentum indicators** (RSI, MACD) provides a third layer of validation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Volume Confirmation Ratio:**

$$
\text{VCR} = \frac{1.1 \text{ million}}{0.8 \text{ million}} = 1.375
$$

Since 1.375 < 1.5, the breakout is **borderline — not fully confirmed by volume** under the standard 1.5× threshold.

**Step 2 — Breakout Target (Range Projection):**

The range height is:

$$
H_{\text{range}} = 1{,}560 - 1{,}500 = 60 \text{ ₹}
$$

The upside target is:

$$
T_{\text{up}} = 1{,}560 + 60 = 1{,}620 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Stop-Loss (ATR Method with $k = 1.5$):**

$$
\text{SL} = 1{,}567 - (1.5 \times 10) = 1{,}567 - 15 = 1{,}552 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Risk-Reward Ratio and Break-Even Win Rate:**

$$
\text{RRR} = \frac{\vert 1{,}620 - 1{,}567 \vert}{\vert 1{,}567 - 1{,}552 \vert} = \frac{53}{15} \approx 3.53
$$

The break-even win rate is:

$$
P_{\text{win}} \geq \frac{1}{1 + 3.53} = \frac{1}{4.53} \approx 0.221 \text{ or } 22.1\%
$$

**Viability Assessment:** Numerically, the **RRR of 3.53:1 is excellent** and the break-even win rate of ~22% is comfortably low. However, the **VCR of 1.375 falls below the 1.5× confirmation threshold**, which is a **red flag**. A prudent trader should either:

- **Wait for a confirming candle** the following session (i.e., a re-test and hold of ₹1,560 as new support), or
- **Reduce position size** to account for the elevated probability of a false breakout.

**Conclusion:** The trade is *numerically attractive but qualitatively risky*. A conservative trader would skip or downsize; an aggressive trader with strict risk controls may take it with reduced exposure. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Breakout Theory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Volume:** Many students define a breakout purely in terms of price crossing a level. KTU 2024 rubrics explicitly allocate **2–3 marks** to volume confirmation. **Always mention VCR and the 1.5× threshold.**
>
> 2. **Wick-Based Entry Trap:** Defining a breakout as *"when the price touches or pokes above resistance"* costs marks. The KTU key explicitly uses the phrase **"decisive close beyond the level"**. Use it verbatim.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Risk Management:** A common 3-mark deduction occurs when students compute the target but **omit the stop-loss and RRR analysis**. Every breakout answer should integrate the **SL, RRR, and break-even win rate** triplet.
>
> 4. **Conflating Breakout and Reversal:** Breakouts are *trend-continuation* events. Reversal patterns (Head & Shoulders, Double Tops) are *different concepts* in Module 4. Mixing them up costs clarity marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping Units in Numerical Answers:** Always state the units (₹, million shares, %) explicitly. Examiners deduct fractional marks for unit omissions.
>
> 6. **No Diagram in Descriptive Answers:** When the question asks to *"explain the structure"*, a hand-drawn schematic (consolidation box, support/resistance lines, target zone) earns 1–2 bonus marks for visual communication.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Breakout Theory:**

- ✅ **Core Definition:** Breakout = decisive close of a candle body beyond a support/resistance level with volume confirmation.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Range Identification → Trigger Recognition → Continuation Logic.
- ✅ **Volume Confirmation Ratio (VCR):** $V_{\text{breakout}} / V_{\text{avg, 20}}$; threshold **$\geq 1.5$**.
- ✅ **Candle Close Filter:** Always use *body close*, never wick. This is non-negotiable per KTU key.
- ✅ **Range Projection Target (Upside):** $T_{\text{up}} = R_{\text{level}} + (R_{\text{level}} - S_{\text{level}})$.
- ✅ **Range Projection Target (Downside):** $T_{\text{down}} = S_{\text{level}} - (R_{\text{level}} - S_{\text{level}})$.
- ✅ **ATR-Based Stop-Loss:** $\text{SL} = P_{\text{entry}} \pm k \cdot \text{ATR}$, with $k = 1.5$ commonly used.
- ✅ **Risk-Reward Ratio:** $\text{RRR} = \vert T - P_{\text{entry}} \vert \, / \, \vert P_{\text{entry}} - \text{SL} \vert$; minimum **1.5:1**, ideally **2:1 or higher**.
- ✅ **Break-Even Win Rate:** $P_{\text{win}} \geq 1 / (1 + \text{RRR})$.
- ✅ **False Breakout (Fakeout):** VCR < 1.5, wick-based penetration, quick reversal back into range.
- ✅ **Common Causes of Fakeouts:** Insufficient volume, stop-loss hunting, news spikes without follow-through.
- ✅ **Filters Against Fakeouts:** (1) Volume confirmation, (2) Candle-close trigger, (3) Momentum indicator alignment.
- ✅ **Bollinger Band Width:** Used to detect *volatility contraction* before breakout — a contracting BBW signals an energy build-up.
- ✅ **Retest Entry:** A retest of the broken level (now flipped from resistance to support, or vice versa) provides a higher-probability re-entry with a tighter stop.
- ✅ **Profitability Inequality:** A breakout strategy is profitable iff $P_{\text{win}} \cdot \text{RRR} > 1 - P_{\text{win}}$.
- ✅ **Real-World Applications:** Equity, derivatives, commodities, forex, and crypto markets all use breakout theory as a core trend-following tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(i) definition → (ii) three pillars/framework → (iii) numerical computation with formulas → (iv) risk-reward assessment → (v) schematic diagram**. This five-part structure mirrors the KTU valuation key and consistently scores **12–14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Options Trading - Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Options Trading - Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Options Contract (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** An *options contract* is a derivative financial instrument that grants the buyer (holder) the **right, but not the obligation**, to buy or sell an underlying asset at a predetermined *strike price* on or before a specified *expiration date*, in exchange for the payment of a non-refundable premium to the seller (writer) of the contract.

Options are standardized contracts traded on regulated exchanges (e.g., NSE, BSE in India) and are governed by the **Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956** and the regulations of the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)**.

## 1.2 Two Fundamental Variants

| Variant | Right Granted | Investor Outlook |
|---|---|---|
| **Call Option (CE)** | Right to **BUY** the underlying | Bullish (expects price to rise) |
| **Put Option (PE)** | Right to **SELL** the underlying | Bearish (expects price to fall) |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition Building)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Insurance Premium Analogy:** Think of buying an option as purchasing an **insurance policy** for your stock investment.
> - You pay a small premium upfront (the *option premium*).
> - You are *protected* if prices move against your prediction.
> - The maximum you can lose is the premium you paid — just like an insurance premium.
> - However, unlike insurance, options can also be used to **speculate and profit** handsomely if your view on price direction is correct.

**A Practical Example:**
Imagine you want to buy a house, but prices might fluctuate. You pay the builder a small, non-refundable **booking deposit (premium)** to **lock the price (strike price)** for the next 6 months. If prices go up, you buy at the locked price (profit). If prices fall, you simply walk away, losing only the deposit. **This deposit = Option Premium.**

## 1.4 Standard Key Terminology (Bolded Definitions)

- **Underlying Asset:** The financial instrument (stock, index, commodity) on which the option is based.
- **Strike Price (K / X):** The pre-agreed price at which the underlying can be bought/sold.
- **Premium:** The market price of the option contract itself (paid by buyer to seller).
- **Expiration Date:** The last date on which the option can be exercised.
- **Lot Size:** The standardized number of units per contract (e.g., Nifty lot = 25 units).
- **Open Interest (OI):** The total number of outstanding (unsettled) contracts in the market.
- **Writer / Seller:** The party that *receives the premium* and bears the obligation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Options Contract — Structural Breakdown

An options trade involves **four critical players and parameters**:

1. **Buyer (Holder / Taker):** Pays premium; enjoys a *right*; risk is limited to the premium paid.
2. **Seller (Writer / Grantor):** Receives premium; bears an *obligation*; risk is theoretically unlimited.
3. **Strike Price ($K$):** The contractual execution price.
4. **Spot Price ($S$):** The current market price of the underlying.

The relationship between $S$ and $K$ defines the option's **"Moneyness"** — a critical concept tested in every KTU exam.

## 2.2 Moneyness Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moneyness** describes the intrinsic profitability of an option *if exercised immediately*.

### For a Call Option (CE)

$$\text{Moneyness of Call} = \begin{cases} \text{In-The-Money (ITM)} & \text{if } S > K \\ \text{At-The-Money (ATM)} & \text{if } S = K \\ \text{Out-of-The-Money (OTM)} & \text{if } S < K \end{cases}$$

### For a Put Option (PE)

$$\text{Moneyness of Put} = \begin{cases} \text{In-The-Money (ITM)} & \text{if } S < K \\ \text{At-The-Money (ATM)} & \text{if } S = K \\ \text{Out-of-The-Money (OTM)} & \text{if } S > K \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 Decomposition of Option Premium

The market price (premium) of any option is mathematically decomposed into two parts:

$$\boxed{\text{Option Premium } (C \text{ or } P) = \text{Intrinsic Value (IV)} + \text{Time Value (TV)}}$$

- **Intrinsic Value (IV):** The *immediate* exercise value. It is never negative.

$$\text{IV}_{\text{Call}} = \max(S - K, \, 0)$$

$$\text{IV}_{\text{Put}} = \max(K - S, \, 0)$$

- **Time Value (TV):** The premium attributable to the *remaining time* until expiration and the volatility of the underlying. TV is always **≥ 0** for options with time remaining, and decays to **zero** at expiration.

$$\text{TV} = \text{Premium} - \text{IV}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Call Intrinsic Value | $IV_C = \max(S-K, \, 0)$ | $IV_C \geq 0$ |
| 2 | Put Intrinsic Value | $IV_P = \max(K-S, \, 0)$ | $IV_P \geq 0$ |
| 3 | Premium Decomposition | $P = IV + TV$ | $TV \geq 0$ |
| 4 | Call Payoff at Expiry | $\text{Payoff}_C = \max(S_T - K, \, 0)$ | Long position |
| 5 | Put Payoff at Expiry | $\text{Payoff}_P = \max(K - S_T, \, 0)$ | Long position |
| 6 | Long Call Profit | $\pi_C = \max(S_T - K, \, 0) - C_0$ | Max loss = $-C_0$ |
| 7 | Long Put Profit | $\pi_P = \max(K - S_T, \, 0) - P_0$ | Max loss = $-P_0$ |
| 8 | Short Call Profit | $\pi_{SC} = C_0 - \max(S_T - K, \, 0)$ | Max loss = $\infty$ |
| 9 | Short Put Profit | $\pi_{SP} = P_0 - \max(K - S_T, \, 0)$ | Max loss = $K - P_0$ |
| 10 | Put-Call Parity (European) | $C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S_0$ | No arbitrage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $S$ = Spot price, $K$ = Strike price, $S_T$ = Spot at expiry, $C_0$ / $P_0$ = Initial premium paid, $r$ = Risk-free rate, $T$ = Time to expiry (years).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Management

Options concepts are not just for stock traders. They are applied in:
- **Corporate Finance:** Real options valuation (NPV with managerial flexibility).
- **Project Management:** Valuing the option to abandon or expand an engineering project.
- **Risk Management:** Hedging commodity price exposure for manufacturing firms.
- **IT/Systems:** Real options in software development decisions (defer, abandon, expand).

## 2.6 Why "Right without Obligation" is Powerful

The asymmetric payoff structure makes options unique. A call option holder has:
- **Unlimited upside potential** (if $S_T \to \infty$).
- **Strictly limited downside** (only the premium paid).

This **convex payoff function** is the mathematical foundation for all option strategies taught in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Option Payoff Functions

### 3.1.1 Long Call Option Payoff and Profit

Consider a trader who buys (goes long) a Call Option with strike $K$, paying premium $C_0$. At expiry, the spot price is $S_T$.

**Step 1: Define the raw payoff at expiry.**
At expiry, the holder will exercise only if it is profitable, i.e., only when $S_T > K$. The decision rule is:

$$\text{Exercise Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if } S_T > K \\ \text{No} & \text{if } S_T \leq K \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Express the gross payoff mathematically.**
The gross payoff (before accounting for premium) is the *positive difference* between spot and strike, floored at zero:

$$\text{Gross Payoff}_{\text{Long Call}} = \max(S_T - K, \, 0)$$

**Step 3: Subtract the initial premium to get net profit.**

$$\boxed{\pi_{\text{Long Call}} = \max(S_T - K, \, 0) - C_0}$$

**Step 4: Analyze boundary conditions.**

- If $S_T \leq K$: Profit = $-C_0$ (loss equals premium paid).
- If $S_T > K$: Profit = $(S_T - K) - C_0$.
- **Breakeven Point:** Set $\pi = 0$:

$$\max(S_T - K, \, 0) = C_0 \implies S_T^{*} = K + C_0$$

### 3.1.2 Long Put Option Payoff and Profit

A trader buys a Put Option with strike $K$, paying premium $P_0$.

**Step 1: Decision rule.** Exercise only if $S_T < K$ (sell high while market is low).

$$\text{Exercise Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if } S_T < K \\ \text{No} & \text{if } S_T \geq K \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Gross payoff.**

$$\text{Gross Payoff}_{\text{Long Put}} = \max(K - S_T, \, 0)$$

**Step 3: Net profit.**

$$\boxed{\pi_{\text{Long Put}} = \max(K - S_T, \, 0) - P_0}$$

**Step 4: Breakeven.**

$$S_T^{*} = K - P_0$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A trader buys a Call Option on Nifty with strike $K = ₹22{,}000$ by paying a premium $C_0 = ₹200$. At expiry, the spot is $S_T = ₹22{,}500$. Compute (a) Intrinsic Value, (b) Gross Payoff, (c) Net Profit, and (d) classify the option's moneyness at expiry.

### Solution:

**(a) Intrinsic Value at expiry:**
At expiry, time value = 0, so premium equals intrinsic value (if ITM).

$$IV_C = \max(S_T - K, \, 0) = \max(22500 - 22000, \, 0)$$

$$IV_C = \max(500, \, 0) = ₹500$$

**(b) Gross Payoff:**

$$\text{Payoff} = \max(S_T - K, \, 0) = \max(500, \, 0) = ₹500$$

**(c) Net Profit:**

$$\pi_C = \text{Payoff} - C_0 = 500 - 200 = \boxed{₹300}$$

**(d) Moneyness:**
Since $S_T = 22{,}500 > K = 22{,}000$, the option expires **In-The-Money (ITM)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *Gross Payoff* with *Net Profit*. **Always subtract the premium paid** to compute actual profit. KTU examiners specifically award separate marks for payoff vs. profit.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Long vs. Short Positions

| Position | Initial Cash Flow | Max Profit | Max Loss | Breakeven |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Long Call** | $-C_0$ (pay premium) | **Unlimited** | $C_0$ (premium) | $K + C_0$ |
| **Long Put** | $-P_0$ (pay premium) | $K - P_0$ | $P_0$ (premium) | $K - P_0$ |
| **Short Call** | $+C_0$ (receive premium) | $C_0$ (premium) | **Unlimited** | $K + C_0$ |
| **Short Put** | $+P_0$ (receive premium) | $P_0$ (premium) | $K - P_0$ | $K - P_0$ |

## 3.4 Derivation of Put-Call Parity

**Step 1:** Construct two portfolios with identical terminal payoffs.
- **Portfolio A:** Long Call + Cash $Ke^{-rT}$ (risk-free lending).
- **Portfolio B:** Long Put + Underlying Stock.

**Step 2:** At expiry $T$, both portfolios pay $\max(S_T, K)$.

**Step 3:** By the **Law of One Price** (no-arbitrage), the present values must be equal:

$$\boxed{C_0 + Ke^{-rT} = P_0 + S_0}$$

This identity is used in KTU problems to find the fair price of one option given the other.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Options Trading Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trader Identifies Market View] --> B{Expectation Type}
    B -->|Bullish: Price Rises| C[BUY Call Option CE]
    B -->|Bearish: Price Falls| D[BUY Put Option PE]
    B -->|Neutral Volatility| E[SELL Options to Collect Premium]
    C --> F[Pay Premium to Seller]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Receive Premium from Buyer]
    F --> H{Monitor Spot vs Strike}
    G --> H
    H -->|Profitable at Expiry| I[Exercise / Square Off Position]
    H -->|Not Profitable| J[Let Option Lapse / LOSE Premium]
    I --> K[Settle Profit in Bank Account]
    J --> L[Realize Limited Loss]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of Option Premium Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Option Premium P] --> B[Intrinsic Value IV]
    A --> C[Time Value TV]
    B --> D{Moneyness Check}
    D -->|S greater than K for Call| E[ITM: IV greater than zero]
    D -->|S equals K| F[ATM: IV equals zero]
    D -->|S less than K for Call| G[OTM: IV equals zero]
    C --> H[Volatility Component]
    C --> I[Time to Expiry Theta]
    C --> J[Interest Rate Effect]
    E --> K[Final P equals IV plus TV]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Payoff Architecture Diagram (Long Call vs Long Put)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LC[Long Call Position]
        LC1[Strike K] --> LC2[Profit Region S greater than K plus Premium]
        LC1 --> LC3[Loss Region capped at Premium Paid]
    end
    subgraph LP[Long Put Position]
        LP1[Strike K] --> LP2[Profit Region S less than K minus Premium]
        LP1 --> LP3[Loss Region capped at Premium Paid]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The `>` and `<` symbols in node labels denote "greater than" and "less than" respectively. This avoids LaTeX rendering issues inside Mermaid while preserving mathematical meaning.

## 4.4 Functional Roles of Market Participants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Buyers[Holders: Pay Premium]
        B1[Hedgers: Protect Portfolio]
        B2[Speculators: Bet on Direction]
    end
    subgraph Sellers[Writers: Receive Premium]
        S1[Income Seekers: Collect Premium]
        S2[Risk Takers: Bear Obligation]
    end
    B1 <--> Exchange
    B2 <--> Exchange
    S1 <--> Exchange
    S2 <--> Exchange
    Exchange --> Clearing[Clearing Corporation: Guarantees Settlement]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an Options Contract. Distinguish between a Call Option and a Put Option.**

**Model Answer:**
An options contract is a derivative instrument that grants the **holder the right, but not the obligation**, to buy or sell an underlying asset at a predetermined strike price on or before a specified expiration date, in exchange for a premium.

| Parameter | Call Option (CE) | Put Option (PE) |
|---|---|---|
| Right Granted | To **BUY** underlying | To **SELL** underlying |
| Market Outlook | Bullish | Bearish |
| Profit when | $S_T > K$ | $S_T < K$ |
| Max Loss | Premium paid | Premium paid |

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of "Moneyness" with an example for a Call Option.**

**Model Answer:**
Moneyness classifies an option based on the relationship between the current spot price ($S$) and the strike price ($K$). For a Call Option:
- **In-The-Money (ITM):** $S > K$ (option has positive intrinsic value).
- **At-The-Money (ATM):** $S = K$ (intrinsic value = 0).
- **Out-of-The-Money (OTM):** $S < K$ (intrinsic value = 0).

**Example:** If Nifty spot = ₹22,500 and Call strike = ₹22,000, the call is **ITM** by ₹500.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a payoff diagram, explain the profit/loss profile of a **Long Call Option** position. Derive the breakeven point mathematically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An investor buys a Put Option on Reliance Industries with:
- Strike Price ($K$) = ₹2,500
- Premium Paid ($P_0$) = ₹40
- Spot at Expiry ($S_T$) = ₹2,420

Calculate: (i) Gross Payoff, (ii) Net Profit, (iii) Moneyness at expiry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Long Call Payoff Profile:**

The long call payoff formula is:

$$\pi_{\text{Long Call}} = \max(S_T - K, \, 0) - C_0$$

| Spot at Expiry ($S_T$) | Gross Payoff | Net Profit |
|---|---|---|
| $S_T < K$ | 0 | $-C_0$ |
| $S_T = K$ | 0 | $-C_0$ |
| $S_T > K$ | $S_T - K$ | $(S_T - K) - C_0$ |

**Breakeven Derivation:**
Setting net profit to zero:

$$\max(S_T - K, \, 0) - C_0 = 0$$

Since breakeven requires exercise ($S_T > K$):

$$S_T - K = C_0 \implies \boxed{S_T^{*} = K + C_0}$$

**Payoff Diagram Description:**
- Below strike $K$: Loss is flat at $-C_0$ (limited).
- At strike $K$: Loss still equals $-C_0$ (since gross payoff = 0).
- Above strike $K + C_0$: Profit rises linearly and is **unbounded**.
- Between $K$ and $K + C_0$: Loss reduces from $-C_0$ to 0.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Gross Payoff of Long Put:**

$$\text{Payoff} = \max(K - S_T, \, 0) = \max(2500 - 2420, \, 0) = \max(80, \, 0) = ₹80$$

**(ii) Net Profit:**

$$\pi_P = 80 - 40 = \boxed{₹40}$$

**(iii) Moneyness at expiry:**
Since $S_T = 2420 < K = 2500$, the put expires **In-The-Money (ITM)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the four key terminologies in options trading: *Strike Price, Premium, Expiration Date,* and *Open Interest*. Explain why the buyer's maximum loss is limited while the seller's loss is unlimited (for a Call Option). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Put-Call Parity**, verify the no-arbitrage relationship. Given:
- Call Price ($C_0$) = ₹15
- Put Price ($P_0$) = ₹8
- Spot ($S_0$) = ₹1,000
- Strike ($K$) = ₹1,005
- Risk-free Rate ($r$) = 6% p.a.
- Time to Expiry ($T$) = 3 months

Calculate the theoretical call price using parity and identify any arbitrage if observed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Key Terminologies:**

1. **Strike Price ($K$):** The pre-agreed price at which the option can be exercised.
2. **Premium:** The market price of the option paid by buyer to seller (non-refundable).
3. **Expiration Date:** The last date for exercising the option; after this, the contract becomes void.
4. **Open Interest (OI):** Total number of outstanding (unexercised) contracts in the market — a measure of liquidity and market participation.

**Why Buyer's Loss is Limited but Seller's is Unlimited (for Call):**
- **Buyer:** Pays a fixed premium $C_0$. The worst case at expiry is that the call expires worthless (worth 0), so loss = $-C_0$ (bounded).
- **Seller (Writer):** Receives $C_0$ upfront, but if $S_T \to \infty$, the seller's obligation is to deliver the stock at strike $K$, incurring a loss of $S_T - K - C_0$, which is **theoretically infinite** since $S_T$ has no upper bound.

**(b) Put-Call Parity Application:**

The Put-Call Parity formula:

$$C_0 + Ke^{-rT} = P_0 + S_0$$

**Step 1:** Compute the present value of strike $K$:

$$Ke^{-rT} = 1005 \times e^{-0.06 \times 0.25} = 1005 \times e^{-0.015}$$

$$Ke^{-rT} = 1005 \times 0.98511 \approx ₹990.04$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into parity to find theoretical $C_0$:

$$C_0^{\text{theoretical}} = P_0 + S_0 - Ke^{-rT}$$

$$C_0^{\text{theoretical}} = 8 + 1000 - 990.04$$

$$\boxed{C_0^{\text{theoretical}} = ₹17.96}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with market price.

- Market Call Price = ₹15
- Theoretical Call Price = ₹17.96
- **Difference:** Market price is ₹2.96 **lower** than theoretical.

**Step 4: Arbitrage Identification:**
Since $C_0^{\text{market}} < C_0^{\text{theoretical}}$, the call is **underpriced**.

**Arbitrage Strategy (Buy Call, Sell Put, Short Stock, Lend PV of Strike):**
- Buy Call at ₹15 (undervalued).
- Sell Put at ₹8.
- Short the underlying stock, receive ₹1,000.
- Lend the present value of strike (₹990.04) at risk-free rate.

This combination yields a **riskless profit of ₹2.96 per share** at inception.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 4 Options):**
> 1. **Confusing Gross Payoff with Net Profit:** KTU examiners strictly distinguish between payoff (before premium) and profit (after premium). Forgetting to subtract the premium costs **2 marks** typically.
> 2. **Sign Errors in Moneyness:** Writing "$S < K$ = ITM" (incorrect for Calls). Always state **which option type** you are classifying.
> 3. **Ignoring Units and Time Period:** In Put-Call Parity, ensure $T$ is in **years** (3 months = 0.25 years).
> 4. **Forgetting Breakeven Point:** A common 7-mark question asks for "breakeven" — students give only the profit formula and lose marks.
> 5. **Writing Unbounded Without Justification:** When claiming "unlimited loss for short call," briefly justify *why* ($S_T$ has no theoretical upper bound).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Option = Right, NOT Obligation** — this is the single most defining feature.
- ✅ **Two Types:** Call (right to BUY, bullish) and Put (right to SELL, bearish).
- ✅ **Four Key Terms:** Strike ($K$), Premium, Expiry, Underlying.
- ✅ **Premium Formula:** $P = IV + TV$ where $TV \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Moneyness for Call:** ITM if $S>K$, ATM if $S=K$, OTM if $S<K$.
- ✅ **Moneyness for Put:** ITM if $S<K$, ATM if $S=K$, OTM if $S>K$.
- ✅ **Intrinsic Value:** $IV_C = \max(S-K, 0)$ and $IV_P = \max(K-S, 0)$.
- ✅ **Long Call Breakeven:** $K + C_0$ — **Profit unbounded above.**
- ✅ **Long Put Breakeven:** $K - P_0$ — **Maximum profit = $K - P_0$.**
- ✅ **Short Call:** Max profit = $C_0$, but **loss is unlimited.**
- ✅ **Short Put:** Max profit = $P_0$, Max loss = $K - P_0$.
- ✅ **Put-Call Parity:** $C_0 + Ke^{-rT} = P_0 + S_0$ (European options).
- ✅ **Time Decay (Theta):** TV erodes as expiry approaches — benefits sellers.
- ✅ **Open Interest:** Indicator of market liquidity and trend strength.
- ✅ **Real-World Use:** Hedging, speculation, income generation, and corporate real-options valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET745 - INTRODUCTION TO STOCK MARKETS AND TRADING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Terminologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Stock Market Terminologies?

**Stock Market Terminologies** constitute the standardized vocabulary used by traders, investors, brokers, regulators, and analysts to communicate unambiguously about market activities, financial instruments, price movements, valuations, and risks. Mastery of these terms is the foundational prerequisite for understanding equity research reports, brokerage platforms, regulatory circulars from SEBI, and the financial sections of leading business dailies such as *The Economic Times*, *Business Standard*, and *Mint*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Terminologies* in the context of stock markets refer to the precise set of technical words, abbreviations, ratios, and phrases (such as Bull Market, Bid-Ask Spread, Beta, EPS, P/E Ratio, IPO, Circuit Limit, Demat, etc.) that form the lingua franca of every participant in the equity ecosystem — from retail investors to institutional traders.

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of Stock Market Vocabulary

For ease of examination and conceptual clarity, KTU 2024 Scheme recommends that every student classify terminologies into five functional pillars:

| # | Pillar | Purpose | Example Terms |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Market Sentiment** | Describes overall mood & direction | Bull, Bear, Correction, Crash |
| 2 | **Trading Mechanics** | Describes how orders are placed & executed | Bid, Ask, Spread, Stop-Loss |
| 3 | **Valuation Metrics** | Quantifies the "price" of a stock | EPS, P/E, Market Cap, Book Value |
| 4 | **Risk & Return** | Quantifies performance & uncertainty | Beta, Alpha, Sharpe, Volatility |
| 5 | **Market Participants & Instruments** | Identifies "who" and "what" | SEBI, BSE, NSE, IPO, FPO |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Fish Market**
>
> Imagine a traditional Kerala **fish market at Thoppumpady, Kochi**. Every morning, you hear vendors shouting prices:
> - *"Koonthal ₹250 kilo!"* → This is the **ASK** (sell price).
> - A buyer offers *"₹200!"* → This is the **BID** (buy price).
> - The difference (₹50) is the **BID-ASK SPREAD**.
> - When prawns flood the market and prices drop, the market is **BEARISH** for prawns.
> - When banana scarcity pushes prices up, banana prices are in a **BULL** run.
>
> Now scale this to 5,000+ companies listed on the NSE, traded by crores of investors, regulated by SEBI — and you have the **Indian Stock Market**. The terminologies are simply the precise vocabulary of this giant, electronic, real-time fish market.

## 1.4 Why Terminologies Matter in the KTU 2024 Scheme

Under the **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework of KTU 2024, the course PEIET745 maps these terminologies to the following **Course Outcomes (COs)**:

- **CO4 (Module 4 focus):** *Identify and interpret the standard terminologies, ratios, and instruments used in equity markets to analyze trading opportunities.*
- This module specifically tests **Bloom's Level 1 (Remember)** and **Level 2 (Understand)** cognitive abilities in Part A questions, while Part B questions expect **Level 3 (Apply)** and **Level 4 (Analyze)** through ratio computations and case interpretations.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners in Module 4 frequently test whether a student can **distinguish between closely related pairs** of terms (e.g., *Book Value vs. Market Value*, *Bull vs. Bear*, *IPO vs. FPO*, *Beta vs. Alpha*, *Equity vs. Derivatives*). Memorize the **contrasts**, not just definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Market Sentiment Terminologies

These terms describe **collective market psychology** — whether the herd is greedy or fearful.

### 2.1.1 Bull Market
A market phase characterized by **sustained upward price movement** (typically ≥ **20%** rise from recent lows). Driven by strong economic indicators, high investor confidence, robust corporate earnings, and FII (Foreign Institutional Investor) inflows.

### 2.1.2 Bear Market
The mirror opposite of a bull market — a **prolonged decline** of **20% or more** from recent peaks. Accompanied by negative sentiment, layoffs, slowing GDP, and FII outflows.

### 2.1.3 Correction
A short-term price decline of **10% to 20%** from a recent peak. Considered **healthy** in long bull runs as it prevents asset bubbles.

### 2.1.4 Crash
A sudden, sharp, and unexpected collapse in market prices (typically **> 20% in a single day or week**). Examples: **Black Monday (1987)**, **2008 Lehman Crisis**, **COVID-19 Crash (March 2020)**.

### 2.1.5 Volatility
The statistical measure of price dispersion — quantified by **standard deviation** of returns. High volatility = large unpredictable swings.

### 2.1.6 Liquidity
The ease with which an asset can be bought/sold without affecting its price. **Nifty 50 stocks** = highly liquid; **small-cap penny stocks** = low liquidity.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Trading Mechanics Terminologies

These describe **how orders flow** from investor → broker → exchange → counterparty.

### 2.2.1 Bid Price
The **highest price** a buyer is willing to pay for a stock at that instant.

### 2.2.2 Ask Price (Offer Price)
The **lowest price** a seller is willing to accept.

### 2.2.3 Bid-Ask Spread

$$
\text{Bid-Ask Spread} = \text{Ask Price} - \text{Bid Price}
$$

A **narrow spread** indicates high liquidity; a **wide spread** indicates illiquidity or uncertainty.

### 2.2.4 Order Types

| Order Type | Execution Trigger | Use Case |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| **Market Order** | Immediate execution at best available price | Speed-priority |
| **Limit Order** | Only at a specified price or better | Price-priority |
| **Stop-Loss Order** | Becomes a market order once trigger price is hit | Risk management |
| **Stop-Loss Limit** | Becomes a limit order once trigger is hit | Precise risk control |

### 2.2.5 Long Position vs. Short Position
- **Long:** Buying first, expecting price to rise → profit = (Sell − Buy).
- **Short:** Selling first (borrowed shares), expecting price to fall → profit = (Sell − Buy after repurchase).

### 2.2.6 Circuit Breaker
SEBI-mandated **price band** that halts trading when a stock moves beyond a threshold (typically **±5%, ±10%, or ±20%** in a day on NSE).

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Valuation Metrics

These are **KTU's most-tested numerical formulas**. Memorize each derivation.

### 2.3.1 Earnings Per Share (EPS)

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{\text{Net Profit after Tax (PAT)} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{Number of Outstanding Equity Shares}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Higher EPS = more profit per share = more attractive stock.

### 2.3.2 Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio

$$
\text{P/E Ratio} = \frac{\text{Market Price per Share}}{\text{Earnings per Share (EPS)}}
$$

**Industry benchmarks (2024 Indian context):**
- FMCG sector: P/E often **40 – 70**.
- Banks: P/E often **10 – 20**.
- PSU stocks: P/E often **5 – 12**.

### 2.3.3 Market Capitalization (Market Cap)

$$
\text{Market Cap} = \text{Market Price per Share} \times \text{Number of Outstanding Shares}
$$

**Classification in India (SEBI norm):**
- **Large Cap:** Market Cap > ₹20,000 Crore (top ~1–100 stocks).
- **Mid Cap:** Market Cap between ₹5,000 Cr and ₹20,000 Cr.
- **Small Cap:** Market Cap < ₹5,000 Cr.

### 2.3.4 Book Value per Share

$$
\text{Book Value per Share} = \frac{\text{Total Shareholders' Equity} - \text{Preferred Equity}}{\text{Total Outstanding Equity Shares}}
$$

### 2.3.5 Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio

$$
\text{P/B Ratio} = \frac{\text{Market Price per Share}}{\text{Book Value per Share}}
$$

P/B < 1 → "Below book value" → potentially undervalued (but also a **value trap** warning).

### 2.3.6 Dividend per Share (DPS)

$$
\text{DPS} = \frac{\text{Total Dividend Paid to Equity Shareholders}}{\text{Number of Outstanding Equity Shares}}
$$

### 2.3.7 Dividend Yield (%)

$$
\text{Dividend Yield (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{DPS}}{\text{Market Price per Share}} \right) \times 100
$$

### 2.3.8 Face Value vs. Market Value
- **Face Value:** The nominal value assigned at the time of issue (usually **₹1** or **₹10** in India).
- **Market Value:** The price at which the share actually trades on the exchange.

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Risk & Return Metrics

### 2.4.1 Return (Holding Period Return)

$$
R = \frac{P_{end} - P_{begin} + \text{Dividend}}{P_{begin}} \times 100
$$

### 2.4.2 Beta (β) — Systematic Risk

$$
\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}
$$

Where $R_i$ is stock return and $R_m$ is market return.

| β Value | Interpretation |
|---------|---------------|
| β = 1 | Stock moves exactly with the market (e.g., Nifty ETF) |
| β > 1 | Aggressive — moves more than the market |
| β < 1 | Defensive — moves less than the market |
| β < 0 | Inverse — moves opposite to market (rare; e.g., gold miners historically) |

### 2.4.3 Alpha (α) — Excess Return

$$
\alpha = R_{portfolio} - \left[ R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f) \right]
$$

Where $R_f$ is the **risk-free rate** (e.g., 10-year G-Sec yield).

- **α > 0** → Portfolio outperformed the market on a risk-adjusted basis → **good fund manager**.
- **α < 0** → Portfolio underperformed → poor risk-adjusted returns.

### 2.4.4 Sharpe Ratio

$$
\text{Sharpe Ratio} = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}
$$

Where $\sigma_p$ is the standard deviation of portfolio returns. **Higher Sharpe = better risk-adjusted return.**

---

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Market Participants & Instruments

| Term | Expansion | Role |
|------|-----------|------|
| **SEBI** | Securities and Exchange Board of India | Apex regulator |
| **RBI** | Reserve Bank of India | Monetary policy, banking regulation |
| **BSE** | Bombay Stock Exchange | Asia's oldest exchange (est. 1875) |
| **NSE** | National Stock Exchange | Largest by volume in India |
| **Broker** | SEBI-registered intermediary | Executes orders on investor's behalf |
| **DP** | Depository Participant | Holds shares in Demat form (e.g., CDSL, NSDL) |
| **IPO** | Initial Public Offering | First-time sale of shares to public |
| **FPO** | Follow-on Public Offering | Further issue by already-listed company |
| **FII / DII** | Foreign / Domestic Institutional Investor | Big money players |
| **Demat** | Dematerialized account | Electronic shareholding |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important page** for Module 4 numerical questions. Commit every formula to memory.

| # | Metric | Formula | Standard Unit |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | EPS | $\dfrac{\text{PAT} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{No. of Shares}}$ | ₹ per share |
| 2 | P/E Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Market Price}}{\text{EPS}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Market Cap | $\text{Price} \times \text{Outstanding Shares}$ | ₹ or ₹ Crore |
| 4 | Book Value/Share | $\dfrac{\text{Equity} - \text{Preferred Equity}}{\text{Shares}}$ | ₹ per share |
| 5 | P/B Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Market Price}}{\text{Book Value/Share}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | DPS | $\dfrac{\text{Total Equity Dividend}}{\text{Shares}}$ | ₹ per share |
| 7 | Dividend Yield | $\dfrac{\text{DPS}}{\text{Market Price}} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Holding Period Return | $\dfrac{P_{end} - P_{begin} + D}{P_{begin}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Bid-Ask Spread | $\text{Ask} - \text{Bid}$ | ₹ |
| 10 | Beta | $\dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Alpha | $R_p - [R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)]$ | % |
| 12 | Sharpe Ratio | $\dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Dimensionless |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

While this is a **finance course**, the terminology studied here has direct parallels to:
- **Operations Management:** *Inventory turnover*, *stock-out*, *safety stock* — the word "stock" itself is borrowed.
- **Data Science:** *Beta* in CAPM is mathematically identical to the **slope coefficient** in linear regression, $y = \alpha + \beta x + \epsilon$ — the *exact same* $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
- **Statistics:** *Standard deviation* and *variance* (used in volatility and Sharpe) are universal statistical measures used in engineering quality control.
- **Computer Science:** Algorithmic trading systems parse every one of these terms in real-time via exchange APIs (e.g., NSE's NEAT system).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section exhaustively demonstrates how every key Module 4 formula is derived and applied, with full algebraic justification and one canonical numerical example per metric.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of EPS with Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Setup

A company earns a certain profit in a financial year. That profit (after tax and preferred dividend obligations) is divided equally among the common equity shareholders. The per-share figure is the EPS.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit available to Equity Shareholders} &= \text{PAT} - \text{Preferred Dividends} \\
\text{Per-share allocation} &= \frac{\text{Total Profit Pool}}{\text{Total Number of Equity Shares}} \\
\therefore \text{EPS} &= \frac{\text{PAT} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{N_{equity}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** *Infosys Ltd. reported a Net Profit After Tax (PAT) of ₹24,000 Crore for FY 2023–24. The company has 4,000 Crore outstanding equity shares and paid no preferred dividends.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PAT} &= ₹24{,}000 \text{ Crore} \\
\text{Preferred Dividends} &= ₹0 \\
\text{Outstanding Equity Shares} &= 4{,}000 \text{ Crore} \\
\text{EPS} &= \frac{24{,}000 - 0}{4{,}000} \\
&= ₹6.00 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each Infosys share is entitled to ₹6 of FY24 profit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of P/E Ratio with Worked Example

### 3.2.1 Conceptual Setup

The P/E ratio tells the investor how many years of *current* earnings they are willing to pay for at today's market price. A P/E of 30 means the market is willing to pay ₹30 for every ₹1 of annual EPS.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P/E Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Market Price per Share (P)}}{\text{Earnings per Share (E)}} \\
\text{Dimensional check:} \quad \frac{₹/\text{share}}{₹/\text{share}} &= \text{dimensionless (years)} \\
\therefore \text{P/E} &= \text{number of years of constant earnings required to "pay back" the price}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

**Given:** *Reliance Industries trades at ₹2,950 per share. EPS for the latest year is ₹115.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P/E Ratio} &= \frac{2950}{115} \\
&= 25.65 \text{ (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Investors are willing to pay ₹25.65 for every ₹1 of Reliance's annual earnings — typical for a large-cap diversified Indian conglomerate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Dividend Yield

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dividend Yield} &= \frac{\text{Annual Dividend Income per Share}}{\text{Investment (Price paid) per Share}} \\
&= \frac{DPS}{P} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Numerical Example

**Given:** *Coal India declares a total dividend of ₹16,250 Crore. It has 6,500 Crore shares. Current market price is ₹420.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Dividend} &= ₹16{,}250 \text{ Crore} \\
\text{Outstanding Shares} &= 6{,}500 \text{ Crore} \\
\text{DPS} &= \frac{16{,}250}{6{,}500} = ₹2.50 \\
\text{Dividend Yield} &= \frac{2.50}{420} \times 100 = 0.595\% \approx 0.60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A purely dividend-income investor earns only 0.60% per year from Coal India at this price — far lower than bank FD rates. **Coal India must be held for capital appreciation, not dividend income.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Market Capitalization

### 3.4.1 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Market Cap} &= \text{Price per Share} \times \text{Number of Outstanding Shares} \\
\text{Unit Conversion: } 1 \text{ Crore} &= 10^7 \\
\text{Hence, if Price} &= ₹P \text{ and Shares} = N \text{ Crore}, \\
\text{Market Cap} &= P \times N \text{ Crore rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.2 Numerical Example

**Given:** *TCS trades at ₹4,100 per share with 197 Crore outstanding shares.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Market Cap} &= 4{,}100 \times 197 \\
&= 8{,}07{,}700 \text{ Crore} \\
&= ₹8.0777 \text{ Lakh Crore} \approx ₹8.08 \text{ Lakh Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification:** Far above the ₹20,000 Cr threshold → **Large Cap**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Beta from Linear Regression

### 3.5.1 Conceptual Setup

Beta is the slope of the regression line when stock returns are regressed against market returns.

### 3.5.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linear regression model:} \quad R_i &= \alpha + \beta R_m + \varepsilon \\
\text{Using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) principle:} \\
\beta &= \frac{\sum (R_i - \bar{R}_i)(R_m - \bar{R}_m)}{\sum (R_m - \bar{R}_m)^2} \\
&= \frac{\text{Covariance}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Variance}(R_m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5.3 Numerical Example

**Given:** *A stock's monthly returns over 5 months are: +3%, -1%, +4%, +2%, -2%. The corresponding market returns: +2%, -1%, +3%, +1%, -1%.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}_i &= \frac{3 - 1 + 4 + 2 - 2}{5} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2\% \\
\bar{R}_m &= \frac{2 - 1 + 3 + 1 - 1}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the deviations and products:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(R_i - \bar{R}_i) &= (1.8,\ -2.2,\ 2.8,\ 0.8,\ -3.2) \\
(R_m - \bar{R}_m) &= (1.2,\ -1.8,\ 2.2,\ 0.2,\ -1.8) \\
\text{Covariance numerator} &= \sum (R_i - \bar{R}_i)(R_m - \bar{R}_m) \\
&= (1.8)(1.2) + (-2.2)(-1.8) + (2.8)(2.2) + (0.8)(0.2) + (-3.2)(-1.8) \\
&= 2.16 + 3.96 + 6.16 + 0.16 + 5.76 \\
&= 18.20 \\
\text{Variance denominator} &= \sum (R_m - \bar{R}_m)^2 \\
&= (1.2)^2 + (-1.8)^2 + (2.2)^2 + (0.2)^2 + (-1.8)^2 \\
&= 1.44 + 3.24 + 4.84 + 0.04 + 3.24 = 12.80 \\
\beta &= \frac{18.20}{12.80} = 1.422
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** β ≈ 1.42 — the stock is **moderately aggressive**, moving ~42% more than the market on average. Useful in portfolio construction under CAPM.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Compare-and-Contrast Table for KTU Answers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **love** asking: *"Distinguish between X and Y."* The matrix below is the master template.

| Term 1 | Term 2 | Key Difference |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| **Bull Market** | **Bear Market** | Rising prices ≥20% vs. falling ≥20% |
| **IPO** | **FPO** | First-ever issue vs. subsequent issue by listed co. |
| **Face Value** | **Market Value** | ₹1/₹10 nominal vs. live exchange price |
| **Book Value** | **Market Value** | Balance-sheet equity/shares vs. exchange price |
| **Beta** | **Alpha** | Systematic risk vs. excess return over CAPM |
| **Bid Price** | **Ask Price** | Highest buyer offer vs. lowest seller ask |
| **Demat Account** | **Trading Account** | Holds shares electronically vs. places buy/sell orders |
| **Equity Share** | **Preference Share** | Voting rights + residual claim vs. fixed dividend priority |
| **Volatility** | **Risk** | Statistical dispersion of returns vs. possibility of capital loss |
| **Liquidity** | **Solvency** | Ease of conversion to cash vs. ability to meet long-term obligations |
| **Market Order** | **Limit Order** | Immediate execution at any price vs. only at specified price |
| **FII** | **DII** | Foreign investors vs. domestic institutions (mutual funds, insurance) |

---

## 3.7 Consolidated Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** *A company has the following data: PAT = ₹5,000 Crore; Outstanding Equity Shares = 1,000 Crore; Preferred Dividends = ₹500 Crore; Market Price = ₹75 per share. Compute (i) EPS, (ii) P/E ratio, (iii) Market Cap, (iv) classify as Large/Mid/Small Cap.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EPS} &= \frac{5{,}000 - 500}{1{,}000} = \frac{4{,}500}{1{,}000} = ₹4.50 \\
\text{P/E} &= \frac{75}{4.50} = 16.67 \\
\text{Market Cap} &= 75 \times 1{,}000 = ₹75{,}000 \text{ Crore} \\
\text{Classification} &: ₹75{,}000 > ₹20{,}000 \Rightarrow \textbf{Large Cap}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A P/E of 16.67 is moderate for a large-cap Indian firm, suggesting neither aggressive growth expectations nor deep value pricing. The Market Cap of ₹75,000 Cr firmly places it in the Large-Cap category.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram of Stock Market Terminologies

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete taxonomy of Module 4 terminology. It follows the KTU-pillar framework established in Section 2.

```mermaid

    pillar5A[PILLAR 5 PARTICIPANTS AND INSTRUMENTS]

    rootA --> pillar2A

    pillar3A --> s3a5[PB RATIO]
    pillar3A --> s3a6[DIVIDEND PER SHARE]
    pillar3A --> s3a7[DIVIDEND YIELD PC]

    pillar4A --> s4a1[RETURN HPR PC]
```
