Every transaction in the Indian cash-equity segment passes through the following **six-stage pipeline**. Mastering these stages is mandatory for KTU Part-B questions.

### Stage 1 — Order Origination
The investor decides to transact and places an order through the broker's trading platform (web, mobile, or desktop terminal). The order must specify:
- **Symbol** (e.g., RELIANCE, TCS)
- **Action** (BUY or SELL)
- **Quantity** (must be in integer multiples of the lot/free-float lot)
- **Price** (Limit price or Market price)
- **Order Type** (Regular, Bracket, Cover, GTT, etc.)
- **Product Code** (CNC = Delivery, MIS = Intraday, NRML = F&O Carry-forward)

### Stage 2 — Order Routing
The broker's system routes the order to the exchange. In India, most retail orders flow through:
**Investor → Broker's OMS (Order Management System) → Exchange's CO-LOCATION / NEAT/NOW terminal → Order Matching Engine.**

### Stage 3 — Trade Execution (Matching)
The exchange's algorithm matches the order on **Price-Time Priority**:
- **First** by the best quoted price (highest bid / lowest ask).
- **Then** by the time the order entered the order book.

When matched, a **Trade Confirmation** is generated with a unique **Trade Number** and the trade is reported to the clearing corporation in real-time (T+0).

### Stage 4 — Clearing
**NSE Clearing** (or **Indian Clearing Corporation Ltd – ICCL** for BSE) becomes the **Central Counterparty (CCP)**. Through *novation*, the original buyer and seller are discharged of their mutual obligations; each now transacts with the clearing corporation. This is the single most important innovation that makes modern markets safe.

### Stage 5 — Settlement (T+1)
On **T+1 (Trade date + 1 working day)**:
- **Pay-in:** Sellers deliver shares to the pool account; buyers deposit funds with the clearing bank.
- **Pay-out:** The clearing corporation credits shares to buying Demat accounts and releases funds to selling bank accounts.

### Stage 6 — Post-Trade Reporting
The broker issues a **Contract Note** within **24 hours** (T+1) of the trade (as mandated by SEBI). This is the legal evidence of the transaction for tax and audit purposes.

---

## 2.2 Types of Transactions

| Transaction Type | Holding Period | Settlement | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delivery / Cash Market (CNC)** | Indefinite | T+1 normal settlement | Long-term investing |
| **Intraday (MIS)** | Squared-off same day | Same day (no settlement) | Quick scalping trades |
| **BTST (Buy Today, Sell Tomorrow)** | 1 day | Sold before delivery | Bridge between intraday and delivery |
| **Auction Transaction** | N/A | Through exchange auction | Settlement shortages |
| **Block / Bulk Deals** | N/A | Special window 9:15–9:30 AM | High-value negotiated deals |
| **Off-Market Transfer** | N/A | Outside exchange, via DIS slip | Gift / inheritance / private deal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BTST and STBT** are now *deprecated* for retail by many brokers. KTU examiners, however, still test the **conceptual understanding** of why a T+1 cycle makes BTST riskier than before.

---

## 2.3 The Transaction Cost Stack (The Real "Why" Behind Every Rupee)

A common KTU Part-B question asks: *"Calculate the total cost of buying 100 shares of XYZ at ₹500 each."* The answer is **not** ₹50,000. The student must add the cost stack:

| Cost Component | Levied On | Rate (Indicative, 2024) | Charged To |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brokerage** | Turnover value | ₹0 (discount brokers) or 0.1%–0.5% (traditional) | Buyer & Seller |
| **STT (Securities Transaction Tax)** | Transaction value | 0.1% (delivery buy), 0.025% (delivery sell), 0.02% (intraday sell) | Buyer (delivery) / Seller (intraday) |
| **Exchange Transaction Charge** | Turnover value | ~0.00297% (NSE) | Buyer & Seller |
| **SEBI Charges** | Turnover value | ₹10 per crore (≈ 0.0001%) | Buyer & Seller |
| **GST** | Brokerage + Exchange charge | **18%** | Buyer & Seller |
| **Stamp Duty** | Transaction value | 0.015% (delivery buy) – varies by state | Buyer only |
| **DP (Depository Participant) Charges** | Per scrip sold | ₹13 + GST (approx) per scrip per day | Seller only |
| **SEBI Turnover Fees** | Turnover value | Included in exchange charge | Both |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are exam-ready. Variables are defined in the text after each formula.

### Formula 1 — Total Purchase Cost

$$
\text{Total Buy Cost} = (Q \times P) + \text{STT}_{\text{buy}} + \text{Brokerage} + \text{GST} + \text{Stamp} + \text{Exch.Charge} + \text{SEBI}
$$

### Formula 2 — Total Sale Proceeds (Net to Seller)

$$
\text{Net Sale Proceeds} = (Q \times P) - \text{STT}_{\text{sell}} - \text{DP} - \text{Brokerage} - \text{GST} - \text{Exch.Charge} - \text{SEBI}
$$

### Formula 3 — Securities Transaction Tax (Delivery, Buy Side)

$$
\text{STT}_{\text{buy,delivery}} = 0.10\% \times Q \times P
$$

### Formula 4 — STT (Delivery, Sell Side)

$$
\text{STT}_{\text{sell,delivery}} = 0.10\% \times Q \times P
$$

### Formula 5 — Brokerage (Traditional Full-Service)

$$
\text{Brokerage} = \min\bigl(0.50\% \times Q \times P,\ \text{₹}\,20 \text{ per executed order}\bigr)
$$

### Formula 6 — Goods & Services Tax

$$
\text{GST} = 18\% \times (\text{Brokerage} + \text{Exchange Transaction Charge})
$$

### Formula 7 — Stamp Duty (Delivery, Buy Side, Maharashtra/Kerala indicative)

$$
\text{Stamp Duty} = 0.015\% \times Q \times P
$$

### Formula 8 — Break-Even Price for Round-Trip Delivery Trade

$$
P_{\text{BE}} = P_{\text{buy}} \times \left[ 1 + r_{\text{buy}} + r_{\text{sell}} + 2 \times r_{\text{other}} \right]
$$

Where the variables $\text{r}_{\text{buy}}$, $\text{r}_{\text{sell}}$, and $\text{r}_{\text{other}}$ capture the *round-trip* proportional cost rates.

### Formula 9 — Net Realised P&L on a Round Trip

$$
\text{P\&L}_{\text{net}} = Q \times (P_{\text{sell}} - P_{\text{buy}}) - \text{TC}_{\text{buy}} - \text{TC}_{\text{sell}}
$$

Where $\text{TC}$ denotes the **transaction cost** of each leg.

### Formula 10 — Bid-Ask Spread (Liquidity Indicator)

$$
\text{Spread} = P_{\text{ask}} - P_{\text{bid}}, \qquad \text{Spread \%} = \frac{P_{\text{ask}} - P_{\text{bid}}}{P_{\text{ask}}} \times 100
$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Fintech Utility

The transaction pipeline you have just studied is the literal backbone of every modern **algorithmic trading desk**, **HFT (High-Frequency Trading) colocation facility**, and **RBI-integrated UPI–securities linkage** (e.g., the 2024 SEBI UPI-as-payment-mechanism mandate). Software engineers build OMS (Order Management Systems), RMS (Risk Management Systems), and FIX-protocol gateways around the very cost stack and settlement logic covered above. Even as a non-CS engineering student, understanding this pipeline is essential if you are building any **fintech product**, **wealthtech app**, or **trading simulation** as a project.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Buying Delivery Shares (Long-Term Investor)

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical 14-Mark Sub-Part):**
Mr. Arun buys **200 shares of Infosys Ltd** at ₹1,500 per share through a traditional broker who charges 0.5% brokerage. Calculate the **total amount debited** from his bank account. Assume the broker pays 18% GST on brokerage and exchange charges, and STT of 0.1% is applicable on the buy side for delivery trades. State stamp duty = 0.015%. Exchange transaction charge = 0.00297%.

### Step 1 — Compute the Gross Transaction Value

$$
\text{Gross Value} = Q \times P = 200 \times 1{,}500 = ₹\,3{,}00{,}000
$$

**Logic:** This is the *face value* of the trade — the cost of the shares alone, before any fee. It forms the base on which nearly all percentage-based costs are calculated.

### Step 2 — Compute the Brokerage

$$
\text{Brokerage} = 0.50\% \times ₹\,3{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}500
$$

**Logic:** 0.5% of ₹3,00,000 = (0.5 / 100) × 3,00,000 = 1,500. Since the per-order minimum of ₹20 is irrelevant here, the percentage figure applies.

### Step 3 — Compute the Securities Transaction Tax (STT)

$$
\text{STT} = 0.10\% \times ₹\,3{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,300
$$

**Logic:** STT on delivery buy is 0.1% of transaction value. SEBI mandates this on the *buyer* for delivery transactions; it is the largest single cost in the stack.

### Step 4 — Compute the Exchange Transaction Charge

$$
\text{Exch. Charge} = 0.00297\% \times ₹\,3{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,8.91
$$

**Logic:** NSE/BSE levy a minuscule percentage on turnover. Multiply 0.00297/100 by 3,00,000.

### Step 5 — Compute the SEBI Charges

$$
\text{SEBI Charge} = 0.0001\% \times ₹\,3{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,3.00
$$

**Logic:** The regulator also charges a tiny fee — currently ₹10 per crore of turnover, which is 0.0001%.

### Step 6 — Compute the GST

$$
\text{GST} = 18\% \times (\text{Brokerage} + \text{Exch. Charge}) = 18\% \times (1{,}500 + 8.91)
$$

$$
\text{GST} = 18\% \times 1{,}508.91 = ₹\,271.60
$$

**Logic:** GST is **not** levied on the full transaction value. It is levied *only* on the brokerage plus the exchange transaction charge, as per the 2022 SEBI/CCoI clarification.

### Step 7 — Compute the Stamp Duty

$$
\text{Stamp Duty} = 0.015\% \times ₹\,3{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,45.00
$$

**Logic:** Stamp duty is a state subject; 0.015% is the indicative rate for delivery buys (varies by state, e.g., Maharashtra vs Kerala). The exchange collects it on behalf of the state.

### Step 8 — Sum All Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{Gross Value} + \text{STT} + \text{Brokerage} + \text{GST} + \text{Stamp} + \text{Exch.Charge} + \text{SEBI} \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 + 300 + 1{,}500 + 271.60 + 45 + 8.91 + 3 \\
&= ₹\,3{,}02{,}128.51
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Round to Two Decimal Places

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Amount Debited} = ₹\,3{,}02{,}128.51}
$$

**Interpretation:** Mr. Arun pays an *effective* additional cost of approximately **0.71 %** of the trade value. This is the **break-even hurdle** the share price must clear on the *sell side* before he realises any profit.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Selling Delivery Shares (Net Realisation)

**Problem Statement:**
Mr. Arun later sells all **200 shares of Infosys** at ₹1,600 each (same broker, same cost structure). DP charges = ₹13 + 18% GST per scrip. Compute the **net amount credited** to his bank account.

### Step 1 — Compute the Gross Sale Value

$$
\text{Gross Sale} = 200 \times 1{,}600 = ₹\,3{,}20{,}000
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Brokerage

$$
\text{Brokerage} = 0.50\% \times 3{,}20{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}600
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the STT (Delivery Sell)

$$
\text{STT}_{\text{sell,delivery}} = 0.10\% \times 3{,}20{,}000 = ₹\,320
$$

**Logic:** On the *sell* leg of a delivery trade, STT is 0.1% of the sale value — the same as the buy side. (For intraday, however, STT is only 0.025% and is levied on the *sell* leg only.)

### Step 4 — Compute Exchange + SEBI Charges

$$
\text{Exch. Charge} = 0.00297\% \times 3{,}20{,}000 = ₹\,9.50
$$

$$
\text{SEBI Charge} = 0.0001\% \times 3{,}20{,}000 = ₹\,3.20
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the GST

$$
\text{GST} = 18\% \times (1{,}600 + 9.50) = 18\% \times 1{,}609.50 = ₹\,289.71
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the DP Charges

$$
\text{DP} = ₹\,13 + 18\% \times 13 = 13 + 2.34 = ₹\,15.34
$$

**Logic:** DP charges are flat *per scrip per day* — meaning if you sold 3 different stocks in one day, you would pay ₹15.34 × 3. There is **no DP charge on the buy side**.

### Step 7 — Sum the Deductions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Deduction} &= 1{,}600 + 320 + 9.50 + 3.20 + 289.71 + 15.34 = ₹\,2{,}237.75
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute the Net Realisation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Credit} &= \text{Gross Sale} - \text{Total Deduction} \\
&= 3{,}20{,}000 - 2{,}237.75 = ₹\,3{,}17{,}762.25
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Net Credited to Bank} = ₹\,3{,}17{,}762.25}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Net P&L and Break-Even Calculation

Continuing the Arun example, compute the **net profit** and the **break-even selling price**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Gross P&L

$$
\text{Gross P\&L} = Q \times (P_{\text{sell}} - P_{\text{buy}}) = 200 \times (1{,}600 - 1{,}500) = ₹\,20{,}000
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Total Round-Trip Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TC}_{\text{buy}} &= \text{STT}_{\text{buy}} + \text{Brokerage}_{\text{buy}} + \text{GST}_{\text{buy}} + \text{Stamp} + \text{Exch}_{\text{buy}} + \text{SEBI}_{\text{buy}} \\
&= 300 + 1{,}500 + 271.60 + 45 + 8.91 + 3 = ₹\,2{,}128.51
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TC}_{\text{sell}} &= 320 + 1{,}600 + 289.71 + 15.34 + 9.50 + 3.20 = ₹\,2{,}237.75
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{TC}_{\text{total}} = 2{,}128.51 + 2{,}237.75 = ₹\,4{,}366.26
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Net P&L

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net P\&L} &= \text{Gross P\&L} - \text{TC}_{\text{total}} \\
&= 20{,}000 - 4{,}366.26 = ₹\,15{,}633.74
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Net Profit} = ₹\,15{,}633.74}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Break-Even Selling Price

At break-even, the net P&L = 0. Let $P_{BE}$ be the unknown break-even sell price.

$$
\begin{aligned}
200 \times (P_{BE} - 1{,}500) &= 4{,}366.26 \\
P_{BE} - 1{,}500 &= 21.83 \\
P_{BE} &= ₹\,1{,}521.83
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Infosys must rise by **at least ₹21.83 per share (≈ 1.46 %)** from Mr. Arun's buy price for him to recover all transaction friction and start booking actual profit. This 1.46 % is the **round-trip breakeven hurdle rate** — a concept KTU examiners love.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Order Type Behaviour

| Order Type | Guarantee of Execution? | Guarantee of Price? | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Order** | Yes (if liquidity) | No | Speed is paramount |
| **Limit Order** | No | Yes | Discipline / target price |
| **Stop-Loss (SL)** | No | No | Risk management |
| **Stop-Loss Market (SL-M)** | Yes (after trigger) | No | Gap-down protection |
| **Stop-Loss Limit (SL-L)** | No | Yes (after trigger) | Tight risk control |
| **Bracket Order (BO)** | Auto squared-off | Yes | Fixed risk-reward |
| **Cover Order (CO)** | Mandatory SL attached | Yes | Intraday with stop |
| **GTT (Good Till Triggered)** | No | Yes | Long-term target / SL |
| **Iceberg** | Yes (partial) | Yes | Large institutional orders |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, when asked to "compare market and limit orders", use the *guarantee of execution vs guarantee of price* framing. Examiners award full marks only when both axes are discussed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Transaction Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Places Order via Trading App] --> B{Broker Validates Order RMS}
    B -- Rejected --> X[Order Rejected: Margin Risk]
    B -- Approved --> C[Order Routed to Exchange NEAT NOW]
    C --> D[Exchange Order Matching Engine]
    D --> E{Price Time Priority Match Found}
    E -- No Match --> Y[Order sits in Order Book]
    E -- Match Found --> F[Trade Executed Trade Number Generated]
    F --> G[Trade Sent to Clearing Corporation NCL ICCL]
    G --> H[Novation: CCP Becomes Counterparty]
    H --> I[Pay-in on T 1: Shares and Funds]
    I --> J[Pay-out on T 1: Demat Credit and Bank Credit]
    J --> K[Broker Issues Contract Note within 24 hrs]
    K --> L[Transaction Complete and Auditable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU board exam, you can re-draw the above using simple **rectangular blocks** if the examiner specifies a hand-drawn diagram. The flow is: *Investor → Broker → Exchange → Clearing → Settlement → Contract Note*.

---

## 4.2 Cost Stack — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1[Gross Transaction Value]
        GV[Q times P]
    end

    subgraph IN2[Buyer Side Cost Components]
        STTB[STT 0.10 percent]
        BRB[Brokerage 0 to 0.50 percent]
        GSTB[GST 18 percent on Brokerage and Exchange Charge]
        STB[Stamp Duty 0.015 percent indicative]
        ECB[Exchange Transaction Charge 0.00297 percent]
        SEBIB[SEBI Charge 0.0001 percent]
    end

    subgraph OUT1[Seller Side Cost Components]
        STTS[STT 0.10 percent delivery or 0.025 percent intraday]
        BRS[Brokerage]
        GSTS[GST]
        ECS[Exchange Charge]
        SEBIS[SEBI Charge]
        DPS[DP Charge 13 rupees per scrip]
    end

    GV --> STTB
    GV --> BRB
    GV --> STB
    GV --> ECB
    GV --> SEBIB
    BRB --> GSTB
    ECB --> GSTB

    GV --> STTS
    GV --> BRS
    GV --> ECS
    GV --> SEBIS
    BRS --> GSTS
    ECS --> GSTS
    DPS --> OUT1
```

---

## 4.3 Settlement Cycle Map — T+1 Rolling Settlement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Trade Day T 0: Orders Matched on Exchange] --> T0A[Clearing Corp Computes Net Obligations]
    T0A --> T1A[T 1 Morning: Pay-in Window 9 00 to 11 00 AM]
    T1A --> T1B[Clearing Corp Runs Delivery versus Payment]
    T1B --> T1C[Pay-out: Shares and Funds Released by Evening]
    T1C --> T1D[Contract Note Issued by Broker]
    T1D --> T1E[Auction if Short: T 2 Morning Exchange Auction]
    T1E --> T1F[Final Closeout: T 2 Plus]
```

**Pedagogical Note:** Before January 2023, India followed a **T+1 trade-day + 1 working-day** cycle, which was the world's shortest major-market settlement cycle. The migration reduced systemic risk and margin requirements by ~50%.

---

## 4.4 Bid-Ask Spread Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Market Maker Quotes Best Bid 1500] --> C[Order Book]
    B[Market Maker Quotes Best Ask 1501] --> C
    C --> D[Spread = 1 Rupee]
    C --> E[Spread Percentage = 0.066 percent]
```

A **narrow spread** (e.g., ₹1 on a ₹1500 stock) indicates **high liquidity**; a wide spread indicates **low liquidity** and higher implicit transaction cost.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET745]

**"Define a securities market transaction. List the three pillars of a valid transaction under the SCRA framework."**

**Model Answer:**
A securities market transaction is the complete process of transferring ownership of a financial instrument between a buyer and a seller, executed through a recognised stock exchange, cleared by a clearing corporation, and settled through a depository against the exchange of cash and scrip.

The three pillars under the **Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956** are:
1. **Trade Execution** — orders matched on the exchange's order-matching engine.
2. **Trade Clearing** — the clearing corporation guarantees both sides by becoming the central counterparty (novation).
3. **Trade Settlement** — final exchange of money and shares, currently on a **T+1 rolling settlement** basis.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET745]

**"Distinguish between a market order and a limit order. When would a trader prefer a stop-loss order over a limit order?"**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Market Order | Limit Order |
|---|---|---|
| Guarantee of execution | Yes (with available liquidity) | No — only executes at desired price or better |
| Guarantee of price | No — fills at best available price | Yes — fills only at the limit price or better |
| Use-case | Speed-critical, momentum trades | Disciplined, target-price entries |

A trader prefers a **stop-loss order** over a limit order when the primary goal is **risk management** rather than price targeting — i.e., the trader wants to *automatically exit* a long position if the price falls below a pre-defined trigger, thereby capping the loss without needing to monitor the screen continuously. A limit order, by contrast, may never execute if the price does not reverse to the limit level.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** When a question asks *"explain the transaction cost stack"*, students often list the components but **forget to specify which side (buyer/seller) pays each charge**. STT and DP charges are side-specific; the examiner deducts 2–3 marks if you write vaguely like "STT is charged" without "STT is charged on the *buyer* in a delivery trade."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Many students use **outdated T+2 settlement data** in their answers. From January 2023, India operates on **T+1**. Using T+2 in a 2024 exam costs 1 full mark on any question that references the settlement cycle.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET745]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the various components of the transaction cost stack in the Indian cash-equity market. Clearly indicate which side (buyer or seller) bears each cost and the indicative rate for 2024.**

**Model Answer:**

A stock market transaction in India is never settled at the *face value* of the trade. The investor pays (or receives less than) a stack of regulatory, statutory, and service charges collectively known as the **transaction cost stack**. The components are:

**(i) Securities Transaction Tax (STT):**
Levied by the Government of India under the **STT Act, 2004** and collected by the exchange. The current rates (2024) are:
- **0.10% on delivery buy and sell** — charged on transaction value, paid by both buyer and seller.
- **0.025% on intraday sell** — paid by the seller only; no STT on intraday buy.
- **0.05% on options premium (sell side)** and **0.15% on futures (sell side)** for F&O.

**(ii) Brokerage:**
The fee charged by the stockbroker. Discount brokers charge **₹0** (Zerodha, Groww). Traditional brokers charge **0.1% to 0.5%** of turnover, or a minimum of ₹20 per executed order, whichever is lower. Both buyer and seller pay it.

**(iii) Goods & Services Tax (GST):**
**18%** levied on **brokerage + exchange transaction charge** (and also on DP charges for the seller). It is *not* levied on STT, stamp duty, or the principal value of the trade.

**(iv) Exchange Transaction Charge:**
NSE charges approximately **0.00297%** of turnover; BSE rates are similar. Both buyer and seller pay it.

**(v) SEBI Charges:**
A regulatory fee of **₹10 per crore** of turnover (≈ 0.0001%). Both buyer and seller pay it.

**(vi) Stamp Duty:**
A state-level duty, currently (post-July 2020 amendment) collected uniformly by the exchange at source. Indicative rate is **0.015% on delivery buy**. Sellers do not pay stamp duty on stock purchases; buyers do not pay it on sale.

**(vii) Depository Participant (DP) Charges:**
Levied only on the **seller** for each scrip sold on a given day. Typical rate: **₹13 + 18% GST per scrip per day**. This covers the cost of debiting shares from the Demat account.

**(viii) IPFT / SEBI Investor Protection Fund Trust:**
A tiny charge bundled into the exchange transaction charge.

A clear side-by-side table is the recommended way to present this in the answer script (mark a 5×2 or 6×2 table and shade the cost side).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Mr. Sharma buys 500 shares of TCS at ₹3,800 per share on a delivery basis. His broker charges 0.3% brokerage. STT on delivery buy is 0.1%, exchange charge 0.00297%, SEBI charge 0.0001%, GST 18% on (brokerage + exchange charge), and stamp duty 0.015%. Compute the total amount debited from Mr. Sharma's bank account.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Gross Value:**

$$
\text{GV} = 500 \times 3{,}800 = ₹\,19{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Brokerage:**

$$
\text{Brokerage} = 0.30\% \times 19{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,5{,}700
$$

**Step 3 — STT:**

$$
\text{STT} = 0.10\% \times 19{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}900
$$

**Step 4 — Exchange Transaction Charge:**

$$
\text{Exch.} = 0.00297\% \times 19{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,56.43
$$

**Step 5 — SEBI Charge:**

$$
\text{SEBI} = 0.0001\% \times 19{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,1.90
$$

**Step 6 — GST (on Brokerage + Exchange):**

$$
\text{GST} = 18\% \times (5{,}700 + 56.43) = 18\% \times 5{,}756.43 = ₹\,1{,}036.16
$$

**Step 7 — Stamp Duty:**

$$
\text{Stamp} = 0.015\% \times 19{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,285
$$

**Step 8 — Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 19{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}700 + 1{,}900 + 56.43 + 1.90 + 1{,}036.16 + 285 \\
&= ₹\,19{,}08{,}979.49
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Debited} = ₹\,19{,}08{,}979.49}
$$

**Additional Insights to Write:**
- Effective cost over and above trade value = ₹8,979.49 (≈ 0.473 %).
- For Mr. Sharma to break even, TCS must rise by at least ₹17.96 per share (₹8,979.49 ÷ 500).
- The single largest avoidable cost is brokerage — switching to a discount broker would save ₹5,700 (about 60% of total friction).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET745] — INTERNAL CHOICE

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Describe the six-stage transaction lifecycle in the Indian securities market, beginning from order origination to the issuance of a contract note."**

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1 — Order Origination:**
The investor decides to buy or sell a security. The order is entered into the broker's trading platform with mandatory parameters — symbol, action (buy/sell), quantity, price, order type, and product code. Identity verification and OTP-based authentication (post-2020 SEBI mandate) ensure only the registered account holder places the order.

**Stage 2 — Order Routing:**
The broker's Order Management System (OMS) routes the order to the exchange's electronic trading platform. For NSE, this is the **NEAT/NOW** system; for BSE, the **BOLT Plus** system. Brokers often use **colocation** to reduce latency to microseconds.

**Stage 3 — Trade Execution:**
The exchange's order-matching engine matches orders on **price-time priority** — the best price gets priority; ties are broken by the time the order entered the book. A successful match generates a **Trade Confirmation** with a unique **Trade Number**, time-stamped at the millisecond level.

**Stage 4 — Trade Clearing:**
The trade data is pushed in real-time to the clearing corporation (**NSE Clearing Ltd** or **Indian Clearing Corporation Ltd** for BSE). The clearing corporation becomes the **Central Counterparty (CCP)** through **novation**, guaranteeing settlement to both buyer and seller. Margin collection, collateral management, and risk management (e.g., **VaR-based SPAN margin**) happen here.

**Stage 5 — Settlement (T+1):**
On the next working day (T+1), the **pay-in** happens between 9:00 AM and 11:00 AM: buyers deposit funds, sellers deposit shares with the clearing corporation. After reconciliation, the **pay-out** happens by evening: shares are credited to the buyer's Demat account, and funds are credited to the seller's bank account via the clearing bank.

**Stage 6 — Contract Note:**
Within 24 hours of the trade (mandated by SEBI), the broker issues a **Contract Note** — a legal document listing every trade with the unique trade number, quantity, price, brokerage, STT, exchange charge, SEBI charge, GST, stamp duty, and the net amount debited/credited. The contract note is the legal basis for capital gains computation and tax filing.

**Compliance Add-on:** All of the above are recorded in the broker's books and reported to SEBI via the **Compliance Reporting System (CRS)** and to the tax department via **Form 26AS / AIS (Annual Information Statement)**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Compare and contrast delivery (CNC) and intraday (MIS) transactions in terms of settlement, cost stack, margin, and risk profile."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Delivery (CNC) | Intraday (MIS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Holding Period** | Indefinite (until investor decides to sell) | Squared-off before market close (3:15 PM NSE) |
| **Settlement** | T+1 normal settlement (shares actually go into Demat) | Same day; no real settlement of shares (contract is squared off in cash) |
| **STT** | 0.1% on **both** buy and sell side | 0.025% on **sell** side only; 0% on buy side |
| **Margin Required** | Full payment (100% of trade value) up-front | Brokerage-tier based; often 1x to 5x leverage available |
| **Brokerage** | Same | Same (but base is turnover on each side) |
| **Stamp Duty** | 0.015% on buy side | 0.003% on buy side (lower) |
| **DP Charges** | Yes, on sell | Not applicable (no Demat movement) |
| **Risk Profile** | Moderate — overnight gap risk | High — intraday volatility + auto square-off risk |
| **Suitability** | Long-term investors, SWP/STP users | Active traders, day-traders, scalpers |
| **Tax Treatment** | LTCG (>12 months) taxed at 10% above ₹1 lakh; STCG at 15% | Always taxed as **speculative business income** (Section 43(5)) at slab rate |

**Conclusion to Write:** A delivery transaction is *settlement-rich* (actual share movement) and *tax-efficient for long horizons*; an intraday transaction is *cash-settled*, *cheaper in fees on a single leg*, but *tax-inefficient* (every trade is taxed as business income) and *riskier* due to intraday volatility and the broker's auto-square-off trigger.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** When comparing CNC vs MIS, students often forget to mention the **T+1 settlement difference** and the **taxation difference (Section 43(5))**. Both are *favourite* valuation spots. Allocating at least 1 mark each for these two points is essential.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board-Revision Punch-List — Module 4: Transactions**

- [ ] A **transaction** is the *trade + clearing + settlement* process; not merely an order click.
- [ ] Current Indian settlement cycle is **T+1** (since **23 January 2023**). Earlier it was T+2.
- [ ] Three pillars: **Trade → Clearing → Settlement**, governed by **SCRA 1956** and **SEBI Act 1992**.
- [ ] Clearing corporation acts as **Central Counterparty (CCP)** through **novation** — this is the key risk-mitigation innovation.
- [ ] **Price-Time Priority** is the matching algorithm on Indian exchanges.
- [ ] The **cost stack** has **8 components**: Brokerage, STT, GST, Exchange Charge, SEBI Charge, Stamp Duty, DP Charges, and IPFT.
- [ ] **STT (delivery)** = 0.1% on **both** buy and sell. **STT (intraday)** = 0.025% on **sell** only.
- [ ] **GST** = **18%** on **brokerage + exchange transaction charge** (and on DP charge for seller). **Not on STT, stamp duty, or principal.**
- [ ] **Stamp duty** = **0.015% on delivery buy** (0.003% on intraday buy). Sellers do not pay.
- [ ] **DP charges** = **₹13 + GST per scrip per day**, paid **only by seller** on **delivery** trades.
- [ ] **Brokerage** discount brokers = **₹0 flat**; traditional = **0.1%–0.5%** or **₹20 min** per executed order.
- [ ] **Contract Note** must be issued by the broker **within 24 hours** — it is the legal tax document.
- [ ] **Market order** = execution guarantee, no price guarantee. **Limit order** = price guarantee, no execution guarantee. **Stop-loss** = risk-management tool, not a price-targeting tool.
- [ ] **Bid-Ask Spread** = Ask − Bid. **Narrow spread** = high liquidity. **Wide spread** = low liquidity & higher implicit cost.
- [ ] **Break-even hurdle rate** for a delivery round-trip = sum of all *round-trip* percentage costs (typically 0.5% – 1.5% depending on broker).
- [ ] **Delivery (CNC) vs Intraday (MIS)**: differ in *settlement, STT, stamp duty, DP, margin, and tax treatment (Section 43(5))*.
- [ ] The **Depositories Act, 1996** governs Demat. The two depositories are **NSDL** and **CDSL**.
- [ ] The **mandatory** retail-payment route for cash market is the **UPI** mechanism (SEBI 2024 mandate for small-ticket trades).
- [ ] Remember to **mention the regulatory authority** (SEBI / RBI / CBDT) for each charge in any 7-mark or 14-mark answer — examiners explicitly reward this.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-day mnemonic for the cost stack:** *"**B**ig **S**tudents **G**et **E**xcellent **S**tamp **D**iscounts"* → **B**rokerage, **S**TT, **G**ST, **E**xchange charge, **S**EBI, **S**tamp duty, **D**P charge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: COMMODITY TRADING - BENEFITS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commodity Trading** refers to the buying and selling of raw or primary products (such as gold, silver, crude oil, natural gas, agricultural products like wheat, rice, cotton, spices, and metal commodities like copper, aluminum, zinc) through regulated, standardized futures and spot contracts on recognized commodity exchanges, with the primary purpose of facilitating **price discovery**, **risk transfer**, and **investment diversification**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the **Benefits of Commodity Trading** are classified into two distinct functional categories:

1. **Economic Benefits** — Benefits derived from the market structure itself (price discovery, transparency, liquidity).
2. **Investment Benefits** — Benefits that accrue to traders, hedgers, speculators, and portfolio managers (diversification, inflation hedge, leverage).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Spice Bazaar Analogy:** Imagine you are a farmer in Wayanad, Kerala, who grows pepper. You plant your crop today, but it will only be ready in 6 months. You are worried — what if by harvest time, the price of pepper crashes because someone in Vietnam produced too much? What if a drought destroys your crop and you have no money?
>
> **Commodity markets solve this twin problem:**
> - You can **lock in today's price** for pepper to be delivered in December, even though your crop isn't harvested yet (this is called **hedging**).
> - Alternatively, a trader in Kochi who has never seen a pepper farm can **bet on the future price** of pepper and profit if she guesses correctly (this is **speculation**).
>
> The exchange acts like a giant, fair, government-supervised marketplace where farmers, traders, companies, and speculators can all meet, agree on prices today for delivery tomorrow, and transfer risk to whoever is best equipped to bear it.

### 1.3 Classification of Commodities Traded

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Categorization of Tradable Commodities:**
>
> | Category | Examples | Indian Exchange |
> |----------|----------|-----------------|
> | **Precious Metals** | Gold, Silver, Platinum | MCX |
> | **Base Metals** | Copper, Aluminum, Zinc, Lead, Nickel | MCX |
> | **Energy** | Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Heating Oil | MCX |
> | **Agricultural** | Wheat, Rice, Cotton, Sugar, Spices | NCDEX, MCX |
> | **Bullion** | Gold Guineas, Silver Bars | MCX |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Core Benefits of Commodity Trading (Decomposed)

Below is the exhaustive KTU-aligned decomposition of all benefits taught in **PEIET745 Module 4**.

### Benefit 1: Portfolio Diversification

- **Why it works:** Commodity returns have a historically low or even negative correlation with equity (stock) and bond returns.
- **How it helps:** When the stock market crashes, gold and crude oil often move independently, cushioning the portfolio from total collapse.
- **Engineering/Finance Utility:** A well-diversified portfolio combining equities + commodities has a **lower standard deviation (σ)** for the same expected return, due to the variance reduction formula.

### Benefit 2: Inflation Hedge

- **Why it works:** Commodity prices rise when the general price level in the economy rises.
- **How it helps:** Gold, silver, and crude oil historically preserve purchasing power during inflation, which erodes the real value of cash and fixed-income instruments.
- **Real-world context:** In India during 2022-2023, when WPI inflation peaked, MCX Gold delivered ~14% returns, far outpacing fixed deposits.

### Benefit 3: High Leverage & Margin Trading

- **Why it works:** Commodity exchanges require only a small fraction (margin) of the contract value to trade.
- **How it helps:** A trader can control a ₹50 lakh gold contract with just ₹3-5 lakh margin, magnifying both gains and losses.
- **Key Risk:** Leverage is a double-edged sword — it is a **benefit and a hazard**.

### Benefit 4: High Liquidity

- **Why it works:** Major commodity contracts (Gold, Crude Oil) have globally standardized specifications traded 24 hours.
- **How it helps:** Traders can enter and exit positions almost instantly at fair market prices without significant slippage.

### Benefit 5: Price Transparency & Discovery

- **Why it works:** Prices are determined by open outcry or electronic matching of buy/sell orders visible to all participants.
- **How it helps:** Eliminates information asymmetry — a small farmer in Karnataka has access to the same global price as a multinational in Singapore.

### Benefit 6: Lower Transaction Costs

- **Why it works:** Brokerage in commodity futures is significantly lower than equity delivery trades.
- **How it helps:** High-frequency traders and hedgers benefit from cheap round-trip transactions.

### Benefit 7: Risk Management Through Hedging

- **Why it works:** Producers (e.g., wheat farmers) and consumers (e.g., flour mills) face opposite price risks.
- **How it helps:** Both can use futures contracts to **lock in prices**, eliminating uncertainty. This is the **original economic purpose** of commodity markets.

### Benefit 8: Global Market Access & 24-Hour Trading

- **Why it works:** Electronic connectivity links Indian MCX with global benchmarks (COMEX, LME, NYMEX).
- **How it helps:** Indian traders react to overnight global events and benefit from continuous price discovery.

### Benefit 9: Speculative Profit Opportunities

- **Why it works:** Volatility in commodity prices creates frequent trading opportunities.
- **How it helps:** Speculators provide liquidity to hedgers in exchange for bearing price risk — they earn the **risk premium**.

### Benefit 10: Regulated & Safe Environment

- **Why it works:** SEBI regulates commodity markets in India since 2015, ensuring transparency, segregated client funds, and dispute resolution.
- **How it helps:** Reduces counterparty default risk compared to informal commodity trading.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes all mathematical relationships a KTU student must know for this topic.** Note: Use `$\vert$` for absolute values to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mark-to-Market (MTM) Margin** | $\text{MTM} = (P_t - P_{t-1}) \times Q \times \text{Lot Size}$ | Daily settlement of futures |
| 2 | **Contract Value** | $V_c = P \times Q \times \text{Lot Size}$ | Total notional exposure |
| 3 | **Effective Leverage Ratio** | $\text{Leverage} = \dfrac{V_c}{\text{Margin Paid}}$ | Quantifying leverage benefit |
| 4 | **Portfolio Variance (2 assets)** | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1w_2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2$ | Diversification benefit |
| 5 | **Correlation Range** | $-1 \leq \rho \leq +1$ | Measuring diversification benefit |
| 6 | **Hedge Ratio (Optimal)** | $h^{\ast} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F}$ | Minimum variance hedge |
| 7 | **Return on Margin (ROM)** | $\text{ROM} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\text{Margin \%}}$ | Leverage benefit quantification |
| 8 | **Inflation-Adjusted Return** | $\text{Real Return} \approx \text{Nominal Return} - \text{Inflation Rate}$ | Inflation hedge benefit |
| 9 | **Bid-Ask Spread (Liquidity)** | $\text{Spread} = P_{\text{ask}} - P_{\text{bid}}$ | Liquidity benefit measure |
| 10 | **Speculator Profit (Long)** | $\text{Profit} = (P_{\text{sell}} - P_{\text{buy}}) \times Q - \text{Charges}$ | Speculation benefit |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility Across Engineering & Finance Fields

- **For Industrial Engineers:** Aluminum and copper futures help procurement managers hedge raw material costs.
- **For Civil Engineers:** Steel and cement commodity hedging protects infrastructure project budgets.
- **For IT/Data Engineers:** Algorithmic trading in commodities requires real-time data pipelines.
- **For MBA Students:** Portfolio managers use commodity ETFs (like Gold Bees) for diversification.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Derivation 1: Quantifying the Leverage Benefit

**Problem:** A trader buys 1 MCX Gold Mini futures contract.
- **Lot Size:** 100 grams
- **Margin Required:** 5% of contract value
- **Spot Price at Entry:** ₹6,000/gram
- **Spot Price after 5 days:** ₹6,180/gram

**Find:** (a) Contract Value, (b) Margin Paid, (c) Effective Leverage, (d) Absolute Profit, (e) Return on Margin.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the Total Contract Value**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= P \times Q \times \text{Lot Size} \\
V_c &= 6000 \times 1 \times 100 \\
V_c &= \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The contract value equals the price per gram multiplied by the number of lots and the standardized lot size.

**Step 2: Calculate the Margin Paid (Capital Outlay)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= V_c \times \text{Margin \%} \\
M &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 \\
M &= \text{₹}30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The trader deposits only 5% of the contract value as margin with the exchange.

**Step 3: Calculate the Effective Leverage Ratio**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Leverage} &= \frac{V_c}{M} \\
\text{Leverage} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} \\
\text{Leverage} &= 20\times
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The trader controls ₹6 lakh worth of gold with only ₹30,000 capital, achieving 20:1 leverage.

**Step 4: Calculate the Absolute Profit on Price Movement**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= (P_{\text{exit}} - P_{\text{entry}}) \times Q \times \text{Lot Size} \\
\text{Profit} &= (6180 - 6000) \times 1 \times 100 \\
\text{Profit} &= 180 \times 100 \\
\text{Profit} &= \text{₹}18{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The ₹180 price rise per gram, multiplied by 100 grams, yields ₹18,000 profit.

**Step 5: Calculate the Return on Margin (ROM)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM} &= \frac{\text{Profit}}{M} \times 100 \\
\text{ROM} &= \frac{18{,}000}{30{,}000} \times 100 \\
\text{ROM} &= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The price of gold rose only 3% (180/6000), but the trader's capital grew by 60%, demonstrating the leverage benefit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This 20× leverage is exactly why commodity trading is considered a powerful wealth-creation tool — but also why it carries amplified risk. A mere 3% adverse move would wipe out the entire ₹30,000 margin.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivation 2: Quantifying the Diversification Benefit

**Problem:** A portfolio manager holds two assets:
- **Asset A (Equities - Nifty 50):** Standard Deviation $\sigma_A = 20\%$, Weight $w_A = 0.6$
- **Asset B (Gold Futures):** Standard Deviation $\sigma_B = 15\%$, Weight $w_B = 0.4$
- **Correlation:** $\rho_{AB} = -0.2$ (negative, due to safe-haven effect)

**Find:** Portfolio standard deviation with and without diversification.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Apply the 2-Asset Portfolio Variance Formula**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho \sigma_A \sigma_B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= (0.6)^2 (0.20)^2 + (0.4)^2 (0.15)^2 + 2(0.6)(0.4)(-0.2)(0.20)(0.15)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate Each Term**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1: } (0.36)(0.04) &= 0.0144 \\
\text{Term 2: } (0.16)(0.0225) &= 0.0036 \\
\text{Term 3: } (0.48)(-0.2)(0.03) &= -0.00288
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Sum the Terms**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p^2 &= 0.0144 + 0.0036 - 0.00288 \\
\sigma_p^2 &= 0.01512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Take the Square Root to Get Portfolio Standard Deviation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p &= \sqrt{0.01512} \\
\sigma_p &= 0.1229 \text{ or } 12.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compare with the Weighted-Average (No Diversification) Standard Deviation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{no div}} &= w_A \sigma_A + w_B \sigma_B \\
\sigma_{\text{no div}} &= (0.6)(0.20) + (0.4)(0.15) \\
\sigma_{\text{no div}} &= 0.12 + 0.06 \\
\sigma_{\text{no div}} &= 0.18 \text{ or } 18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the Diversification Benefit**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Benefit} &= \sigma_{\text{no div}} - \sigma_p \\
\text{Benefit} &= 18\% - 12.29\% \\
\text{Benefit} &= 5.71\% \text{ reduction in portfolio risk}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The negative correlation between gold and equities reduces total portfolio volatility from 18% to 12.29% — a clear, quantifiable **diversification benefit**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Derivation 3: Quantifying the Inflation Hedge Benefit

**Problem:** An investor holds ₹10,00,000 in a bank FD yielding 7% nominal return. WPI inflation is 6.5%.

**Find:** (a) Nominal return, (b) Real return, (c) How Gold (returning 14%) preserves wealth.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Nominal Return on FD**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{nominal}} &= \text{Principal} \times \text{Rate} \\
R_{\text{nominal}} &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.07 \\
R_{\text{nominal}} &= \text{₹}70{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Real Return (using Fisher Approximation)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{real}} &\approx R_{\text{nominal}} - \text{Inflation} \\
R_{\text{real}} &\approx 7\% - 6.5\% \\
R_{\text{real}} &\approx 0.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Return on Gold Investment (Same Principal)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{gold}} &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.14 \\
R_{\text{gold}} &= \text{₹}1{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Inflation-Adjusted Return on Gold**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{gold, real}} &\approx 14\% - 6.5\% \\
R_{\text{gold, real}} &\approx 7.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The FD earned a real return of just 0.5%, while gold earned a real return of 7.5% — a **15× superior inflation-adjusted return**. This is the **inflation hedge benefit** of commodities.

---
